visits.text: A Coruña—Coruña in Galician. City of 245,000 inhabitants facing the Atlantic, with a more open and modern character than other Galician cities. The nickname: "city of glass," for the glazed galleries on Avenida da Mariña façades—typical of the 19th century, designed to capture every sun ray and protect from sea winds. It's Galicia's most emblematic urban waterfront: take the Mariña from the port to Méndez Núñez gardens, about 1 km promenade.

The Tower of Hercules, 2.5 km north of center. UNESCO-listed in 2009—it's the only Roman lighthouse operating since antiquity, still today. Built late 1st century CE, 34 meters Roman + 21 meters neoclassical restoration in 1791 by Eustaquio Giannini, totaling 55 meters. The farum Brigantium guided ships on the Atlantic sea route linking Mediterranean to northern Europe. Celtic legend claims Hercules buried the giant Geryon's head there. 234 steps to summit, 360° view over Atlantic, Cíes Islands south on clear days.

The sculpture park around the Tower of Hercules houses some emblematic works: Manolo Paz's Menhirs for Peace, Daniel Buren's Marine Snail, and especially the colossal statue of Breogán—mythological Celtic hero who allegedly founded the city of Brigantia (ancient Coruña). The Monte dos Bicos petroglyphs, carved in the Iron Age, recall the site was sacred well before Romans. Beside it, the 19th-century Muslim cemetery—surprising in Coruña—where Maghrebi sailors who died in shipwrecks are buried. Moving.

Plaza de María Pita, heart of the modern city, owes its name to the local heroine who allegedly saved the city from Francis Drake's English attack in May 1589. According to chronicle: her husband dead in defense, María Pita seized a lance, killed the English standard-bearer and routed the assailants crying "Quien tenga honra, que me siga." 160 by 100 meters, surrounded by arcades, city hall (1909) at center. On sunny terraces, Coruñeses take coffee at any sun ray—rare commodity in Galician winter.

The Castle of San Antón, at the port entrance, occupies a rocky islet connected by footbridge. Fortress built in the 16th century to defend Coruña from English attacks—Drake's in 1589, precisely, which inspired the heroine María Pita. Today provincial archaeological museum: Iron Age petroglyphs, Celtic torque of Burela in solid gold, Roman funerary stelae. Small museum, never crowded, magnificent bay view from ramparts.

The Old Town, on the peninsula north of the port, keeps Coruña's historic soul. Plaza de Santa Bárbara, with its convent founded in 1613, is one of the city's quietest corners—cobbles, cloistered monastery, cats in sun. Beside it, the Church of Santiago, 12th-century Romanesque, the city's oldest. The Collegiate Church of Santa María del Campo, 13th-century Gothic, worth it for its carved tympanum. Pedestrian quarter, perfect for strolling.

The Domus—Casa do Home—is one of Coruña's three science museums, dedicated to humans: genetics, anatomy, perception, brain. Building on cliff designed by Arata Isozaki in 1995, curved slate façade hugging the coast's contour. Beside it, the house-museum of Emilia Pardo Bazán, Spanish naturalist novel pioneer (1851-1921), feminist figure before her time. Rare combination: contemporary science and 19th-century literature, 200 meters from each other.

To breathe sea air, climb Monte de San Pedro at the Paseo Marítimo's western end. Former military fort converted to panoramic park, accessible by glass elevator (€1.50 round trip) climbing the cliff face. At summit, two 1929 Vickers cannons, 360° view over Atlantic and Tower of Hercules east. Memorable sunset. Panoramic restaurant-viewpoint for lunch on site. Bus 14 or car to elevator base.

activities.text: Walk along the Paseo Marítimo—13 kilometers of waterfront, one of Europe's longest. Coruñeses do all their sports here: running, cycling, skating, walking. Historic tram in summer service, covering part of the paseo. Urban beaches: Riazor (sporty, winter surfing) and Orzán (wilder). Sunset from Monte de San Pedro (western end, panoramic view, glass panoramic elevator) is a highlight.

Dinner in the Pescadería quarter or around Plaza de María Pita—Coruña's cuisine is entirely fish and seafood-focused. Reliable addresses: Mesón do Pulpo, Pablo Gallego, Casa Pardo, As Salinas (Bib Gourmand starred). Specialties: centollo (spider crab), percebes (goose barnacles, controversial but cult), nécoras (velvet crabs), pulpo a feira, lubina (sea bass). Albariño or Godello bottles to accompany. Count €50 per person for quality complete dinner.

Half-day excursion to wild beaches northwest of Coruña—toward Caión, Sabón, Razo. Immense beaches, turquoise waters, but strong currents and unsupervised swimming. Caión is also a preserved fishing village: morning auction, ultra-fresh fish restaurants. For surf enthusiasts, Razo is one of Galicia's spots. 30-40 min from Coruña.

If you prefer staying in the city: walk in Os Mallos quarter then dinner-show at Centro Ágora or Caracolas, two concert halls programming Galician folk (gaita, pandereta, muiñeira). Galician traditional music survives as a living genre, not tourist folklore. Check program the day before—concerts mainly Friday-Saturday evenings.

days_plan.itin_title: Galicia Road Trip: Santiago de Compostela and the Coast of Death
Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Galicia: Complete 10-Day Circuit

places.name: Aguilas

visits.text: An 18th-century castle built against pirates - and you can tell Águilas looks like a real fishing town, not a beach resort. The castillo de San Juan overlooks the bay from a volcanic cliff. Inside: short exhibition on the harbor's history. From the battlements, view over the municipality's 35 beaches and calas.

Golden sand, volcanic formations, turquoise water - Calarreona and La Carolina are two of the protected beaches in the Cuatro Calas sector. La Carolina measures 500 meters, is Blue Flag certified, and remains less known than its Murcia neighbors. Access from the RM-D14, track the last few kilometers.

Cabo Cope, 10 km north of Águilas, is a rocky cape with a medieval watchtower and waters reputed among the best on the Costa Cálida for diving. The Cabo Cope-Puntas de Calnegre regional park protects this stretch of coast. Access via the RM-332 toward Lorca, signposted. No facilities on site.

activities.text: Águilas eats well: fresh fish, no folklore. The marina lines up restaurants with no tourist menu. Caldero de pescado (fish rice) and grilled dorada are the standards. Dine early - kitchens close around 10pm, unlike the big Andalusian cities.

The seabeds of the Cuatro Calas sector are among Murcia's least frequented - intact marine fauna, dense seagrass, a few moray eels under the lava blocks. Snorkel from Calarreona or La Carolina, no special equipment needed. Mask rental at Águilas harbor. Avoid July-August weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Road Trip: Baroque Splendor & Costa Cálida
Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca
Wild Coast: Málaga to Cartagena in 9 Days

map_keywords: Castillo San Juan de las Águilas

places.name: Alba De Tormes

visits.text: Alba de Tormes is inseparable from Saint Teresa of Ávila. She died here on October 4, 1582, exhausted from a final journey from Burgos, in the Carmelite convent she had founded in 1571. Her body—declared incorruptible at the three tomb openings in 1750, 1914, and 2024—rests in the Church of the Annunciation, in a carved marble urn above the main altar. You can see two reliquaries displaying her heart and left arm. John Paul II came on pilgrimage here November 1, 1982, for the fourth centenary of the saint's death.

The Theresian Basilica, built in 1896 on orders of the Bishop of Salamanca to accommodate the influx of pilgrims, is Alba de Tormes's most visible religious building from the road. Its tower measures 34.6 meters. It's the heart of Marian pilgrimage in Castile and León. The combined visit with the Carmelite Museum (CARMUS)—installed in the convent's renovated cloister—offers the most complete historical and spiritual context on Teresa and John of the Cross.

The Castle of Alba, whose watchtower has guarded the town since the 12th century, symbolizes the Duchy of Alba de Tormes—one of Spain's great noble houses. In 1472, García Álvarez de Toledo obtained the title of Duke of Alba and made Alba a major cultural center of the Golden Age, attracting poets and thinkers. The El Espolón park laid out around the old fortress offers fine views over the Tormes.

The Church of San Juan, built in the 12th-13th centuries, is classified as a National Monument. Its Romanesque-Mudéjar style and the coexistence of brick and stone make it an interesting monument. It preserves the three apses of the original chevet. It's also the first church in the world dedicated to Saint John of the Cross—friend of Teresa of Ávila and co-reformer of the Carmelites.

activities.text: Walk along the banks of the Tormes below the castle—this is where Teresa loved to pray, drawn by the view of the river from her convent. The medieval bridge and old ruined mills on the Tormes compose a calm, photogenic landscape.

At the table, look for rosquillas de Ledesma—sweet doughnuts—and bollo maimón, a soft egg cake found in bakeries throughout Salamanca province. Alba restaurants also serve cabrito (kid goat) and roast lamb.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

map_keywords: Monastère de l'Annonciation Alba de Tormes
Basilique Sainte Thérèse Alba de Tormes
Musée thérésien CARMUS Alba de Tormes
Église San Juan Alba de Tormes

visits.text: Provincial capital without appeal? False. The Pasaje de Lodares, shopping arcade built between 1925 and 1929 by Valencian architect Buenaventura Ferrando, unfolds 90 metres of columns, ironwork, and allegorical sculptures under a skylight that floods everything with white light. Free entry, open access from the street. In the morning, before the shops open, the atmosphere is strange and photogenic.

The Museo Municipal de la Cuchillería is really worth the hour it asks: it traces the tradition of Albacete's cutlers since the Bronze Age, with a reconstruction of an artisan workshop and remarkable pieces from European collections. Europe has only three museums dedicated to cutlery—this is among the best designed. Entry €3 (free Wednesday). Closed Monday.

Join Plaza del Altozano to admire the Baroque façade of the Catedral de San Juan Bautista (construction began in the 16th century, façade unfinished) and the modernist sculptures of the square—the Gran Dama Oferente and the Bicha de Balazote (reproduction). The plaza is the living heart of Albacete; café and tapas on the terrace to breathe.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: Albarracín appears around a bend in the Guadalaviar, clinging to a rose sandstone cliff at 1,100 meters elevation. Declared a National Monument in 1961, this village of 1,000 inhabitants is considered one of Spain's most beautiful. Its facades of reddish rodeno sandstone and wooden balconies create a fairytale medieval atmosphere.

Albarracín's ramparts extend across an area far larger than the village itself. Three construction phases can be read in the stone: the 10th-century bastion and Torre del Andador, the 11th-century walls of the Berber taifa of Banu Razin, then Christian fortifications after the Reconquest. From atop the Torre del Andador, the panorama embraces the entire valley.

The Cathedral of El Salvador, built in the 16th century on a medieval base, surprises with the austerity of its facade and the richness of its interior. The adjoining Diocesan Museum displays Flemish tapestries, a 14th-century processional cross, and a 16th-century rock crystal fish.

Get lost in the sloping alleys climbing toward the castle. The Casa de la Julianeta, crooked and lopsided near the Portal de Molina, has become the village's photographic symbol. The absence of vertical lines in its structure gives it an unreal air, as if lifted from an illustrated storybook.

The Plaza Mayor, surrounded by arcaded galleries, is dominated by the 16th-century Town Hall and its fine wooden balconies. From the upper floor, the view plunges onto the Guadalaviar meander that encircles the village like a natural moat.

activities.text: Follow the paseo fluvial, a trail along the Guadalaviar, to admire the ramparts from the valley. The contrast between the red sandstone of the walls, the green of the pines, and the blue of the river composes one of Aragon's most photogenic landscapes.

A few kilometers from the village, the red rocks of Pinar del Rodeno shelter UNESCO-listed rock paintings dating from 6,000 to 1,000 BC. These white figures of animals and archers stand out against reddish rock, unique in the entire Levantine rock art arc.
The Sierra de Albarracín offers exceptional night skies for astrotourism. Far from any light pollution, starry nights reveal the Milky Way to the naked eye.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragon Circuit: Zaragoza & Mudéjar Villages

visits.text: Alcossebre unfolds 10 kilometers of unspoiled coastline. Its beaches—Las Fuentes, Cargador, Tres Playas—and watchtowers dot the Blue Flag-certified shore.
The Sierra d'Irta plunges into the Mediterranean for 12 kilometers. The last undeveloped coastal range in the region, it shelters cliffs, wild coves, and protected wildlife.
The castle of Xivert dominates the sierra at 500 meters. An 11th–12th-century Templar fortress of Moorish origin, recently restored, it still stands watch over the Alcalà plain.
The Torre Ebrí rises 8.5 meters on a clifftop. Built in the 16th century, this watchtower alerted the castle of Xivert whenever pirate ships appeared.

The Church of San Juan Bautista boasts the tallest octagonal bell tower in the Valencia Region (58 meters). Its classical baroque interior preserves a painting by Traver Calzada depicting the martyrdom of Saint John.
The Hermitage of Santa Lucía y San Benito crowns Monte San Benito. This lookout offers one of the finest panoramas in Castellón province, sweeping across the coast and hinterland.
Cala Mundina and Cala Ribamar nestle within the Sierra d'Irta Natural Park. These wild coves surrounded by Mediterranean scrub invite quiet contemplation.

activities.text: Coastal bike paths wind between sea and sierra. The gentle route lets you discover viewpoints and hidden coves at your own pace.
Rent a kayak to explore the sheltered calas. The crystal-clear water reveals the rocky seabed and marine life of the reserve.
The port of Alcossebre serves fish and seafood straight from the day's catch. Marina restaurants offer grilled sea bream, zarzuela, and black rice with cuttlefish.
Hiking trails cross the Sierra d'Irta all the way to where the Columbretes Islands are visible on the horizon. The marked network offers several difficulty levels.

days_plan.itin_title: Castellón and Maestrazgo Route: Castles and Coves

visits.text: Begin your journey 30 km from Madrid. Alcalá de Henares, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1998, was the world's first purpose-built university town. Cardinal Cisneros founded the university in 1499 with the ambition of creating the City of God (Civitas Dei)—a blueprint later exported to the Americas. Head to the Colegio Mayor de San Ildefonso, the founding building: its Plateresque façade, completed in 1553 by Rodrigo Gil de Hontañón, ranks among Spain's finest. Inside, the Paraninfo (ceremonial hall) hosts the annual Cervantes Prize ceremony every 23 April—the Nobel of Hispanic letters, presented by the King of Spain.

Walk down Calle Mayor, one of Europe's longest arcaded streets, to the Casa Natal de Cervantes. Miguel de Cervantes Saavedra was born here in 1547—his baptismal certificate (9 October 1547) is preserved in the town hall. The house, reconstructed as a 16th-century bourgeois residence, is now a free museum: period furniture, the father's medical office (he was a barber-surgeon), and early editions of Don Quixote. Right next door, don't miss the Corral de Comedias (1601), Spain's oldest documented theatre, restored and still in use.

The Magistral Cathedral of Santos Niños Justo y Pastor is one of only two in the world to bear the title 'Magistral' (the other being Leuven)—meaning all its canons had to be doctors of theology from the university. Built in the 15th–16th centuries in Isabelline Gothic style, it houses Cardinal Cisneros' tomb. Climb the bell tower for views over the town's rooftops and the storks nesting on the spires—Alcalá has one of Spain's largest white stork populations.

activities.text: Spend the late afternoon wandering around Plaza de Cervantes (the old Plaza Mayor), the town's beating heart, lined with cafés and terraces. The central bandstand and colourful façades create a wonderfully lively atmosphere. The university quarter around the Colegio Mayor buzzes with the energy of 25,000 students. Sample the costrada (puff pastry cake filled with cream) and rosquillas (anise doughnuts)—Alcalá's signature sweets.

days_plan.itin_title: Alcarria and Guadalajara: Between Madrid and Aragon

visits.text: White houses suspended above the canyon: Castillo de Alcalá del Júcar crowns a rocky spur surrounded by a meander of the río Júcar whose vertical walls reach 150 metres. A Historic-Artistic Ensemble, castle entry costs €2.50 and includes a visit to a traditional cave-house carved into the rock. From the ramparts, you simultaneously see both sides of the canyon.

Alcalá del Júcar's natural caves are its real surprise: part of the village isn't built on the rock but in it. The Cueva del Diablo, carved into the cliff, is a visitable troglodyte house (paid entry). The extent of the natural underground network fitted out as dwellings over the centuries—Moorish, then Christian—has no equivalent in the province.

Cross the Puente Romano to reach the mirador on the opposite bank—that's where the classic silhouette of the village on the rock is photographed. The 'Roman bridge' is actually medieval, but the name stuck. The right bank also offers the Ermita de San Lorenzo (16th century) 3 km away on the CM-3201 road.

activities.text: The hoz del Júcar deserves to be walked: despite its name of 'gorge', the riverside senda at the canyon bottom remains easy and highly photogenic (shade, running water, cliffs). Start from the bridge, about 4 km round trip to the abandoned balnearios. Closed shoes recommended; the trail can be slippery after rain.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: Alcaraz's Plaza Mayor, built between 1518 and 1578, has been a Cultural Heritage Site since 1945: a rectangle of Renaissance loggias in golden stone, with arcades that nothing masks. Architect Andrés de Vandelvira left his mark here before building Jaén Cathedral—this is where he learned. Free and open visit at all hours.

Between the two towers facing each other—the Torre del Tardón (the clock) and the Torre de la Trinidad (the church)—there's an almost theatrical tension: they've been staring at each other for 500 years without touching. The Torre del Tardón has an irregular prismatic plan, like its neighbour, which makes the whole even more singular. A bench by the plaza fountain to appreciate them is the best seat in Castile.

Push open the door of the Iglesia de la Santísima Trinidad, a Gothic jewel of Albacete province with 16th-century Renaissance additions. Inside: a statue by Salzillo, two paintings by the Master of Albacete, and a Gothic star vault above the apse. Check opening hours at the neighbouring tourist office (same building as the Lonja).

activities.text: Spectacular after rain, ordinary in dry summer: the Nacimiento del Río Mundo flows from a karst gallery in the Calar del Mundo as a waterfall into a rock breach. The reventón phenomenon—sudden gushing after heavy rains—can project the water several metres. Trail of 2 km from the Riópar parking to the foot of the waterfall. Very busy on weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca
Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: Alcoy presents itself as an industrial city—and it doesn't lie. Nicknamed 'the city of bridges,' its complex terrain has spawned an architecture of viaducts and unusual footbridges. What few guides mention: a Llotja by Santiago Calatrava in the center, spare and geometric. The covered market merits a pause before climbing toward the Serra.

activities.text: The park air smells of thyme, rosemary, and warm clay. The Serra de Mariola, designated a natural park in 2002, covers 17,000 hectares between Alcoy and Banyeres. It harbors the southernmost yew forest in Europe and over 1,200 different plant species. The summit of Montcabrer (1,390 m) offers views all the way to the sea. Visitor center on the Alcoy-Banyeres road, km 18.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

visits.text: Alcúdia's ramparts date to the 14th century. Stretching 1.5 kilometers with 26 towers, they once guarded the town from pirates. Access begins at Porta de Vila Roja.
The monumental Porta de Xara opens onto the old town. Its golden stones and rounded arch exemplify Majorcan medieval architecture.
The Roman site of Pollentia was founded in 123 BC. Remains include a theater, forum, and patrician houses from the ancient Balearic Roman capital.
The Monographic Museum displays artifacts unearthed at Pollentia. Pottery, jewelry, coins, and sculptures trace 2,000 years of history.
Sant Jaume Church rises at the heart of the old town. Its Baroque interior contrasts with the medieval cobbled lanes surrounding it.
Alcúdia Beach stretches for 3.5 kilometers. Its fine sand, shallow waters, and Blue Flag status make it one of Majorca's most family-friendly.
Port d'Alcúdia is the modern resort zone. Its seafront promenade, restaurants, and marina animate the waterfront.

activities.text: The coastal bike path traces the north shore for 15 kilometers, running through beaches, pine forests, and S'Albufera Natural Park wetlands.
Kayaks set out toward Cap de Formentor. The rocky peninsula reveals wild coves and plunging cliffs.
Alcúdia Market convenes Tuesday and Sunday mornings. Fruit, vegetables, crafts, and local products spread out before the ramparts.
S'Albufera Natural Park shelters over 200 bird species. Flamingos, herons, and ducks populate the marshes and reedbeds.

days_plan.itin_title: Majorca Road Trip: Serra de Tramuntana & Hidden Coves

places.name: Aldea Del Rey

visits.text: Calatrava la Nueva Castle crowns the Cerro Alacranejo at 936 meters altitude. Headquarters of the Order of Calatrava from 1217 to 1804, it's a fortified castle-convent that controlled the main pass between the Meseta and the Guadalquivir valley. The 13th-century church preserves a Mudéjar doorway with a double horseshoe arch.

The Order of Calatrava, founded in the 12th century by Abbot Raimundo de Fitero, was the Iberian Peninsula's first military order. The cobbled path climbing to the castle dates from Philip II's visit in 1560. Across the way, Salvatierra Castle forms a spectacular defensive pair.

From the castle ramparts, views stretch across the Sierra Morena dehesas and the volcanic Campo de Calatrava. In the 16th century, the fortress was protected by a star-shaped bastioned enclosure to withstand new artillery. On clear days, you can make out Salvatierra Castle on the far side of the pass.

activities.text: In October, the Jornadas Medievales recreate the castle's atmosphere with markets, combat displays, and period food. The rest of the year, the site is tranquil and the walk up from the parking area takes about fifteen minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Campo de Calatrava and Valle de Alcudia in 5 Days

map_keywords: Calatrava la Nueva Castle Aldea del Rey

visits.text: In 1086, García Jiménez held this rocky stronghold for six years against the Almoravids, harassing territories as far as Granada and Alicante from a handful of towers. The village perched at 800 m dominates the Totana plain with undiminished insolence. The Torre del Homenaje (13th-14th century, 19 m high) houses the tourist office—admission €1.50 on weekdays. From the terrace, you see Sierra Espuña behind and the coast on clear days.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca

visits.text: Twelve caves carved into the cliff at Alfafara, in the barranco del Pont Trencat. These artificial cavities from the Andalusian period look from below like windows cut into the rock—hence their name. Access via a wooden ladder, with several interconnected chambers inside. They likely served as granaries. Free entry, 30-45 min visit. Signage sometimes absent: take the track from the ermita de San Antonio.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

places.name: Alfaro

visits.text: Rioja Oriental—renamed in 2018, formerly called 'Rioja Baja' (the lower), a second-rate connotation local winemakers finally shook off. Clay-iron soil, drier heat, nearly desert terrain in places. Territory of pure-blooded Garnacha, some vines 100 years old. This is the sub-region collectors snubbed, that négociants drowned in blends. Today, a handful of winemakers are proving you can make greatness here—and they're right.

The Collegiate Church of San Miguel in Alfaro, a Baroque masterpiece from the late 17th century, is also the most populated building in Europe—but not by worshippers. Over 400 white storks nest here every year from December to August, making it the largest stork colony in Europe on a single structure. The church's towers and alcoves offer the birds natural protection from predators—an unintended combination nobody sought to correct. In season, the clatter of beaks echoes across the plaza. Look closely at the top: nests everywhere, chicks squabbling for space, adults swooping in.

Bodegas Palacios Remondo in Alfaro—the family estate of Álvaro Palacios, the man who revolutionized Catalan Priorat in the 1990s with L'Ermita and Les Terrasses. He returned to his family's lands to attempt the same with Rioja Oriental Garnacha. The Propiedad cuvée, from the estate's oldest grenaches, is the most accomplished expression. Visit by appointment, around €30.

activities.text: Market-style lunch in Alfaro—vegetables from the local huerta are among Spain's best: Alfaro piquillo peppers have their own designation, artichokes and asparagus served everywhere. A seasonal meal in a village restaurant is the best introduction to the Rioja that eats before it drinks.

Walk through the Sotos del Ebro nature reserve, a wetland zone along the river minutes from Alfaro: grey herons, black kites, all sorts of ducks. A good counterpoint to cellars and bottles—and the air is genuinely clean.

days_plan.itin_title: La Rioja in 7 Days - The Kingdom of Tempranillo
Rioja in 7 Days: The Kingdom of Tempranillo Between Cathedral-Bodegas and Star Architecture

map_keywords: San Miguel Collegiate Church Alfaro storks

visits.text: Alhama de Granada—'al-hammam,' the bath—is one of the strongholds that fell before Granada during the Reconquest war. On February 28, 1482, the Catholic Monarchs seized it by night assault. The fall was so striking that a Castilian romance, '¡Ay de mi Alhama!,' was composed immediately after and sung for centuries as the announcement of the Nasrid kingdom's end. The village of 6,000 residents today clings to the edge of a vertiginous 50 m canyon, the Tajo de Alhama. Intact old quarter, white sloping alleys, houses with flowered balconies. Half a day suffices.

The Casa de la Inquisición, on the main square, is an early 16th-century Gothic-Renaissance palace that never served as a Holy Office tribunal—the local name is misleading. But the golden stone facade with its coats of arms and Gothic windows is one of western Andalusia's finest. Today private property, exterior visit only, free and open. The supposed presence of the Inquisition in Alhama more likely relates to the persecution of Moriscos after 1568 in the neighboring Alpujarras.

The Iglesia del Carmen, 17th-century baroque, is a spectacular ruin on the edge of the Tajo. Burned during the Civil War in 1936, its roof collapsed in the 1960s after heavy snowfall. Restoration project underway since 2018. Belvedere on the neighboring square for the plunging view into the canyon—one of the village's signature photos.

Alhama's Mazmorras, underground dungeons carved during the Almohad period in two jar-shaped silos (12th century), served as medieval prison, then grain reserve, then shelter during the Civil War, and today as cultural space. Exceptional acoustics for concerts, permanent exhibition on historical uses. 2 euros, closed Mondays.

activities.text: Bath at the Alhama Thermal Baths, 2 km from the village. Natural springs at 47°C known since Roman times (1st-century BC bridge right next door), developed into free outdoor pools by the town hall. Open 365 days a year. Bring swimsuit, towel, non-slip sandals. Go early in the morning (before 9 AM) or on weekdays to avoid weekend crowds. Ideal in winter, the water steams in contact with the cold air.

days_plan.itin_title: Dark Andalusia: 10 Days from Seville to Málaga

visits.text: Reforested from 1889 after near-total ecological disaster, Sierra Espuña is a lesson in forest resilience. Engineer Ricardo Codorníu planted thousands of Aleppo pines on bare slopes. Result: a 17,804-hectare park peaking at 1,585 m (Morrón de Totana). Declared a regional park in 1992, listed as Natura 2000. The Ricardo Codorníu visitor center is the right starting point—it explains the entire reforestation story.

activities.text: Locals still call them the frigos, and for good reason: the Pozos de Nieve, 25 circular structures dug into the ground around 1,400 m altitude, stored snow from the 16th century to supply villages in summer. You reach them via the Senda del Caracol, a switchback trail climbing to 1,500 m. Allow 3 hours. View to the coast on clear days.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca

visits.text: Santa Bárbara Castle stands at 166 meters on Mount Benacantil—visible from everywhere in the city. Its origins date to the 9th century; it was reconquered by the Castilians in 1248 (on Saint Barbara's feast day, hence the name). It was converted into a prison during the civil war. Free access via the elevator carved through the rock from Postiguet beach (€2.70 for the elevator) or on foot from Santa Cruz.

The Explanada mosaic crunches underfoot with thousands of tri-colored tiles. This 500-meter seafront promenade runs along Alicante's port with its palms and terraces. A stone's throw away, the Basilica of Santa María—built in the 15th century on the former main mosque—conceals an interesting Baroque altarpiece. Entry is free.

A bustling regional capital, yet its beaches are underestimated by those who just pass through. San Juan beach, 6 km from the center, is the longest and least crowded. The MARQ—Provincial Archaeological Museum—brings together collections from prehistory through the Islamic period with modern scenography worthy of attention. Allow 2h for a complete visit.

activities.text: Lose yourself in Santa Cruz, the old quarter that climbs the slopes of the castle. Cobbled lanes, geraniums on balconies, hidden little squares: here the city still breathes. In the evening, the terraces on Calle Labradores and around the central market come alive with locals. Arroz a banda or calamar a la romana are the must-try dishes.

If you have an appetite for markets, the fish auction at Mercado de Abastos (Monday-Saturday mornings) gathers fresh catch of the day and produce from the Vega Baixa. The TRAM light rail connects the center to San Juan in 20 minutes—handy for ending the day at the beach without a car. Line L1 from the bus station.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days
Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

visits.text: The Almadén Mining Park takes visitors 50 meters underground in a mine cage, into the heart of 16th-century galleries. These mercury mines, worked from Roman antiquity until 2003, have been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2012. The guided tour lasts between 2.5 and 4 hours.

The Royal Hospital of San Rafael Miners, an 18th-century building, cared for miners suffering from hidrargirismo, mercury poisoning. Prisoners sentenced to forced labor were also treated here. Three museum rooms trace Almadén's medical, metallurgical, and social history.
Almadén's hexagonal bullring, built from 1752 as miners' housing, is one of Spain's oldest. National Monument since 1979. It now houses the tourist office and a hotel.

activities.text: The Mercury Museum offers interactive exhibits on mercury's physics and chemistry. Models show how cinnabar was shipped by galleon to America to extract silver. Advance booking required, adult admission around €17.

days_plan.itin_title: Campo de Calatrava and Valle de Alcudia in 5 Days

visits.text: Two hours' drive south from Madrid and you enter the heart of La Mancha—Don Quixote's plains. Almagro, declared a Historic-Artistic Ensemble, is a complete surprise in this landscape of windmills and fields. The Plaza Mayor is its jewel: a rectangular square bordered by two stone-arcaded galleries with 81 columns, and characteristic green-painted glazed balconies—a legacy of the German Fugger bankers (los Fúcares in Spanish) who settled here in the 16th century to manage the Almadén mercury mines on behalf of Charles V.

The Corral de Comedias is the reason for your visit to Almagro. Built in 1628 by Leonardo de Oviedo in the former Mesón del Toro, it's the only Spanish Golden Age theatre preserved intact and still active in the world. Its 300 m² rectangular courtyard, its two-storey wooden galleries, its cazuela (balcony reserved for women), and its tablado (stage) transport you directly to the era of Lope de Vega, Calderón de la Barca, and Tirso de Molina. Closed in the 18th century by royal decree, it was rediscovered by chance in 1954 beneath the partitions of a boarding house. Every summer, the International Festival of Classical Theatre revives Golden Age plays there.

The Museo Nacional del Teatro, housed in the 13th-century Maestral Palace of the Knights of Calatrava, is Spain's only national museum devoted to theatre. It traces the history of performing arts from the Middle Ages: set models, period costumes, manuscripts, puppets, and posters. An entire section is dedicated to the corrales de comedias and theatrical life during the Golden Age.

The Almacén de los Fúcares—the Fuggers' warehouse—is an imposing 16th-century building that served as a grain store and mercury warehouse for the Augsburg bankers. These financiers were so powerful they lent money to Charles V in exchange for the Almadén mercury monopoly. The building, sometimes called a palace because its construction is so refined, now hosts exhibitions. Just next door, the Church of San Agustín, declared of Cultural Interest, deserves a look for its Renaissance cloister.

activities.text: The gastronomy of Almagro and the Campo de Calatrava belongs to the heart of La Mancha. Try the berenjenas de Almagro—small round aubergines pickled in vinegar with cumin and chilli, a local speciality with its own protected designation. Add to that migas manchegas (fried breadcrumbs with chorizo), pisto manchego (a local ratatouille), and a glass of Valdepeñas wine from the neighbouring appellation.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
La Mancha Road Trip: Don Quixote's Windmills and Cuenca
The Don Quixote Route: A Road Trip Through Castilla-La Mancha's Windmills & Plains

visits.text: On 25 April 1707, the plain of Almansa saw the War of Spanish Succession tip: Philip V's victory over the troops of Archduke Charles defined the monarchy that would govern Spain to this day. Castillo de Almansa, planted on Cerro del Águila with a spiral staircase carved into the rock, dominates this battlefield. Free guided tours Saturday at 1 p.m. (reservation: 967 344 771).

If you visit late April, the International Historical Re-enactment of the Battle of Almansa mobilises hundreds of participants and thousands of spectators—one of the region's most important historical re-enactments. For other months, the Museo de la Batalla (in the Ermita de San Blas) tells the tactics, uniforms, and political consequences of 1707 with models and audiovisuals. Free entry.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: The Alcazaba of Almería towers over the city from its hilltop perch. Built in 955 by Caliph Abd al-Rahman III, this citadel—Spain's largest after the Alhambra—unfolds across three walled enclosures: the gardens of the first, the five-nave caliphal cistern of the second, and the Christian castle erected by the Catholic Monarchs in 1489.

Step inside the Cathedral of the Incarnation, a unique fortress-temple built after the 1522 earthquake. Its thick walls, battlements, and arrow slits bear witness to attacks by Barbary pirates. Inside, the neoclassical cloister and ambulatory chapels deserve close attention.

The Civil War Shelters burrow 9 meters beneath Paseo de Almería. These 4.5-kilometer underground galleries, designed by architect Guillermo Langle Rubio in 1937, could shelter 40,000 residents. The reconstructed operating room is particularly striking.

The Cable Inglés, an ore-loading pier built in 1904 in Eiffel-style architecture, juts into the sea from Almadrabillas beach. Declared a Cultural Heritage Site in 1998, it offers beautiful perspectives over the port at sunset.

Make your way to Plaza Vieja, the city's administrative heart. The 19th-century neoclassical town hall borders this shaded square. The Monument to the Martyrs of Liberty at its center commemorates victims of absolutist repression in 1824.

Paseo de Almería connects Puerta de Purchena to the port. This shopping avenue, lined with century-old ficus trees, cuts through downtown and passes above the underground shelters. Café terraces come alive as evening falls.

activities.text: Walk the waterfront promenade of Parque Nicolás Salmerón to the marina. Palm trees line the path, with the illuminated Alcazaba glowing in the backdrop.
Head to Mercado Central for a tapas crawl. Almería upholds the tradition of free tapas served with every drink—the bars around the market compete in generosity.
Late afternoon swim at El Zapillo beach, Almería's urban beach, with views of the Cable Inglés and the Sierra de Gádor mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Almería Road Trip: Volcanic Coves and Tabernas Desert
Wild Coast: Málaga to Cartagena in 9 Days
White Villages & Sierras: 9 Days Málaga-Almería

visits.text: Twenty minutes west of Córdoba, the castle of Almodóvar del Río appears from afar, perched on a 252-meter hill above the Guadalquivir. This fortress of Umayyad origin, built around 760, is one of the best-preserved in Spain. Its crenellated towers—the Cuadrada, the Redonda, and the Keep—stand out against the sky like an illustration from a medieval book. The interior can be visited: courtyards of arms, furnished halls, armor, dungeons, and oubliettes where Peter I the Cruel supposedly imprisoned his enemies in the 14th century. The building was restored between 1901 and 1936 by the Count of Torralva, which explains its remarkable condition. If the place seems familiar, that's normal—scenes from Game of Thrones (Season 7, Highgarden and Casterly Rock) were filmed here. Allow 1.5 hours for the visit.

In the afternoon, push toward the Sierra de Hornachuelos Natural Park, 30 km northwest. It's one of the least-visited natural areas in Andalusia—and one of the wildest. The Mediterranean forest of holm oaks and cork oaks shelters one of the last populations of Iberian lynx, black vultures, Spanish imperial eagles, and deer. The village of Hornachuelos, listed among Spain's Most Beautiful Villages, overlooks a spectacular gorge of the Río Bembézar. From the village's mirador, the view over the gorges and the Sierra Morena is breathtaking.

The Monastery of San Jerónimo de Valparaíso, on the edge of the park, deserves a stop if timing permits. Founded in the 15th century, this is the monastery where Gonzalo Fernández de Córdoba—the Gran Capitán, the most famous Spanish general of the Renaissance—spent his final years. The cloister and church preserve Gothic and Mudéjar elements.

activities.text: Operators in the Hornachuelos park offer guided hikes with wildlife observation—the Fuente del Valle trail (8 km, 3 hours) crosses a dense cork oak forest with good chances of seeing deer and raptors. In season (autumn-winter), the deer rut is spectacular in these forests. Kayak excursions on the Bembézar reservoir are also available.

days_plan.itin_title: Córdoba and the Sierra Morena: From the Caliphate to the Dehesas of Los Pedroches

visits.text: Founded under the name Sexi, Almuñécar already existed when the Phoenicians set foot here nearly 3,000 years ago. The castle of San Miguel, perched on its hill, has witnessed every successive occupation. In the neighboring archaeological museum (Cueva Siete Palacios), the centerpiece is the Vase of Apophis I: an Egyptian alabaster vase dating from the 16th century BC, considered by the municipality to be the oldest written document found on the peninsula.

The El Majuelo botanical park occupies former Roman salt works - the evaporation basins are still visible beneath the vegetation. Around a hundred Mediterranean species. Free entry. Archaeologists uncovered a Roman aqueduct here whose pillars surface between the paths. Perfect for a break before the long drive to Almería.

activities.text: Before heading on, taste the tropical fruits grown in the local microclimate: avocados, mangoes, chirimoyas (custard apples) - the Thursday morning market stalls are best stocked. Almuñécar chirimoya has protected designation status. Prices well below French rates.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Coast: Málaga to Cartagena in 9 Days

visits.text: In the morning, stop at Aínsa, former capital of the Kingdom of Sobrarbe and one of Spain's Most Beautiful Villages. Perched at the confluence of the Ara and Cinca rivers, beneath the gaze of Peña Montañesa, the medieval village seems frozen in the 12th century. The Plaza Mayor, trapezoidal and bordered by Romanesque arcades, is one of the world's best-preserved medieval squares. Climb the square tower of Santa María church (11th-12th centuries) for a panoramic view of the Pyrenees. The restored castle houses an ecomuseum and hosts a music festival in its courtyard each July.

In the afternoon, drive to Alquézar (1h from Aínsa). The name comes from Arabic al-qasr (the fortress) - it was a 9th-century Muslim stronghold conquered by Sancho Ramírez in 1064. The village, clinging to a promontory above the Río Vero canyon, is classified as a Historic Site and ranks among Spain's Most Beautiful Villages. Climb up to the Collegiate Church of Santa María la Mayor, which preserves a Romanesque cloister with carved capitals and a major altarpiece. From the square, the view plunges into the limestone gorges - a dizzying spectacle.

activities.text: Don't miss the Pasarelas del Vero, Aragón's most popular aerial trail. This 3 km route (approximately 2h loop) descends into the Río Vero canyon via metal walkways attached to limestone walls above turquoise water. The trail is paid and well-secured - no technical difficulty, but spectacular views over the gorges and natural pools. In warm weather, bring a swimsuit for a dip.

Near Alquézar, the Río Vero Cultural Park contains over 70 shelters with rock paintings listed as UNESCO World Heritage (1998). The most accessible are the Quizans shelters (schematic art, 5000-1500 BC) and Chimiachas (a deer in Levantine art of striking realism, 6000-3000 BC). Allow 45 minutes' walk from Alquézar to reach the shelters. Access is free but a guide significantly enriches the visit.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel

places.name: Altea

visits.text: The cobbled streets climb toward a blue-and-white dome visible from across Altea bay. The Iglesia Nuestra Señora del Consuelo, built in the 19th century modeled on a 17th-century Baroque chapel, crowns the old town from its promontory. White façades, flower-draped stairways, wrought-iron balconies: Altea's old quarter stands in sharp contrast to the concrete of Benidorm 10 km away. Church entry free.

An artists' village, yes—but the bougainvillea cascades down the walls long before the galleries appear. The viewpoint at Plaza de la Iglesia offers an unobstructed view of the bay and the Sierra Bernia mountains. Heading back down, the alleyways between Portal Vell and Portal Nou preserve stretches of Renaissance-era walls. Caution in August: packed on weekends, restaurants fill up early.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

map_keywords: Iglesia Nuestra Señora del Consuelo Altea

visits.text: The valleys of Ansó and Hecho are the hidden jewels of the western Aragonese Pyrenees. Ansó, at 860 m altitude, is a museum-village of stone and slate, listed among Spain's Most Beautiful Villages. Its traditional ashlar stone houses, with their cylindrical chimneys typical of Pyrenean architecture, have remained virtually unchanged for centuries. The parish church of San Pedro (16th century), in late Gothic style, dominates the village. Wander through the sloping streets and don't miss the Museo del Traje Ansotano, which displays the valley's traditional costumes - worn until the mid-20th century.

Then head to the Hecho Valley (30 min drive), parallel to Ansó. The village of Hecho is another magnificent example of Pyrenean stone architecture, with narrow streets and a Romanesque church. Three kilometers north, the Monastery of San Pedro de Siresa (9th-11th centuries), one of Aragón's oldest, was founded in the Carolingian era and is said to have housed the Holy Grail before its transfer to San Juan de la Peña. The church, massive and austere, is a gem of Aragonese pre-Romanesque art.

activities.text: Drive up the Hecho Valley to the Selva de Oza, a beech and fir forest occupying the floor of an ancient glacial valley - one of the most pristine landscapes in the Pyrenees. The Camino de la Boca del Infierno (Mouth of Hell) follows a spectacular trail along limestone gorges. Allow 2 hours round trip for this easy walk that follows the Aragón Subordán river between 200-meter vertical walls.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel

visits.text: The Dolmens of Antequera open another dimension in the road trip: death on a grand scale, 5,700 years before us. The Dolmen de Menga, built around 3750-3650 BC, is one of Europe's largest megalithic tombs—27.5 m long, 32 stone blocks, the heaviest weighing 180 tonnes (slightly more than a Boeing 737). When it was opened in the 19th century, the skeletons of several hundred people were found inside. Atypical orientation: not toward the rising sun like most European dolmens, but toward the Peña de los Enamorados, a human face-shaped mountain 7 km away. Direct ritual link with the landscape. UNESCO 2016.

The Dolmen de Viera, 70 m from Menga, is later (2500-2000 BC) and more modest—long narrow corridor leading to a rectangular burial chamber. It's the only one of the three oriented toward the rising sun: at the spring and summer equinoxes, the sun's rays traverse the corridor and illuminate the chamber. Five minutes to visit, but see it to understand the diversity of funerary conceptions on a single site.

The Peña de los Enamorados, an 880 m limestone mountain toward which Menga points, owes its name to a local legend from Al-Andalus times: Tagzona, Moorish princess of Antequera, falls in love with Christian soldier Tello, taken prisoner. Pursued by the girl's father, they jump together from the top of the cliff. The rock's shape, seen from Antequera, indeed evokes a human face in profile looking toward the sky. Legend or not, the dolmen's prehistoric orientation predates this story by 5,000 years.

activities.text: Lunch in Antequera before resuming the road to Málaga. Restaurante Arte de Cozina, calle Calzada 27, seasonal Andalusian cuisine at reasonable prices (25-35 euros menu). Local specialty: porra antequerana, Antequera's version of Cordoban salmorejo, thicker, garnished with hard-boiled egg and tuna.

days_plan.itin_title: Dark Andalusia: 10 Days from Seville to Málaga
White Villages & Sierras: 9 Days Málaga-Almería

visits.text: Beneath downtown Aracena, through an unassuming door on Calle Pozo de la Nieve, lies another world: 2,130 meters of karst galleries, 1,200 of them visitable, with 12 chambers and 6 underground lakes. Opened to the public in 1914, it's Spain's first tourist cave. The interior temperature stays between 16 and 19°C—bring a jacket even in August. Mandatory guided tour, 45 minutes.

Aracena climbs toward its medieval castle following a maze of whitewashed lanes—the ensemble is well preserved and uncrowded except on weekends. The Plaza Alta with its church of Santa María de los Reyes, the viewpoint over the Huelva sierra below: two hours suffice to see it all, unhurriedly.

The Museo del Jamón in downtown Aracena explains the Iberian ham production circuits: the dehesa, the montanera, the curing shed. Small museum, modest entry fee. Not essential if you're already visiting a secadero, but useful for understanding the appellations and avoiding getting ripped off on labels when buying.

activities.text: The smell of hanging ham hits you at the entrance to a secadero. In the Sierra de Aracena, black pigs (cerdo ibérico) roam freely in oak dehesas, feeding on acorns (bellotas) during the montanera. The final product—100% jamón ibérico de bellota—is cheaper and fresher here than in Madrid. Cinco Jotas or Eíriz offer tours with tastings.

If you buy a ham to take home, ask the seller for the date the curación began—a bellota ham is cured for at least 24 months, often 36. The skin (rind) should be supple, the fat white-yellow and melting. Pieces marked with a DOP Huelva or Jabugo appellation guarantee origin.

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva, the Forgotten Land: Doñana to Faro in 7 Days

visits.text: The Royal Palace of Aranjuez (16th–18th centuries) served as the Bourbon monarchs' spring residence. Rococo salons, the porcelain-clad Chamber, and the gilded Throne Room unfold in opulent succession.
The Jardín del Príncipe (18th century) stretches 150 hectares along the Tagus. Shaded avenues, neoclassical fountains, and the Casa del Labrador pavilion create an Enlightenment paradise.
The Jardín de la Isla embraces a Tagus island in geometric Renaissance-Baroque harmony. Parterres, canals, mythological statues, and water jets compose a verdant symphony.
The Royal Barges Museum displays 18th-century royal boats. These ornate vessels carried monarchs on leisurely river excursions—floating thrones on the Tagus.
Plaza de San Antonio (1750) arrays Baroque façades around the town church. This regular square forms Aranjuez's urban heart—elegant geometry meets everyday life.
The Casa del Labrador (1804) showcases neoclassical salons and Empire décor. This pavilion glittering with marble and bronze epitomizes Bourbon refinement at its peak.

activities.text: Embark on a mini-cruise along the Tagus. Narrated tours glide past royal gardens, historic bridges, and tree-lined banks—Aranjuez from the water.
Picnic with Aranjuez strawberries. These famed fresas are harvested April through June and sold at local markets—sweet, sun-warmed perfection.
Cycle along the Tagus. Bike paths wind through royal gardens, orchards, and the huertas (market gardens) that made Aranjuez agriculturally legendary—easy riverside riding.
Ride the Tren de la Fresa (Strawberry Train). This heritage train links Madrid to Aranjuez with strawberry tastings and period-costumed entertainment—nostalgia on rails.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid & Castile Road Trip: Royal Palaces, Ancient Walls & World-Class Museums

places.name: Arcos De La Frontera

visits.text: The void begins at your feet: the Plaza del Cabildo, perched at the cliff top above the Guadalete, is one of the most vertiginous viewpoints in Andalusia. Below, more than 100 meters of limestone wall, and the plain beyond. The medieval castle (an 11th-century Andalusí alcázar) and the national parador occupy the corner of the square. You can't go in, but the exterior can be freely admired.

Built on the ruins of a mosque and extensively transformed from the 1520s onwards under the direction of Alonso Rodríguez then Diego de Riaño, the Basílica de Santa María de la Asunción juxtaposes late Gothic, Plateresque and Baroque across six centuries of construction. Declared a National Monument in 1931. Its Baroque tower watches over the gorge. Paid entry, a few euros.

San Pedro is a church on the edge of the precipice: literally built at the extremity of the rocky promontory, it gives the impression of being poised over the void. The interior is more discreet than Santa María, but the location is unique - turn toward the valley from its forecourt.

activities.text: If you arrive outside monument opening hours, it's the right time for the historic center. Arcos's labyrinthine layout - alleys a meter wide, passages under houses, dead ends - is inherited directly from the Arab medina. Two hours getting lost beats any guide.

The people of Arcos say that the view over the plain from the Mirador de la Peña Nueva is worth the journey alone. In late afternoon, when the low sun gilds the olive groves below, the light is unlike anything else you'll see in Andalusia. Ten minutes on the stone balcony, no effort required.

days_plan.itin_title: White Villages: Arcos, Grazalema, Ronda - 6 Days

map_keywords: Plaza del Cabildo Arcos de la Frontera

visits.text: The castle of Aroche has occupied a hilltop since the 12th century—Almohad structure reworked after the Reconquista, then again during border conflicts with Portugal. The surprise: a bullring was built inside the ramparts in the early 19th century. The two still coexist. Panoramic view of the sierra from the battlements.

More than two thousand rosaries donated by personalities from around the world—popes, heads of state, actors, astronauts. The Aroche Rosary Museum was founded in the 1960s by a local resident, Paulino Díaz Alcaide, and appears in record books for the collection's scale. Entry via the tourist office. An unapologetic local curiosity, strangely fascinating.

activities.text: From the Cerro del Castillo, the first Portuguese hills are visible in clear weather—the border is less than 5 km away. This sense of world's edge, before tipping into Portugal, is one of the itinerary's strongest moments. The Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park covers 186,827 hectares around: no noise, no main roads.

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva, the Forgotten Land: Doñana to Faro in 7 Days

visits.text: The Charco de San Ginés defines Arrecife: a natural seawater lagoon in the heart of the capital, lined with whitewashed fishermen's houses. This natural harbor has existed for over six centuries—the city was born here after the Franco-Norman conquest in the 15th century. César Manrique redesigned it as a serene promenade. Walk its perimeter in early morning when the light is soft and the terraces still quiet.

Castillo de San Gabriel deserves a full hour. Built in the 16th century on a small island to defend against Berber pirates—it was first burned by the corsair Morato in 1586 before being rebuilt in stone—it now houses the Museum of the History of Lanzarote with over 3,000 artifacts. You reach it via the Puente de las Bolas, a drawbridge crowned by two stone cannonballs: this is Arrecife's postcard image. The castle was declared a national historic monument in 1972.

The Castillo de San José, perched on the bay of Los Mármoles port, houses the International Museum of Contemporary Art, founded in 1975 on César Manrique's initiative. The building itself dates to the 18th century, and its construction traces back to a legend: in 1630, a French merchant named Francisco García Santaella supposedly founded a hermitage here. The interior blends Baroque, Mudéjar, and Neoclassical styles—a typical Canarian architectural fusion. The castle's panoramic restaurant offers harbor views worth the visit alone.

End with Calle Real—officially called León y Castillo—Arrecife's main artery for centuries. You'll find the Plazuela (Constitution Square) and San Ginés Church, whose white dome dominates the square where an artisan market sets up every Saturday. The church became a parish in 1798 and was dedicated to San Ginés, the city's patron saint. The complete walk takes 2 to 3 hours.

activities.text: Lunch around Charco de San Ginés: the area brims with restaurants serving Canarian classics—papas arrugadas with mojo verde or mojo rojo, fresh fish from the port, and malvasía wines from the island. It's also the best spot to watch fishing boats maneuver through the lagoon.

Swimming and relaxation at Playa del Reducto, Arrecife's main beach. It stretches over half a kilometer with golden sand and calm waters, perfect for a mid-afternoon break. The promenade bordering it is the island's busiest.

Late afternoon, return to stroll around Charco de San Ginés at sunset. Locals gather here for terrace drinks. If you're there on a Wednesday or Thursday, a popular market sets up around the lagoon—a chance to pick up local crafts.

days_plan.itin_title: Lanzarote & La Palma: Where Fire Shaped Art and Nature

places.name: Astorga

visits.text: The Episcopal Palace of Astorga is one of Gaudí's rare works built outside Catalonia. After the old palace burned in 1886, Bishop Joan Grau i Vallespinós—a Catalan and Gaudí's personal friend—commissioned him to rebuild. The project began in 1887: Gaudí designed a neo-Gothic building in white granite that blends castle, temple, and palace, with turrets, battlements, and multicolored stained glass inspired by León Cathedral. The bishop's death in 1893 caused a rupture between Gaudí and the church authorities—he walked off the job, leaving the top floor unfinished, which another architect completed more soberly. Today the palace houses the Museum of the Ways, dedicated to pilgrimage routes. Allow at least 90 minutes.

Astorga's Cathedral of Santa María, built from the 15th to 18th centuries, is one of Spain's most composite monuments—Gothic, Renaissance, Baroque, and Plateresque succeed each other on its façades and interior. On one of its apsidal pinnacles, look for the statue of Pedro Mato, a Maragato about whom nothing is certain but local legends are numerous. The cathedral museum houses a magnificently chiseled silver casket by Alfonso III the Great.

Astorga was the Roman capital of Asturica Augusta, crossroads of several Roman roads. The Roman walls preserved in several sections throughout the city are among the best preserved on the peninsula. Don't miss the Roman Museum to understand the city's importance in imperial times—and the Chocolate Museum, since Astorga was historically one of the first European cities to import and process cacao from the Americas via Maragato muleteers.

Castrillo de los Polvazares is La Maragatería's most perfectly preserved village. Its cobbled streets, red-stone houses with large round-arched doorways and windows painted blue or green form a homogeneous 17th-century ensemble declared a Historic-Artistic Site in 1980. Those oversized doorways weren't architectural whimsy: they allowed carts and mules to enter interior courtyards. For Castrillo was from the 16th to 19th centuries the heart of the Maragato muleteer network—those semi-nomadic transporters who linked Galicia and Madrid with their mule caravans. English writer Richard Ford described them in 1846: 'as nomadic as Bedouins, but with mules instead of camels; they cost dearly, but their honesty compensates.' The arrival of the railroad in Astorga in 1866 spelled their end. The village had 1,200 inhabitants in 1870; it has about a hundred today.

Santa Colomba de Somoza, 13 km away, is another preserved Maragato village, inhabited since the 11th century and linked to the muleteering trade since the 16th. The Casa Maragata—an ethnographic museum set in a real muleteer house—recreates the domestic and professional spaces of Maragato life: kitchen with large hearth, cart shed, agricultural tools, and traces of the chocolate trade. Each August, residents open their courtyards for a crafts fair.

activities.text: Bar and restaurant tour around Plaza Mayor to dine on Maragatería specialties: cocido maragato (the Spanish stew eaten backwards—meat first, soup last), mantecadas de Astorga (butter and cinnamon cakes), and artisanal chocolate that's made the city's reputation since the 18th century. Book a restaurant for the cocido—it's a meal lasting several hours.

Lunch at Castrillo de los Polvazares with cocido maragato: the legend about the inverted eating order (meat before soup) is tied to the War of Independence—Spanish soldiers ate the most nourishing parts first before imminent battle against the French. Book ahead, especially weekends—the restaurant is often full.

Hiking the 'Senda Maragata' from Santa Colomba de Somoza—several marked circuits let you discover remnants of Roman mines and the Maragatería landscapes between oak groves and heathland. The Mirador de la Laguna Cernea offers views over the worn hills and Roman gold workings of the region.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León Road Trip: Cathedrals and Medieval Villages
The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province
The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks
Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Castile and León - 14-Day Grand Tour

visits.text: At 1,131 meters altitude, Ávila is one of Spain's highest cities—and you feel it in winter, when it often snows. The city reveals itself first through its ramparts: 2.5 kilometers of intact wall, 88 semi-circular towers, 9 monumental gates. Built from 1080 on orders of Raymond of Burgundy, son-in-law of Alfonso VI of Castile-León, completed in 1101. It's the best-preserved medieval wall in Europe—it nearly got demolished in the 19th century when authorities judged it stifled urban expansion. Fortunately they gave up. The walkway runs 1,700 meters.

On the wall itself, the Cathedral del Salvador. It gave birth to the term "fortress-cathedral" in architectural vocabulary. Its apse, nicknamed the Cimorro, literally forms part of the defensive system—it's one of the rampart towers, pierced with arrow slits. Spain's first Gothic cathedral, begun around 1170 by master Fruchel, a Burgundian architect influenced by Saint-Denis. Built in oxblood sandstone that gives it its unique color. Inside, the main altarpiece by Pedro Berruguete and Juan de Borgoña, the tomb of El Tostado (the bishop-writer who published 50 volumes in 25 years, died 1455).

Outside the walls, five minutes on foot from Puerta del Alcázar, the Basilica of San Vicente is one of Spain's finest Romanesque buildings. Built between the 11th and 13th centuries on the site where saints Vincent, Sabina, and Christeta were allegedly martyred in 304 under Diocletian. The stone tomb-cenotaph at the nave's center recounts their martyrdom in moving sculpted bas-reliefs: flagellation, torture, embalming by a converted Jewish lady. West façade with its grand portico, historiated capitals, golden light in the morning. A stop on the Camino del Sur.

Ávila makes no sense without Teresa. Born here in 1515 to a family of converted Jewish merchants, she reformed the Carmelite order, founded 17 monasteries, wrote The Interior Castle, and became the first female Doctor of the Church in 1970. Her birthplace was transformed in the 17th century into a convent and baroque basilica. The adjoining museum displays some strange relics: a finger of the saint preserved in a monstrance, her cane, autograph manuscripts. More moving: the Monastery of the Incarnation, outside the walls, where she lived 27 years and experienced the famous "transverberation" (vision where a seraph pierces her heart).

For the postcard photo of Ávila, exit through Puerta del Carmen and take the northwest road—toward Salamanca—1.5 km on foot to the Cuatro Postes. Four Doric columns crowned by a cross, erected in 1566 on the path where young Teresa was allegedly caught by her uncle while fleeing with her brother to get martyred by the Moors (legend says). Panoramic view of the illuminated ramparts, especially late in the day. That's where you take the best photos of Ávila.

activities.text: Sample yemas de Santa Teresa, Ávila's specialty since 1860. Small round balls made of beaten egg yolk and sugar, slowly cooked, presented in paper cases. Too sweet for many, but it's the city's sweet identity. Reference: La Flor de Castilla confectionery, and the one holding the secret of the original recipe, Antigua Confitería La Talavera. Three yemas are enough—they're dense.

Dinner of chuletón de Ávila—the charcoal beef rib that's the province's emblem. The avileña-negra ibérica breed produces marbled meat, mountain-raised in the Sierra de Gredos, slaughtered on average at 5-6 years (versus 18-24 months elsewhere). It's one of Spain's tenderest meats. Restaurant El Almacén or Las Cancelas for the classic version, with rare cooking. Count €60 for two. If you happen upon the Jornadas Medievales in early September, the city transforms into a medieval market for three days.
