visits.text: Walls plunge several hundred metres above the río Mundo from the Mirador del Diablo, 1.5 km from Ayna on the CM-3203—a few steps in the rock bring you to a platform overlooking the complete canyon, the village, and its orchards. Locals have called Ayna the 'Suiza Manchega' for so long it's become official. Stop the car in the parking on the road; the climb to the stairs takes 3 minutes.

The Cueva del Niño, on the outskirts of Ayna, shelters Paleolithic rock art dated to around 16,000 BC, according to published archaeological studies. Ask at Ayna's tourist office for access arrangements—the cave is not freely accessible and may require reservation or a guide.

activities.text: Follow the ruta de los miradores: five viewpoints dot Ayna's casco urbano, including the Mirador de los Picarzos and the Sidecar (a nod to the motorcycle from the film 'Amanece que no es poco'). The loop is walkable in under an hour. On the way down, try the 'suspiros'—local cakes sold at the bakery on the main street.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: Baeza is one of the two towns inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2003 for their Renaissance ensembles. But before becoming a 16th-century jewel, it was a stronghold of the Reconquest: Ferdinand III conquered it in 1227, and it became Castile's forward base facing the Kingdom of Granada for two centuries. Begin at Plaza del Pópulo, the civic heart of the town. The Fuente de los Leones (Lion Fountain), which resembles the one in the Alhambra, is actually an Ibero-Roman remnant from the ancient city of Cástulo. Around the square, the old slaughterhouse (Antigua Carnicería) and the Villalar Arch complete a harmonious ensemble.

Climb to Plaza de Santa María, the religious and educational heart of Baeza. The Cathedral of the Nativity, rebuilt in the 16th century on a former mosque, blends Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque. Its main façade is sober, but the interior surprises with the brightness of its vaults and the richness of its chapels. Just opposite, the Palacio de Jabalquinto is Baeza's most photographed monument: its Isabelline façade, covered with carved stone diamond points, is a masterpiece of stone lacework from the 15th century. It now houses the headquarters of the International University of Andalusia.

The Old University of Baeza, founded in 1538, is an austere Renaissance building with a beautiful arcaded courtyard. The poet Antonio Machado taught French there between 1912 and 1919—his classroom has remained intact, with his desk, blackboard, and student benches. It was here that he wrote part of his Campos de Castilla. On the wall, a plaque quotes his verses: 'En estos campos de la tierra mía...' Entry is free and the emotion is real.

Finish with a walk along the Paseo de la Constitución and the ramparts, from where the view embraces the olive fields stretching to the horizon. The sunset over this green and silver sea is a daily spectacle that Baeza's inhabitants never tire of watching.

activities.text: Baeza is a town of tapas and olive oil. The bars around Plaza de la Constitución and Calle San Pablo serve generous portions drizzled with Sierra de Cazorla PDO olive oil. Try the ochíos (olive oil bread typical of Jaén), pipirrana (cold salad of tomato, pepper, and cucumber), and revuelto de ajetes (scrambled eggs with tender garlic). Olive oil here isn't a condiment—it's a main ingredient.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
Jaén's Renaissance Jewels: Olive Grove Landscapes & Wild Sierras of Andalusia
Al-Andalus: From the Mezquita to the Alpujarras in 10 Days

visits.text: Balouta is the last village in León province before the borders with Galicia and Asturias converge. With about twenty inhabitants, this Ancares hamlet preserves the highest concentration of pallozas in the entire region—those circular stone houses with thatched rye roofs that look like giant haystacks. Pallozas date back at least to the Middle Ages; their round form helped them withstand the winds and snows of these mountains over 1,000 meters up. Some once sheltered both people and animals under the same roof, the livestock's body heat warming the dwelling. One restored palloza now serves as a bar—make sure to go inside to see the interior. Book your casa rural well in advance; accommodations are extremely scarce.

The neighboring village of Suarbol, 4 km away, has only seven permanent residents (about thirty in summer) and also preserves pallozas. The road between the two villages follows the valley floor of the Ancares, flanked by slopes thick with oaks and chestnuts. You'll also find a small church dedicated to San Justo, with a noble coat of arms on one of the neighboring façades—one of the rare witnesses to the valley's former prosperity.

activities.text: Hiking in the Ancares Biosphere Reserve. Marked trails from Balouta lead to the brañas—high-altitude pastures—where views sweep across multiple valleys simultaneously. Wildlife is remarkable in this seldom-visited area: brown bears (presence confirmed), wolves, deer, and remarkable bird density. Bring proper hiking boots and water—there are no services outside the village.

Watch the sunset from the heights above Balouta. The late-day light on the rye-thatched palloza roofs and green slopes creates one of the Ancares' most striking images. Bring a flashlight for the return.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province

places.name: Banos De La Encina

visits.text: The road south from Almagro crosses the Sierra Morena through the Despeñaperros gorge—the natural frontier between Castile and Andalusia. This narrow pass between quartzite cliffs has been the gateway to Andalusia for centuries: Roman armies, Visigoths, Moors, and Christian armies all passed through. Stop at the viewpoint for a view over the gorges, then detour to the Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa Interpretation Centre in Santa Elena. On 16 July 1212, the Christian coalition led by Alfonso VIII of Castile crushed the Almohad army here—a battle that reversed the course of the Reconquest and definitively opened Andalusia to the Christian kingdoms.

Baños de la Encina appears among the olive groves, dominated by the spectacular silhouette of Castillo de Burgalimar. This Caliphate fortress, built in 968 on the orders of Caliph Al-Hakam II of Córdoba, is one of the best-preserved Muslim fortresses in Europe. Its 14 towers and its wall of tapial (rammed earth, lime, and stone) still stand at their original height—a millennium later. A 15th tower, the Keep, was added by the Christians after the reconquest. It's called 'the fortress of seven kings' because seven monarchs passed through, from Alfonso VII to Ferdinand the Catholic. Climb the 18-metre keep: the panorama over the endless sea of olive trees is one of the most striking images in the province of Jaén.

Coming down from the castle, stroll through the streets of Baños de la Encina. Unlike Andalusia's white villages, here the façades are ashlar stone—16th and 17th-century manor houses with carved coats of arms line the streets. The Church of San Mateo (15th century), in late Gothic style, dominates the main square. Don't miss the anthropomorphic tombs carved into the rock near the Molino del Santo, vestiges of a Visigothic past.

activities.text: Baños de la Encina sits at the heart of the world's largest olive grove—the province of Jaén alone produces about 20% of the world's olive oil. Local operators offer visits to olive groves and oil mills with tastings. You'll learn to distinguish the Picual variety (the most planted here, intense and fruity) from Arbequina, and to recognise a 'virgen extra' from a simple 'virgen'. It's an experience that permanently changes your relationship with olive oil.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance

map_keywords: Castillo de Burgalimar Baños de la Encina
Iglesia San Mateo Baños de la Encina

places.name: Barcelona

visits.text: The Sagrada Família tells the story of a construction begun in 1882 and still unfinished - making it the world's largest incomplete Catholic church. Gaudí took over the project in 1883 and devoted the final 43 years of his life to it. In October 2025, the central tower reached 162 meters, finally surpassing Montjuïc mountain by just one meter - Gaudí believed no human creation should exceed nature. Book your tickets online well in advance, even off-season: 4.5 million visitors flock here annually.

Just steps away, Passeig de Gràcia showcases Gaudí's two wildest houses. Casa Batlló, renovated between 1904 and 1906, is nicknamed the 'house of bones' - its limestone pillars evoke skeletons and its balconies, skulls. Casa Milà (La Pedrera), built between 1906 and 1912, was the last house Gaudí designed before dedicating himself entirely to the Sagrada Família. Its rooftop, with twisted chimneys, offers one of the city's finest vantage points - the classic spot for photographing the Sagrada Família through the arches.

The Gothic Quarter (Barri Gòtic) stands on the ruins of the Roman colony of Barcino, founded around 15 BC. The Cathedral of Saint Eulalia, begun in 1298, houses 13 white geese in its cloister - a tradition linked to the legend of Saint Eulalia, co-patron of Barcelona, who reportedly suffered 13 tortures in Roman times. Get lost in the narrow streets: the Pont del Bisbe, a neo-Gothic footbridge built in 1928 between the Palau de la Generalitat and Casa dels Canonges, is one of the city's most photographed corners.

Park Güell is a 17-hectare park that Gaudí designed from 1900 as a private garden-city for 60 families - a project that never materialized for lack of buyers. The main terrace, covered in multicolored trencadís mosaics and shaped like a winding serpent, offers panoramic views across the entire city to the sea. Arrive early in the morning: the monumental zone is ticketed and subject to visitor quotas.

The Palau de la Música Catalana, built between 1905 and 1908 by Lluís Domènech i Montaner, is one of the world's most beautiful concert halls and a UNESCO World Heritage site. Its red-brick facade and interior skylight of multicolored stained glass filter unique light during concerts. If you're not attending a performance, the guided tour of the hall comes highly recommended.

The El Born neighborhood houses the Picasso Museum, installed in five medieval palaces from the 13th and 15th centuries connected to one another. The collection traces the painter's formative years in Barcelona, notably featuring the complete Las Meninas series (58 variations on Velázquez, created in 1957). The visit requires at least 90 minutes - book your tickets online.

The Basilica of Santa Maria del Mar, built between 1329 and 1383 in Catalan Gothic style, is often preferred to the cathedral by Barcelonans themselves. Its nave is taller, brighter, more austere. It was collectively financed by the port's stevedores - each neighborhood resident brought a stone. It's 'the cathedral of the sea' described by Ildefonso Falcones in his novel of the same name.

If you missed sites during days 1 and 2, this final day in Barcelona is your chance to catch up. Palau Güell, in the El Raval neighborhood, is the first major commission Barcelona gave to Gaudí (1886-1890) - much less known than the Sagrada Família or Casa Batlló, but fascinating for its dark interiors and sculptural chimneys on the roof. The El Raval neighborhood has changed dramatically since the 1990s: it now mixes museums, galleries, and restaurants from around the world.

La Boqueria market, on La Rambla, is one of Europe's most photographed markets. Arrive before 9 am to avoid tour group crowds and find the freshest stalls. Freshly pressed fruit juices are worth the stop. However, the tapas in the front rows are often prepared for tourists - go eat a bit further into El Raval for a more local address.

activities.text: Start the day with coffee and a coca de recapte (Catalan flatbread with toppings) in the Eixample, the modernist district built according to Cerdà's 1860 plan - its perfect grid with chamfered corners is visible from the air. The markets at Sant Antoni or La Boqueria are ideal for a standing breakfast.

In late afternoon, take the cable car or funicular up to Montjuïc to catch the sunset over the port and Barcelona's skyline. The castle and Laribal gardens are worth exploring - the view embraces the entire bay.

In the evening, head to the Gràcia neighborhood for an aperitif vermouth in one of its old-school bars. This neighborhood, formerly an independent village annexed to Barcelona in 1897, has preserved its lively little squares and village atmosphere. Continue with creative tapas in the Eixample or El Born.

In the morning, stroll down La Rambla to the port, then follow the waterfront promenade to Barceloneta beach. This neighborhood, built in the 18th century on a grid plan to house families expropriated from the Ciutadella, retains its characteristic narrow streets. The beach stretches nearly a kilometer - perfect for a morning swim before the crowds arrive.

In the afternoon, explore the markets and boutiques of the El Born neighborhood, one of the city's most creative areas. Don't miss the medieval archaeological remains of the Born market, visible beneath the 1876 iron hall - an entire neighborhood preserved under glass after its destruction in 1714.

Finish with dinner in the Poble-sec neighborhood, at the foot of Montjuïc, where Carrer de Blai avenue is famous for its pintxos bars. It's the local alternative to tourist tapas: cheaper, more authentic, and the portions are generous.

Final swim at Barceloneta or stroll along the waterfront to Port Olímpic, built for the 1992 Olympic Games. The transformation of Barcelona's waterfront after 1992 is one of Europe's most studied urban projects: 4 km of beaches created from scratch where there had been only warehouses and railway tracks.

To finish the trip on a high note, climb to Tibidabo by blue tram or funicular: the hill at 512 meters dominates the entire bay of Barcelona. On clear days, you can see all the way to Majorca. The amusement park at the summit, opened in 1901, is one of Europe's oldest still operating.

visits.text: Baza sits on the Altiplano of Granada, a high plateau at 850 meters altitude whose arid landscapes sometimes evoke Bolivia or New Mexico. The town of 20,000 inhabitants is best known for the Lady of Baza (Dama de Baza), a 4th-century BC Iberian sculpture discovered on July 22, 1971, in the necropolis of Cerro del Santuario. This seated female figure on a throne, originally polychrome (red, blue, white, black), served as a funerary urn - a cavity in her side contained the ashes of a high-ranking woman. The original is in Madrid's National Archaeological Museum, but the local archaeological museum displays a replica and contextualizes the discovery.

Baza's center preserves traces of the Al-Andalus period: the Arab baths (baños árabes), the ruined Alcazaba and the San Juan quarter, the former morería (Moorish quarter). The Iglesia Mayor, in Gothic and Renaissance styles, dominates the Plaza Mayor. Stroll through the narrow streets of the center - Baza has the slow rhythm of a provincial Andalusian town where people still live outdoors, on the squares, between tapas bars and churches.

activities.text: Like Guadix, Baza also has a cave-house quarter, smaller but equally authentic. The Altiplano around the town offers spectacular badlands landscapes - ravines of ochre and red clay sculpted by erosion, reminiscent of the American desert. Several hiking trails cross these lunar landscapes.

In the evening, taste the Altiplano specialties: migas (sautéed semolina crumbs with chorizo and peppers), cordero segureño (local lamb), and goat cheeses. Baza is a tapas town - here, they give you one free with each drink, in the best Granada tradition.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Granada: Alpujarras, Guadix and the Sierras

visits.text: The route northward crosses the first dehesa landscapes—those pastures dotted with centuries-old holm oaks that are the birthplace of the Iberian pig. You're entering the Valle de los Pedroches, the largest continuous dehesa in Europe, extending over 3,600 km² in northern Córdoba province. First stop: Belalcázar, whose Sotomayor y Zúñiga castle can be seen from kilometers away. Built in the 15th century, it has the tallest Torre del Homenaje (Keep) in the entire Iberian Peninsula—47 meters of granite flanked by seven massive towers. The castle's interior unfortunately cannot be visited (under restoration), but its silhouette seen from below is one of the most impressive in Spain. Walk around via the path that circles the hill for the best perspectives.

The Convento de Santa Clara de la Columna, in the village of Belalcázar, is the second most important religious monumental complex in Córdoba province after the Mezquita. Founded in the 15th century, it's a cloistered convent still inhabited by nuns. Guided tours (often dramatized) allow you to discover the cloister, the artesonados (coffered wooden ceilings in Mudéjar style), and the chapel. The nuns sell artisanal pastries through a rotating hatch—a typically Spanish tradition.

Continue 9 km east to Hinojosa del Duque. Its church of San Juan Bautista is nicknamed 'the Cathedral of the Sierra'—and rightly so. Built in Pedroches granite between the 15th and 16th centuries, its 56-meter tower is the work of Hernán Ruiz II, the same architect who transformed the Mezquita's minaret into a bell tower in Córdoba and remodeled the Giralda's campanile in Seville. The interior houses a remarkable baptistery and Baroque altarpieces. The ethnological museum, in the village center, traces rural life in Los Pedroches with agricultural tools, antique toys, and even an old cinema projector.

activities.text: The gastronomy of Los Pedroches revolves around the Iberian pig. The jamón ibérico de bellota DOP Los Pedroches is one of the most renowned in Spain—the black pigs graze freely in the dehesa and feed on holm oak acorns during the montanera (October to February). Regional operators offer visits to ham-curing facilities with tastings and explanations of the aging process. It's a first-rate gastronomic experience. But Los Pedroches isn't just about ham: also try the goat cheeses, rejones (local pastries), and salmorejo serrano.

days_plan.itin_title: Córdoba and the Sierra Morena: From the Caliphate to the Dehesas of Los Pedroches

visits.text: Built in 1456 for Juan Pacheco, Marquis of Villena, Belmonte Castle is one of the best-preserved Gothic-Mudéjar fortresses in Spain. What makes it fascinating: Eugenia de Montijo, Empress of France through her marriage to Napoleon III, owned it in the 19th century and restored the halls—the period furniture is still there. The parapet walk and Mudéjar coffered rooms are worth every cent of the €10 entry.

The organ of the Colegiata de San Bartolomé echoes through 15th-century walnut choir stalls—the first sculpted choir with narrative scenes in Spain, they say. The Colegiata also holds valuable silverwork and paintings, often overlooked by visitors fixated on the castle. Ask at reception if a Mass or musical rehearsal is scheduled: the acoustics are exceptional.

The casco histórico of Belmonte deserves twenty minutes' stroll: the town retains three gates from its medieval walls, the birthplace of poet Fray Luis de León, and Plaza de Correos. Everything is walkable from the castle. Avoid eating on the main square—prices are high; there are better options two streets away.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

places.name: Benicassim

visits.text: The Paseo Pilar Coloma runs along the Mediterranean shore. Fifty-one Belle Époque villas built between 1887 and 1930 line the route—Villa Elisa, Villa Ana, Villa del Mar—recalling the era when Benicàssim was dubbed the "Valencian Biarritz."
Villa Elisa opens for cultural exhibitions. This modernist palace showcases Nolla-style ceramics and salons preserved in their original state.
The Torre de San Vicente dates from the 16th century. This Renaissance watchtower guarded the coast against Barbary pirates. Its square plan and machicolations are characteristic of the period.
The Church of Santo Tomás de Villanueva is an 18th-century neoclassical gem. Its sober facade contrasts with the richness of its interior.
The Desert de les Palmes Natural Park extends over 3,200 hectares. Its peaks—Bartolo (729m) and Mola del Morico (694m)—offer views all the way to the Columbretes Islands.
The Monastery of the Discalced Carmelites dates from the 17th century. The Sacred Art Museum displays antique books and the instruments the monks still use to make their carmelitano liqueur.
The castle of Montornés crowns a promontory at 500 meters altitude. This 10th-century Arab fortress, expanded by Christians after 1233, overlooks the Castellón plain.

activities.text: The trails of Desert de les Palmes wind through pine, holm oak, and aromatic plants. The route to Bartolo from the convent takes about 4 hours.
Benicàssim's beaches total 6 kilometers of fine sand. Voramar, Almadraba, Torre de San Vicente, Els Terrers, and Heliópolis all fly the Blue Flag.
Benicàssim hosts several international festivals—FIB, Rototom Sunsplash, Blues Festival. Check the calendar to catch the summer lineup.
The coastal bike path runs for 15 kilometers along the shore. The panoramic route passes through beaches and the town center with breathtaking Mediterranean views.

days_plan.itin_title: Castellón and Maestrazgo Route: Castles and Coves

map_keywords: Parc Natural Desert de les Palmes

places.name: Betancuria

visits.text: The Iglesia Santa María de Betancuria, founded in 1410 by Jean de Béthencourt in French Gothic style, was Fuerteventura's first cathedral. Rebuilt after pirates destroyed it in 1593, it now blends Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque elements into a harmonious whole.

The Sacred Art Museum displays paintings, sculptures, and religious artifacts from the 15th and 16th centuries. Ornate chalices, ancient missals, and Béthencourt's banner carried during the conquest reveal the island's spiritual heritage.

The Archaeological Museum showcases pottery, bone tools, and fishing equipment of the Mahos, the indigenous people. Discover how they survived with limited resources before Norman arrival, adapting to one of the Canaries' harshest environments.

Wander among the ruins of the San Buenaventura Convent, founded in 1414 by Franciscan monks. As the island's oldest convent, it bears witness to Betancuria's central religious role in the archipelago.
The Mirador de Morro Velosa offers sweeping panoramas across the Betancuria massif. Two bronze statues represent Guise and Ayose, the legendary Maho kings who ruled before conquest.

Lose yourself in the cobbled streets of the historic center. Traditional whitewashed houses, carved wooden doors, and flower-filled balconies create an atmosphere where time seems to have stopped centuries ago.

activities.text: Hike through the Betancuria Rural Park, which covers 90% of the municipality. Ravines, hills, and a small pine forest—Fuerteventura's only one—shelter endemic flora and fauna found nowhere else on Earth.

Descend to Vega del Río Palmas to visit the Ermita de Nuestra Señora de la Peña. This mountain chapel houses the Virgin of the Peña, the island's patron saint, honored during a colorful procession each September.
Savor authentic Canarian cuisine in the village restaurants. PDO Majorero cheese, roasted goat, and traditional Canarian stew feature on every menu, paired with local wines.

days_plan.itin_title: Fuerteventura Road Trip: Dunes, Lagoons & Timeless Villages

map_keywords: Ermita Virgen de la Peña Vega Río Palmas

visits.text: Guggenheim Museum — titanium and Gehry's curves.
Casco Viejo — Siete Calles and cathedral.
Zubizuri Bridge and banks of the Nervión.

activities.text: Pintxos route in the center.
Artxanda cable car for the panorama.
Short river cruise on the Nervión.

days_plan.itin_title: Bay of Biscay Coastal Drive: Fishing Villages & Basque Culinary Soul in France

visits.text: Binibeca Vell, a village rebuilt between 1964 and 1972, unfolds 165 whitewashed houses connected by a labyrinth of winding lanes.
Arched passageways and dark wooden balconies create striking contrasts against the brilliant white walls. A charming faux chapel punctuates the winding route.
The paseo marítimo encircles the village from the small harbor, offering sweeping views over white rooftops meeting the Mediterranean.
Playa de Binibeca stretches 200 meters of fine sand just one kilometer from the village. Shallow, crystalline waters and shady pine groves make it ideal for families.
Cala Torret, at Binibeca's eastern edge, houses a tourist information center and supermarket for practical needs.

activities.text: Visit Binibeca at dawn or early morning to catch the golden light painting the white walls and avoid the midday crowds that flood the narrow lanes.
The crystal-clear waters are perfect for snorkeling, with exceptional visibility revealing rich marine life along the rocky seabed.
Watch sunset from the harbor as the white houses glow pink and gold, creating that quintessential Mediterranean atmosphere in perfect tranquility.

days_plan.itin_title: Menorca Road Trip: Harbors, Whitewashed Villages & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Rome on the Atlantic: that's what Baelo Claudia is. Founded in the 2nd century BC on a Phoenician settlement, it received Roman municipium status under Emperor Claudius—hence the name. Forum, basilica, theater, Temple of Isis, aqueducts, and salting workshops for garum (fish sauce exported throughout the Empire): it's all there, almost intact. Listed as an archaeological site in 1989. Free entry for EU citizens. Allow 2 hours with the museum.

Bolonia beach measures 3.8 km long, 70 m wide, and ends at the west with a 30-meter dune. Not a hotel in sight, not a development—the Strait Natural Park froze construction. The village of Lentiscal at the end of the beach has three chiringuitos; Sirocco is the most reputed for grilled fish. Ideal for lunch between Baelo Claudia and the dune.

activities.text: Cows walk down to Bolonia beach in the morning—nobody explained to them it's a beach. The 30-meter dune, declared a natural monument in 2001, takes 20 minutes to climb from the parking lot: from the summit, the African coast appears on clear days. In summer, the sand burns bare feet. In the afternoon, continue 22 km toward Tarifa for the next stop.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa de la Luz: 7 Days from Seville to Tarifa

places.name: Brihuega

visits.text: Brihuega is nicknamed the "Garden of Alcarria"—and in July, "Spanish Provence." This fortified medieval village, declared a historic ensemble in 1973, overlooks the Tajuña valley from its hill. Begin with the 12th-century ramparts, nearly two kilometers long, pierced by monumental gates: the Puerta de la Cadena, Puerta del Juego de Pelota, and Arco de Cozagón. The Castillo de la Piedra Bermeja, south of the village, dates from the same era and offers a panoramic view over the surrounding fields.

Descend into the Cuevas Árabes, a network of underground galleries carved beneath the village. These cellars once served as water reserves and refuges. The cool atmosphere and stone vaults contrast with the Meseta's summer heat. The guided tour lasts about 30 minutes.

The Real Fábrica de Paños is Brihuega's emblematic civil monument. This royal cloth factory, founded by Ferdinand VI in 1750, was given to the inhabitants as reward for their support during the War of Succession. The 18th-century building, with its characteristic rotunda, is a rare example of industrial architecture from this period in Spain. The gardens surrounding it, laid out in 1813 in a geometric style inspired by Versailles, with cypresses, clipped boxwood, and fountains, justify the town's nickname. Writer Camilo José Cela described them as "a romantic garden, a garden to die of adolescent love."

activities.text: If visiting in July, the lavender fields surrounding Brihuega are in full bloom—over 1,000 hectares of plantations representing 10% of global lavender essential oil production. The spectacle of violet expanses stretching to the horizon is breathtaking. Operators offer guided tours of the fields and local distillery. The rest of the year, Alcarria's hills remain beautiful with their fields of cereals, olive trees, and aromatic plants.

Sample Alcarria honey, one of Spain's most renowned—its production is protected by a designation of origin. Village shops also sell lavender-derived products: soaps, essential oils, liqueurs, and even lavender ice cream. For dinner, order cabrito asado (roast kid), the region's specialty.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

map_keywords: Castillo de la Piedra Bermeja Brihuega

visits.text: Less visited than its neighbors, Bubión has woven its rugs for centuries. At 1,296 meters, the quietest village in the gorge lived for a long time on textile craft—the jarapas alpujarreñas, colorful weavings from recovered fabric scraps, are still made here. Its 16th-century Mudéjar church is more sober than Pampaneira's. An hour, time for a coffee and a tour of the workshops.

The Casa Alpujarreña de Bubión, a small house-museum in a restored traditional dwelling, shows how Morisco then Christian families lived in these villages: slate roofs, wood-fire kitchen, chestnut furniture. Symbolic entry fee, often staffed by local volunteers.

activities.text: The ridge trail between Bubión and Capileira (about 45 minutes uphill) offers the best views of the Poqueira gorge—the ravine below, the Sierra Nevada's residual glaciers across. Marked path, no technical difficulty. Start early morning for light and cool air.

days_plan.itin_title: Al-Andalus: From the Mezquita to the Alpujarras in 10 Days

places.name: Buitrago

visits.text: Buitrago's medieval walls, built between the 11th and 12th centuries, rank among the best preserved in Spain. 800 meters of stone ramparts encircle the old town. The río Lozoya curves around in a natural moat.

Buitrago Castle (Castillo de los Mendoza), a 15th-century Gothic and Mudéjar fortress, dominates the river bend. Property of the powerful Mendoza family. An imposing silhouette with square tower and curtain wall.

The Picasso Museum - Eugenio Arias Collection displays 75 works gifted by Picasso to his barber and friend over 26 years (1948-1972). Drawings, lithographs, ceramics, posters. A moving collection testament to an extraordinary friendship.

The Church of Santa María del Castillo blends Gothic and Mudéjar styles (14th-15th centuries). Commissioned by the Marquis of Santillana. Remarkable Mudéjar ceiling, interior restored after Civil War fire. Adjacent medieval cemetery.
The 14th-century Puente del Arrabal, built on the Cañada Real Segoviana (royal drove road), offers magnificent views of the fortified town. Ancient stone gracefully spanning the Lozoya.
Pass through the Torre del Reloj, a 16-meter-high clock tower standing apart from the main wall. The principal gateway into the medieval citadel.

activities.text: Walk the rampart walkways (Adarve). The Adarve Bajo follows the Lozoya at 6 meters high and 2 meters thick. The Adarve Alto to the south features defensive towers and bastions. Exceptional panoramas at every turn.
Wander the cobbled lanes of the historic center. Traditional whitewashed houses, the lively Plaza de la Constitución. The Arco de las Nieves and fortified gates punctuate your route.
Riosequillo Reservoir, 2 km away, boasts one of the largest swimming pools in the Community of Madrid. Play areas, gardens and full services for a perfect summer day by the water.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Guadarrama Road Trip: Castles and Forests

map_keywords: Iglesia Santa María del Castillo Buitrago

visits.text: The Cueva Turche hides 15 minutes' drive from Buñol, in a valley most visitors ignore in favour of the Tomatina. Result: a near-deserted forest trail leading to a cave with an interior waterfall. The marked circuit is 3km return from the car park.

activities.text: The water drops seven meters into a natural pool where swimming is possible from June to September. The water temperature stays cool even in midsummer—count on 18°C. Walking shoes essential on the trail, the ground is slippery after rain.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages

places.name: Burgos

visits.text: On the road between Sigüenza and Burgos, obligatory stop at Santo Domingo de Silos. The Benedictine monastery was refounded in the 11th century by Saint Dominic, and El Cid himself offered it two villages on May 12, 1076. Centerpiece: the Romanesque cloister from the 11th-12th centuries, considered Spain's most beautiful—64 all-different capitals, eight large reliefs at the corners recounting Christ's life, and on the ceiling a painted Mudéjar framework. Don't miss the 1705 apothecary. The monks sing mass in Gregorian chant each morning at 9am (11am Sundays)—their album Canto sold over 6 million copies in 1994. Umberto Eco made the central character of The Name of the Rose a monk from here. Entry €4, closed Mondays.

If one had to name a single Spanish Gothic cathedral, it would be this one—the country's only one inscribed alone at UNESCO, without urban context around it. Four centuries of construction: laid by Ferdinand III in 1221, completed in 1567. Plan from Bourges, west façade recalling Paris and Reims, openwork spires completed in the 15th century by German master Jean of Cologne. Under the dome rests El Cid Campeador, repatriated in 1921 with Doña Jimena. Look up at the nave's foot: the Papamoscas, 16th-century automaton, opens its mouth every hour on the hour. And in the Holy Christ chapel, one of the peninsula's 'dressed christs,' draped with real skin (animal, despite the legend of human skin) and real hair. It's macabre, you can't look away. Two full hours, minimum.

Leave the center by 3 km, toward Fuentes Blancas park, for the Cartuja de Miraflores. The setting is almost silent: a Carthusian monastery, few visitors, and at the back of the church, three alabaster tombs that pass for the absolute summit of Spanish funerary sculpture. Isabella the Catholic commissioned them in 1486 from Gil de Siloé, for her parents John II and Isabella of Portugal, and for her little brother who died at 14. The royal tomb is carved as an eight-pointed star—a starry vault projected to the ground, you walk around it not knowing where to look. The polychrome altarpiece (1496-1499), just behind, is gilded with fine gold from Christopher Columbus's expedition: the first gold from the Americas. Free entry, suggested donation. Go early morning, you'll be almost alone.

Stop at the Museum of Human Evolution, right in the center. It's the smart briefing to do before seeing Atapuerca tomorrow—otherwise, you'll arrive at the site, see a hole in the rock, and leave frustrated. Here, everything is made legible: life-size reconstructions of the Sima de los Huesos, original fragments of Homo antecessor and heidelbergensis under glass, videos on forty years of excavations. The building is by Juan Navarro Baldeweg, white, luminous, designed so you cross the ages by climbing levels. Allow 2 hours, it's not wasted time.

If time permits, climb to Burgos Castle through the Cid gardens—20-minute climb, complete panorama over the cathedral and Castilian plain. The castle ruins (11th century, demolished during the Napoleonic wars in 1813) house a small museum and underground galleries. Free entry for exterior.

Atapuerca is a quarry. Really. An abandoned railway trench in the sierra, 15 km from Burgos. A shuttle from Ibeas de Juarros, a guide for three hours, and you leave with the idea of having seen Europe's oldest face. In the Gran Dolina, fragments of Homo antecessor unearthed in 1994 and named in 1997 by Bermúdez de Castro, Arsuaga and Carbonell. In the Sima del Elefante, a 1.4-million-year-old jaw. And in the Sima de los Huesos, 6,500 fossils of Homo heidelbergensis piled at the bottom of a shaft—deposited there 430,000 years ago, intentionally. If confirmed, it's the very first funerary gesture of our lineage. You look at a hole in the rock, you understand this, and that's it, the vertigo. Advance booking required, €6 combined with CAREX (experimental archaeology workshop, perfect for children).

On the return road to Burgos, stop at San Pedro de Cardeña, 10 km southeast. It's in this monastery that El Cid, banished by King Alfonso VI in 1081, left Doña Jimena and his two daughters before departing to die of honor elsewhere—the scene is in the Cantar de mio Cid, around verse 250, and it's one of medieval Spanish literature's most beautiful farewell sequences. El Cid himself then rested here 700 years, from 1102 to 1842, before being transferred to the cathedral. Today the monastery is held by Trappists, the Romanesque church and cloister can be visited (€3), and the shop sells their chocolate—which is advised to take. Seriously.

If you skipped Las Huelgas yesterday, now's the time to return. West of the city, founded in 1187 by Alfonso VIII and his English wife Eleanor Plantagenet—sister of Richard the Lionheart, yes. Officially, it's the pantheon of the kings of Castile. Unofficially, it's one of the Middle Ages' most striking feminine narratives in Spain: for seven centuries, the abbess of Las Huelgas reigned over 14 towns and 50 villages, appointed priests, signed sentences, answering only to the pope. She sometimes had herself represented in miter and crosier, bishop's attributes, which no canonical law authorized her to wear. The chapter house holds the royal sepulchers and the pendón—the standard torn from the Almohad caliph's tent at the Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa in 1212. Guided tour only.

activities.text: Dinner at Cobo Vintage (starred, formerly Cobo Tradición), Calle La Merced—Miguel Cobo works with IGP lechazo lamb from Castilla y León (under 35 days), morcilla de Burgos (rice blood sausage), and honors Ribera vegetables. Otherwise, La Favorita for a more simple Castilian taberna, local product platters, roast cordero to share.

Morcilla bar crawl around Calle San Lorenzo—Burgos morcilla is eaten as hot tapas on toasted bread, with a Ribera del Duero crianza. La Cabaña Arandina, Casa Pancho, El Huerto de Roque are safe bets. Count €2-3 per tapa.

Lunch before departure at La Favorita or Casa Ojeda, Calle Vitoria—Casa Ojeda is the institution since 1912, wood-fired lamb is their signature. Specialties to try: sopa castellana (garlic soup), olla podrida (red bean stew), tarta de queso with Burgos cheese.

Return to Madrid by AVE from Burgos-Rosa de Lima station, 7 km from center. Journey takes 1h40, around €50-65 depending on booking. The station is recent (2008), minute parking, easy taxi from center (€8-10). Trains roughly every 2 hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León Road Trip: Cathedrals and Medieval Villages
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks
Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Castile and León - 14-Day Grand Tour
Castile and León - 14-Day Grand Tour
Mystical Castile: Toledo, Ávila, Salamanca & Burgos in 11 Days
Mystical Castile: Toledo, Ávila, Salamanca & Burgos in 11 Days

map_keywords: San Pedro de Cardeña Monastery Burgos

places.name: Cabeza Del Buey

visits.text: French abbot Henri Breuil catalogued the schematic paintings of the sierras around Cabeza del Buey in 1933. Around 70 rock-art sites are now recorded across the sierras de Tiros, Rinconada, and Pedregoso. The Centro de Interpretación de la Pintura Rupestre, Plaza de la Constitución, serves as the starting point. Free, but open on the 2nd Saturday of the month: book ahead.

The modest Iglesia de Nuestra Señora de Armentera, in the centre, conceals a surprise: the 1788 sacristy is entirely covered with murals on eucharistic themes. Barrel vaults, decorated lunettes. Free, often open in the morning.

activities.text: The sierras de Tiros and Pedregoso harbour rock shelters painted 5,000 years ago: Valle del Aliso, Cerro Estanislao, Morro de la Venta. Some are classified as Bien de Interés Cultural and form part of a European route. Allow half a day with a guide.

The Centro de Interpretación de la Pintura Rupestre is more than a museum: it's the tourist information point for all of Cabeza del Buey. Hiking maps, trail advice, permanent exhibition on Extremadura's rock art. Plaza de la Constitución 3.

days_plan.itin_title: La Siberia and La Serena: Secret Extremadura in 6 Days

map_keywords: Rock Art Interpretation Centre Cabeza del Buey

visits.text: Wild, truly. No café, no paved parking—just 17 km of virgin coast protected since 1992. The Cabo Cope and Puntas de Calnegre Regional Park resisted several killer real-estate projects, including the infamous Marina de Cope canceled by the Constitutional Court in 2012. The Torre de Cope (16th century, heritage site) still watches for imaginary pirates. The spur-thighed tortoise, unique in Europe, nests here.

activities.text: If you come in spring, orchids and caralluma (endemic plant) blanket the cliffs. The route from Torre de Cope to the summit of the cape takes 2 hours round trip. On strong levante days, the blowholes (bufaderos) in the rock put on a sonic show. Dolphins and pilot whales are regularly spotted from the cliffs.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca

places.name: Cabo De Gata

visits.text: Mónsul beach is the natural park's most famous stretch. Enormous black lava tongues frame its golden sand—this is where a scene from Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade was filmed. At the beach's center, a massive wind-sculpted rock serves as a landmark.

Want a kilometer of pristine sand? Los Genoveses beach stretches across a bay bordered by dunes, without a single building in sight. The shallow water slopes gently, perfect for swimming. In summer, vehicle access is limited to a few cars per day.

The Arrecife de las Sirenas (Mermaid Reef) at the cape's tip owes its name to the monk seals sailors once mistook for mermaids. Dark volcanic chimneys emerge from turquoise waters, right at the foot of the Cabo de Gata lighthouse built in 1863.
The village of San José serves as the park's main gateway. Its white houses and small fishing port live by the rhythm of the seasons. Terraces facing the sea serve fresh fish cuisine.

The Cabo de Gata salt flats extend across 400 hectares between the road and the sea. Over 80 bird species frequent these shallow basins, including flamingos visible from observation posts along the site.
Climb up to Mirador de La Amatista for a plunging view over the volcanic cliffs and Pico de los Frailes, the park's highest point. The violet-hued rocks give the viewpoint its name.

activities.text: Coastal hike between Genoveses and Mónsul along the ridges. The trail follows the cliffs and crosses several deserted little coves with colorful geological formations—white, orange, gray.
Snorkeling in the clear waters around San José. Posidonia seagrass meadows shelter wrasses, white seabreams, and octopuses among the volcanic rocks.
Flamingo and black-winged stilt watching at the salt flats, ideally late in the day when the low light colors the basins pink and gold.

days_plan.itin_title: Almería Road Trip: Volcanic Coves and Tabernas Desert
Wild Coast: Málaga to Cartagena in 9 Days

map_keywords: Playa de Mónsul Cabo de Gata
Playa de los Genoveses San José
Arrecife de las Sirenas Cabo de Gata
Las Salinas de Cabo de Gata
Mirador de La Amatista Cabo de Gata

places.name: Cabo De Palos

visits.text: The Cabo de Palos lighthouse rises 81 meters above the sea, built in 1865. Climb 300 steps for views to Alicante on clear days and the distant Algerian coast. Spain's second-highest lighthouse.
Calblanque Regional Park protects fossil dunes, virgin beaches, and salt lagoons. Coastal trails wind from Cala Flores to Calarreona through untouched wilderness.
The Islas Hormigas rise 2.5 nautical miles offshore. Volcanic seabeds, giant groupers, and posidonia meadows—voted Europe's best dive site in 2016 and 2018.
The fishing village retains whitewashed houses, a harbor lined with brightly painted boats, and grilled-fish restaurants that open onto the sea.

activities.text: Hike from Cabo de Palos to Calblanque. Start at the harbor, climb to the viewpoint, then descend to Cala Marina. 4 km one way, 1 hour 15 minutes.
The Marchamalo salt pans host flamingos, black-winged stilts, and avocets. Birdwatching hide, best from September to April.
Lunch at the harbor: grilled sea bream, red mullet, octopus. Terraces overlook the bobbing fishing boats at anchor.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Road Trip: Baroque Splendor & Costa Cálida
Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca

map_keywords: Playa de Levante Cabo de Palos

visits.text: The Torre de Bujaco (12th century) towers over Plaza Mayor. This 25-meter Almohad tower, built on Roman foundations, once guarded the northwestern flank of the walled city.
Step through the Arco de la Estrella, main gateway to the walled city since the 15th century. The Catholic Monarchs swore their oath to honor Cáceres' privileges here in 1477 and 1478.
The Museo de Cáceres occupies two 16th-century palaces. In the basement, a perfectly preserved Almohad cistern reveals the medieval city's ingenious water system.
Plaza de Santa María brings together the Co-Cathedral (13th century) and the palaces of noble families. The transition from Romanesque to Gothic is visible in the sculpted portals.
Wander through the Jewish quarter's whitewashed alleyways. The Ermita de San Antonio rises on the ruins of an ancient synagogue.
The Almohad ramparts stretch for 1,174 meters with some thirty towers. Climb to the Baluarte de los Pozos for sweeping views across the city.
Make your way to Plaza de San Jorge to see the Iglesia de San Francisco Javier. This 18th-century Jesuit baroque church conceals an ancient underground cistern.

activities.text: Climb the ramparts at sunset. The golden stone blazes with light as the towers stand silhouetted against the Extremadura plains.
Sample Torta del Casar, a creamy sheep's milk cheese with an oozing, spreadable texture. Local cheesemakers offer guided tastings.
Ibérico tapas on Plaza Mayor. The pata negra ham comes from pigs fattened on acorns in the surrounding oak forests.
After nightfall, the palaces light up. Walk through medieval lanes where not a single modern sign breaks the spell.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas
Extremadura Road Trip: Cáceres, Mérida & Trujillo

places.name: Cadaques

visits.text: Cadaqués is accessible only by a winding road from Roses - which long preserved it from mass tourism and attracted Dalí. The village of white houses by the water has changed very little since the early 20th century. Start with the 16th-century Church of Santa Maria, whose gilded baroque altarpiece was painted by local artists. The port with its colorful boats moored facing the white facades is one of the most copied views of the Costa Brava.

The Salvador Dalí House-Museum in Portlligat, 2 km north of the village, is the only permanent residence the artist ever had. In 1930, he bought a 22 m² fisherman's hut - penniless, estranged from his family - and expanded it over 40 years by buying neighboring cottages, piece by piece. He lived and worked there until Gala's death in 1982, then never returned. Reservations are mandatory and groups are limited to 8 people - book at least 2 to 3 weeks in advance in summer.

Cap de Creus, 15 km from Cadaqués, is the easternmost point of the Iberian Peninsula and one of Catalonia's wildest natural parks. The granite rocks sculpted by the tramontana - the north wind that blows here up to 150 km/h - directly inspired Dalí's lunar landscapes. The lighthouse restaurant offers a 180-degree view of the sea. Bring a jacket even in summer: the tramontana can pick up without warning.

activities.text: Hike to Cap Norfeu, between Roses and Cadaqués: from the viewpoint, the view plunges over a succession of wild coves accessible only on foot or by boat. The well-marked 6 km round-trip trail from the parking lot takes about 3 hours.

In late afternoon, settle into one of the tapas bars on Cadaqués' waterfront. The low light on the white facades and boats at this hour is extraordinary - Dalí himself said that Cadaqués had taught him aesthetics. Grilled cuttlefish and Empordà anchovies are the local specialties to order.

days_plan.itin_title: Barcelona and Costa Brava Road Trip: Coves and Villages

map_keywords: Salvador Dalí House Museum Portlligat Cadaqués

visits.text: Cádiz Cathedral blends Baroque grandeur with neoclassical restraint. Its construction spanned 116 years—from 1722 to 1838—under five successive architects. The dome, sheathed in gleaming golden tiles, once served as a landmark for sailors navigating the Atlantic. In the crypt lies composer Manuel de Falla, a native son of the city.

Climb the 170 steps of Torre del Poniente for a sweeping 360-degree panorama of Cádiz Bay, whitewashed rooftops, and the endless Atlantic. This vantage point makes it clear why the Phoenicians chose this peninsula in 1100 BC, making Cádiz the oldest continuously inhabited city in Western Europe.

The Roman Theatre of Gades was discovered by chance in 1980 beneath the houses of El Pópulo quarter. Built in the 1st century BC by Balbus, it seated 20,000 spectators. With a diameter of 120 meters, it ranks among the largest Roman theatres in the empire, a testament to Cádiz's ancient prominence.

Enter the Barrio del Pópulo through one of its three 13th-century medieval arches: Arco del Pópulo, Arco de la Rosa, or Arco de los Blancos. This oldest neighborhood in Cádiz preserves its labyrinth of narrow lanes and shaded squares, straddling Roman ruins and medieval foundations.

Torre Tavira stands tallest among the 160 watchtowers that Cádiz merchants built to spot their ships arriving from the Americas. At 45 meters high, its camera obscura projects live images of the city onto a concave screen—a mesmerizing 18th-century technology still captivating visitors today.

Walk along the Paseo Campo del Sur facing the ocean to reach Parque Genovés. This clifftop botanical garden showcases century-old palms, artificial waterfalls, and exotic species brought back from the Americas by Cádiz navigators during the golden age of transatlantic trade.

activities.text: La Caleta beach, the only beach in the historic center, nestles between the castles of Santa Catalina and San Sebastián. It featured in the James Bond film "Die Another Day." At sunset, colorful fishing boats and the castle silhouette create a picture-perfect scene against the amber sky.

Step into a freiduría and order a paper cone of pescaíto frito: crispy-fried shrimp, baby squid, hake strips, and bienmesabe in golden batter. This is Cádiz's signature street food, best enjoyed standing in the lively La Viña neighborhood with a cold beer in hand.

The Gran Teatro Falla, built in neo-Mudéjar style, hosts Cádiz Carnival every February—one of Spain's most celebrated festivals. Outside carnival season, catch a performance in this 19th-century architectural gem that seamlessly blends Moorish and Spanish influences.

days_plan.itin_title: Cádiz Road Trip: Jerez, Sherry Bodegas & White Villages
Costa de la Luz: 7 Days from Seville to Tarifa

visits.text: Cala Galdana curves in a perfect horseshoe bay spanning 300 meters of powdery sand. Calm, shallow waters and Blue Flag status make it Menorca's most family-friendly beach.
Cala Macarella, reachable on foot from Cala Galdana, dazzles with brilliant white sand embraced by 40-meter cliffs. Dense pine forests shade the approach to this postcard-perfect cove.
Cala Macarelleta, a tiny adjacent cove, lies just 15 minutes beyond Macarella. Turquoise waters lap against pristine shores in this wonderfully unspoiled hideaway.
Viewpoints punctuate the coastal trail between Galdana and Macarelleta, offering vertiginous views down limestone cliffs to hidden coves gleaming impossibly blue below.
Cala Mitjana, accessible via the Camí de Cavalls, reveals an isolated beach surrounded by lush vegetation. Far fewer visitors means rare tranquility even in summer.

activities.text: The Camí de Cavalls GR223, a historic 185-kilometer trail, encircles the entire island along the coast. The Galdana-Macarella section covers an easy, scenic three kilometers.
The hike to Macarelleta winds through fragrant pine forests and rocky ravines. Well-marked trails lead over dramatic terrain with spectacular coastal vistas at every turn.
Shaded picnic areas beneath umbrella pines offer perfect lunch spots. Bring provisions from Cala Galdana to enjoy lazy afternoon meals in dappled Mediterranean light.

days_plan.itin_title: Menorca Road Trip: Harbors, Whitewashed Villages & Hidden Coves

visits.text: World's first cereal PDO, granted in 1986: Calasparra Bomba rice preceded all others. The terraced paddies, irrigated by cold waters from the Segura and Mundo rivers between 350 and 500 m altitude, produce a short-grain rice with resistant texture and high absorption. The October visit (manual harvest) is particularly photogenic. The Sanctuary of the Virgen de la Esperanza, carved into the rock above the river, is the site's other curiosity.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca

places.name: Calatanazor

visits.text: To optimize the day before heading to Segovia in the afternoon, start at opening time with La Fuentona de Muriel: a karst spring that surges from a rocky amphitheater as if water appears from nowhere. An accessible trail follows the Abión River to the spring, then a fork leads to a waterfall (with water in wet season). Start from the Muriel de la Fuente parking lot, about 7 km round trip, 3 hours with breaks.

Two kilometers from La Fuentona, the Sabinar de Calatañazor Nature Reserve is Europe's densest forest of Spanish junipers (Juniperus thurifera). Some specimens reach 15 meters in height and 5 meters in diameter, with ages potentially exceeding 400 years. The resinous, slightly incense-like scent emanating from these twisted trees is unforgettable. Allow about an hour's walk.

Finish with the village of Calatañazor—'Qal'at al-Nusur' in Arabic, the 'castle of eagles.' Declared a historic and artistic ensemble in 1962, it preserves its arcaded medieval main street, adobe and juniper-beam houses, and castle ruins. History says that here in July 1002 Christian armies inflicted on Arab general Almanzor the wound from which he would die a month later, giving birth to the Spanish saying 'At Calatañazor, Almanzor lost his drum.' Head to Segovia around 1pm.

activities.text: Walk through the juniper forest where centenarian trees create a mystical atmosphere. Thrushes, magpies and crows feed on their berries in winter.
Push on to the natural monument of La Fuentona, a karst rock spring giving birth to the Abión River. Underwater caves open in its depths.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

map_keywords: Casa del Parque Sabinar Muriel de la Fuente

visits.text: Twenty-five minutes' drive east from Medinaceli brings you to the Monastery of Santa María de Huerta, one of Spain's most beautiful Cistercian monasteries. Founded in 1162, expropriated in 1835 during the Mendizábal disentailment, then returned to the order in 1930, it's still home to some twenty monks. Push through the door and you enter eight centuries of silence. The Gothic cloister (13th century), with its pointed arcades and austere garden, prepares you for the shock of the refectory—an immense hall covered with sexpartite rib vaults, pierced by tall windows that flood the space with light. It's one of Europe's most beautiful monastic refectories.

Continue 1h15 west to enter Aragon and reach Calatayud, second city of Zaragoza province. The ancient Bilbilis (birthplace of the poet Martial) is dominated by the ruins of the Castillo de Ayub, the Arab citadel that gave the town its name (Qal'at Ayyub—Ayyub's castle). But it's Aragonese Mudéjar that makes Calatayud rich: the Collegiate Church of Santa María la Mayor boasts a unique open-air cloister and a Mudéjar brick portal decorated with ceramics. Calatayud's Mudéjar art forms part of the UNESCO inscription of Mudéjar Architecture of Aragon (2001).

Stroll through Calatayud's historic centre, between Plaza de España (with its fountain and arcades) and the lanes climbing towards the castle ruins. The church of San Pedro de los Francos (14th century), with its leaning Mudéjar tower (yes, it leans—about 2 degrees), is the building where the Aragonese Cortes proclaimed Ferdinand the Catholic heir to the throne in 1461. The medieval Jewish cemetery, discovered in 1980, testifies to the significant Sephardic community that lived here before the 1492 expulsion.

activities.text: If you're a wine lover, Calatayud sits at the heart of the DO Calatayud, an appellation producing Garnacha from old vines growing on slate soils at 700–1,000 m altitude. Several cellars offer tastings. Pair your glass with Aragonese tapas: migas, roast ternasco lamb, or frutas de Aragón (candied fruits coated in chocolate).

days_plan.itin_title: Alcarria and Guadalajara: Between Madrid and Aragon

places.name: Callosa De Ensarria

visits.text: 'Algar' comes from the Arabic for cave. The water of the Algar River has carved over millennia a karst landscape of waterfalls and natural pools known as tolls. The 1.5 km circuit follows the river from the Moro spring to the grand cascade of Toll de la Caldera. Declared a protected wetland in 2002. Entry between €4-5 depending on season.

activities.text: Swim in the tolls, these pools carved by water into the limestone rock over thousands of years. The water temperature hovers around 17°C year-round—invigorating in summer. The most crystalline pools lie upstream, past the Pou dels Esclaus. Arrive at opening (9 AM) to avoid school groups in season. Water shoes recommended.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

map_keywords: Fuentes del Algar Callosa de Ensarriá

visits.text: 332 meters of raw limestone surging from the sea: the Peñón de Ifach dominates the landscape before you even reach Calpe. This 53-hectare natural park, protected since 1987, harbors more than 300 plant species and some 80 bird species. An Iberian village from the 4th century BC left traces at its base. The tunnel carved in 1918 by a private landowner provides access to the upper section. Online reservation required to climb.

activities.text: Climb after 4 PM in summer when the heat subsides and the views toward Benidorm and Altea take on a golden light. The round-trip hike covers 2 km with 291 meters of elevation gain. Beyond the tunnel, ropes fixed in the rock help with the final climb. Hiking boots mandatory—rangers turn away anyone wearing flip-flops at the entrance.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

visits.text: Cambados presents itself as the capital of Albariño—6,000 inhabitants, port backed onto the Arousa ría, vineyards all around. The Praza de Fefiñáns, the main square, is one of Galicia's finest: 5,000 m² of cobbles, framed by the 17th-century Pazo de Fefiñáns (still private property, tasting possible), the 18th-century Church of Saint Benedict and the northern arcades. Sunday morning market, music festivals in July. Allow 45 minutes for the square and adjacent lanes. Noble and maritime atmosphere, without tourist hordes.

The ruínas de Santa Mariña Dozo, 800 m from the centre, are one of Galicia's most mysterious images: a 15th-century Gothic church, abandoned in 1840, whose walls and round arches still stand in the middle of a still-active cemetery. Collapsed roof, sky visible through the ogives, ivy on the stones. Century-old cypresses, fishermen's graves with repeated names. Free access, photogenic at dusk. Allow 30 minutes. Avoid visits during funerals (often Friday-Saturday mornings).

The mosteiro de Armenteira, 12 km east of Cambados, is a Cistercian monastery founded in 1149 by Don Ero—a knight turned monk according to legend, who supposedly slept for 300 years in the forest listening to a bird sing. The 12th-century Romanesque church, 17th-century conventual buildings and a marked meditative path in the surrounding woods ('Ruta da Pedra e da Auga', 8 km, 3h, gentle slopes) merit the stop. Monks still present, simple welcome. Free entry, donations appreciated.

activities.text: Albariño DO Rías Baixas is one of Europe's great whites—grape planted here for 800 years (origin legend: Cistercian monks brought the vine from the Rhine), harvested late August, aged on lees. 4,800 hectares, 180 wineries. Visit + tasting at Martín Códax (best known, easy parking, €12), Pazo de Señoráns (superior quality, €20) or Bodegas Granbazán (17th-century noble architecture, €15). Booking advised. Combining with lunch on-site is the perfect option.

If you catch the first Sunday in August, the Festa do Albariño is in full swing—100,000 visitors over 5 days, tastings at €1 per glass in the streets, gaita concerts, seafood cooked in the open air. Off-season, Cambados keeps the maritime vibe—port 2 km from the centre, mussel farms visible from the jetty (boatmen offer tours in traditional dorna boats at €20 for 1h). The marisqueada (6-seafood platter) at Yayo Daporta (1 Michelin star, €75) or A Esmorga (€35) remains the local reference.

days_plan.itin_title: Galicia: Complete 10-Day Circuit

visits.text: Stop in Reus en route, 20 km from Cambrils—essential for Modernist architecture enthusiasts. This is where Antoni Gaudí was born on June 25, 1852. His birthplace isn't open to visitors, but the Gaudí Centre pays homage with tactile models, 360° projections, and a three-story journey. Rarer still: Casa Navàs, designed by Lluís Domènech i Montaner for a wealthy textile merchant, retains its period furniture intact—a unique case in European Art Nouveau. The Modernist Route passes by 26 buildings in the center.

The fishing port of Cambrils is one of Catalonia's oldest. This is where, in 1229, part of James the Conqueror's fleet departed—155 ships—to retake Majorca from the Moors. Today, 13 trawlers still go out each morning. The Far Roig (Red Lighthouse) at the end of the jetty is the standard-bearer of Cambrils photography. Walk along the Mollet del Rec, sit at a terrace, watch the nets dry.

The Vila Vella, old Cambrils, is the peasant counterpoint to the port. Fortified under Peter IV of Aragon in the 14th century, it's organized around the Plaça de la Vila—a small arcaded square where the town hall once met. The parish church of Santa Maria mixes Baroque, Renaissance, and neo-Gothic. Further on, the Torre de l'Ermita de la Mare de Déu del Camí, from the 14th century, and the Roman villa of La Llosa recall that the area has been inhabited for 2,000 years.

5 km north, the Parc Samà is a colonial-style botanical garden commissioned in 1881 by the Marquis of Marianao, a wealthy industrialist returned from Cuba. Lakes with swans, century-old palms, waterfalls, mini-zoo, neoclassical summer residence: it feels like Havana transposed to Catalonia. A good green escape after the beach. Kids love it.

activities.text: At 5 p.m., the trawlers return to port. Hundreds of seagulls circle above the holds, fishermen unload the crates: sea bream, squid, sole, red mullet, mantis shrimp, and the famous gamba roja de Tarragona that fetches premium prices. The auction has been modernized—electronic screens instead of shouting—but the spectacle of the unloading remains intact. Cambrils stops fishing in May–June for biological closure: avoid these two months if you want to see the arrival. Monday to Friday only.

Cambrils has two Michelin-starred restaurants and around 150 restaurants total—the highest gastronomic density on the Costa Daurada. House classics: suquet de peix (fish stew), arròs negre (squid ink rice), fideuà (noodle paella), and romesco sauce in every variation. For luxury, Rincón de Diego or Can Bosch, both Michelin-starred. For value, Casa Macarrilla 1966 and Denver, whose owner buys his catch daily at the fish auction.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Catalonia in 10 Days: Tarragona, Priorat & the Ebro Delta
Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour
Southern Catalonia in 5 Days: Roman Tarragona, Poblet, Priorat and the Port of Cambrils

places.name: Campo De Criptana

visits.text: Ten white windmills stand atop the Sierra de los Molinos, remnants of the 32 that dominated the town in the 16th century. Three—Infante, Sardinero and Burleta—retain their period machinery intact and are designated national monuments.
The Poyatos mill serves as both tourist office and interpretation center. Inside, models and maps explain the workings of Manchegan windmills and how the blades were oriented to face the wind.

From the Cerro de la Paz, the view sweeps across the white rooftops of the village and the immensity of the Castilian plain. This is where Cervantes set Don Quixote's battle against the 'thirty outrageous giants.'

Descend toward the Pósito Real, a 16th-century grain warehouse expanded under Charles III. Its façade bears two coats of arms of the Order of Santiago. It regulated the price of grain during periods of scarcity.
Cave houses carved into the rock line the windmill route. The Casa Cueva de Mambrino houses a museum that recreates troglodyte life with agricultural tools and 16th-century utensils.

The hermitage of the Virgen de Criptana, in Renaissance style, has dominated the hill since the 16th century. Below, the hermitage of Santa Ana and that of the Vera Cruz complete the religious heritage.

activities.text: Arrive at dawn on the Sierra de los Molinos. The absence of vegetation, arid soil and low morning light create an austere, photogenic landscape—the windmills stand out one by one against the horizon.
The Bodega Cooperativa Vinícola del Carmen, founded in 1897, is considered the oldest wine cooperative in Spain. Taste DO La Mancha wines made from Tempranillo and Airén grapes.
Sample gachas manchegas, a flour porridge accompanied by chorizo and fried lard, and Manchegan ratatouille (pisto), typical regional dishes.

days_plan.itin_title: La Mancha Road Trip: Don Quixote's Windmills and Cuenca
The Don Quixote Route: A Road Trip Through Castilla-La Mancha's Windmills & Plains
Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

Sierra de los Molinos Campo de Criptana

places.name: Cangas De Onis

visits.text: The so-called 'Roman' bridge of Cangas de Onís actually dates from the 13th century, built on ancient foundations. Its raised central arch, flanked by two smaller asymmetric arches, spans the Sella River. A replica of the Cross of Victory has hung from it since 1939.
Steps from the bridge, the Hermitage of Santa Cruz conceals a megalithic dolmen beneath its altar. This small edifice founded in 737 covers one of the oldest funerary monuments in Asturias.

Take the winding road up to the Sanctuary of Covadonga, birthplace of the Reconquest. The neo-Romanesque basilica in pink limestone, built between 1877 and 1901, raises its twin spires above the narrow valley.

Descend into the Santa Cueva, a cave carved into the cliff face that houses the statue of Our Lady of Covadonga and the tomb of King Pelayo. Beneath the rocky vault, a waterfall plunges into a pool of green water. Below, the Fuente de los Siete Caños invites the superstitious: drinking from all seven spouts supposedly promises marriage within the year.

Cangas de Onís was the first capital of the Kingdom of Asturias until 774. On the central square, a bronze statue of Pelayo the Conqueror commemorates the victory of 722 that launched eight centuries of Reconquest.

activities.text: Embark on a canoe descent of the Sella, a popular route between Cangas de Onís and Ribadesella. The river flows through landscapes of green hills, dense forests, and small Asturian villages.
Try the fabada asturiana in one of the town's restaurants. This hearty stew of white beans, chorizo, morcilla, and pork is Asturias' signature dish.
On Sunday mornings, Cangas de Onís market takes over the square with stalls of Cabrales cheese, heather honey, artisan cider, and smoked mountain charcuterie.

days_plan.itin_title: Asturias Road Trip: Cantabrian Coast and Picos de Europa

map_keywords: Ermita Santa Cruz Cangas de Onís

visits.text: From Granada, expect about an hour's drive to reach Lanjarón, the gateway to the Alpujarras. This small spa town is famous for its mineral water (Spain's best-selling brand) and its ruined Arab castle, the last Moorish stronghold to fall in 1500. Make a quick stop to refill your water bottle at one of the public fountains and grab a coffee on the plaza before continuing.

Pass through Órgiva (the valley's main town, market day on Thursdays) and climb towards the Barranco de Poqueira, the spectacular gorge where the three most famous villages of the Alpujarras cascade down the mountainside. First stop: Pampaneira (1,058 m), the lower village. Its 340 inhabitants live among whitewashed houses with flat roofs (the "terraos"), sloping cobbled lanes, covered passages (the "tinaos") and spring-fed fountains. On the main square, taste the water from the San Antonio fountain - legend says it helps you find love. Pampaneira is also the Alpujarras' artisan village: the "jarapas", those colorful rugs woven from recycled fibers, are the local specialty. Step into a weaving workshop to watch the artisans at work.

Climb to Bubión (1,296 m), 2 km higher up - on foot via the old mule track (30 minutes) or by car. This is the quietest of the three villages, a member of Spain's "slow tourism" network. The Berber architecture is best preserved here: cubic houses with flat slate roofs, cylindrical chimneys topped with a stone cap. At the Museo Casa Alpujarreña, you'll see how the inhabitants lived in the past - kitchen, bedroom and workshop recreated in a traditional house. Don't miss the Artesa de los Moros, a large 2-meter stone whose original purpose remains mysterious.

Capileira (1,436 m) is the highest of the trio, at the gates of Sierra Nevada National Park. From the Mirador de Perchel, the view is vertiginous: to the north, the peak of Veleta (3,396 m); to the south, the rooftops of Bubión and Pampaneira cascading down the valley; and on clear days, the Mediterranean and even the Rif mountains of Morocco on the horizon. This is where the trails begin towards the highest peaks of the Iberian Peninsula, including Mulhacén (3,479 m). The water from the public fountains comes directly from the Sierra Nevada's snowmelt - it's ice-cold even in midsummer.

activities.text: If you have the time and energy, the circular hike linking the three villages via the old mule tracks is the best way to discover the valley. Allow 3 to 4 hours for the complete loop, through chestnut groves, walnut trees, terraced olive groves and irrigation channels inherited from the Moorish era. You might spot Iberian ibex on the slopes.

In the evening, dine in Capileira or Bubión on a "plato alpujarreño" - the local dish: potatoes, fried egg, blood sausage (morcilla), ham and peppers. It's hearty and perfect after a day in the mountains. Pair it with a local Alpujarras wine.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Granada: Alpujarras, Guadix and the Sierras
Al-Andalus: From the Mezquita to the Alpujarras in 10 Days

visits.text: You're leaving Andalusia for the Murcia region. Caravaca de la Cruz is the fifth holy city of Christianity, along with Jerusalem, Rome, Santiago de Compostela and Santo Toribio de Liébana. This privilege, granted by Pope John Paul II in 1998, allows a perpetual Jubilee to be celebrated every seven years. Everything revolves around the Vera Cruz, a reliquary containing fragments of Christ's cross. According to legend, in 1231, the Moorish king Abou Zeïd asked a captive priest to celebrate mass in the castle. Since a cross was missing, two angels brought one from heaven - and the Moorish king converted to Christianity.

Climb to the Castillo via the steep paved lane that runs through the medieval quarter. The fortress, of Moorish origin (10th-11th century), was enlarged in the 15th century by the Templars then taken over by the Order of Santiago. Fourteen towers guard the walls. Inside stands the Sanctuary of the Santísima y Vera Cruz, built from 1617 onwards - its baroque facade in red marble from Cehegín is spectacular. The interior museum displays the chasuble of the priest Chirinos at the moment of the legendary apparition, as well as paintings dedicated to the miracle. From the ramparts, the view embraces the entire town and the mountains of northwestern Murcia.

Descend into the center to see the El Salvador church, considered the pinnacle of Murcian Renaissance art. The Carmelite convent, founded by Saint John of the Cross in 1586, is a place of calm and contemplation. On calle Mayor and calle Rafael Tegeo, admire the manor houses with wrought-iron balconies. The Templete (or Bañadero) is a small hexagonal baroque building where, every May 3rd, the relic of the Vera Cruz is bathed in water - a ritual celebrated since 1384.

activities.text: Late in the day, go relax at the Fuentes del Marqués, a natural area just outside town: an abundant spring surrounded by poplars and plane trees, with shaded paths, caves carved by Arab settlers (once used for tanning hides) and the Torre de los Templarios. It's the locals' favorite walking spot.

If you visit between May 1st and 5th, you'll witness the fiestas of the Santísima y Vera Cruz, declared of International Tourist Interest. On May 2nd, the Caballos del Vino (Horses of Wine) gallop up the castle slope, sumptuously harnessed - a unique spectacle. The Moors and Christians reenactments in the following days are equally colorful.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Granada: Alpujarras, Guadix and the Sierras
Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca

visits.text: The fortress of Cardona dominates the Cardener valley at 589 meters, perched on a pinnacle no attacker ever took by force—hence its nickname the "unconquered fortress." Built in 886 by Wilfred the Hairy, Count of Barcelona, it held out during the War of Succession until September 18, 1714, capitulating after Barcelona fell on September 11—this Barcelona date became Catalonia's national Diada. The castle is now a luxury Parador, but the historic spaces can be visited (€5).

The collegiate church of Sant Vicenç, inside the fortress, is an absolute masterpiece of Catalan Lombard Romanesque—consecrated in 1040, it predates the churches of the Vall de Boí. Three naves, dome on squinches, intact crypt beneath the high altar. Exceptional acoustics: Jordi Savall has recorded most of his discography here over the past 30 years, and Orson Welles shot scenes from Falstaff here in 1965. Visit included in the castle ticket.

The Torre de la Minyona, a Romanesque keep 15 meters tall and 10 meters in diameter, takes its name from the legend of Adélaïs, the viscount's daughter locked here by her father in the 11th century to prevent her marrying a Moorish lord. Partially dismantled in 1794 by French troops to deprive the fortress of its key defensive piece. Now open at the top, 360° views over the saline valley below—the orange-red color of the salt is visible even from a distance.

The Gothic church of Sant Miquel, in the old town below, merits a quick detour for the contrast with the austere Romanesque of the castle. Built in the 13th-14th centuries, single nave, late Baroque altarpieces. The medieval center has been a cultural asset of national interest since 1992: arcaded houses on Plaça del Mercat, cobbled lanes leading to the old fortified gate. An hour's stroll suffices.

activities.text: Guided tour of the Salt Mountain Cultural Park—descent by vehicle to 86 meters underground in the Nieves mine, exploited until 1990. Cardona's mine is Spain's deepest, 1,310 meters at its lowest point, 300 km of galleries in total. Beneath your feet, the red salt diapir descends 2 km—visible at the surface for only 120 meters, it's the tip of a mineral iceberg. The illuminated galleries reveal the salt's folds like a Baroque fresco. €18, duration 90 minutes, booking essential.

Evening at the castle Parador—even if you're not staying, the courtyard bar serves Reus vermouth at €4, to sip on the ramparts at sunset. For dinner, restaurant La Volta in the village offers hearty Catalan cuisine around €30: simmered escudella, rabbit with Sant Antoni mustard. Cardona's violet salt, sold in village shops, makes a fine souvenir at €6 a jar.

days_plan.itin_title: Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour
Catalonia Wine Route - 7 Days from Penedès to Priorat

visits.text: The red clay of the ramparts burns in late-morning light. Carmona has perched on a promontory since the Bronze Age. Its two monumental gates—Seville to the west, Córdoba to the east—have Punic foundations reinforced by Rome, then modified by the Umayyads. Three civilizations stacked in the same stone.

The Alcázar del Rey Don Pedro, at the eastern tip of the promontory, is an Arab-origin fortress transformed by Pedro I of Castile in the 14th century. Now a luxury hotel, the mirador entrance is still free—the view from the battlements over the Sevillian plain is worth the stop alone.

activities.text: Two centuries before Christ, Carmona buried its dead outside the walls in underground chambers carved into bedrock, some fitted with niches for cinerary urns. The 1st-century Roman necropolis, which yielded the Tomb of the Elephant and the Tomb of Servilia, is one of the peninsula's best-preserved Roman burial complexes. Allow 90 minutes. Closed Mondays.

days_plan.itin_title: Al-Andalus: From the Mezquita to the Alpujarras in 10 Days

places.name: Carrion De Calatrava

visits.text: Calatrava la Vieja closes the loop: here, on the banks of the Guadiana, the Order of Calatrava was born in the 12th century before moving south. The Umayyad fortress, documented as early as 785, controlled the route between Córdoba and Toledo. The 750-meter moat and pentagonal towers are still visible.

The site forms part of the Alarcos-Calatrava Archaeological Park. Systematic excavations since 1984 have uncovered the medina, alcázar, suburbs, and Bronze Age remains. Guided tour included with admission, about 1.5 hours.

activities.text: In June and October, dramatized tours recreate the site's history with costumed actors. The rest of the year, silence reigns and the Meseta light carves the walls. The site is 13 km northeast of Ciudad Real.

days_plan.itin_title: Campo de Calatrava and Valle de Alcudia in 5 Days

map_keywords: Calatrava la Vieja Carrión de Calatrava

visits.text: The Roman theater of Carthago Nova, 7,000 seats, built between 5 and 1 BC under Augustus - and rediscovered in 1988 by pure chance during construction work. It had slept beneath the cathedral quarter for twenty centuries. Museum by Rafael Moneo, opened in 2008. Allow 1h30 for museum and theater. Closed Mondays.

Behind a plain wall in the city center, a Punic wall from 227 BC - the one Hasdrubal the Fair had built when he founded Qart Hadasht on this site. Scipio Africanus stormed it in 209 BC.
