The interpretation center lets you walk on the original stones. Only Carthaginian remains visible in Spain in the open air.

Roman stones surface everywhere in the Barrio del Foro Romano - beneath the alleys, in the house walls. The city has twenty-two centuries of layered history. Some colonnade sections are visible in the Molinos de Viento quarter. Free stroll, explanatory panels, 30 minutes.

activities.text: Walk along the naval harbor before catching your train or getting back on the road - the Cartagena naval base is one of Spain's three main bases, along with Rota and Ferrol. The moored submarines are visible from the public quay. Strange contrast with yesterday's beaches. Coffee in Paseo Alfonso XIII to digest three thousand years of history.

The Museo Naval de Cartagena, on the harbor, traces 2,000 years of maritime military presence in this natural harbor - one of the best protected in the western Mediterranean. Raised Roman wrecks, models of Punic galleys, history of the Peral submarines built here in 1888. Free entry. Open mornings Tuesday to Sunday.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Road Trip: Baroque Splendor & Costa Cálida
Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca
Wild Coast: Málaga to Cartagena in 9 Days

places.name: Castillo De Loarre

visits.text: The Castle of Loarre, built from 1020 by King Sancho III of Navarre on a rocky spur at 1,071 meters elevation, is the best-preserved Romanesque fortress in Europe. Its 172-meter perimeter wall, flanked by eleven towers, dominates the plain of the Hoya de Huesca. The site served as a filming location for "Kingdom of Heaven" and "The Name of the Rose."

Pass through the Romanesque portal adorned with a checkerboard pattern in the Jaca style, then climb the barrel-vaulted staircase. The Crypt of Santa Quiteria, to the left, served as a burial site. Two narrow staircases lead to the Church of San Pedro, a marvel of Romanesque art: its 26-meter-high dome crowns a semicircular apse with capitals carved with biblical and fantastical motifs.

The keep, 22 meters high and spanning five floors, was connected to the castle by a drawbridge. The Torre de la Reina, above the entrance to the military section, offers a vertiginous panorama over the Pyrenean foothills and agricultural plains below.

In 1071, King Sancho Ramírez transformed the fortress into an Augustinian monastery. The Church of Santa María, predating the monastery, preserves alabaster stained-glass windows whose white, milky light bathes the nave with an unexpected softness in this military setting.

From the parking lot, before entering the castle, take time to admire the fortress silhouette that seems to spring from the rock itself. The winding road leading up crosses a landscape of scrubland and limestone cliffs where griffon vultures nest.

activities.text: Walk the trail that circles the castle's outer ramparts, added in the 13th century. The path offers varied perspectives on the towers, walls, and cliffs that naturally protect the fortress's southern side.

The limestone cliffs around Loarre shelter a colony of griffon vultures. From the castle's surroundings, look up: these raptors with nearly 3-meter wingspans circle above the rock faces, carried by thermal currents.

The Collegiate Church of Santa María de Bolea, about twenty kilometers away, deserves a detour for its monumental 15th-century Hispano-Flemish altarpiece. This 16-meter-high altarpiece, with panels painted in extraordinary finesse, is considered one of Aragon's most important.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragon Circuit: Zaragoza & Mudéjar Villages

map_keywords: Iglesia de San Pedro Castillo de Loarre
Torre del Homenaje Castillo de Loarre

places.name: Castro Urdiales

visits.text: The Church of Santa María de la Asunción sits enthroned on the rocky promontory above the harbor. Built from the 13th to 15th centuries in rose-colored stone, it is the finest example of Cantabrian Gothic. Its flying buttresses and soaring naves rival French cathedrals.
The Castle of Santa Ana, a 12th-century fortress, houses a lighthouse installed in 1853 in its southeast tower. In the courtyard, a Roman milestone bears witness to the ancient road to Flaviobriga.
Cross the medieval bridge—locally nicknamed the 'Roman bridge'—to reach the hermitage of Santa Ana, perched on a rock battered by the waves. At high tide, the sea surges beneath the arches.
Plaza del Ayuntamiento is ringed by arcades and dominated by the town hall with its crenellated tower. Café terraces come alive from aperitif hour onward.
The lanes of the Puebla Vieja, declared a Historic-Artistic Ensemble in 1978, preserve their wooden-balconied houses and medieval facades.
The Palacio de Ocharan, with its Greek-inspired columned portico, illustrates 19th-century bourgeois splendor. Its gardens open onto the sea.

activities.text: Castro Urdiales is a working fishing port. Grilled sardines, preserved anchovies and bonito stew are the specialties to savor beneath the harbor arcades.
Walk along Avenida de la Constitución, parallel to the sea, then descend to Brazomar beach. The golden sand stretches at the harbor's foot, facing the cliffs.
The pintxo bars in the center show Basque Country influence from nearby. Order at the counter and pair with a chilled txakoli overlooking the harbor.

days_plan.itin_title: Cantabria Road Trip: Atlantic Bays and Picos de Europa

map_keywords: Iglesia Santa María de la Asunción Castro Urdiales
Castillo-Faro de Santa Ana Castro Urdiales
Ermita de Santa Ana Castro Urdiales

visits.text: A former flour mill converted into a Museo del Turrón: the building alone is worth the trip, with its original machinery. Four themed rooms trace the evolution of the turrón industry since the late 19th century. Opened in 2005, it's the only museum in Spain dedicated to turrón. Calle Huertos 46, free entry.

If you have time, push on to the Centro de Interpretación de la Naturaleza La Serena y Sierras Periféricas, in the same building as the Museo del Turrón. Models, panels, local fauna. Good complement before diving into the comarca.

Walk through the streets of Castuera's centro histórico: white façades, glimpsed patios, the atmosphere of a prosperous farming town. The Plaza de España concentrates bars and shops. Allow twenty minutes for a full circuit.

activities.text: Castuera turrón, the forgotten cousin of Jijona's, has been made here since the late 19th century with Marcona almonds and sierra honey. Four factories still operate, the largest exporter being Rey. Buy directly in town, it's half the price.

At the Centro de Interpretación de la Naturaleza, panels identify raptors visible in the comarca: griffon vultures, golden eagles, lesser kestrels. Useful before the stages at Cabeza del Buey and Puebla de Alcocer, two birdwatching hotspots.

days_plan.itin_title: La Siberia and La Serena: Secret Extremadura in 6 Days

visits.text: Explore the archaeological site of Cástulo, 5 kilometers from Linares, one of the most important Ibero-Roman settlements on the peninsula. This ancient city was the capital of Oretania and a strategic point in the wars between Rome and Carthage, with remains spanning from the Bronze Age to medieval times.

activities.text: Walk through the archaeological excavations where exceptional Roman mosaics, walls, necropolises, and public buildings have been discovered. The site continues to reveal treasures and offers a unique glimpse into life in ancient Hispania.

days_plan.itin_title: Jaén's Renaissance Jewels: Olive Grove Landscapes & Wild Sierras of Andalusia

visits.text: The road from Úbeda to Cazorla crosses a sea of olive trees stretching as far as the eye can see—the province of Jaén has over 66 million olive trees. Cazorla is a white village clinging to a hillside at the foot of the natural park. Plaza de Santa María, one of the two main squares, is built around the spectacular ruins of the Iglesia de Santa María—a 16th-century Renaissance church bombed during the Civil War and never rebuilt. Its open-air arches, with the mountain backdrop, create a unique theatrical setting.

The Castillo de la Yedra (Ivy Castle), of Arab origin then enlarged by the Christians, dominates Cazorla from its rocky spur. It houses the Museo de Artes y Costumbres Populares del Alto Guadalquivir—an ethnographic museum tracing rural life in the sierras. From the ramparts, the view plunges over Cazorla's rooftops and the first forests of the natural park. It's also an excellent spot to watch griffon vultures soaring above the gorges.

Two kilometres from Cazorla, the village of La Iruela has one of the most spectacular sites in the province: a castle perched on a rocky pinnacle so narrow it seems to defy gravity. The adjoining ruined church, clinging to the same rock, reinforces the vertiginous impression. The climb is short but steep—the view from the summit embraces the entire Cerezuelo valley and the distant sierras.

Full day in the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas Natural Park—at 210,000 hectares, it's Spain's largest protected area and Europe's second largest. Declared a Biosphere Reserve by UNESCO in 1983, it's home to over 2,170 plant species including 34 endemics (Cazorla violet, the world's smallest narcissus, a carnivorous plant). Wildlife includes ibex, deer, wild boar, mouflon, griffon vultures, golden eagles, and the rare bearded vulture.

The Río Borosa hike is the park's flagship trail—and one of Andalusia's most beautiful. From the Torre del Vinagre visitor centre, the path follows the Borosa, the Guadalquivir's first major tributary, for about 10 km one way. You first cross the Cerrada de Elías, a narrow gorge where the path is carved into the rock above the turquoise water—wooden walkways span the torrent. Higher up, the path passes through forests of centuries-old pines before reaching two mountain lakes, Laguna de Aguas Negras and Laguna de Valdeazores. Allow 6 to 7 hours return. Bring water, food, and good shoes—there's no refreshment along the trail.

activities.text: Cazorla is the ideal starting point for exploring the natural park, but it's also a town where you eat well. The restaurants around Plaza de la Constitución serve game (deer, wild boar), Segureño lamb, seasonal mushrooms, and of course Sierra de Cazorla PDO olive oil on everything. Rin-ran (cod, tomato, and pepper salad) is the local speciality. Dine early—mountain restaurants close earlier than in the city.

If you prefer a less physical option, operators offer 4x4 excursions through the park, with stops at viewpoints and wildlife observation points. The Tranco route follows the large dam and crosses pine forests with spectacular views. You can also take a boat trip on the Embalse del Tranco—the artificial lake at the heart of the park. It's from the lake that you have the best chances of seeing deer coming to drink at dusk.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
Jaén's Renaissance Jewels: Olive Grove Landscapes & Wild Sierras of Andalusia

visits.text: The Fuenfría Valley Interpretation Center unveils the natural and cultural history of the valley. Permanent exhibitions showcase flora, fauna, and human heritage. Try the "Treasure of La Tuerta" scavenger hunt to discover the park's riches.
The Calzada Romana (Antonine Way XXIV) once linked Toledo to Segovia. Restored in 2009, this ancient section reveals the original Roman route. Informative panels line the path, marked by metal posts.

The 18th-century Puente del Descalzo spans the Arroyo de la Fuenfría. Three granite arches with stone benches built into the pillars. Commissioned by Philip V to connect the Royal Palace of La Granja.

Dense forests of Scots pine (Pinus sylvestris)—the best preserved in the Central System—rise on all sides. Oak, cherry, maple and ash mix near the Arroyo de la Venta. Clearings open to vast panoramas.
Puerto de la Fuenfría at 1,796 meters marks the boundary between Madrid and Segovia. A crossroads of historic paths where Calzada Romana, Calzada Borbónica and modern roads converge.

activities.text: Follow the Calzada Romana up to Puerto de la Fuenfría. A gentle ascent through pine forests, 641 meters of elevation over 7.9 km. Track the green circles painted on trees to stay on course.
The Camino Schmidt, laid out in 1926 by mountaineer Eduardo Schmidt, traces the base of Siete Picos. Mature Scots pine forests alternate with meadows. Follow white circles marking the way.
Return via the scenic mountain train (Cercanías C-9). This historic line snakes through the Sierra with spectacular views of valleys and peaks. Departs from Cercedilla station.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Guadarrama Road Trip: Castles and Forests

visits.text: Castillo de Chinchilla, built by the Marquis of Villena in the 15th century on a hill that was Roman before being Islamic, served as a prison for three centuries. César Borgia was imprisoned there—in the highest tower, apparently. Only the exterior is freely accessible; the views over the Manchego plain from the ramparts justify the ascent. BIC status since the 19th century.

Two cannons mutilated by French troops in 1812 lie in front of the Baroque Ayuntamiento on Plaza Mayor de Chinchilla—it was the former provincial capital before Albacete snatched the title in 1833. The plaza hosts the Iglesia de Santa María del Salvador (14th-16th century), the Casino with glazed gallery, and the neoclassical Clock Tower. Free stroll.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: The road to Chulilla seems to lead nowhere—and that's exactly why you should go. The 400-inhabitant village perched on the Turia canyon is one of the region's most renowned climbing destinations, with routes opened on limestone cliffs over 200 meters high.

activities.text: The Turia canyon here is quite different from Valencia's: wild, steep-sided, populated by griffon vultures. The hanging bridges trail (pasarelas) connects two cliffs over a 3km easy walk. Start and finish from the Chulilla car park, total duration 2 hours with photo stops.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages

places.name: Ciudad Real

visits.text: The Cathedral of Santa María del Prado stands out for its impossibly long official title: Priory of the Four Military Orders, Basilica, and Cathedral all rolled into one. Its single nave soars to 34 meters, making it the second-tallest in Spain after Girona. Don't miss the altarpiece by Giraldo de Merlo, completed in 1616. Free admission.

Hunt down the Puerta de Toledo, the lone survivor of the medieval city walls. This 14th-century Mudéjar double arch marks the northern gateway to the old quarter. The city was founded in 1255 by Alfonso X the Wise under the name Villa Real.

The Museo del Quijote, right in the city center, offers an interactive journey through Cervantes' universe. Rooms recreate scenes from the novel using animatronics and projections. Free entry, closed Mondays.

activities.text: Sample pisto manchego in a downtown taberna: tomatoes, peppers, zucchini, and onions sautéed in olive oil, topped with a fried egg. It's the region's signature dish, and every cook guards their own version.

days_plan.itin_title: Campo de Calatrava and Valle de Alcudia in 5 Days

map_keywords: Santa María del Prado Cathedral Ciudad Real

visits.text: Santa Maria Cathedral, built between 1300 and 1365 in Catalan Gothic style, rises where a mosque once stood. The baroque altarpiece gleams, and you can climb the bell tower for rooftop views.
Plaça des Born assembles magnificent 17th-century aristocratic palaces around a central obelisk commemorating the town's brave resistance against Turkish invaders in 1558.
Ciutadella's harbor cuts inland like a dramatic fjord between limestone cliffs. Colorful facades and bustling terraces line the low quays where boats bob gently.
Palau Salort occasionally opens for guided tours, revealing aristocratic interiors, period furniture, and shaded patios that whisper of centuries past.
Museu Palacio Can Saura displays ethnographic and archaeological collections, including the haunting remains of an Andalusian necropolis discovered beneath the building.
Carrer Ses Voltes, lined with graceful arcades, concentrates shops and restaurants beneath medieval arches. These cobbled lanes form the atmospheric heart of the old town.

activities.text: The Sant Joan festival on June 23-24 erupts in spectacular tradition as sleek black Menorcan horses rear and dance through crowds in an ancient ritual unlike anything else.
Old town bakeries tempt with chocolate-filled ensaimadas and formatjades—traditional pastries that embody island flavors in every flaky, sweet bite.
Plaça des Born's terraces come alive at dusk. Sip drinks facing illuminated palaces and the town hall as the golden hour transforms the square into pure magic.

days_plan.itin_title: Menorca Road Trip: Harbors, Whitewashed Villages & Hidden Coves

visits.text: El Capricho de Gaudí, built between 1883 and 1885, is one of the architect's rare works outside Catalonia. Its watchtower, sunflower ceramics and columned portico draw on oriental art with Gaudí's unmistakable flair.
The neo-Gothic Sobrellano Palace was commissioned by the Marquis of Comillas. Beside it, the chapel-pantheon in matching style houses the family tombs.

Climb toward the Pontifical University. This imposing neo-Gothic edifice with Mudéjar influences crowns the hill of La Cardosa. Its red-orange brick stands out against the green of the surrounding hills.
Comillas cemetery, enclosed by medieval walls, is crowned by the Ángel Exterminador, a modernist sculpture keeping watch over the graves facing the sea.
Descend to Plaza del Corro de Campios, the old town's heart. Mansions built by 'Indianos'—émigrés who returned wealthy from the Americas—line the cobbled streets.
The small fishing harbor and sandy beach nestle at the village's foot. The ocean horizon opens onto the dunes of Oyambre Natural Park.

activities.text: Savor octopus, baby squid and shellfish at the harbor restaurants. Cantabrian cuisine celebrates raw ingredients, simply cooked.
Follow the coast toward Oyambre Natural Park. The trail crosses dunes, cliffs and marshes rich in birdlife, with views over wild beaches.
Head to Oyambre beach in the late afternoon. The dunes gilded by the setting sun offer one of the most beautiful panoramas on the Cantabrian coast.

days_plan.itin_title: Cantabria Road Trip: Atlantic Bays and Picos de Europa

visits.text: Twelve white silhouettes on the Cerro Calderico ridge: Consuegra's mills all bear names from the Quijote—Chispas, Mambrino, Caballero del Verde Gabán. Five retain their original mechanism. Molino Rucio still grinds wheat; Bolero houses the tourist office. Free access to the ridge; guided interior visits from €8.

Castillo de la Muela crowns the same ridge, right next to the mills—a medieval castle whose walls witnessed, according to local tradition, the death of El Cid's son during the battle of 1097. The Cid's son dying in a windmill castle: La Mancha's history loves the melodramatic. Guided tours offered by the town.

Head down to the casco histórico to discover Plaza Mayor, the archaeological museum, and Casa de la Tercia. A tip from the tourist office: drive up to the ridge via the south-slope road (less crowded), and walk back down via the senda—4 km round trip, view over the entire La Mancha plain.

activities.text: Head to Cerro Calderico late afternoon. The golden light illuminates the white mills against endless plains—the perfect moment to photograph this icon of La Mancha.
The Consuegra region is one of the main terroirs for Spanish saffron. Local cooperatives offer tastings of olive oil and Manchego saffron.
Climb on foot from the parking at the bottom of the hill (20 min). The gradual ascent reveals the mills and castle one by one, adding suspense to the panorama.

days_plan.itin_title: La Mancha Road Trip: Don Quixote's Windmills and Cuenca
The Don Quixote Route: A Road Trip Through Castilla-La Mancha's Windmills & Plains
Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: The Mezquita-Cathedral of Córdoba is, without exaggeration, one of the most extraordinary monuments on the planet. Begun in 785 by Abd al-Rahman I on the site of a Visigothic basilica, enlarged three times over two centuries, then transformed into a cathedral after the Reconquest of 1236, it layers civilizations within a single 23,400 m² space. Upon entering, you're seized by the forest of more than 850 columns of marble, jasper, and granite—some salvaged from Roman and Visigothic ruins—connected by red-and-white bicolor arches that unfold as far as the eye can see. Push on to Al-Hakam II's mihrab: its golden and blue mosaics, created by artisans sent by the Byzantine emperor, rank among the finest in Islamic art. Then discover the Renaissance cathedral planted in the middle of the mosque—Charles V, who had authorized it, reportedly regretted upon visiting the works: 'You have destroyed what was unique to build what can be seen everywhere.' Arrive at 8:30 AM for free entry (until 9:30 AM)—after that, the queue grows quickly.

Leaving the Mezquita, lose yourself in the Judería, the medieval Jewish quarter that's part of Córdoba's UNESCO listing. Its whitewashed lanes so narrow you can touch both walls with outstretched arms, its patios glimpsed through half-open doors, its pots of geraniums clinging to facades—this is the image of Córdoba you'll take home. Don't miss the Calleja de las Flores, a tiny dead-end alley that opens onto a perfect perspective of the Mezquita's bell tower framed by flowers. The synagogue (14th century), one of only three preserved in Spain along with those in Toledo, can be visited in 15 minutes—it's small, but the Mudéjar stuccowork is remarkable.

The Alcázar de los Reyes Cristianos, built in 1328 by Alfonso XI on the ruins of a Moorish palace, is especially famous for its terraced gardens—rectangular pools bordered by clipped hedges, cypresses, and orange trees that descend toward the Guadalquivir. This is where Ferdinand and Isabella planned the conquest of Granada and where Christopher Columbus presented his project for a voyage to the Indies in 1486. The interior is sober—vaulted halls, Roman mosaics, and Arab baths—but the gardens at sunset are worth the visit.

Cross the Puente Romano, Córdoba's Roman bridge—331 meters, 16 arches, built in the 1st century BC under Augustus, rebuilt several times since. In the middle, pause before the statue of the Archangel Raphael, patron of the city. On the other side, the Torre de la Calahorra (12th century), a former Arab fortified gate, houses a small museum on the Córdoba of three cultures (Muslim, Jewish, Christian). But above all, turn around: the view of the Mezquita and the historic center from the bridge, especially in late afternoon, is the finest in Córdoba.

Devote the morning to Medina Azahara (Madinat al-Zahra), the caliphal city that's been a UNESCO site since 2018. Eight kilometers west of Córdoba, at the foot of the Sierra Morena, Abd al-Rahman III ordered in 936 the construction of an entire palatine city to serve as the seat of the Caliphate of Córdoba. Over 75 years, 10,000 workers built a city of 112 hectares on three terraces—royal palaces at the top, gardens and administration in the middle, mosque and dwellings at the bottom. Then, in 1010, the civil war that ended the caliphate reduced it to ashes. It remained buried for a millennium, until its rediscovery in 1910. Today, only 10% has been excavated, but the Salón Rico (official reception hall), with its arches and carved stone panels of insane delicacy, conveys the measure of what this 'Andalusian Versailles' once was. The museum at the base of the site is excellent—start there to understand what you're about to see. Allow at least 2.5 hours.

Back in the city in the afternoon, visit the Palacio de Viana, nicknamed the 'Museum of Patios.' This aristocratic palace built between the 14th and 18th centuries has 12 patios and a garden—each with its own style, fountains, columns, and vegetation. It's the best way to understand Córdoba's patio culture, inscribed on UNESCO's intangible heritage list since 2012 (Festival of the Patios, in May). If you're there in May, the private patios of the San Basilio and Alcázar Viejo neighborhoods open to the public—it's a spectacle of flowers, colors, and popular pride.

Push on to the Roman Temple on Calle Claudio Marcelo—the eleven Corinthian columns of this 1st-century temple, dedicated to imperial cult, recall that Córdoba was the capital of Roman Baetica and the birthplace of Seneca and Lucan. Just beside it, the remains of the Roman theatre (the largest in Spain in its time) are visible in the cellars of the Archaeological Museum.

Return to Córdoba for the final day, devoted to the less touristy neighborhoods. Start with the Plaza del Potro, mentioned by Cervantes in Don Quixote—the inn bordering it is where Sancho Panza supposedly lodged. The square houses the Museo de Bellas Artes (in the former Hospital de la Caridad, 15th century) and the Museo Julio Romero de Torres, dedicated to the most popular Córdoban painter of the early 20th century, famous for his portraits of Andalusian women with intense gazes.

Head up toward the San Lorenzo neighborhood, one of the most authentic in Córdoba. The church of San Lorenzo (13th century), with its Gothic rose window and arcaded portico, is one of the 'Fernandine churches' built after the Reconquest of 1236—Ferdinand III had 14 parishes constructed in the reconquered city. The neighborhood is calm, residential, with flowery patios you'll only glimpse by craning your neck through half-open doors—this is the Córdoba of Córdobans, far from the tourist groups of the Judería.

Finish with the Palacio de la Merced (18th century), an imposing Baroque building that now serves as the seat of the Provincial Council. Its two-level cloister, with pink marble columns and azulejos, is one of the finest Baroque spaces in Córdoba. Entry is free and the place is rarely mentioned in guidebooks.

activities.text: In the evening, settle into the taverns of the San Basilio neighborhood or the Plaza de la Corredera for a tapas crawl. Córdoba is a city of generous tapas: salmorejo (cold tomato soup thicker than gazpacho, served with ham and hard-boiled egg), flamenquines (breaded ham and pork roll), berenjenas con miel (fried aubergines with honey), and a glass of Montilla-Moriles, the local fortified wine that resembles sherry but with its own character. The Plaza de la Corredera, the only enclosed square plaza in Andalusia (17th century), is perfect for starting.

If you're in Córdoba between May and June, don't miss the flamenco shows in the peñas flamencas of the Santiago and San Lorenzo neighborhoods—more intimate and authentic venues than the tourist tablaos. Córdoba's flamenco guitar tradition is distinct, inherited from Paco Peña and Vicente Amigo.

For your final evening, treat yourself to dinner in the Plaza de las Tendillas or San Miguel neighborhood, where the tapas bars are frequented by locals rather than tourists. Order a salmorejo (the Córdoban version is the most authentic in Andalusia), rabo de toro (braised oxtail, Córdoba's emblematic dish), and a glass of Montilla-Moriles amontillado. From Córdoba, you can take the AVE back to Madrid, Seville, or Málaga, or continue by car toward Jaén (our 'Madrid to Cazorla' itinerary) or toward Granada.

days_plan.itin_title: Andalusia Road Trip: Seville, Córdoba, Granada & Ronda
Córdoba and the Sierra Morena: From the Caliphate to the Dehesas of Los Pedroches
Córdoba and the Sierra Morena: From the Caliphate to the Dehesas of Los Pedroches
Córdoba and the Sierra Morena: From the Caliphate to the Dehesas of Los Pedroches
Dark Andalusia: 10 Days from Seville to Málaga
Al-Andalus: From the Mezquita to the Alpujarras in 10 Days
Al-Andalus: From the Mezquita to the Alpujarras in 10 Days

places.name: Coria

visits.text: Start the day at the Monastery of Yuste, a European Heritage site nestled in the Sierra de Gredos foothills near Cuacos de Yuste. This is where Charles V—Charles I of Spain and V of the Holy Roman Empire, the most powerful man in the world—chose to retire after abdicating in 1556 in favor of his son Philip II. He arrived on February 3, 1557, with an entourage of about sixty people. His chamber was positioned against the church choir so he could attend mass from his bed—he suffered from terrible gout that confined him to his litter. He died here on September 21, 1558, at age 58, from malarial fevers. The visit shows both cloisters (one Gothic, one Renaissance), the 15th-century church, and the emperor's apartments—surprisingly austere for the man who'd ruled an empire where the sun never set.

Head to Coria in the afternoon (50 km, 45 minutes). This town is one of Spain's oldest—the ancient Caurium of the Vettones, which became a Roman municipium. Its Roman walls, built between the 3rd and 4th centuries, are probably the best-preserved as a whole set in all of Europe: over 1,100 meters of ramparts with about twenty square towers and four gates, two of them original Roman (Puerta del Sol and Puerta de la Guía). The walls are 8 meters thick in places. Walk the circuit—it's about a 30-minute stroll that gives you vertigo when you think these stones have been standing for 1,700 years.

Coria's cathedral, built between the 15th and 18th centuries on the remains of a Visigothic church, blends Gothic, Plateresque, and Baroque styles. Its Plateresque facade is remarkable, as is its Churrigueresque tower offering superb views over town and countryside. The cathedral guards an extraordinary relic: what's claimed to be the tablecloth from Christ's Last Supper, attested in a papal bull from 1404. Admission is free and opening hours are generous (closed Monday mornings).

Don't miss the bridge without a river—Coria's most surreal image. This five-arched stone bridge, built in the 16th century probably on earlier Roman foundations, spanned the Río Alagón. But the river changed course—likely gradually following floods, worsened by the 1755 Lisbon earthquake—and now flows several hundred meters away. Result: a bridge without a river and a river without a bridge. Walk under the arches for the best photo, especially late afternoon.

If daylight permits, detour 30 minutes to Granadilla, a fascinating ghost village. This fortified villa of Almohad origin (9th century), which governed 15 municipalities under its jurisdiction in the Middle Ages, was emptied of inhabitants in 1955 when Franco's regime decided to build the Gabriel y Galán dam. Cruel irony: the water never reached the village. Declared a Conjunto Histórico-Artístico in 1980, it was partially restored through an educational program. You can visit the cobbled lanes, the circular wall (one of Spain's best-preserved alongside Lugo and Ávila), and the 15th-century castle built by the Duke of Alba, whose terrace offers striking views over the reservoir and hills.

activities.text: In the evening in Coria, try dulces de las monjas—cloistered convent sweets. The town's convents sell handmade pastries (quemados, dulces de yema) through a rotating hatch, without seeing the nuns who make them. It's a typically Spanish tradition that persists in Extremadura's small towns.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas

map_keywords: Monasterio de Yuste Cuacos de Yuste

visits.text: The Corralejo Dunes Natural Park stretches across 2,600 hectares of pristine wilderness. These white dunes, formed from disintegrated seashells and marine organisms, create a Saharan landscape that contrasts dramatically with the dark volcanic rock.

Climb Montaña Roja, a 314-meter volcano at the park's southern edge. The summit rewards you with 360-degree panoramas over the dunes, the turquoise Atlantic, and the volcanic silhouette of Lobos Island.

The Grandes Playas stretch for 10 kilometers of pure beachfront bliss. Immaculate white sand, turquoise waters, and unobstructed views toward Lobos and Lanzarote. Stone wind shelters called corralitos dot the beach, offering refuge from the Atlantic breezes.
Flag Beach and El Médano attract surfers and kitesurfers from around the world. The constant Atlantic winds create ideal conditions for riding the waves and catching air.

activities.text: Take the ferry to Lobos Island, a 6-square-kilometer protected nature reserve. Once home to a colony of monk seals, the island now shelters the Punta Martiño lighthouse and preserved volcanic landscapes where time stands still.
Swim in the shallow lagoons sheltered by natural reefs. Watch for strong currents in certain areas—always check the flag warnings before entering the water.
The Kite Festival in November draws participants from across the globe. Multicolored kites dance across the natural park's skies throughout an entire weekend of celebration.

days_plan.itin_title: Fuerteventura Road Trip: Dunes, Lagoons & Timeless Villages

visits.text: Jameos del Agua is César Manrique's first major work in Lanzarote, opened to the public in 1966. The site sits inside a lava tunnel formed by the Corona volcano's eruption about 4,000 years ago—a 6 km tube, 1.5 km of which continues under the ocean (the Atlantis Tunnel, the world's longest underwater cave). You descend through Jameo Chico to an underground saltwater lake, fed by the Atlantic, where blind albino crabs (Munidopsis polymorpha) live—1 cm long and endemic to this place alone. Don't throw coins in the water—rust kills them. The visit continues to Jameo Grande, an open-air cave with a turquoise pool and an auditorium seating 600. Book your ticket online to avoid the line.

Cueva de los Verdes lies minutes from Jameos—it's the same Corona volcanic tube, but here the roof remained intact for nearly a kilometer. The guided tour (mandatory) takes you through geological formations lit by a system designed by Manrique that enhances colors without altering the rock. The cave was used in the 17th century as a refuge by locals fleeing pirates. The visit also hides a visual surprise: prepare for a memorable optical illusion that guides never reveal in advance.

Jardín de Cactus in Guatiza is César Manrique's final major work, inaugurated in 1990—he built it in a former abandoned quarry as a vegetable amphitheater. The garden houses about 4,500 cactus specimens from 500 species originating from five continents, planted on black volcanic stone terraces. The area was historically dedicated to prickly pear cultivation for cochineal dye. At the entrance, an 8-meter cactus sculpture signed by Manrique welcomes you. Allow 45 minutes for the visit.

activities.text: Detour to the César Manrique Foundation in Tahíche, 20 minutes from Costa Teguise. The artist built his house directly on the 1730 lava flows, integrating five natural volcanic bubbles into the architecture. Since his death in 1992, it's been a museum exhibiting his works and those of international contemporaries. This is where you best understand his philosophy: art and nature must coexist without one crushing the other.

Volcanic malvasía tasting at one of La Geria's vineyards, en route to Costa Teguise. This protected designation of origin produces white wine from grape varieties planted in pits dug into volcanic lapilli, surrounded by semicircular stone walls (zocos) that protect from wind. The landscape is so singular that a photo of La Geria was exhibited in 1964 at New York's MoMA in the "Architecture Without Architects" exhibition.

Late afternoon swim at one of Costa Teguise's beaches, Lanzarote's most family-friendly northern resort. Or head up to Haría and the Valley of a Thousand Palms—a green oasis in the northern hills, accessible in 20 minutes from Costa Teguise.

days_plan.itin_title: Lanzarote & La Palma: Where Fire Shaped Art and Nature

places.name: Covarrubias

visits.text: The Collegiate Church of San Cosme y San Damián, a 15th-century Gothic gem, houses the tombs of Count Fernán González and his wife in 4th-century Hispano-Roman sarcophagi. The 16th-century cloister preserves the sepulcher of Princess Kristina of Norway, who journeyed from Scandinavia in 1257 to marry a Castilian prince.

Step inside the Collegiate Treasury Museum to admire the Adoration of the Magi triptych, a polychrome wooden masterpiece attributed to Gil de Siloé. Also on display: a Virgin painting attributed to Van Eyck, a canvas by Pedro Berruguete, and the 1620 organ—the oldest still functioning in Castile.

The Torreón de Fernán González, a 10th-century Mozarabic defensive tower, rises at the end of half-timbered streets. Its stone walls are the oldest remnants of the fortified enclosure that once protected the capital of the first County of Castile.

Enter the old town through the arch of the Archives of the Adelantamiento de Castilla, a 16th-century Renaissance building adorned with Philip II's coat of arms. In front of the arch stands a Gothic stone calvary.

Wander around Plaza de Doña Urraca, bordered by the town hall housed in the former palace of Fernán González. The cobbled streets around the square reveal half-timbered houses built with juniper wood on stilts, like the Casa del Obispo Peña with its carved corbels and the house of Doña Sancha with wooden balconies.

The Church of Santo Tomás, dating from the 15th century, preserves a Renaissance pulpit and beautiful altarpieces. Less visited than the collegiate church, it's worth a look for its peaceful atmosphere and quality woodwork.

activities.text: Walk down to the banks of the Arlanza for a riverside stroll. The path follows the remains of the old walls and offers a sweeping view of the village and its tiled roofs.
The Arlanza Valley produces wines under the AO Arlanza designation. Pair them with local sheep's cheese and valley cherries, a village specialty for centuries.

Late in the day, the half-timbered facades take on golden hues in the setting sun. Plaza de Doña Sancha and the triangular streets form the perfect setting to capture the village's intact medieval atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León Road Trip: Cathedrals and Medieval Villages

map_keywords: Collégiale San Cosme y San Damián Covarrubias
Archivo del Adelantamiento de Castilla Covarrubias

visits.text: First shock on arrival: the houses cling to the void. The Casas Colgadas date from the 14th-15th centuries, their wooden balconies projecting over a hundred-meter gorge with no apparent support. Originally there were dozens running along the cliff; only three remain. Two have housed the Spanish Abstract Art Museum since 1966—a magnificent juxtaposition of late Gothic and works by Tàpies or Saura. The best vantage point isn't from the houses but from the iron bridge of San Pablo opposite: a slightly vertiginous footbridge, avoid if you're prone to vertigo.

Cuenca's cathedral is more discreet but surprising: begun in the late 12th century after the reconquest, it's one of Spain's rare examples of Anglo-Norman Gothic, with a neo-Gothic façade rebuilt in 1902 after the tower collapsed. Note the modern stained glass by Henri Dechanet, Bonifacio Alfonso and Gustavo Torner, installed in the 1990s: a quite successful dialogue with the medieval stone. Combined ticket for cathedral, treasury and Tribuna del Pontifical, around €6.

Climb to the Castillo at the northern tip of the old town. Almost nothing remains of the Arab fortifications, but the entrance arch and especially the viewpoint beyond give the most complete view of the upper city's layout, with the two V-shaped gorges on either side. Well windswept, allow twenty minutes to climb without rushing. Descend via Calle San Pedro and its old ochre-façaded houses—one of the city's most beautiful stepped streets.

activities.text: If you're here during Holy Week, don't miss Las Turbas on Good Friday at dawn: hundreds of hooded figures parade drumming and blowing trumpets, their lamentations echoing through the canyon from 5:30am. The tradition dates back at least to the 17th century. Outside this period, the Hoz del Huécar can be walked, a marked trail of about 4 km from the bridge below, return by the stairs—good idea just before sunset for the colors on the cliff.

Dinner at Figón del Huécar, former home of singer Plácido Domingo transformed into a restaurant: terrace hanging over the gorge, well-executed Castilian cuisine (pickled partridge, morteruelo, ajoarriero—a local cod pâté). Try the resolí, local coffee and herb liqueur, as a digestif. Reservations recommended on weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: La Mancha Road Trip: Don Quixote's Windmills and Cuenca
The Don Quixote Route: A Road Trip Through Castilla-La Mancha's Windmills & Plains
Mystical Castile: Toledo, Ávila, Salamanca & Burgos in 11 Days
Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca
Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: Tiny and nearly forgotten, Culla nonetheless appears in Spain's national roster of most beautiful villages. Fewer than 50 permanent residents, limestone-white lanes, and the ruins of a 12th-century Templar castle. The visit takes 90 minutes—take your time finding the house with red geraniums, which is the bakery.

If the sky is clear, the panorama from the old watchtower rewards the 10-minute climb. On fine days you can make out the Columbretes Islands to the east—a spectacle that locals reserve for curious visitors who ask the way. The tower isn't signposted: it's just behind the church.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages

places.name: Daimiel

visits.text: Small (just 3,030 hectares), Tablas de Daimiel National Park is the last representative of Spain's 'fluvial table' wetlands—two rivers with different salinities (the Guadiana and the Cigüela) meet here, creating a unique ecosystem. The wooden walkways of the Ruta de la Isla del Pan (2 km circular) let you advance into the heart of the reeds without damaging the ground. Free entry.

Classified a National Park in 1973, then a Ramsar site in 1982, Tablas de Daimiel shelters purple heron, common crane, and white-headed duck. The Ruta de la Laguna Permanente (1.5 km) offers water and ducks in all seasons. Start at the Molino de Molemocho Visitor Centre for free context.

activities.text: If you arrive at dawn, the aquatic warbler sings in the carrizos and cranes take off above the water mirror—the visual spectacle is well worth the rough wake-up. Sunset is equally effective. Avoid summer afternoons: heat, midges, birds gone to shade. Binoculars recommended.

days_plan.itin_title: Campo de Calatrava and Valle de Alcudia in 5 Days
Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

map_keywords: Las Tablas de Daimiel National Park

places.name: Daroca

visits.text: Final stop: Daroca, 1h drive southeast from Calatayud through the Jiloca valley. This town of 2,000 inhabitants possesses one of Spain's longest fortified enclosures: 4 km of ramparts bristling with 114 towers, built from the 9th century by the Arabs and enlarged after the Christian conquest by Alfonso I the Battler in 1120. Enter through the Puerta Baja (14th century), an impressive gate-tunnel that also served as a river drain—note the Fuente de los Veinte Caños (Fountain of Twenty Spouts) just outside. Climb up Calle Mayor, the town's backbone, between Mudéjar brick churches (San Miguel, San Juan, Santo Domingo) and noble palaces.

The Basilica of Santa María de los Sagrados Corporales, at the town's heart, is one of Aragon's most remarkable. It houses the relics of a Eucharistic miracle that occurred in 1239 during the Battle of Chío, when six consecrated hosts supposedly survived intact wrapped in the corporals despite the heat of battle. The main altarpiece, the Chapel of the Corporals, and the adjacent museum merit careful attention. Exit through the Puerta Alta (15th century), adorned with Charles V's coat of arms.

activities.text: Climb up to the Castillo Mayor via the path that follows the ramparts. Allow 1h30 round-trip. The wall-walk lets you link several towers and offers plunging views over the town squeezed between two ridges and the Jiloca valley. From the castle, the panorama embraces cereal fields, red clay hills and, on clear days, the sierras of the Iberian System. Bring good shoes and water—no shade on the ramparts.

From Daroca, you can continue towards Teruel (1h30 south) to join the 'Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragon' itinerary (UNESCO Mudéjar towers), or head back up to Zaragoza (1h15 north). Daroca is also a starting point for the village of Anento (15 km), member of Spain's Most Beautiful Villages, with its waterfall and hanging gardens.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel
Alcarria and Guadalajara: Between Madrid and Aragon

map_keywords: Fuente de los Veinte Caños Daroca

places.name: Deltebre

visits.text: The Ebro Delta, 320 km² of rice paddies, lagoons, and beaches, is the second-largest delta in the western Mediterranean after the Rhône. A natural park since 1983, it shelters over 300 bird species including 30% of Spain's flamingos - approximately 4,000 individuals wintering. First shock upon arrival: no relief, not a tree, just water and rice paddies stretching endlessly, crossed by irrigation canals inherited from the Moors. The feeling of being at world's edge. Best approach: explore by bike.

The Ecomuseu del Delta in Deltebre is the essential stop for understanding - 600 m² of museography on delta formation (created in 1,500 years by Ebro sediments, but receding since upstream dams retain 99% of silt), rice cultivation (planted April, harvested September), eel fishing. Small aquarium of local species. Allow 1 hour, €4. Combined with traditional boat ride (perxana) at €6 - last used by rice farmers before motorization.

The Far del Fangar, at the tip of the delta's northern peninsula, is an 1864 lighthouse (24 meters high) reached via 6 km of sand track. White mobile dunes reaching 8 meters, nesting birds (plovers, terns), and giant shells at the shore. Protected site, cars forbidden in high season - allow 1.5 hours walking round trip or take the electric shuttle at €4. Views over Fangar Bay (oyster and mussel farming), to taste afterward at Deltebre's mussel bars. Strange, flat, sublime.

activities.text: Rise before 7am: that's when flamingos arrive by the hundreds to the Encanyissada and Tancada lagoons. Observation from free viewing platforms, binoculars useful. Mosquéro to avoid (ferocious insects). The complete delta bike loop covers 50 km flat - rental at Bicicletes del Delta (Deltebre) for €15 per day. Paved paths along canals, rare shade, plan for 3 liters of water per person in July-August. Rice harvest happens in September, golden landscape, ideal for photos.

For lunch, arròs negre - black rice with squid ink - or arròs a banda (rice in fish broth, served separately) are delta classics. Restaurant Lo Mas de la Cuixota in Deltebre and Casa Nuri in Riumar rank among references, expect €25-30 for the menu. Delta rice (Bahia or Bomba variety, PDO Arròs del Delta) holds perfectly during cooking - inimitable texture. Request a bottle of Terra Alta, an overlooked neighboring PDO.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Catalonia in 10 Days: Tarragona, Priorat & the Ebro Delta
Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour

map_keywords: Trabucador Beach Sant Carles de la Ràpita

visits.text: A UNESCO Creative City of Gastronomy since 2015, Denia earned its stripes through its celebrated red prawn—the gamba roja—and its rice dishes rooted in Arab tradition. The 11th-century Almohad castle watches over the sea from its hilltop: built by successive occupiers, its walls blend Moorish architecture with Renaissance additions. Climb early in the morning when the raking light over the bay is at its finest. Paid entry, audioguide available.

The salt air, the brightly painted wooden hulls, the tightly packed fishermen's houses: the Baix la Mar quarter captures everything Denia has managed to preserve despite the tourism. Squeezed between the castle and the twin marinas, this little maze takes thirty minutes to walk through but deserves longer. Finish with a coffee on the Paseo de Cervantes watching the ferries depart for Ibiza—the closest mainland port to the Balearics.

activities.text: Touristy without being overwhelming, Montgó (753 m) towers over Denia and Jávea in a way that makes you want to pack away the beach towels. Six marked trails leave from the town center, ranging from 1h40 to 6h of hiking. In summer, set out before 8 AM without fail and carry at least two liters of water—the park rangers enforce this strictly.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

visits.text: Don Benito doesn't appear in any mainstream guidebook, and that's all for the better. The Plaza de España is vast, lined with terrace cafés and dominated by the Iglesia de Santiago Apóstol, a 16th-century Gothic-Renaissance block with a baroque-Herrerian façade. It's the natural starting point for entering La Serena.

The Iglesia de Santiago Apóstol, dating from the 16th century, blends late Gothic interiors with baroque-Herrerian exteriors. Large, austere, tourist-free. Remodelled in 1965, it lost some charm but kept its imposing bulk. Free entry, open in the morning.

The Museo Etnográfico smells of waxed wood and raw wool. Opened in 2000 thanks to donations from locals, it gathers 19th- and 20th-century objects: farming tools, costumes, kitchen utensils. Small but sincere. Calle Pedro Granada, behind the Palacio Municipal.
Make a detour to the Museo del Automóvil Clásico, near the station. Over 200 vintage cars, from the 1900s to the 1970s. Unexpected in an Extremaduran town, but well maintained. Allow 30 minutes.

activities.text: Start at the Plaza de España late morning: the bars serve café con leche and the tapa of the day for next to nothing. The monument to water and earth at the centre sums up the town's agricultural identity. Local atmosphere guaranteed.
Locals call La Velá their most popular festival, in September. If you're visiting off-season, the Ruta del Aperitivo offers a bar crawl with local tapas. Ask at the tourist office, Plaza de España.

days_plan.itin_title: La Siberia and La Serena: Secret Extremadura in 6 Days

places.name: Donana

visits.text: Doñana National Park, created in 1969, spans 54,000 hectares of marshlands, mobile dunes, pine forests, and Mediterranean scrub at the Guadalquivir estuary. Designated a Biosphere Reserve and UNESCO World Heritage site, it protects Western Europe's largest wetland and over 360 bird species.

Four-wheel-drive circuits cover 70 kilometers through the park's four ecosystems in a 4-hour journey: beach and mobile dunes reaching 30 meters high, flooded marshlands, wooded reserves, and Guadalquivir riverbanks. Guides spot flamingos, imperial eagles, red deer, fallow deer, and marsh horses in their natural habitat.

The village of El Rocío, with its unpaved streets and low whitewashed houses, seems suspended in another era. The Ermita del Rocío, a Marian sanctuary, draws over a million pilgrims each spring during the Romería—one of Spain's largest religious pilgrimages.

El Acebuche Visitor Center, the park's main gateway, offers marked hiking trails and bird observatories. The more remote José Antonio Valverde observatory, accessible by 4x4, features a glass wall overlooking the marshlands where flamingo colonies can be observed at water level.

Palacio del Acebrón, near the Arroyo de la Rocina, houses an exhibition on the park's ecosystems. A circular trail through gallery forest allows you to interpret animal tracks and discover plant species endemic to Doñana's unique environment.

activities.text: Rise before dawn for a birdwatching session. Doñana sits at a migratory crossroads between Europe, Africa, and Asia: over 500,000 waterbirds winter here annually. Flamingos, spoonbills, storks, glossy ibises, and gray herons create a perpetual spectacle of wings, color, and sound.

With patience and considerable luck, you might glimpse the Iberian lynx, the world's most endangered feline. Around twenty individuals live in or near the park. Wooded areas crossed late in the day during the 4x4 circuit offer the best chances.

On June 26, for over five centuries, Almonte ranchers have organized the Saca de las Yeguas: hundreds of mares and foals cross the park in a spectacular transhumance. Outside this date, horseback rides in the park's peripheral zones offer immersion in the dune and forest landscapes.

days_plan.itin_title: Cádiz Road Trip: Jerez, Sherry Bodegas & White Villages
Huelva, the Forgotten Land: Doñana to Faro in 7 Days

map_keywords: Centro de Visitantes El Acebuche Doñana

visits.text: Two kinds of goat, one denomination: El Bosque's payoyo cheese is the pride of the sierra gaditana. The small local Museo del Queso explains production in 20 minutes. The village itself is modest, but its position on the Majaceite river makes it the greenest gateway to the Grazalema park.

The El Castillejo botanical garden, on the village edge, brings together the endemic species of the natural park in a few hundred meters - potted pinsapos, cistus, local medicinal plants. Small but educational, and free. A good introduction before entering the sierra.

activities.text: The green water of the Majaceite flows between reeds as tall as a person - the trail following the river from El Bosque to Benamahoma (5 km, about 2 hours round trip) is the most popular in Grazalema park, and for good reason: almost flat, shaded year-round, with trout visible in the pools. Swimming forbidden, but the cool air is enough.

days_plan.itin_title: White Villages: Arcos, Grazalema, Ronda - 6 Days

places.name: El Cotillo

visits.text: El Cotillo's lagoons (Playa de los Lagos) stretch from the village to the lighthouse. These natural swimming pools, protected by coral reefs, shimmer with immaculate white sand and crystalline waters that glow emerald in the sunlight.

The Torre del Tostón, an 18th-century circular fortress, once defended the port from pirate raids. Thick local stone walls, a drawbridge, and quarters designed for 12 soldiers tell tales of maritime vigilance.

The Faro del Tostón lighthouse complex includes three structures built in 1897, 1955, and 1986. The site houses the Museum of Traditional Fishing, chronicling the village's timeless bond with the sea.
Playa de La Concha curves like a crescent moon of white sand. Shallow emerald waters make it perfect for families. Despite its proximity to the village, the beach retains a wild, untouched character.
Explore the old fishing harbor with its colorful boats bobbing in the water. Fishermen continue their ancestral trade, delivering fresh catch daily to the village's seafood restaurants.

activities.text: Feast on grilled fish and seafood at the harbor restaurants. Fresh squid, vieja parrotfish, and traditional fish stew are the house specialties, caught just hours before reaching your plate.
The surf spots south of the village draw surfers, windsurfers, and kitesurfers seeking Atlantic power. Consistent waves and steady winds create world-class conditions.
Watch the sunset from the old harbor or lighthouse. El Cotillo faces due west, offering spectacular views as the sun sinks into the Atlantic horizon in a blaze of orange and gold.

days_plan.itin_title: Fuerteventura Road Trip: Dunes, Lagoons & Timeless Villages

map_keywords: Playa de La Concha El Cotillo
Playa de los Charcos El Cotillo

visits.text: The fishermen of El Palmar served this rice dish with duck and eels long before Valencia claimed it. The Albufera is a coastal lagoon of around 2,800 hectares separated from the sea by a strip of dunes—the ecological birthplace of Valencian rice cultivation. Arrive at sunset to watch the lake turn red-orange.

activities.text: If you rent a barco, skirt the reed beds to the north: ducks and grey herons are denser there and local guides avoid that corner. Boat trips from El Palmar or El Saler, duration 1 hour, guide not compulsory but useful for identifying birds.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages

visits.text: Transition stop in the heart of the Catalan Pyrenees. El Pont de Suert (841 m altitude) is the capital of Alta Ribagorça, midway between Lleida and Vall de Boí. Mentioned as early as 1016, the town developed at a crossroads between valleys—hence its role as a historic market. Plaça Major and Plaça del Mercadal form the medieval center, and Església Vella (Romanesque origin, modified in the 18th) warrants a quick detour. Not touristy, sincere yes: we sleep here because it's on the route, not for anything else.

Small curiosity 3 km south, on the road to La Pobla: the ruins of Lavaix monastery on the edge of the Escales reservoir. Founded in the 11th century, abandoned in the 19th, it was partially submerged by dam waters from 1950—when the level drops, the vestiges reappear. Photos guaranteed if you hit the right season.

activities.text: Dinner at Hotel Cotori or Lavaix, two village institutions. No-fuss mountain cuisine: wild boar stew, river trout, Alta Ribagorça cheese. Good bill for €25-30. Typical Pyrenean family welcome, and after a day on the road it feels good.

days_plan.itin_title: Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour

visits.text: A ghost town 51 weeks a year, a city of hundreds of thousands of pilgrims during Pentecost week. The streets are sand—not from neglect, but because horses still circulate here. In front of every verandah house: rings to tether mounts. The Ermita de Nuestra Señora del Rocío, facing the lagoon, is open year-round.

Flamingos landing on the lagoon adjacent to the village, just meters from the first restaurants—that's what makes El Rocío singular. These are the Doñana marshes beginning here. Walk the shoreline early in the morning, before the day-trippers arrive.

activities.text: Walk or ride the sandy streets on horseback: rentals available from the ranchos on the village edge. The ride to the lagoon viewpoint takes 30 minutes. Bring water. In intense heat, flamingos move deeper into the park—morning remains the best window.

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva, the Forgotten Land: Doñana to Faro in 7 Days

visits.text: The UNESCO-listed palm grove, designated in 2000, unfolds its fronds in the heart of the city—a landscape laid out in the late 10th century during construction of the Islamic settlement. More than 200,000 palms across 144 hectares, half of them date palms. It's the largest palm grove in Europe, and the irrigation practice inherited from al-Andalus—the tandeo system—remains active today.

activities.text: Gardeners call it the tandeo, this water distribution system inherited from the Arab period: each orchard receives water in turn, according to a thousand-year-old calendar. The Municipal Park, at the heart of the palm grove, offers guided tours (inquire at the tourist office). The Alcudia museum and its Iberian collections round out the day nicely. Elche is also the capital of Spanish footwear—factory outlets abound.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

places.name: Falset

visits.text: Falset, capital of the Priorat comarca, preserves its medieval center intact around Plaça de la Quartera. The 3,200 inhabitants live from wine and know it: stone farmhouses, sloping lanes, 12th-century castle at the summit. The partially ruined castell belonged to the Counts of Prades—free visit, panorama over the terraced vineyards climbing to 700 meters altitude. Here people say "I'm going to Priorat" even when they're already there.

Falset's cooperative winery (1919), designed by Cèsar Martinell, a student of Gaudí, is one of some forty Modernist "wine cathedrals" he built in Catalonia between 1917 and 1923; seven have been nationally protected cultural assets since 2002. Exposed brick, Catalan fan vaults, interior air stays at a constant 16°C in summer. Tour and tasting €12, two cooperative wines included—Étim Verema Tardana in white, harvested in late November, is remarkable. Allow 75 minutes.

The Falset Wine Museum, housed in the same cooperative winery, tells the story of Priorat's wine revolution. Before the 1980s, wines from here were considered rustic, sold in bulk. Five young winemakers—René Barbier, Álvaro Palacios, Josep Lluís Pérez, Daphne Glorian, and Carles Pastrana—catapulted the region into the world's top tier starting in 1989. Today their bottles reach €600 in cellars. Entry €4, allow 45 minutes.

The Scala Dei Charterhouse, 25 km from Falset, is the cradle of Priorat viticulture. Founded in 1194 by Carthusian monks from Provence on the orders of King Alfonso II, this is where the first vines were planted and where the word "priorat" takes its meaning—the entire region depended spiritually and economically on this charterhouse. The majestic ruins, partially restored, include a monk's cell reconstructed identically. Entry €5, French audioguide included.

Siurana, perched at 737 meters on a vertiginous limestone spur, is arguably Catalonia's most beautiful village. Thirty year-round inhabitants, the last Moorish fortress in Catalonia to fall in 1153. Legend has it that Abdelazia, the last Moorish queen, threw herself from the cliffs with her horse rather than surrender—the print of a horseshoe in the rock is still shown to visitors. Romanesque church of Sant Pere from the 12th century, plunging views over the turquoise water reservoir below. Round trip 1 hour from Falset.

Porrera, to the southeast, is the village of sundials—14 sundials from the 18th-19th centuries are painted on facades, identifiable via a 45-minute marked route. It's also Priorat's most northerly terroir, where the tautest grenaches grow. Virtually empty on weekdays, perfect for a pause after winery visits. The Vinícola del Priorat cooperative shop open Tuesday to Saturday, free tasting.

activities.text: Head to Gratallops, Priorat's most emblematic village—200 inhabitants, perched at 312 meters above the vines. Tour and tasting at Buil & Giné or Saó del Coster (€35-45 depending on estate), booking essential the day before. The llicorella soil, crushed black slate, gives the reds their signature mineral and smoky character—record concentration in grenache and carignan, sometimes from 80-year-old vines. 90 minutes on site, plus time to understand why these wines are so difficult to produce: yields of 5-10 hl/ha versus 50 elsewhere.

Dinner at Brichs in Falset, a creative dining room in an old stone house—inventive Mediterranean cuisine exclusively from Priorat products, menu €38 + wine pairing €22. The sommelier opens bottles directly in the cellar. Dishes to try: pasture-raised lamb roasted with Montsant herbs, Siurana DOP almond tart. Booking essential, capacity 25 covers.

Hike in the Serra de Montsant Natural Park, created in 2002—limestone cliffs rising to 1,100 meters overlooking Priorat's vineyards. Classic trail: from La Morera de Montsant, 3-hour loop to the hermitages of Sant Bartomeu and Sant Joan, 350 m elevation gain, moderate level. Griffon vultures nest in the ledges, easily observed soaring at midday. Bring water, no sources on the trail in summer.

Before returning to Falset, comparative tasting at Mas Doix in Poboleda (€45, by reservation)—family estate owning carignan vines planted in 1902, among Spain's oldest. Winemaker Valentí Llagostera recounts his family like a saga since 1850. Five wines served, including Doix Costers de Vinyes Velles, regularly ranked among the world's 50 finest wines. To finish: coffee at Lotus Priorat in Falset, shaded terrace on Plaça de la Quartera.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Catalonia in 10 Days: Tarragona, Priorat & the Ebro Delta
Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour
Catalonia Wine Route - 7 Days from Penedès to Priorat
Catalonia Wine Route - 7 Days from Penedès to Priorat

map_keywords: Scala Dei Charterhouse La Morera de Montsant

visits.text: The Dalí Theatre-Museum is the only architectural work that Dalí designed himself, from A to Z. He built it on the ruins of Figueres' municipal theatre, burned at the end of the civil war in 1939. Dalí wanted to make it 'the world's largest surrealist object' - and he's not wrong: giant eggs crown the walls, loaves adorn the facade, and a glass geodesic dome tops the central hall. The museum opened on October 28, 1974, and has become Spain's second most visited museum after the Prado. Dalí is buried in its crypt, beneath the stage, against his will - he wished to rest at Púbol alongside Gala.

The Sant Ferran fortress is 18th-century Europe's largest bastioned fortification: 32 hectares, 3.2 km perimeter, 9 million liters of water capacity in its underground cisterns. Built from 1751 under the reign of Fernando VI to block a potential French invasion, it nevertheless capitulated twice without fighting - to French troops in 1793 and again in 1808. Salvador Dalí did his military service here. Allow at least 2 hours, including the cistern tour if available.

Salvador Dalí's birthplace, at 6 Carrer Monturiol, is open to the public with an audio-visual tour tracing his childhood in Figueres. It's the intimate complement to the Theatre-Museum spectacle: here, you understand where the man came from before measuring the myth.

activities.text: The Rambla de Figueres, with its cafes and neoclassical and modernist buildings, is the city's commercial heart. Stop here for lunch with Empordà products: Alt Empordà anchovies are among Spain's finest. The markets on Tuesday, Thursday, and Saturday at Plaça del Gra are an opportunity to buy olives, Empordà AOC oils, and local cheeses.

Wine tasting of AOC Empordà wines at one of the downtown bodegas or in the surroundings: Empordà vineyards produce powerful reds and mineral whites influenced by the tramontana. The region also produces an excellent sweet fortified wine, Garnatxa d'Empordà.

days_plan.itin_title: Barcelona and Costa Brava Road Trip: Coves and Villages

places.name: Fisterra

visits.text: Cape Finisterre—Fisterra in Galician—is the Romans' end of the world, who called the place Finis Terrae ('land's end'). Granite cliff rising 138 m into the Atlantic, constant winds, the feeling of standing at the edge of a cosmic precipice. The 1853 lighthouse marks the unofficial finishing point of the Camino—many pilgrims continue from Santiago on an extra 90 km to reach it. Controversial tradition of burning your socks at sunset on the rocks: a gesture of rupture, journey's end. Now forbidden but still practised in secret.

The Pedras Santas, 200 m below the lighthouse, are rounded granite rocks, a Celtic-Roman solar cult site predating Christianity—hence the nickname Costa da Morte (coast of death). Pilgrims came here for purification before Christianity, a ritual later co-opted by the Church. The main flat rock still serves for end-of-Camino ceremonies. Feasts, meditation, sometimes symbolic weddings—tolerated as long as nature isn't degraded. Strange atmosphere, more pagan than Christian.

The igrexa de Santa María das Areas, in Fisterra village 3 km from the cape, is the official spiritual stop for pilgrims—a 12th-century Romanesque sanctuary housing the Cristo de la Barba Dourada, a medieval Christ with a gold-painted beard that supposedly floated ashore according to legend. Church open only during mass times, usually noon and 7pm. Small Atlantic cemetery attached—gravestones facing the ocean, some very recent. Allow 30 minutes.

The Costa da Morte, stretching 200 km from Malpica to Fisterra, owes its ominous name to over 600 recorded shipwrecks since antiquity. Winds, fog, invisible cliffs; HMS Serpent in 1890 lost 172 English sailors at Camariñas. Cemiterio dos Ingleses (Cemetery of the English) 30 km north of Fisterra, moving 1h walk. Mar de Fora beach, 2 km from Fisterra, inadvisable for swimming (killer currents) but sublime on foot. Perfect at dusk.

activities.text: For dinner, Fisterra's port serves what the trawlers brought in that afternoon. O Centolo (calle del Puerto) has been the institution since 1957—percebes (goose barnacles) at €60 per 200g portion, whole spider crab (centola) at €30/kg, white wine clams at €18. Booking essential at weekends. For limited budgets, A Marina on the port serves a fisherman's menu at €18 with the day's fish dish. Bottle of Pazo de Señorans Albariño at €22.

For a morning off the beaten track, the Lighthouse Way (Camiño dos Faros)—200 km route from Malpica to Fisterra along the coast—offers an easy stage between Fisterra and O Pindo (12 km, 4h, green-marked). Cliffs, deserted beaches, lost hermitages, sacred Mount Pindo ('Celtic Olympus'). Taxi return €25 from Fisterra. Perfect in the morning before the wind picks up. Walking poles useful, water and windbreaker essential.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Galicia: Complete 10-Day Circuit

map_keywords: Santa María das Areas Church Finisterre

visits.text: Fornells, a fishing village of 1,000 souls, stretches along a protected four-kilometer bay. Whitewashed houses cluster around a working harbor where tradition lives on.
Torre de Fornells, built in 1801 during British rule, stands guard at the bay's entrance. Menorca's largest watchtower commands the seaward approach.
From the tower's summit, sweeping panoramas reveal the sheltered bay, the village below, and dramatic Cap Cavalleria beyond. An exhibition chronicles the island's defensive history.
Sant Antoni Abad Church dates to the 17th century, its understated architecture perfectly suited to this humble fishing community.
Remnants of Sant Antoni Castle, constructed in the 17th century then later dismantled, dot the landscape around the village as ghostly reminders of strategic importance.

activities.text: Caldereta de langosta—a slow-simmered lobster stew made with the day's catch—is Fornells' signature dish. Harborside restaurants serve this legendary specialty nowhere else found.
The sheltered four-kilometer bay offers ideal kayaking conditions with calm, protected waters. Rental shops at the harbor equip adventurers for peaceful exploration.
The Northern Marine Reserve's seabed invites snorkeling among posidonia meadows teeming with Mediterranean biodiversity.

days_plan.itin_title: Menorca Road Trip: Harbors, Whitewashed Villages & Hidden Coves

visits.text: Frías is considered Spain's smallest 'town'—it received this title in 1435 and today has only about a hundred inhabitants, a member of Spain's Most Beautiful Villages since 2014. From the bottom of the Tobalina valley, the castle stands atop the limestone outcrop called La Muela: built between the 12th and 15th centuries on Alfonso VIII's orders, it contains 13th-century Romanesque capitals depicting warriors on horseback and fantastic birds. The panoramic view over the valley from the keep is worth the climb. Buy the ticket at the tourist office before heading up.

Along the slope below the castle, Frías's medieval lanes reveal the 'hanging houses'—half-timbered constructions clinging to the cliff edge, upper floors jutting out into space above the gorge. Descend to the medieval bridge over the Ebro (143 meters, 9 arches) with its 14th-century defense tower. The San Vicente church is also notable: its original Romanesque portal was detached and shipped to New York when the tower collapsed in 1904—it's now in an American museum.

Forty-six kilometers from Frías, Puentedey takes its name from its geology: the 50-inhabitant village is built on a natural limestone bridge sculpted by the Nela River over 85 to 90 million years. It's the most photographed image in Burgos province after the cathedral. From the suspended viewpoint of San Andrés, the overall view of the natural bridge and the houses perched on it is striking. On the arch itself, the San Pelayo church (11th-century Romanesque elements) and the Brizuela Palace (15th century, two rectangular towers with coats of arms) merit a stop.

activities.text: Walk the cobbled lanes climbing toward the castle. The medieval structure of the town can be read in the tangle of passages and stairs.
Go down to the bridge to photograph Frías from the opposite bank. The silhouette of the village and its castle stands out against the sky.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

visits.text: Frigiliana earns its reputation: the Barribarto is a real Moorish labyrinth, not a reconstructed film set. Steep cobbled lanes, whitewashed houses adorned with flowerpots, azulejos tiles telling the story of the Moorish revolt of 1570. Declared a Historic-Artistic Ensemble in 2014. Avoid midday in summer: the alleys offer little shade.

The old cane honey mill El Ingenio occupies the palace of the Counts of Frigiliana - it's still operational, the last of its kind in Andalusia. Short visit possible. Local cane honey (miel de caña) is sold at the village entrance, cheaper and better than what you'll find in Nerja.

activities.text: Climb to the last alleys before the path tips over into the sierra. The El Fuerte trail begins there - 2 km out for sea views. Bring closed shoes: wet cobbles are slippery. Return via the upper road for variety.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Coast: Málaga to Cartagena in 9 Days

places.name: Fromista

visits.text: Frómista, 800 inhabitants, laid flat in Tierra de Campos—that Castilian grain plateau where sky occupies three-quarters of landscape. Camino Francés stop since the 12th-century Codex Calixtinus, the village has lived off pilgrim passage for a thousand years. The main street, Calle Mayor, is actually the Camino de Santiago itself: yellow arrows painted on ground, shells in pavement, pilgrim hostales, municipal gîtes. A village that likely wouldn't have survived the 20th century if the Camino hadn't been resurrected in the 1980s.

The Church of San Martín de Frómista is why you stop here. Founded in 1066 by Doña Mayor, Countess of Castile and widow of Sancho the Great of Navarre, as part of a Benedictine monastery. The building seen today dates from around 1100. Basilical plan with three barrel-vaulted naves, three circular apses, polygonal lantern tower above crossing, two circular towers flanking the façade. Geometric form of a purity making it the prototype of Castilian Romanesque.

Inside San Martín, the capitals are what hold attention. Over fifty, carved with fantastic animals, biblical scenes, representations of medieval vices and virtues. The two-tailed mermaid, the miser strangled by his purse, contorted acrobats—the complete iconography of 11th-century Romanesque imagination. Above the altar, a late 13th-century Gothic crucified Christ, in polychrome wood, preserved in place. The restoration led between 1894 and 1904 by architect Aníbal Álvarez removed all later additions to return the building to its original Romanesque form—controversial decision at the time, since validated.

The Canal of Castile crosses Frómista—18th-century hydraulic engineering project, designed to transport Castilian wheat to northern ports. Launched in 1753 under Ferdinand VI, completed only a century later, and already rendered obsolete by railway arrival. But locks and towpaths remained. In Frómista, the quadruple lock—14-meter drop distributed over four basins—is one of the route's most spectacular. Walk along canal, tourist barge in season.

activities.text: Dinner in village, simple but authentic—Frómista isn't a gastronomic hotspot but its Castilian cuisine is worth the detour. Mesón Hostería de los Palmeros (former 12th-century pilgrim hospital converted) or Restaurante San Telmo: lechazo asado (milk lamb oven-roasted), morcilla de Burgos, queso de Burgos, nearby Cigales and Ribera del Duero wines. Count €25 per person. The atmosphere is pilgrim-village: sober, welcoming, scallop shells carved on woodwork.

Quiet evening in village. 20 km north, Aguilar de Campoo concentrates the world's highest Romanesque density—over a hundred 11th-12th-century Romanesque churches within 30 km radius. If you have time late in the day, detour via Santa María la Real monastery in Aguilar (12th century, partially converted to hotel-school of Romanesque studies) or via Carrión de los Condes (15 km west), another major Camino stop with exceptional Santiago church tympanum.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León Road Trip: Cathedrals and Medieval Villages
Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic

map_keywords: Church Santa María del Castillo Frómista

places.name: Fuente

visits.text: The Fuente Dé cable car climbs 753 vertical meters in just 4 minutes. From the lower station at 1,094 m, the glass cabin rises to 1,823 m into the heart of the central massif of the Picos de Europa.
The Mirador del Cable at the top offers a sweeping panorama over the glacial cirque of Fuente Dé, the Liébana valley and, on clear days, the Cantabrian Sea to the north.
Before or after the cable car, detour to Mogrovejo. This stone-built village boasts a medieval tower and spectacular views of the Picos.
The Monastery of Santo Toribio de Liébana, 3 km from Potes, shelters what tradition holds to be the largest surviving fragment of the True Cross. It is one of Christianity's holy sites.
Potes, capital of the Liébana valley, spreads its arcaded houses and bridges along the Río Quiviesa. The 15th-century Torre del Infantado dominates the small town.

activities.text: Take the PR-PNPE 24 trail, the Ruta de los Puertos de Áliva. From the upper station, a descending path crosses high pastures and karst landscapes down to Espinama.
Picnic facing the peaks. The high mountain pastures, dotted with shepherds' huts, provide an ideal setting between June and September.
In Potes, try cocido lebaniego, a chickpea stew with cabbage and compango sausage typical of the valley. Orujo de Liébana, the local eau-de-vie, accompanies dessert.

days_plan.itin_title: Cantabria Road Trip: Atlantic Bays and Picos de Europa

map_keywords: Puertos de Áliva Picos de Europa
Monasterio de Santo Toribio de Liébana

visits.text: The Palau Ducal dels Borja dates from the 15th century and was the Borgia family's residence before Francis Borgia became a Jesuit and was canonized. The palace is remarkably well preserved, with a gilded gallery and rooms decorated with Manises ceramics. 45-minute guided tour, available in Spanish and Valencian.

activities.text: Gandia's beach stretches five kilometers of fine sand, separated from the town centre by a residential zone. Less dense than Benidorm, still managed on a human scale. The north section of the beach, near the fishing port, stays quieter and retains a few chiringuitos without amplified music.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages

visits.text: Ochre, white, turquoise. Three colors for a landscape you'd swear belongs on another planet. The Barrancos de Gebas are badlands of argillaceous marls deposited 10 million years ago, when Sierra Espuña was an island. Protected since 1995, the site covers over 2,000 hectares. The viewpoint (built in 2002) offers an overview from the road. The hike down to the turquoise lake of Algeciras reservoir is 8 km round trip, with no shade—bring water.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca

visits.text: Balenciaga Museum — haute couture and clean lines.
Port of Getaria — wood-fired grilling.
San Salvador Chapel and narrow streets.

activities.text: Txakoli tasting at an estate.
