Walk towards the Urdaibai reserve.
Basque history workshop at the museum.

days_plan.itin_title: Bay of Biscay Coastal Drive: Fishing Villages & Basque Culinary Soul in France

places.name: Haro

visits.text: Haro's Barrio de la Estación is the most improbable place in Rioja: a railway district built in 1880 to export wine to Bordeaux during the phylloxera crisis, around which seven great century-old houses planted their flags. The station loaded the wagons, the bodegas set up within reach of the platform. Today the trains no longer run, but barrels still slumber in the same cellars.

Bodegas Muga, founded in 1932 by Isaac Muga and Aurora Caño, is the only winery in Spain that still employs a master cooper and three journeymen—all barrels are made in-house, from A to Z. Not a single stainless steel tank in the place: fermentation, malolactic, aging, everything happens in wood. Visitors can watch the cooperage in action in the morning—that's the highlight of the tour. The Reserva Selección Especial, 26 months in barrel, is the house benchmark. Very technical tour, about 1.5 hours.

R. López de Heredia Viña Tondonia: founded in 1877 by Rafael López de Heredia, who arrived from Santiago, Chile at age 20 and fell in love with Haro wine. Now in its 4th generation, and nothing has changed: more than 13,000 American oak barrels sleep in cellars where even a Bordeaux cave would look like a sanitized IKEA. The white Gran Reserva, aged 6 years in barrel then years in bottle, has been released in fewer than 20 vintages since 1890. In 2002, to celebrate their 125th anniversary, they commissioned Zaha Hadid to design a pavilion shaped like a decanter to house their kiosk from the 1910 Brussels World's Fair. The result is bewildering—architecture once again barging in to shake up tradition.

activities.text: Lunch or dinner in Haro—try Las Duelas or Atamauri for classic Basque-Riojan cuisine: roast milk lamb, patatas riojanas with chorizo, perfectly paired with a Haro Reserva. It's simple, honest, and washes down beautifully.

Stroll through Haro's historic center: the San Tomás church with its 16th-century Plateresque portal, the compact old town, and the authentic Spanish provincial vibe. Haro isn't touristy like Laguardia—that's an advantage.

days_plan.itin_title: La Rioja Circuit: Wine Route and Monasteries
La Rioja in 7 Days - The Kingdom of Tempranillo
Rioja in 7 Days: The Kingdom of Tempranillo Between Cathedral-Bodegas and Star Architecture

map_keywords: R. Lopez de Heredia Viña Tondonia Haro

visits.text: Millennia on 16 metres of wall: the Abrigo Grande de Minateda, on the outskirts of Hellín, contains Levantine and schematic rock paintings dated between 6,000 and 1,500 BC—more than 500 recorded figures, classified UNESCO World Heritage in 1998. Free guided tours offered by Hellín's tourist office on the last Sunday of the month; reservation required. For other days, enquire at 967 541 500.

activities.text: The Tolmo de Minateda, neighbouring rock platform, is a first-rate archaeological site: Iberian, Roman, Visigoth, Islamic—the layers stack up on this natural promontory that was a crossroads of routes. Excavations have shown continuous occupation for over two millennia. Guided tours organised occasionally by Hellín Council—check the calendar on culturahellin.com.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: Few tourists find their way to Herrera del Duque, and those who arrive all say the same thing: they didn't know Badajoz was hiding this. The 15th-century Almohad castle, perched on the Sierra Chamorro at 745 m, dominates the town and the comarca. Visitable on guided tours via the tourist office.

Naturalists spot in the Torre del Homenaje of Herrera del Duque Castle a Cruziana: a trilobite trace fossil 360 to 570 million years old, embedded in the stone of the second level. Ask the guide to show you. Lesser kestrels also nest in the ramparts.

Head down to the centro histórico for the Iglesia de San Juan Bautista and the miradors: Puerto de Consolación (stone and wood), Las Víboras (balcony over the valleys), Puerto de las Navas (towards the Cíjara reserve). Each offers a different angle on the dehesas and sierras.

activities.text: In autumn, the bellowing of stags echoes through the Reserva Regional de Cíjara, a few kilometres away. The Observatorio de la Berrea is the official spot to observe deer, wild boar, and fallow deer without disturbing them. Bring binoculars and patience.

The pedanía of Peloche, on the edge of the Embalse de García de Sola, has two river beaches with clean, warm water. Quiet spot to finish the road trip with a swim. Access by road from Herrera, 10 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: La Siberia and La Serena: Secret Extremadura in 6 Days

places.name: Hervas

visits.text: Devote the morning to the Valle del Jerte, one of Extremadura's most spectacular landscapes. The name comes from Arabic "xerete"—crystalline river—and that's exactly what it is: a deep V-shaped valley framed by the Sierra de Tormantos and the Traslasierra mountains, where dozens of gargantas (gorges) run with mountain water. The valley holds 1.5 million cherry trees whose simultaneous flowering, usually late March to early April, transforms the slopes into a vast white mantle—this is the Cerezo en Flor festival, declared a National Tourist Interest celebration. In summer, the gargantas become natural swimming spots of rare beauty.

The valley's jewel is the Reserva Natural de la Garganta de los Infiernos, 7,000 protected hectares since 1994—Extremadura's second most-visited natural area. The circular trail (about 7 km, 2 hours) starts from the Interpretation Centre and descends to Los Pilones—giant's kettles carved into granite by water over centuries, forming natural pools of crystalline water where you can swim in summer. The path threads through oak, chestnut, and cherry forests, with waterfalls at every turn. Motor access is forbidden—tour operators offer trips in authorized 4x4s to the trailhead.

Heading down the valley, stop at Cabezuela del Valle, whose historic center is classified as a Conjunto Histórico-Artístico. Its narrow streets with half-timbered chestnut houses, overhanging balconies, and heraldic doorways make it one of the valley's prettiest villages. The church's detached tower and the Plaza de la Independencia deserve a photo stop.

In the afternoon, head to Hervás in the neighboring Valle del Ambroz. This village has one of Spain's best-preserved juderías—classified as Conjunto Histórico-Artístico since 1969. The Jewish community settled here after the 1391 pogroms, welcomed by the Duke of Béjar. At its peak in the 15th century, nearly 50 Jewish families lived here. Walk down Calle Abajo, the main street of the barrio judío: the adobe and timber-framed chestnut houses, streets so narrow you could touch both walls with outstretched arms (Travesía del Moral is 55 cm wide—one of Spain's narrowest), Stars of David carved on facades, and street names like "Amistad Judeo-Cristiana" (Jewish-Christian Friendship) all testify to a past that remains very present.

Finish at the Puente de la Fuente Chiquita, a sturdy medieval granite bridge spanning the Río Ambroz at the foot of the judería. It's one of Hervás's most photographed images, especially in autumn when the chestnuts turn golden. At number 19 Calle Rabilero, a building wider than the others occupies the site of the old synagogue. In July, the Fiesta de los Conversos reenacts medieval life and the 1492 expulsion in an open-air performance by the river.

activities.text: Hervás and Valle del Ambroz cuisine centers on the chestnut—which provides timber for houses, autumn fruit, and accompaniment to many dishes. In autumn (late October through December), the Otoñada celebrates chestnuts with markets, tastings, and walks through centuries-old chestnut groves. The rest of the year, try dulces sefardíes (Sephardic-inspired pastries) that some Hervás restaurants offer on themed menus.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas

map_keywords: Garganta de los Infiernos Valle del Jerte

visits.text: You expect a classic Andalusian port city and instead encounter a British mining town: the Barrio Reina Victoria, built in 1916 by the Rio Tinto Company Limited for its English workers. Seventy-one timber-gabled houses, gardens and lawns, pitched roofs—in the middle of Andalusia. Declared a site of historical interest in 2002.

The Monumento a Colón de la Rábida, a massive 37-meter column planted at the confluence of the Tinto and Odiel rivers, points toward the estuary through which the caravels sailed out to the Atlantic in 1492. Gertrude Vanderbilt Whitney's work dates from 1929. From the viewpoint at its base, the view of the two rivers merging is striking.

activities.text: The blood-red waters of the Río Tinto arrive here, at Muelle del Tinto, the old wrought-iron loading pier that loaded ore onto ships. The smell of iron and acidic water lingers at the pier's end. The sunset over the ría, between the orange reflections of the river and the Atlantic on the horizon, is one of the strangest in all Andalusia.

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva, the Forgotten Land: Doñana to Faro in 7 Days

places.name: Huesca

visits.text: The church and cloister of San Pedro el Viejo, a national monument since 1885, form one of Aragon's most important Romanesque ensembles. The 1149 cloister preserves 18 original capitals sculpted by the Master of San Juan de la Peña, depicting the life of Christ. The Chapel of San Bartolomé houses the royal pantheon of kings Alfonso I the Battler and Ramiro II the Monk.

Huesca's Gothic cathedral, erected on the former mosque, houses the monumental alabaster altarpiece by Damián Forment, a masterpiece of the Aragonese Renaissance. Its high vaults and stained glass filter soft light onto the side chapels.

Facing the cathedral, the Town Hall occupies a 16th-century Renaissance palace. This is where the bloody legend of the "Bell of Huesca" allegedly occurred: King Ramiro II supposedly beheaded rebellious nobles and arranged their heads in the shape of a bell to impose his authority.

The Provincial Museum of Huesca, housed in the 16th-century Casa de la Comunidad, displays a Roman archaeological section, a reconstructed old pharmacy, and, on the top floor, very fine ceramics from the Al-Andalus period. The terrace offers a view over the city's rooftops.

Wander through Huesca's Casco Antiguo, among arcaded squares and medieval lanes. The remains of the Roman theater of Semonia beneath the modern city remind us that Huesca was an important city of Hispania under the name Osca.

activities.text: Parque Miguel Servet, Huesca's green lung, lines its shaded walkways with century-old plane trees, fountains, and sculptures. It's the favorite walking spot for Oscenses, especially pleasant in late afternoon.

Sample the specialties of Upper Aragon: sheep cheeses from the Pyrenean valleys, longaniza de Graus sausage, chiretas (stuffed tripe with rice and meat), and ternasco, the roasted milk lamb that is the region's gastronomic emblem.

Around August 10th, the Fiestas de San Lorenzo, declared of national tourist interest, transform Huesca into a popular celebration where white and green invade the streets. Off-season, the terraces of Plaza de la Catedral let you enjoy the evening bustle.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragon Circuit: Zaragoza & Mudéjar Villages

map_keywords: Monasterio San Pedro el Viejo Huesca

visits.text: Dalt Vila—the fortified citadel—has towered over Ibiza Town since the 16th century. This UNESCO site unfolds Renaissance ramparts, bastions, and cobbled lanes offering sweeping harbor views.
Portal de ses Taules opens onto Dalt Vila. This monumental gateway flanked by Roman statues is the upper town's main entrance.
Santa María Cathedral was erected in the 14th century atop the former mosque. Its Gothic character gained Baroque additions in the 18th century.
The Archaeological Museum traces 3,000 years of history. Housed in the former university, it exhibits Phoenician, Punic, and Roman remains.
Ibiza's coves glow an unreal turquoise. Cala Comte, Cala d'Hort facing Es Vedrà, and Cala Salada rank among the most spectacular.
Eivissa harbor pulses day and night. Yachts, fishing boats, and Formentera ferries line the marina.
Ses Salines Natural Park extends between Ibiza and Formentera. This UNESCO site harbors flamingos, traditional salt pans, and pristine beaches.

activities.text: Ferries reach Formentera in 30 minutes. The neighboring island unveils white-sand beaches and crystalline waters worthy of the Caribbean.
Cala Comte delivers Ibiza's finest sunsets. The sun plunges behind rocky islets while beach bars spin chill music.
Dalt Vila's lanes come alive at night. Art galleries, bohemian boutiques, and terraces drift in a laid-back atmosphere.
The hippy markets of Las Dalias and Punta Arabí showcase crafts, jewelry, and bohemian clothing. Live music and a relaxed vibe reign beneath the pines.

days_plan.itin_title: Majorca Road Trip: Serra de Tramuntana & Hidden Coves

places.name: Jaca

visits.text: Jaca was the first capital of the Kingdom of Aragón in the 11th century and a major stopping point on the Camino Aragonés to Santiago de Compostela. Begin with the Cathedral of San Pedro, the first Romanesque cathedral built in Spain - construction began in 1077, almost simultaneously with Santiago de Compostela. The tympanum of the western portal features a Chi-Rho flanked by two lions, one protecting a repentant sinner, the other crushing a serpent. Inside, the pendentive dome above the transept crossing is the oldest in Spain. Note the checkerboard pattern (ajedrezado jaqués), created here and later adopted throughout Romanesque churches along the Way of Saint James.

Don't miss the Diocesan Museum of Jaca, housed in the cathedral's cloister - it's an underrated gem. The museum preserves Romanesque frescoes recovered from small churches throughout the Aragonese Pyrenees - some were detached from walls to save them from abandonment and looting. The Romanesque painting gallery, with its figures sporting enormous eyes and intense blue backgrounds, is worth the visit alone. Take the guided tour (included in the ticket price), which lasts about an hour and reveals details invisible to the naked eye.

The Citadel of Jaca is the only entirely preserved pentagonal fortress in Spain - one of only two in Europe, along with Liège. Designed by Italian engineer Tiburzio Spannocchi in 1592 under orders from Philip II to defend the border with France, it features a perfect star-shaped plan with five bastions connected by walkways, a deep moat (now home to deer), and a drawbridge. Walk around the ramparts to appreciate the perfect geometric precision of the design. Inside, the chapel and powder magazine are accessible, and the Museum of Military Miniatures recreates battles with 32,000 lead soldiers.

activities.text: If you have time at the end of the day, drive 20 minutes southwest to reach the Monastery of San Juan de la Peña, one of Aragón's most spectacular monuments. This 11th-century monastery is semi-troglodytic, wedged beneath an enormous rocky overhang that serves as its natural roof. It was the pantheon of the early kings of Aragón and one of the kingdom's cultural centers. The Romanesque cloister, carved directly beneath the rock, features historiated capitals of extraordinary fineness.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel

map_keywords: Monasterio San Juan de la Peña

visits.text: Vandelvira had a problem: Jaén Cathedral is too large for the city that contains it. The architect's last great 16th-century work, it became the formal template for many Latin American cathedrals. On the UNESCO indicative list. Its twin-towered façade, 63 meters high, dominates from any point in the city.

The Castillo de Santa Catalina, an Arab fortress dominating Jaén from 330 meters elevation, is now partly a parador. The terrace is freely accessible and offers the widest view over the sea of olive trees covering the province—millions of trees as far as the eye can see. Drive up recommended, 15 minutes from downtown.

activities.text: Beneath the slabs of a 16th-century palace, 450 square meters of interconnected rooms—cool, dark, perfumed with damp stone. The Arab baths of the Palacio de Villardompardo, built in the 11th century, are the most extensive you can visit in Spain. A viceroy of Peru buried them beneath his palace in the 16th—a fortunate mistake. Free entry. Closed Mondays.

days_plan.itin_title: Al-Andalus: From the Mezquita to the Alpujarras in 10 Days

places.name: Javea

visits.text: Locals simply call it 'el cap'—and that says it all. Cabo de la Nao is the easternmost point in Alicante province, marking the end of the Gulf of Valencia. Its octagonal lighthouse stands 122 meters above the sea and is visible from 23 nautical miles away. On clear days, Ibiza appears on the horizon. The winding road from Jávea offers breathtaking views over the coves of Portixol and La Granadella. Free parking at the cape.

Twenty kilometers of coastline, yet not a single dull beach along this stretch. The fortified church of San Bartolomé in the historic center—Gothic and massive like a castle disguised as a church—contrasts sharply with the bustling marina. The old town, two kilometers from the sea, unfolds through medieval streets built to withstand pirate raids of the 16th and 17th centuries.

activities.text: Arrive before 10 AM at La Granadella and you'll have the cove to yourself. This little gem of white rock and turquoise water is also a renowned diving site. Kayak rental operators can be found at both La Granadella and Portixol for exploring the sea caves. Plan on 3h for a kayak trip to the cape and back. Avoid July-August after 11 AM: access becomes difficult and parking turns chaotic.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

map_keywords: Cap de la Nau lighthouse Jávea

places.name: Jerez De La Frontera

visits.text: The Alcázar of Jerez, a 12th-century Almohad fortress, contains nearly every element of a Moorish palace: vaulted halls, Arab baths with starlight filtering through ceiling perforations, a tiny mosque converted into a chapel, and an ancient olive oil mill. From the tower summit, views sweep across the cathedral and Jerez's rooftops.

San Salvador Cathedral rises on a platform where a mosque once stood. It mingles Gothic, Renaissance, and neoclassical styles across centuries of construction. The bell tower, likely a former minaret, forms a beautifully illuminated ensemble with the Alcázar at night. Inside, a Zurbarán painting depicts the Virgin Mary as a contemplative child.

Step inside a sherry bodega to understand the solera and criaderas system: American oak barrels stacked in tiers, with wine slowly flowing from youngest to oldest, creating a perpetual blend that maintains consistency across decades. The Atlantic air from 12 kilometers away gives fino its characteristic saline tang.

The Royal Andalusian School of Equestrian Art, founded in 1973, occupies a 19th-century palace. Its 1,600-seat arena hosts "Cómo bailan los caballos andaluces"—a ballet of Andalusian horses in 18th-century costumes, accompanied by traditional Spanish music in a breathtaking display of precision and grace.

Wander through the Santiago quarter, birthplace of Jerez flamenco, where colorful buildings and flower-filled patios line picturesque alleys. The Gothic church of San Miguel rises at the end of a plaza where flamenco peñas keep the tradition of bulería alive with passionate performances.

activities.text: Join a guided tasting of different sherry types: bone-dry, saline fino; amber amontillado with nutty aromas; dark, powerful oloroso; and rare, complex palo cortado. Jerez, El Puerto de Santa María, and Sanlúcar form the "Sherry Triangle," the only region where authentic sherry can be produced.

Attend the equestrian show on performance days. Riders execute classical dressage and doma vaquera—the traditional horsemanship of Andalusian cattle herders—on pure Spanish horses selected for their grace, power, and connection with their riders.

In the evening, settle into a tabanco in the old town. These traditional sherry bars serve wine straight from the cask, paired with tapas: crispy chicharrones, shrimp fritters, and cheese from the Sierra de Cádiz mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Cádiz Road Trip: Jerez, Sherry Bodegas & White Villages
Costa de la Luz: 7 Days from Seville to Tarifa

map_keywords: Alcázar de Jerez de la Frontera
Real Escuela Andaluza Arte Ecuestre Jerez

places.name: Jerez De Los Caballeros

visits.text: Jerez de los Caballeros is the final stop on this itinerary—and what a finale. Birthplace of Vasco Núñez de Balboa, the first European to sight the Pacific Ocean in 1513, it has a spectacular historic center dominated by the silhouettes of its baroque towers. The Templar fortress, built in the 13th century when the Knights Templar controlled this frontier with Muslim Andalusia, overlooks the town from a hilltop. It's in the Torre Sangrienta (Bloody Tower) that Jerez's last Templars were supposedly massacred in 1312 during the dissolution of the order. The legend is probably exaggerated, but it fits the mood of the place.

Jerez's two baroque churches are among Extremadura's finest. The Iglesia de San Bartolomé has a bell tower covered in colorful azulejos that recalls Seville's bell towers—it's the town's iconic image. The Iglesia de La Encarnación, with its own slender baroque tower, completes an urban landscape you'd swear was Andalusian. Climb the sloping streets to discover the noble palaces, carved coats of arms, and flower-filled patios that built the reputation of this conquistador town.

On Plaza de Vasco Núñez de Balboa, a statue honors the town's most famous son. Born here around 1475, Balboa sailed for the New World in 1500 and crossed the Isthmus of Panama in 1513 to become the first European to see the Pacific Ocean. The plaza also offers fine views over the surrounding hills covered in holm oaks—the dehesa landscape that characterizes all of Lower Extremadura.

activities.text: Jerez de los Caballeros sits at the heart of the jamón ibérico de bellota production zone—the world's finest ham. Black Iberian pigs roam free in the dehesas, feeding on holm oak acorns during the montanera (autumn). If you're passing October through February, you'll see the pigs under the oaks—a unique landscape. Regional tour operators offer visits to ham-curing facilities with tastings. Even out of season, every bar in Jerez serves hand-sliced jamón ibérico—it's the best way to close this Extremadura journey.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas

map_keywords: Fortaleza Templaria Jerez de los Caballeros
Iglesia San Bartolomé Jerez de los Caballeros
Iglesia La Encarnación Jerez de los Caballeros
Torre Sangrienta Jerez de los Caballeros

places.name: La Alberca

visits.text: La Alberca became the very first village in all of Spain to be declared a Historic-Artistic Site—in 1940, by decree of September 6, published in the BOE on the 18th of the same month. This pioneering title is well deserved: its granite houses with ground floors topped by upper stories in adobe and timber forming warm half-timbering are the perfect model of serrana architecture. The village name comes from the Arabic 'Al-Bereka,' meaning pond or water place—the numerous fountains dotting the lanes are its legacy. The Plaza Mayor, with its irregular layout, granite arcades, and flower-decked balconies, displays an 18th-century cross carved with symbols of the Passion.

The Church of Nuestra Señora de la Asunción, built in 1730 by Manuel de Larra Churriguera (architect of Salamanca Cathedral), is a baroque jewel in this medieval setting. Its grand tower was erected in 1518 by the first Dukes of Alba—their coat of arms still visible on one corner. Inside, ask to see the Cristo del Sudor, attributed to Juan de Juni, and the 16th-century polychrome wooden altarpiece.

Walk down to the Puente del Arroyo, the old granite bridge spanning the stream below the village—a classic view of serrana architecture. Along the way, you'll pass the Hornacina de las Ánimas, a wall niche where each evening a local woman comes out with her bell (esquila) to recite a prayer for the souls in purgatory. This nighttime tradition is still alive.

The Casa Museo Sátur Juanela, adjacent to the square, preserves the interior of a typical serrana house as it was in the early 20th century—farmyard, wine cellars on the ground floor, kitchen under the eaves. It's the most concrete way to understand how mountain villagers lived.

activities.text: Each year in the village, a pig is released into the streets on June 13 (Saint Anthony's Day) and fed by residents until January 17, when it's raffled off. Proceeds now go to an NGO. If you visit outside this period, you'll still find the statue of the marrano de San Antón at the church corner. This tradition reaches back to the Middle Ages and is one of the most photographed in Spain.

The route into Parque Natural Las Batuecas-Sierra de Francia starts from La Alberca. The Batuecas Path descends into a steep, secret valley where Neolithic cave paintings and a Carmelite monastery survive. Count on a half-day for the round trip to the valley floor.

On the square, sample the obleas and artisan turrones sold in local shops—La Alberca's sweet specialties found throughout the region. Local markets also offer a good opportunity to buy honey, cheese, and sierra cured meats.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

map_keywords: Église Nuestra Señora de la Asunción La Alberca
Casa Museo Satur Juanela La Alberca

visits.text: Two seas on either side—one calm as a millpond, the other open to the swell—that's the strange promise of La Manga. This 24 km sand spit separates the Mar Menor, Europe's largest saltwater lagoon, from the Mediterranean. Formed over millennia by currents and volcanic reefs, the place is both overdeveloped beach resort and nature reserve. Avoid July-August when the population explodes from 10,000 to 200,000.

activities.text: Cross the strip by bike or on foot from the Venetian bridge on the north end down to Calblanque Park in the south. The two sides offer radically different vibes: on the Mediterranean, waves and wind; on the Mar Menor side, warm water and near-perfect stillness. Bike rentals available at the main access points. Allow 2 hours for the north-south ride at a leisurely pace.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca

places.name: La Seu D Urgell

visits.text: On the road from Lleida, stop at Coll de Nargó. The village harbors the Romanesque church of Sant Climent, but especially its paleontological site: one of Europe's most important dinosaur egg deposits, 70 million years old. The Dinosfera center lets you see fossilized nests in situ and reconstructs the late Cretaceous ecosystem. Curious fact: Ramon Lambard, the architect who completed La Seu d'Urgell's cathedral, also came from Coll de Nargó. Count on an hour on site.

Arguably the masterpiece of Catalan Romanesque art: the Cathedral of Santa Maria d'Urgell, the only entirely Romanesque cathedral surviving in the region. Construction launched in 1116 by Bishop Saint Ot, completed in 1183 under Ramon Lambard. Basilica plan with three naves, transept with five apses, and especially that Italian gallery above the façade—a detail found nowhere else in Catalonia. The very thick walls hide another truth: the cathedral also served as a fortress, which is why it has lasted eight centuries. The central portal concentrates all the sculptural decoration.

The adjoining cloister is slightly trapezoidal in plan, an oddity you notice from the entrance. Three 12th-century Romanesque galleries with historiated capitals—vegetal motifs, human figures, monsters; the fourth was redone in the 17th century. It serves as passage to the church of Sant Miquel and the diocesan museum. The museum is worth the detour for one piece alone: the Beatus of La Seu d'Urgell, an illuminated Mozarabic manuscript of the Commentary on the Apocalypse, early 10th century. One of the oldest preserved in the world.

The Carrer dels Canonges, the old town's main street, runs along the former defensive moats filled in during the 16th century. Seigneurial houses in a row: Cal Tarragona, Cal Calçot, Cal Patalín. Originally, this was the street of the cathedral canons. Today, cheese shops (La Seu d'Urgell is Catalonia's cheese capital), artisan boutiques, terrace cafés. The Tower of Solsona, isolated from the rest of Castellciutat's walls, is a 5-minute walk above town and offers the best view of the cathedral.

activities.text: The Parc Olímpic del Segre, created for the canoe-kayak events of the 1992 Barcelona Olympics, still offers rafting and kayaking on whitewater on the artificial canal and natural river. World championships have been held here several times. Good option for an afternoon of adrenaline if you prefer to save the Noguera Pallaresa, wilder, for tomorrow. Morning or afternoon sessions, from €35 per descent.

Cheese tasting in La Seu. The region produces formatge de l'Alt Urgell i la Cerdanya, PDO, from high-altitude pasture dairy cows, washed rind, soft paste. Several cheese dairies and a cheese museum 5 km away in Castellciutat. Combine with a tasting of local vermut or ratafia (green walnut liqueur typical of the Catalan Pyrenees). More original than the standard evening paella meal.

days_plan.itin_title: Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour
Catalan Pyrenees and Romanesque Art in 8 Days: Lleida, Pallars, Vall de Boí and Val d'Aran

map_keywords: Sant Miquel de la Seu d'Urgell

places.name: La Seu Durgell

visits.text: The Cathedral of Santa Maria is the only entirely Romanesque cathedral preserved in Catalonia - reason enough to come. Begun around 1116, completed in 1175 by a certain Ramon Lambard, probably Italian: this explains the unusual Lombard arcading on the facade, unique in the region. Local peculiarity: the Bishop of Urgell has been, since the 13th century, one of the two co-princes of Andorra, the other being the French president. Combined ticket with the cloister and museum: €7. Allow 90 minutes for the complete tour.

The 13th-century cloister is arguably the finest part of the complex. Three original galleries, the fourth rebuilt in 1603 - look for the Romanesque capitals: monsters, mermaids, lions devouring each other, far from the edifying discourse you'd expect. At the centre stands Sant Miquel church from 1035, the only remnant of Saint Ermengol's original cathedral. Accessible with the combined ticket. If you're here on 7 July, it's the feast of Saint Ot, patron of the city, with a medieval procession.

The Diocesan Museum, attached to the cloister, houses the piece that alone justifies the detour: the Beatus of La Seu d'Urgell. This illustrated 10th-century manuscript is one of only five complete Beatus codices in the world - 239 folios, 89 miniatures on the Apocalypse with colours still vivid a thousand years later. The rest of the collection brings together Gothic altarpieces and goldwork salvaged from Pyrenean parishes during the Civil War. Enthusiasts spend two hours here, others a good hour.

Carrer dels Canonges is the oldest street in town, lined with medieval arcades in black stone - you'd think you were in Siena, only rougher. Allow twenty minutes to walk up to Plaça dels Oms. Halfway along, spot Las medidas de cereales embedded in a wall: these are the official grain measures from the medieval market, still in place. Many visitors walk past without seeing them.

activities.text: The Segre Olympic Park, 200 metres from the historic centre, was built for the canoe-kayak events at the Barcelona '92 Olympics. Today, anyone can try rafting on the engineered rapids: €25 for a 90-minute descent, instruction included. Water at 12-14°C even in high summer - wetsuit provided. The local club still trains Spain's national slalom team, you sometimes spot champions in training.

Head to the cheese market on Tuesday and Saturday mornings on Plaça del Comtat - the town is surrounded by farms that supply all Catalonia with raw milk. Taste the tupí, pungent fermented cheese with eau-de-vie, sold in stoneware pots. It's harsh, it's sharp, it's local and 99% of tourists miss it. For dinner, terrace on Plaça dels Oms facing the cathedral, daily special €18.

days_plan.itin_title: Catalan Pyrenees - 6-Day Discovery

map_keywords: Santa Maria Cathedral La Seu d'Urgell
Sant Miquel Church La Seu d'Urgell
Carrer dels Canonges La Seu d'Urgell
Segre River Park La Seu d'Urgell

visits.text: Lake Enol appears first around the bend of the road, at 1,070 meters altitude. This glacial lake reaches 25 meters deep. Beneath its waters lies a replica of the Virgin of Covadonga, raised to the surface each September 8th by divers.

Two kilometers higher, Lake Ercina rests at 1,108 meters. Smaller than Enol, it's surrounded by pastures where Asturian cattle and horses graze freely. In the background, the summit of Peña Santa de Enol rises to 2,486 meters.

The Pedro Pidal Visitor Center, near the Buferrera parking lot, traces the glacial geology of the lakes and the history of the national park. Nearby, the old Buferrera manganese mines bear witness to the area's mining past.

Stop at the Mirador de la Reina on the upward road: on clear days, the panorama sweeps from the Cangas de Onís valley all the way to the Cantabrian Sea. The Mirador Entrelagos, on the hill between the two lakes, offers a simultaneous view of both Enol and Ercina.

activities.text: Walk the circuit of the lakes (PR-PNPE-2), a 5 km circular hike starting from the Buferrera parking lot. The marked trail passes through viewpoints, high-altitude meadows, and beech forests of the western Picos massif.
Keep your eyes and ears open: chamois, griffon vultures, and golden eagles frequent the limestone ridges above the lakes. The tinkling of bells from free-roaming herds accompanies the walk.
Set up for a picnic facing Lake Enol with views of the snow-capped peaks. The Vega de Enol, a vast high-altitude meadow dotted with shepherds' barns, is the perfect spot.

days_plan.itin_title: Asturias Road Trip: Cantabrian Coast and Picos de Europa

places.name: Laguardia

visits.text: First stop, and already a stunner: the Guggenheim Bilbao. Inaugurated in 1997 by King Juan Carlos I, this titanium fever dream designed by Frank Gehry spans 24,000 m², with 11,000 dedicated to contemporary art across 19 galleries. 33,000 titanium plates, each one unique, calibrated to the millimeter using CATIA software developed by Dassault—the same program used for Airbus planes. In the first year, 1.3 million visitors flooded through, triple the projections. Economists even coined a term for it: 'the Bilbao Effect.' Richard Serra's gallery (The Matter of Time, 2005) is free and worth the trip alone—130 meters long, with 22-ton steel sculptures you walk through like a labyrinth. Get the audio guide.

Ninety minutes south of Bilbao, Laguardia rises from the plateau: 1,500 inhabitants perched at 635 meters altitude, ringed by 10th-century ramparts, founded to be 'the guardian of the Kingdom of Navarre.' The showstopper is the portico of Santa María de los Reyes church—carved in polychrome stone in the late 14th century, with original 17th-century colors still visible. It's one of the best-preserved Gothic polychrome porticos in Europe. And beneath every house in the village, cellars dug in the 16th-17th centuries, some transformed into hotels or tasting rooms: Laguardia literally sits on a honeycomb of caves.

The drive over the Sierra de Cantabria is pure theater: the mountain drops away suddenly, and the Ebro plain spreads out below, vineyards stretching to the horizon. It's not just a road—it's the frontier between two climatic worlds. This barrier shields the vineyards from Atlantic winds and gives Rioja Alavesa its distinctive character.

Marqués de Riscal in Elciego, founded in 1858, was the first bodega to introduce Bordeaux techniques to Rioja—French oak barrels, aging classifications. But people really come for the hotel designed by Frank Gehry in 2006: a tangle of pink, silver, and gold titanium ribbons that local winemakers still regard with disbelief. The Reserva remains the benchmark cuvée, aged 24 months in barrel. The visit takes about 2 hours and costs €30-40, tasting included. The Michelin-starred restaurant is worth the splurge if you're celebrating something.

Bodegas Ysios, 2 km from Laguardia, was inaugurated in 2001 by Santiago Calatrava—the first 'signature bodega' in all of Rioja. Two 196-meter-long concrete load-bearing walls, spaced 26 meters apart, trace a sinuous form. The copper-treated cedar facade evokes rows of barrels; the aluminum roof undulates in echo of the Sierra de Cantabria just behind. Reflected in a pool, the facade resembles a corkscrew. The interior is modern, the wines fruitier and more accessible than Tondonia. Visit takes 1.5 hours, around €12.

activities.text: Welcome dinner with pintxos at a bar in downtown Laguardia, paired with a local Crianza by the glass. Each bar has its specialty—ask for the 'champiñón al ajillo' or the lamb skewer. The vibe is 100% local; tourists mostly come on weekends.

Evening stroll through Laguardia's cobbled lanes at sunset. The viewpoint from the Torre Abacial offers 360° views of the vineyards and Sierra de Cantabria. At this hour, the light turns golden—the best time to photograph the vineyards.

Comparative tasting of all three sub-regions at Eguren Ugarte in Laguardia: Rioja Alavesa, Rioja Alta, Rioja Oriental side by side. It's one of the few bodegas that vinifies in all three zones, letting you truly taste the difference between northern clay-limestone soils and southern clay. Allow 1.5 hours, 5 minutes from Laguardia.

Hike through the vineyards around Laguardia on the marked trail connecting the village to prehistoric dolmens—some dating to 3,000 BC. The plunging view of the Ebro from the promontory is your reward.

days_plan.itin_title: La Rioja Circuit: Wine Route and Monasteries
La Rioja in 7 Days - The Kingdom of Tempranillo
La Rioja in 7 Days - The Kingdom of Tempranillo
Rioja in 7 Days: The Kingdom of Tempranillo Between Cathedral-Bodegas and Star Architecture
Rioja in 7 Days: The Kingdom of Tempranillo Between Cathedral-Bodegas and Star Architecture

map_keywords: Santa María de los Reyes Church Laguardia
Marqués de Riscal Elciego Gehry hotel

places.name: Laguna Negra De Urbion

visits.text: Laguna Negra is one of Soria province's most impressive natural destinations: a glacial lake nestled at 1,750 meters altitude, encircled by granite walls and pine forests. The name comes from the dark color of its waters, reflecting the cliffs surrounding it. The Urbión Glacial Lagoons and Cirques Natural Park, created in 2010 over 4,617 hectares, protects this ensemble. From the Paso de la Serrá parking (mandatory access in high season), the paved path leads in a few minutes to a wooden walkway at the lake's edge.

activities.text: The loop hike from Paso de la Serrá to Pico de Urbión (2,228 m) via Laguna Negra and Laguna Helada is one of the most beautiful in the entire Iberian System. Allow 12 km and about 4 hours with 664 m of elevation gain—rated difficult on AllTrails with a 4.6/5 score. From the summit, you'll see the plains of Castile to the south, La Rioja and the Ebro to the north. This summit is also where the Duero River is born. Wear high-top shoes: the rocky corridor above the lake is steep. In winter and spring, hard snow may be present.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

map_keywords: Pico de Urbión Sierra de Urbión

visits.text: Lanjarón water has a taste: light, cold, slightly mineral—the water sold in bottles all over Spain comes from this town's springs at the gateway to the Alpujarras. The ruined castle on the northern promontory, called the 'Moors' castle,' offers a panorama over the valley. According to legend, its last defender threw himself from the tower rather than surrender to the Catholic Monarchs in 1490. An hour is enough here.

The Balneario de Lanjarón, a thermal establishment open since the 19th century, uses water from the town's six sulfurous and bicarbonate springs. A day pass to the baths (basic thermal circuit) is offered without an overnight stay requirement—a good break before climbing into the Alpujarras.

activities.text: The Mirador del Castillo, a few minutes' walk from the center, gives the clearest overview of Lanjarón and the Guadalfeo valley toward the sea. A marked trail from the main street climbs to the castle ruins in 20 minutes—rocky path but no difficulty.

days_plan.itin_title: Al-Andalus: From the Mezquita to the Alpujarras in 10 Days

places.name: Las Medulas

visits.text: Las Médulas is Castile and León's strangest landscape: reddish sand pinnacles rise from a forest of oaks and chestnuts, like a fantasy film set. This isn't natural geology—it's the direct result of the Roman Empire's largest open-pit gold mine. The Romans extracted approximately 800,000 kilos of gold here between the 1st and 3rd centuries, using a process called 'ruina montium': they stored colossal amounts of water in mountain basins, then released it all at once to explode the rock. Over 400 km of canals and aqueducts were built to feed this system. Listed as UNESCO in 1997, the site has remained intact since the mines were abandoned. Start at the Interpretation Center in the village to understand the operation's scale before heading out on the trails.

Climb up to the Mirador de Orellán for the overview of the site—from here the spectacle is most striking: the jagged red formations stand out against the green forests, with the Aquilanos mountains in the background. The sunset from this viewpoint is one of the most photographed in León province. Allow 30 minutes uphill from the Orellán parking lot.

activities.text: Walk the Senda de las Valiñas, a circular route from Las Médulas village that penetrates the heart of the mining site. The trail crosses the open-air galleries, skirts Lake Carucedo (formed by stagnant water accumulation during Roman works) and passes through the caves of La Cuevona and La Encantada—galleries hand-dug for the hydraulic system. Easy, marked, about 2 hours. If you have more time, the Peripheral Trail (4h) makes the complete circuit and includes the ruins of the Orellán settlement.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks
Inner Galicia: From Ourense to the Ribeira Sacra

map_keywords: Cueva de La Cuevona Las Médulas

places.name: Las Negras

visits.text: Cala San Pedro must be earned: an hour's walk from Las Negras along a coastal path overlooking the Mediterranean. The ruins of a 15th-century castle guard the entrance to this isolated cove, a former pirate hideout. A freshwater spring—the only one in the park—flows at the beach's edge.

Las Negras takes its name from Cerro Negro, the dark volcanic mass that dominates the village. About 350 people live here year-round, including many foreigners drawn by the tranquility. Fishing boats still beach on the black pebble shore each evening.

Take the road to Rodalquilar, a former mining village reinvented. The ruins of the gold mines, worked until 1966, can be freely visited. The El Albardinal Botanical Garden showcases the park's semi-arid flora.

The Playazo de Rodalquilar offers a striking contrast with the enclosed coves: 400 meters of golden sand bordered by the Playazo tower, an 18th-century fortification. The water here is calm and shallow.
From the trail above Cerro Negro, viewpoints offer spectacular vistas of black cliffs plunging into the sea and, in the distance, the silhouette of Isleta del Moro.

activities.text: Hike Las Negras to Cala San Pedro via the coastal trail. The route crosses semi-arid landscape punctuated by red and yellow cliffs before diving toward the turquoise cove.
Kayak along the volcanic cliffs from Las Negras. Rental shops offer guided outings that follow caves and rock formations inaccessible by land.
Snorkeling in the coves around Las Negras. The volcanic rocky seabeds create varied habitats for Mediterranean underwater fauna.
Evening drinks facing the sea on the Las Negras promenade. The village's bohemian vibe carries through to the small waterfront bars.

days_plan.itin_title: Almería Road Trip: Volcanic Coves and Tabernas Desert
Wild Coast: Málaga to Cartagena in 9 Days

map_keywords: Castillo de San Pedro Cabo de Gata

places.name: Las Palmas De Gran Canaria

visits.text: The Cathedral of Santa Ana has dominated Vegueta since the 16th century. Four centuries of construction blend late Gothic, Renaissance, and Neoclassical styles. Its three naves of equal height—called "hall church" style—rise on slender fluted columns whose ribs burst into palm-shaped vaults.

Step through the door of the Casa de Colón, just behind the cathedral. Built on the exact site where the city was founded in 1478, its green stone façade opens onto colonial courtyards. The museum traces the connections between the Canaries and the Americas.

The Museo Canario, founded in 1879, is the archipelago's most important archaeological museum. Its pre-Hispanic collections evoke the culture of the Guanches—the islands' first inhabitants—through ceramics, mummies, and idols.

Stroll down Calle Triana, the long pedestrian street that concentrates every architectural style the city has known: Modernist façades from 1900–1910, Neoclassical buildings, Neo-Baroque and Art Nouveau in succession.

Playa de Las Canteras stretches three kilometers through the heart of the city. A natural rocky barrier protects swimmers and creates a turquoise lagoon sheltering a marine reserve. Blue Flag beach since 1989.
The Teatro Pérez Galdós, Italian-inspired, has been the city's cultural emblem for over a century. Flanked by a contemporary extension, it programs opera, concerts, and dance year-round.

activities.text: Grab your mask and snorkel at Las Canteras: waters protected by the rocky barrier shelter a wide variety of fish in a protected natural area, accessible right from the sand.

The Mercado de Vegueta, in operation since 1854, comes alive each morning with stalls of spectacular tropical fruits, fresh fish, and Canarian cheeses. On Sundays, a craft market sets up in Plaza del Pilar Nuevo.
Stay on the Las Canteras promenade for sunset. The Auditorio Alfredo Kraus closes the perspective at the end of the beach, its silhouette cut against the Atlantic horizon.

days_plan.itin_title: Canary Islands Road Trip: Tenerife & Gran Canaria

map_keywords: Catedral de Santa Ana Las Palmas
Playa de Las Canteras Las Palmas

visits.text: The Las Tuerces labyrinth resembles a ghost city sculpted by water: for millions of years, karst erosion on Upper Cretaceous limestone has created a tangle of giant stone mushrooms, natural arches, blind corridors and shaded galleries. You feel like you're on another planet—with free access and no queues. The site is accessed from the village of Villaescusa de las Torres. Set off without a fixed route, explore paths at random.

activities.text: At the labyrinth's foot, the Cañón de la Horadada is a gorge carved by the Pisuerga River with vertical walls over 40 meters. The trail from Mave descends into the gorge along the old greenway, passes the former hydroelectric plant and reaches a rock wall allowing you to climb back up for the overview. The combination Las Tuerces + Cañón de la Horadada in a loop from Aguilar de Campoo makes 9.5 km and about 2h30. Site included in the UNESCO Global Geopark 'Las Loras'.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

places.name: Ledesma

visits.text: Ledesma is built on a rocky spur between two meanders of the Tormes—a naturally defensive position inhabited since prehistoric times. The Romans called it Bletisama in honor of Consul Bleto (93 AD). Medieval repopulation dates to 1161, when Fernando II raised the walls and fortress. In 1462, Enrique IV handed lordship of Ledesma to Don Beltrán de la Cueva, his favorite, who made it a city of splendor in the 15th century. Classified as a historic site in 1975, Ledesma preserves fragments of Roman and medieval wall.

Ledesma's castle, partially demolished, has kept what matters: its entrance gate with a Gothic arch of keystones founded directly in bedrock, flanked by a semicircular cube and a polygonal one. It's the most eloquent entrance to the walled enclosure. Inside, the lanes lead to the Gothic church of Santa María la Mayor and the Casa de las Almenas—a 15th-century palace with cloister and interior garden, one of the province's finest noble residences.

The Mocho Bridge, of Roman origin, spans the Tormes at the foot of the cliff on which the town sits. The forest of centuries-old oaks bordering the road to the bridge is a remarkable habitat where you can spot griffon vultures and black kites in flight. A plaque recalls that Mesta flocks crossed this passage for centuries, paying portazgo (toll) to the town's lord.

activities.text: The Mocho Bridge route, marked from the town, crosses a forest of oaks centuries old to reach the Tormes banks. Birdwatchers will easily spot black storks, kites, and vultures from the heights of the riverbank. Allow 1½ hours round trip.

Ledesma is renowned for its thermal mud baths—the aguas termales have been known since Roman times. Wellness establishments still exploit the springs. If you have fatigue from the miles in your legs, it's a welcome break.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

map_keywords: Église Santa María la Mayor Ledesma

visits.text: León Cathedral, called Pulchra Leonina ("the Beautiful Leonese") in Latin, has 1,800 m² of stained glass - one of the world's largest glass surfaces, alongside Chartres and Sainte-Chapelle. Three rose windows and 134 bays from the 13th to 20th centuries, some depicting medieval peasant scenes: harvest, grape picking, boar hunting. The structure is so fragile it was dismantled stone by stone then reassembled in the 19th century to save it from collapse. Entry €7, audioguide included. Best in mid-morning for light on the south windows.

The Royal Pantheon of the Basilica of San Isidoro is called the "Sistine Chapel of Romanesque art" - and it's no exaggeration. Vault entirely painted in early 12th century, Apocalypse scenes, agricultural calendar, Last Supper. Exceptional preservation because the paintings were never covered or restored before the 20th century. Twenty-three kings and queens of León rest here, including Ferdinand I the Great, founder in 1063. Combined entry basilica + pantheon + museum €6.

The chalice of Doña Urraca, preserved in the San Isidoro museum, caused a scandal in 2014: two historians, María Margarita Torres and José Manuel Ortega del Río, published a book claiming it was the Holy Grail, the cup of the Last Supper, brought from Egypt in the 11th century. Agate cup set in gold, offered to the monastery by Queen Urraca of León around 1100. Thesis contested by most medievalists, who date the agates to roughly the Roman period without confirming their provenance. True or false Grail, the piece is beautiful, and the museum is rich beyond: 300 codices, carved ivories, cloisonnés.

Casa Botines, steps away, is one of Antoni Gaudí's three works outside Catalonia - built in 1893 for two textile merchants. Neo-Gothic façade with corner turrets, statue of Saint George above the door. When workers attempted to demolish it in 1955, they found Gaudí's original plans in a bottle sealed by himself inside Saint George's statue - true story. Today a museum, entry €5, allow 45 minutes.

The Convent of San Marcos, converted into a 5-star Parador in 1965, stretches its Plateresque façade 100 meters - one of the longest in Spanish Renaissance, carved like a picture book. Built from 1513 as a hospital for Camino pilgrims and headquarters of the military Order of Santiago. The interior cloister still houses the Provincial Archaeological Museum: Carrizo crucifix in ivory (11th century), iconic piece of Iberian Romanesque art. Free Parador entry, museum €1.

MUSAC, Museum of Contemporary Art of Castile and León, is an exploded cube of 37 glass panels in the 37 colors of the cathedral's stained glass - avant-garde building by Mansilla and Tuñón that won the Mies van der Rohe prize in 2007. Over 1,650 works, emphasis on Spanish art from the 1990s to today. Architecture alone worth the detour if contemporary art doesn't attract you. Entry €5, free Sunday afternoons.

Castrillo de los Polvazares, 50 km west, is the village of the arrieros - muleteers who transported goods and news between the coast and Madrid before the railroad. Reddish stone houses, streets paved with round pebbles, 70 inhabitants. Its car-free main street, 250 meters long, has served as a film set for several movies. Unique specialty: cocido maragato, dish served backwards, meats first, soup at the end. To taste at Mesón Casa Coscolo (€28), reservation required.

activities.text: Tapas tour in the Barrio Húmedo, the "wet quarter" - labyrinth of lanes around Plaza San Martín, nicknamed thus because of the dozens of taverns that make it perspire every evening. Leonese specialty: the tapa is free with each drink, sometimes substantial (piece of cecina, croquette, tortilla). Three or four glasses and you've had dinner. Reliable addresses: Bar La Bicha (house cecina), El Rebote (tortilla), El Tizón (cod croquettes). Figure €12-18 for a real tour.

If you're here in July, don't miss the free concerts on Plaza San Marcelo as part of the early music festival. Otherwise, end the evening on Plaza de Regla, in front of the illuminated cathedral - the backlighting makes the stained glass shine from outside, reverse effect seen nowhere else. Good spot for a mojito with cathedral view: Cafetería La Catedral, terrace at number 5.

Excursion to Valporquero Caves, 47 km north, in the Cantabrian Range natural park - 3 km karst system of which 1.3 km open to the public. Stalactites, underground lakes, Hall of the Fairies with 30-meter-high vault. Temperature constant 7°C: bring a coat even in July. Guided tour 1 hour, departure every 30 minutes, €11. Closed December-February, zigzag access, last kilometer narrow.

Gourmet evening in Castrillo or return to León for pulpo a la feria - Galician-style octopus, present everywhere since the province is adjacent - at Mesón Jabugo in Barrio Romántico. Single dish €16, wash down with a glass of Bierzo, local wine AOC on the rise made from mencía grape. El Bierzo, 30 km west of León, now produces reds that rival those of Ribera, at still reasonable prices.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León Road Trip: Cathedrals and Medieval Villages
The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province
The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province
The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province
Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Castile and León - 14-Day Grand Tour
Castile and León - 14-Day Grand Tour

visits.text: Lerma appears in the distance like an ocean liner perched on a hill. The Palacio Ducal dominates the Plaza Ducal—one of Spain's largest squares at 6,862 m². This 17th-century palace, built between 1601 and 1617 by architect Francisco de Mora, is the work of the Duke of Lerma, favorite of Philip III and the most powerful man in Spain at the time. The duke, so influential he dared add four towers to his palace (a privilege normally reserved for kings), made Lerma a parallel court with six monasteries, a collegiate church, and secret passages connecting everything. Its austere façade has 210 balconies and 35 windows. You can visit the cloister and covered inner courtyard.

Take the Pasadizo del Duque, the covered passage the duke built to attend Mass without stepping into the street. It connected the palace to the Collegiate Church of San Pedro (early 17th century), an austere temple whose interior preserves a gilded bronze praying statue of Archbishop Cristóbal de Rojas, the duke's uncle. From the Mirador de los Arcos at the end of the passage, the view plunges over the vega of the Arlanza—the agricultural plain stretching as far as the eye can see.

Cross through the Arco de la Cárcel, the only preserved medieval gate from the old walls. It now houses the headquarters of the DO Arlanza regulatory council. Around the square, visit the convents: San Blas (where Dominican nuns sell hand-painted ceramics), La Ascensión (where the infanta Margarita, daughter of Philip III, was baptized), Santa Teresa (now city hall and interpretation center). Lerma had six monasteries in the 17th century—a village of 600 inhabitants surrounded by convents, that's Habsburg Castile.

activities.text: Use the afternoon for an excursion to Santo Domingo de Silos, 35 km southeast of Lerma. The Benedictine monastery, founded in the 10th century, houses an 11th-century Romanesque cloister considered one of Europe's finest. The carved capitals and corner pillar bas-reliefs are exceptionally refined. The monks still chant Gregorian chant at services.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

visits.text: No tourists, no pretence: Letur's casco árabe was declared a Historic-Artistic Ensemble in 1983, and it's the best-preserved medieval layout of Moorish origin in Albacete province. Labyrinthine callejuelas coil around a white stone outcrop at 740 metres altitude. The Arco de las Moreras, in natural stone, marks the entrance to the old walled enclosure—it's both a triumphal arch and a geological phenomenon. Free stroll.

Water springs from everywhere in Letur: fountains, irrigation channels, the río Segura below. The Charco de las Canales, a natural rock pool in the heart of the casco histórico, is one of the best-known in the Sierra Segura. Check it's open before coming (reopening varies depending on the riverbed condition after floods). In summer, arrive early to find a spot.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: Liétor literally hangs in balance: the village is perched about 100 metres above the río Mundo canyon, houses jutting right onto the rock, like an extension of the cliff. Classified casco histórico, narrow streets of Islamic origin, coats-of-arms façades. The miradores of the barrio morisco (Calle Portillo) offer a view of the río Mundo that few places in Spain can match.

The Ermita de Nuestra Señora de Belén is worth the climb: it's one of the most memorable scenes in the film 'Amanece que no es poco' (1989) by director José Luis Cuerda, shot here. Inside, a curiosity: everything is painted in trompe-l'oeil, from the altarpieces to the curtains. If it's closed, the frescoes are visible through the grille.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

places.name: Linares

visits.text: Visit the Archaeological Museum of Linares, housed in the former 17th-century Casa del Torreón Palace. Discover a fascinating collection of artifacts from the Cástulo excavations, highlighting the famous Ibero-Roman sandstone lion originally from the northern wall of the ancient city.

Explore the Hospital of the Marquises of Linares, an impressive neo-Gothic building constructed between 1904 and 1917 commissioned by José de Murga and Raimunda de Osorio. Admire its Victorian architecture with gargoyles on the entrance columns and visit the Marquises' crypt with life-size sculptures.

Discover El Pósito, home to the Raphael Museum, one of the main cultural attractions in the singer's hometown. Admire his countless awards, records, and film costumes, including a very rare 1980 uranium disc that only artists like Michael Jackson or Queen have achieved.

Contemplate the Church of Santa María la Mayor, the oldest building in Linares constructed over a mosque in the 13th century. In Gothic-Renaissance style with participation from architect Andrés de Vandelvira, it was declared a basilica by Pope Francis in 2016.

activities.text: Stroll along the Paseo de Linarejos, a pleasant boulevard among green areas where locals enjoy tapas and gather. It begins at the white marble Constitution Fountain and culminates with the statue of guitarist Andrés Segovia, one of the city's most illustrious personalities.

Visit the Andrés Segovia House Museum in the 17th-century Orozco Palace. View the guitars that belonged to the maestro, his recognition medals, and the crypt where his remains rest. In November, it hosts the International Guitar Encounter.

days_plan.itin_title: Jaén's Renaissance Jewels: Olive Grove Landscapes & Wild Sierras of Andalusia

map_keywords: Hospital of the Marquis of Linares

visits.text: The historic center of Llanes preserves about 300 meters of 13th-century wall. The Torreón, a five-story circular crenelated tower, served as a watchtower and defense against seaborne invasions. It adjoins the Casino, an elegant 'Indiano' building from 1910.

The Basilica of Santa María del Conceyu, begun in the 13th century, is a fine example of the Romanesque-Gothic transition. Its claim to fame: it was funded by a tax paid by all inhabitants, making it a people's church rather than a clergy church.

Walk down to the fishing harbor to behold the Cubos de la Memoria. More than 200 concrete blocks on the breakwater were painted by Basque artist Ibarrola between 2001 and 2006: abstract figures, ancestral symbols, and vivid colors that shift with the light and tides.
Toró beach, 500 meters from the center, is distinguished by its erosion-sculpted rocks that rise from the sand like sentinels. At low tide, natural pools form between the boulders.
Head to Barro beach, accessible by a short road from Llanes. More sheltered than Toró, it offers a long stretch of fine sand framed by green meadows sloping down to the water.

activities.text: Walk the Paseo de San Pedro, a coastal path built along the cliffs east of the harbor. This trail overlooks the sea and offers panoramas of Sablón beach, the rocks of Toró, and the Sierra del Cuera in the background.
The municipality of Llanes boasts more than thirty beaches. Sample a smoked Gamoneu or a cave-aged Cabrales accompanied by cider in a harbor bar—mountain cheeses are the local pride.
Stay until sunset from Punta de Toró or the Llanes lighthouse (1860) at the end of the San Antón promenade. Ibarrola's colored cubes catch fire in the low-angle light.

days_plan.itin_title: Asturias Road Trip: Cantabrian Coast and Picos de Europa

places.name: Lleida

visits.text: The Seu Vella crowns the hill that defines Lleida's skyline—you can make it out from the Pyrenees on clear days. Begun in 1203 by Pere de Coma on the remains of a mosque, consecrated in 1278, and completed in 1431 with its bell tower—more than two centuries of construction. It's considered Europe's last great Romanesque cathedral, already showing Gothic elements. Then in 1707, Philip V closed it for worship and turned it into a military barracks for 240 years. Now restored to heritage status, it opens for visits including its giant cloister—one of Europe's largest—and the Gothic bell tower. 238 spiral steps for the view.

Atop the Turó, just above the cathedral, the Castell del Rei—la Suda—overlooks what remains of a Muslim castle documented since 882. Palace of the kings of Aragon from the 12th to 16th centuries, destroyed during the 18th-century wars and finished off by an explosion during the Napoleonic wars. Less than a quarter survives, but the interpretation center housed in the royal hall tells the story of how Lleida shifted from al-Andalus to the Crown of Aragon. A hillside elevator makes the site accessible to those with reduced mobility—rare for this type of monument.

Below, the Catedral Nova on Carrer Major was built between the 18th and 19th centuries to replace the confiscated Seu Vella. Neoclassical style, more sober façade. Inside, the Virgin of Blau bears a mark on her forehead: legend says a sculptor jealous of his apprentice's work tried to smash the statue with a hammer; the hammer bounced back and killed him, leaving the Virgin with that bluish bruise that gives her name. The first name Blau, still common in Lleida, comes from this.

The Palau de la Paeria, on the plaza of the same name, serves as Lleida's town hall—and ranks among Catalonia's finest Gothic civic buildings. Built in the early 13th century by the lords of Sanaüja, donated to the city in 1383 to serve as municipal headquarters, the building has two façades: civil Romanesque facing the plaza, neoclassical facing the Segre. The archives preserve the Charta del Poble, one of the earliest known urban constitutions, dated to the 12th century. Free weekday visits.

One last detour before the break: the Dipòsit del Pla de l'Aigua, nicknamed the "water cathedral." Built in the late 18th century, it's simply the city's first drinking water reservoir—25 stone pillars, 6 barrel vaults, 9-million-liter capacity. Closed then forgotten for decades, it now opens for visits. Don't miss the acoustics: clap beneath the vault, the sound returns eight times.

activities.text: Walk along the Segre at sunset. The river cuts through the city and the banks have been developed—bike paths, picnic areas, even kayak and canoe launches. From here you get the finest view of the Seu Vella lit up after nightfall. Locals come for their evening jog, giving a strangely provincial feel to a city of 140,000. Sample caracoles a la llauna or xató at an old-town tavern to finish.

Sunday's market, on Plaça Sant Joan and all around, is one of inland Catalonia's most authentic. Urgell producers, Pyrenean cheeses, arbequina oil, mountain honey, coca de recapte (Catalan topped flatbread). It's also a chance to hear western Catalan, slightly different from Barcelona's. Starts early—by 9 AM it's already packed.

days_plan.itin_title: Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour
Catalan Pyrenees and Romanesque Art in 8 Days: Lleida, Pallars, Vall de Boí and Val d'Aran

map_keywords: Dipòsit del Pla de l'Aigua Lleida

places.name: Logrono

visits.text: Logroño's Calle Laurel—locals call it 'la Senda de los Elefantes' (the Elephant Trail)—crams over 60 bars into barely 200 meters. Not because they can intoxicate elephants, but because a full bar crawl feels like this: Bar Soriano, founded in 1972, has served only one thing for 50 years—a grilled mushroom, a shrimp, a secret garlic-and-oil sauce. €1.50 for the 'champi.' Bar Blanco y Negro, the oldest on the street (late 19th century), serves the 'matrimonio'—a warm roll with a white anchovy and a black anchovy side by side. Bar Cid for grilled mushrooms with mystery sauce. The philosophy of the single-item bar: if you want variety, change bars.

The Cathedral of Santa María de la Redonda—two twin Baroque towers from the 18th century dominating downtown Logroño. In the ambulatory behind the high altar hides a small Crucifixion canvas attributed to Michelangelo Buonarroti: painted around 1540 for his friend Vittoria Colonna, Roman poetess, before being acquired by the Bishop of Calahorra. Most art historians lean toward a school copy, but the story attached remains beautiful. It's also one of the key stages of the Camino de Santiago Francés—you'll cross paths with pilgrims bearing shells and staffs, faces sunburnt and shoes dusty, gazing at the city as if it were paradise.

The Paseo del Espolón, the promenade along the Ebro riverbanks, is where Logroño families gather at all hours. Centuries-old plane trees, sidewalk cafes, a music kiosk. Here you realize that Logroño isn't a 'tourist city'—it's simply a Spanish city that lives.

Briones—ranked among Spain's most beautiful villages since 2018—perches on a rocky promontory above a spectacular Ebro meander. You enter through medieval gates in stone ramparts, lose yourself in cobbled alleys lined with manor houses bearing carved coats of arms. The Gothic church of Nuestra Señora de la Asunción offers a 360° panorama of Rioja Alta vineyards from its bell tower.

The Museo Vivanco de la Cultura del Vino in Briones is one of the world's most comprehensive wine museums—that's a promise, not hyperbole. 4,000 m², five permanent galleries: an Anatolian jug from 3,000 BC, the world's largest Roman wine press (occupying nearly an entire room), original canvases by Picasso, Miró, Sorolla, and Warhol on wine themes, a mind-boggling collection of corkscrews from around the world. Founded in 2004 by Pedro Vivanco, passionate patron. Outside, the Garden of Bacchus gathers 220 vine varieties from across the globe.

San Vicente de la Sonsierra, 5 km from Briones via the LR-210: a hilltop village crowned by a 12th-century medieval castle, below which an old bridge crosses the Ebro on nine arches of blond sandstone dating from the 13th century—remodeled in the 16th. Over 200 meters long, it's linked the two wine-growing banks for eight centuries. Less spectacular than a highway viaduct, but far more honest.

activities.text: Visit Bodegas Ontañón on the outskirts of Logroño, owned by the Pérez Cuevas family—art patrons whose tasting room is decorated with stained glass and sculptures commissioned from contemporary artists. Excellent introduction to modern Rioja Reserva style. Tour plus 4-wine tasting, around €25.

Wine-by-the-glass tasting at Tastavin, the reference wine bar on Calle Laurel, offering a curated selection of Rioja wines by the glass—a chance to compare a Crianza, Reserva, and Gran Reserva side by side without driving.

Final tasting at Bodegas Sierra Cantabria in San Vicente de la Sonsierra—owned by the Eguren family, which also produces the Viñedo de San Vicente cuvées and Teso La Monja (Toro). Buy a few bottles to take home. The Sierra Cantabria Reserva is a classic of the genre—unbeatable quality-price ratio.
Final evening on Calle Laurel in Logroño. One last bar crawl, more relaxed now that you know what you're drinking and where it comes from. Bar Soriano for the farewell 'champi.'

days_plan.itin_title: La Rioja Circuit: Wine Route and Monasteries
La Rioja in 7 Days - The Kingdom of Tempranillo
La Rioja in 7 Days - The Kingdom of Tempranillo
Rioja in 7 Days: The Kingdom of Tempranillo Between Cathedral-Bodegas and Star Architecture
Rioja in 7 Days: The Kingdom of Tempranillo Between Cathedral-Bodegas and Star Architecture

map_keywords: Santa María de la Redonda Cathedral Logroño
San Vicente de la Sonsierra medieval bridge Ebro

visits.text: From the terrace of the Fortaleza del Sol, the entire Guadalentín valley spreads out 640 m below. This 52,000 m² fortress (640 m long, one of Spain's largest) was the Christian outpost facing the Nasrid kingdom of Granada for over 250 years after the conquest of 1244. The Alfonsine Tower, commissioned by Alfonso X the Wise, and the Torre del Espolón dominate the complex. The medieval judería—unique in Murcia—was discovered during excavations when the neighboring Parador was built.

Descending from the castle, Baroque Lorca takes over: the church of Santiago, Palacio de Guevara, and a Semana Santa declared Festival of International Tourist Interest for its embroideries and processions rivaling Seville. The Teatro Guerra (1861), Murcia's oldest theater still in operation, deserves a look.

activities.text: Go down into the castle's judería: excavations uncovered 12 houses and a 14th-15th century synagogue, the only one ever found in the Region of Murcia. Access to the synagogue is included in the fortress ticket (around €6). Theatrical evening tours (available seasonally) recreate medieval life in a very vivid way.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Road Trip: Baroque Splendor & Costa Cálida
Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca

places.name: Los Llanos De Aridane

visits.text: Los Llanos de Aridane is La Palma's most populous town, set in the green Aridane valley on the western slope—a microclimate warmer and sunnier than the capital's. The historic center houses the Museum of the Street, a permanent outdoor contemporary art exhibition unique in Europe: since 1999, murals painted directly on the city's blank facades by recognized artists cover up to 140 m² of walls. Wander the alleys to find them.

The Benahoarita Archaeological Museum traces the life of La Palma's prehistoric inhabitants, the Benahoaritas—a people of Berber origin who arrived on the island before the Spanish conquest. Reconstructions of their habitat, beliefs, and crafts help understand how they lived in this volcanic environment before being expelled in the 15th century.

Tajogaite volcano—also called Cumbre Vieja—erupted on September 19, 2021 and flowed for 85 days until December. The flow covered 1,218 hectares, destroyed 1,345 houses and 73 km of roads, and expanded the island by 47 hectares into the sea. From Los Llanos, Mirador de Tajuya offers the best view of the entire lava field and the new volcanic cone. Guided 5 km trails allow you to approach the crater's base from Llano del Jable.

Fuencaliente, at La Palma's southern tip, is the territory of the island's most recent volcanoes. San Antonio volcano (430 m) erupted in 1677 and its crater is freely accessible with an interpretation center tracing Canarian volcanic history. Just beside it, Teneguía spewed lava as recently as October 1971—less than 55 years ago. You can walk on its still-warm flanks and observe vegetation slowly recolonizing the lava. Don't miss Fuencaliente lighthouse at the cape's end: from the point, you have a panorama of both island coasts simultaneously.

Fuencaliente's vineyards merit a stop. As in Lanzarote, vines grow here in volcanic soils on terraces—but the landscapes are even more dramatic because Teneguía's lava fields descend right to the vineyards. The La Palma appellation produces volcanic malvasía and red wines from listán negro whose reputation far exceeds the island.

If weather permits, climb to Roque de los Muchachos (2,426 m), the island's highest point. The road winds through Canarian pine forests and alpine zones to the summit, where domes of the astrophysical observatory stand—one of the world's most advanced observation centers, benefiting from the island's low light pollution. On clear days, you can spot Tenerife's Teide from the summit.

activities.text: Hike the Route of the Volcanoes (Cumbre Vieja) from the El Pilar refuge. This GR-131 trail follows the volcanic ridge where all the island's historic eruptions occurred—1949 (Hoyo Negro), 1971 (Teneguía), and 2021 (Tajogaite). The panorama from the ridges embraces the entire Aridane valley on one side and the Atlantic on the other. Start early: wind can be fierce and heat intense on this shadeless terrain.

Relaxation at Puerto de Tazacorte, a colorful fishing port 10 minutes from Los Llanos. Waterfront restaurants serve the day's catch—grouper, vieja (parrotfish), and local prawns. The sunset from the terrace facing the Atlantic is one of La Palma's finest. Boat trips to observe dolphins and whales depart from this port.

Tasting at Casa Museo del Vino Las Manchas, which traces La Palma's winemaking history and offers local wine tastings. The island produces malvasía, listán negro, and several Canarian grape varieties that exist nowhere else.

Swimming in the natural pools of La Fajana or Charco Verde, on La Palma's west coast. These seawater basins carved into black volcanic rock are the island's most photogenic swimming spots—the contrast between lava black, water blue, and foam white is striking. Check weather before departing, as Atlantic swells can close certain pools.

Market or end-of-trip stroll in Los Llanos de Aridane. The town is also the finish line of the Transvulcania, one of the world's best mountain races. The lively pedestrian center and cafe terraces offer a final immersion in local life before heading to the airport.

Closing lunch with La Palma specialties: potaje de berros (watercress soup), pork in mojo sauce, and bienmesabe for dessert. The island is renowned for exceptional agricultural products—avocados, bananas, and palmero PDO cheese that you can bring home vacuum-sealed.

days_plan.itin_title: Lanzarote & La Palma: Where Fire Shaped Art and Nature

visits.text: Lucena is one of Spain's great Sephardic unknowns: a majority-Jewish city between the 9th and 12th centuries under Al-Andalus, nicknamed Eliossana—'God has saved us.' Capital of Andalusian Judaism for 300 years, renowned Talmudic school. Its population fled before the Almohad invasions of 1146-1148, which forced conversion or death. The Castillo del Moral, where Boabdil was reportedly imprisoned after his defeat at Lucena in 1483, is now the municipal museum—the episode is minor in the Reconquista timeline but narrated locally as a great feat.

Lucena's Jewish necropolis, discovered in 2006 following roadworks, is the largest known on the Iberian Peninsula: 346 tombs, dated from the 9th to 11th centuries. Inscriptions in Hebrew and Arabic—proof of a linguistic coexistence unimaginable today. Site partially visible behind a fence at the city's northern exit, free access. Modest but moving.

activities.text: Coffee-tapas break at La Casa del Vino, on Plaza Nueva. Montilla-Moriles wines (neighboring DOC), regional specialty of draft fino. Tapas of salmorejo and Iberian salchichón. Good spot to catch your breath before hitting the road to Granada.

days_plan.itin_title: Dark Andalusia: 10 Days from Seville to Málaga

visits.text: Lugo, 2,000-year-old city. Founded in 13 BCE under the name Lucus Augusti—"sacred grove of Augustus," from the Celtic deity Lugh to whom a sacred grove on site was dedicated. And the city boils down to one absolutely unique thing worldwide: its Roman wall. 2,117 meters circumference, 71 towers preserved out of 85 original, 10 gates (5 Roman, 5 pierced later). Built between 260 and year 320 under Constantine I. It's the only Roman wall in Europe to have preserved its entire perimeter intact, which earned it UNESCO listing in 2000. The walkway, 4 meters wide, traversable on foot or bike—free, open 24/7.

Inside the walls, the Cathedral of Santa María. Construction begun in 1129 on Alfonso VII's order, completed century after century in all styles: Romanesque nave, Gothic ambulatory, baroque west façade, neoclassical cloister. The standout piece: the Chapel of the Virgin of the Great Eyes (Virxe dos Ollos Grandes), spectacular Galician baroque. And a unique privilege in Spain: Lugo Cathedral has exposed the Holy Sacrament perpetually since the 14th century, following a Eucharistic miracle. It's marked in the city's coat of arms—a chalice with host.

Under a pedestrian center street, the House of Mosaics is a recently opened archaeological site (2011): remains of a 3rd-century Roman domus, with mosaics in situ. You see them through glass flooring. Rooms preserve their configuration: tablinum, oecus, peristylium. Digital reconstruction on screens. Small, surprising, free, never crowded. Five minutes away Plaza do Campo, former Roman forum turned medieval market, today heart of tapas evenings.

Outside the walls, 300 meters south, ancient Roman baths are included in the Balneario de Lugo hotel building—ferro-bicarbonate water at 43°C gushing continuously since antiquity.
