The 1st-century changing room and cold bath hall are preserved. Possibility to take a bath in the balneario (count €25 entry, it's a real spa). The Old Bridge, spanning the Miño right beside it, is also Roman origin—rebuilt in the 12th century.

activities.text: Gastronomic tour. The official slogan "To eat, Lugo" is no boast: the city has exceptional density of starred or recommended restaurants. Local specialties: lacón con grelos (ham hock with turnip greens), pulpo a feira, tarta de Mondoñedo, Galician churrasco, tetilla cheese PDO. Don't miss a caña of Estrella Galicia—regional beer brewed in Coruña—with a slice of empanada. Reliable addresses: Mesón Alberto, Paprica, Don Mansito.

If you catch Arde Lucus (weekend closest to summer solstice, mid-June), it's the event not to miss. For three days, the city transforms into ancient Rome: 600 costumed extras, legionaries, senators, slaves, gladiators, Roman weddings, banquets, reconstructed battles in the forum. Over 300,000 visitors in three days. National tourist interest festival since 2007.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Galicia: Complete 10-Day Circuit

visits.text: Begin your Madrid stay along the Paseo del Arte, Europe's most concentrated museum triangle. The Prado Museum, founded in 1819 in a neoclassical building by Juan de Villanueva, houses over 8,000 paintings—only a fraction of which are on display. Velázquez's Las Meninas (1656), Hieronymus Bosch's Garden of Earthly Delights (circa 1500), Rubens's The Three Graces, and Goya's Black Paintings await you in the main galleries. Allow at least 2 hours for the major works, longer if you're a painting enthusiast. Arrive at opening time to avoid the queues—they lengthen quickly after 11am.

A ten-minute walk south along the Paseo del Prado brings you to the Reina Sofía Museum, housed in the former 18th-century San Carlos Hospital. The museum opened in 1992 and covers Spanish art from 1881 onwards. The absolute star is Picasso's Guernica (1937)—7.76 metres wide, painted in response to the bombing of the Basque village by the German Condor Legion during the Civil War. The room is always crowded, but the canvas is so immense you can see it well from anywhere. Don't miss Dalí's The Great Masturbator and Miró's canvases on the upper floors.

For a breather between museums, cross the street to Parque del Retiro—125 hectares of greenery right in central Madrid. The park was created in the 17th century as the garden of the Buen Retiro Palace, residence of Philip IV. Rent a rowing boat on the Estanque Grande, facing the Alfonso XII monument, then head to the Crystal Palace (1887), a glass-and-iron greenhouse inspired by London's Crystal Palace, which hosts contemporary art exhibitions from the Reina Sofía. Entry is free.

Devote the morning to the Royal Palace, official residence of the Spanish monarchs—though the royal family no longer lives there. Built between 1738 and 1764 on the site of the former Habsburg Alcázar destroyed by fire, it's the largest palace in Western Europe by floor area: 135,000 m² and 3,418 rooms. You'll visit the state rooms—the Throne Room with its Tiepolo frescoes, the Gala Dining Room with its 140-seat table, and the Royal Armoury, one of the world's most complete collections with armour belonging to Charles V and Philip II. Allow 1.5 to 2 hours.

Leaving the palace, cross Plaza de la Armería to the Almudena Cathedral. Begun in 1883, it wasn't completed and consecrated until 1993 by Pope John Paul II—over a century of construction. The interior is surprisingly modern, with brightly painted ceilings that contrast sharply with the sobriety of the neoclassical façade. Climb to the dome for views over the palace and Casa de Campo.

Walk down to Plaza Mayor via Calle Mayor. This arcaded rectangular square, completed in 1619 under Philip III, was the scene of Inquisition autos-da-fé, bullfights, and public executions. Today, the terraces beneath the porticos are tourist traps—just admire the architecture and the equestrian statue of Philip III at the centre, then head to Mercado de San Miguel, just next door. This covered market from 1916, with its iron-and-glass structure, has become a gourmet temple: oysters, jamón ibérico, churros, vermouth, and creative tapas.

Head up to Gran Vía, Madrid's main commercial artery, cut through between 1910 and 1929. The Edificio Metrópolis (1911), at the corner of Calle de Alcalá, with its gilded dome and winged Victory, is the city's postcard image. Continue west to the Templo de Debod, an authentic Egyptian temple from the 2nd century BC, gifted by Egypt to Spain in 1968 in thanks for its help saving the Nubian temples threatened by the Aswan Dam. At sunset, the temple's reflections in the pool with Casa de Campo in the background offer one of Madrid's most beautiful views.

activities.text: In the late afternoon, head to Plaza de Santa Ana for a tapas crawl through the Las Letras neighbourhood—the literary quarter where Cervantes, Lope de Vega, and Quevedo lived in the 17th century. The tapas bars around the square and along Calle de las Huertas serve generous portions of tortilla, croquetas, and jamón ibérico. Wash it down with a glass of Ribera del Duero or a vermut de grifo (vermouth on tap), Madrid's traditional aperitif drink.

If you're in Madrid on a Sunday morning, don't miss El Rastro, Spain's largest flea market. It spreads out every Sunday from 9am to 3pm around Calle de la Ribera de Curtidores, in the La Latina neighbourhood. Antiques, vintage clothing, books, vinyl, curiosities—it's a joyful bazaar that attracts Madrileños as much as visitors. After the market, the tapas bars of La Latina are mobbed—it's Madrid's Sunday ritual.

In the evening, catch a flamenco show. Madrid isn't Andalusia, but the city has a historic flamenco scene—many Andalusian artists have settled here since the 19th century. Tablaos in the La Latina and Lavapiés neighbourhoods offer intimate performances with singing, guitar, and dance. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours for a show.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
Madrid & Castile Road Trip: Royal Palaces, Ancient Walls & World-Class Museums
Mystical Castile: Toledo, Ávila, Salamanca & Burgos in 11 Days

visits.text: From the torre del homenaje of Magacela Castle, the whole of La Serena spreads out beneath you. The panorama covers the sierras of Montánchez, Orellana, and Santa Cruz. The rusted iron staircase leading to the top (9 m) isn't for the faint-hearted, but the reward is worth the vertigo. Free access.

The church of Santa Ana, inside the castle, burned down and now bears only its scars. The open-air nave, the abandoned cemetery with its dried flowers, and the blackened walls compose a striking tableau. The 15th-century baptismal font was moved to the new parish church down in the village.

The dolmen of Magacela, dating from the 3rd millennium BC, lies below the village, towards La Coronada. Schematic engravings still visible on the orthostats. Accessible on foot in ten minutes from the castle car park. Free and no fixed hours.

activities.text: Climb up to the castle through the steep alleyways of the village: the stone houses huddle against the slope as if afraid of sliding. The Arabic name of the place, Umm Gazala ("Great Mother"), speaks to the antiquity of occupation. Bring good shoes, the paving is uneven.

days_plan.itin_title: La Siberia and La Serena: Secret Extremadura in 6 Days

places.name: Mahon

visits.text: Mahón's harbor, the world's second-largest natural port, carves six kilometers inland from its narrow entrance to the city waterfront—a stunning fjord-like passage.
The Museum of Menorca, housed in a former Franciscan monastery, traces the island's remarkable journey from prehistoric talayotic culture through British occupation.
Santa Maria Church showcases a spectacular 18th-century organ with over 3,000 pipes. The austere architecture contrasts beautifully with the ornate baroque altarpiece.
Xoriguer Distillery has been crafting Mahón gin since 1736 using the same recipe. Ancient copper stills, heated over wood fires for 250 years, still produce this legendary spirit.
Mercat del Claustre del Carme occupies a beautifully preserved 17th-century convent cloister. Hunt for authentic Mahón cheese, fresh seafood, and local specialties among the atmospheric stalls.
La Mola Fortress, a 19th-century military complex, towers 90 meters above the harbor mouth. The ramparts offer breathtaking panoramic views across the Mediterranean.

activities.text: Hour-long boat tours navigate around King's Island, Quarantine Island, and Lazaretto Island, revealing hidden history and stunning coastal perspectives.
Free tastings at Xoriguer Distillery let you sample artisanal gin and local liqueurs. Don't miss the Pommada cocktail—gin mixed with fresh lemonade, utterly refreshing.
Harborside terraces serve exceptional caldereta de langosta (lobster stew), grilled octopus, and fresh seafood paired with crisp local wines.

days_plan.itin_title: Menorca Road Trip: Harbors, Whitewashed Villages & Hidden Coves

map_keywords: Mercat del Claustre del Carme Mahón

visits.text: The San Rafael Cemetery, 4 km north of downtown Málaga, holds the most brutal Civil War memory on the coast. Between 1937 and 1957, more than 4,000 Republicans were executed and thrown into mass graves here—magistrates, teachers, trade unionists, militants, simple sympathizers. Franco's regime used Málaga, taken in February 1937, as a targeted extermination camp. Exhumations began in 2006, recovering 2,840 bodies. Memorial inaugurated in 2009, with identified names engraved on black granite steles arranged in a circle. Visit with audio guide in English, free entry. A place still little known to tourists, and that's why you must go.

The Gibralfaro Castle, 14th-century Nasrid fortress perched 130 m above the port, offers the view from where the boats of the Desbandá departed in February 1937. 150,000 Republican civilians fleeing the advancing Nationalist troops toward Almeria on the N-340—the coastal road—were machine-gunned from the sea by the cruisers Canarias and Almirante Cervera, and bombed from the sky by Italian and German aviation. Between 3,000 and 5,000 dead in 5 days according to conservative estimates, among them a majority of women, children, and elderly. Doctor Norman Bethune documented the carnage. No plaque on site. Many Spaniards still don't know the episode. 3.50 euros for the castle.

The Alcazaba of Málaga, downhill from Gibralfaro, is one of the best-preserved Muslim palatial fortresses in Spain. Built by the Hammudids in the 11th century on Phoenician and Roman foundations, expanded by the Almoravids then the Nasrids. Formidable defensive system: 110 towers, three concentric enclosures. Plunging view over the Roman theater attached below—Phoenician-Roman-Muslim stacked on 30 m of height. Combined ticket with Gibralfaro at 5.50 euros.

The Roman Theatre of Málaga, uncovered in 1951 during construction work on the House of Culture (which was eventually demolished to save it), dates from the 1st century AD under Augustus. 31 m in diameter, estimated capacity 220 spectators. Built partly with stones recycled from an older edifice, and itself partially recycled by the Muslims to build the Alcazaba above. Free entry.

activities.text: Final lunch at El Pimpi (calle Granada 62), Málaga's mythical taberna since 1971, near the Picasso museum. Barrels of sweet Malagueño wine signed by Antonio Banderas (co-owner), Pablo Neruda, Joaquín Sabina. Classic tapas (croquetas, boquerones, ensaladilla), gentle prices (20-25 euros). Touristy but sincere, well done.

Journey to Málaga-Costa del Sol airport (AGP), 8 km west of downtown. Car return at the terminal—agencies grouped at P2. Cercanías C-1 train from Málaga-Centro Alameda (3 euros, 12 minutes) or taxi flat rate 25-30 euros. Allow 2 hours before a Schengen flight, 3 hours for long-haul.

days_plan.itin_title: Andalusia Road Trip: Seville, Córdoba, Granada & Ronda
Dark Andalusia: 10 Days from Seville to Málaga
Wild Coast: Málaga to Cartagena in 9 Days
White Villages & Sierras: 9 Days Málaga-Almería

places.name: Manzanares El Real

visits.text: Manzanares el Real Castle, built in 1475 for the Mendoza family, rises in granite at the foot of La Pedriza. Architect Juan Guas crafted this masterpiece of Isabelline Gothic with Mudéjar flourishes. Seven levels, a hexagonal tower, Gothic gallery, and a collection of 16th-century tapestries await inside.

The Old Castle (Castillo Viejo) crowns a hillside above the village. This 14th-century rectangular fortress in granite masonry and Mudéjar brickwork still stands proud. Four towers and its feudal layout reveal the Mendoza family's earliest fortifications.

La Pedriza sprawls across 2,600 hectares of granite chaos sculpted by erosion. Balanced boulders, domed rocks like El Yelmo and Las Torres, narrow corridors carved by wind and time. Classified as a Biosphere Reserve, it's the crown jewel of Sierra de Guadarrama National Park.
Follow the shores of Santillana Reservoir, a turquoise mirror reflecting granite peaks. The waterfront path winds through pine groves with sweeping views of the castle and La Pedriza rising beyond.

The 15th-century church of Nuestra Señora de las Nieves preserves its traditional architecture. Nearby, the Puente de la Cañada Real spans the old Segovian transhumance trail where shepherds once drove their flocks.

activities.text: Hike the Trail of Shapes (Senda de las formas) where wind-carved rocks resemble animals: the seal, the turtle. Views open onto Cancho de los Muertos and the dome of El Yelmo rising against the sky.
Climb to Ermita de Nuestra Señora de la Peña Sacra. The ascent rewards you with spectacular views across La Pedriza's mountains and the valley sprawling below.
Rock climbing made La Pedriza famous. These historic cliffs birthed the Madrid climbing school. Routes for all levels on solid granite where generations have tested their mettle.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Guadarrama Road Trip: Castles and Forests

map_keywords: La Pedriza Parque Nacional Sierra de Guadarrama
Iglesia Nuestra Señora de las Nieves Manzanares
Ermita Nuestra Señora de Peña Sacra Manzanares

visits.text: The Dunes of Maspalomas form a 403-hectare nature reserve protected since 1994. Organic sand extends 8.5 kilometers between the lighthouse and Playa del Inglés, with crests reaching 10 meters high. Wind reshapes this Saharan landscape perched on the Atlantic's edge every single day.

The Maspalomas Lighthouse rises 56 meters at Gran Canaria's southern tip. Commissioned in 1861 and completed in 1890, it once guided steamers on routes connecting Europe, Africa, and the Americas. An interpretation center traces the Canaries' maritime history.

The Charca de Maspalomas, a brackish lagoon 200 meters from the lighthouse, hosts up to 24 species of migratory and resident birds: grey herons, little egrets, coots, sandpipers. Boardwalks and observation hides line the protected zone.

The Palmeral de Maspalomas, the last remnant of the ancient palm groves in the island's south, shelters Canary palms (Phoenix canariensis). The Tony Gallardo ecological park offers an interpretive circuit through this oasis.
Ready for the beach? Playa de Maspalomas extends the dunes toward the sea for kilometers of golden sand. Further on, Playa del Inglés offers three more kilometers facing the hotels.

activities.text: Set out early to cross the dunes on foot before the heat arrives. Trail #3 from the information center viewpoint traverses different dune types and reaches the beach.
Follow the Barranco de Fataga inland: volcanic ravine landscapes, white villages clinging to slopes, and almond trees. The contrast with the coast is striking.

Stroll the Paseo Costa Canaria in Meloneras at sunset. The promenade follows the sea from the lighthouse, lined with terraces where you can sample local seafood and wines from the Caldera de Tirajana.

days_plan.itin_title: Canary Islands Road Trip: Tenerife & Gran Canaria

visits.text: The Atlantic wind hits hard at Torre del Loro—a ruined watchtower on the cliff separating Matalascañas beach from Doñana's shifting dunes. It's one of the rare vantage points from which you can see the dunes advancing toward the sea. Access on foot from the beach edge.

El Asperillo is the highest sand cliff in the Iberian Peninsula—dozens of meters of living cliff, eroded by Atlantic winds. Reach it on foot from Matalascañas heading northwest (45-minute walk). No amenities, no safety nets: approach the edge with caution.

activities.text: The resort town is charmless—but the beach itself is vast and Atlantic: pale sand, waves, wind, no crowds outside July-August. It provides access to the park's shifting dune corridor from the southwest. Barefoot in the sand with 2 hours of walking, you can find yourself alone. A stopover rather than an overnight stay.

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva, the Forgotten Land: Doñana to Faro in 7 Days

visits.text: Yellow clay cracks underfoot, and the shapes—mushrooms, pillars, arches—change every year under erosion. The Gredas de Bolnuevo, listed as a Natural Monument, are formations of Pliocene sandstone and marl 4.5 million years old, right on the beach. The visit takes 30 minutes on foot from the free parking lot. Early morning, the light is magnificent and nobody's there.

activities.text: Combine the Gredas with Bolnuevo beach (1.6 km of golden sand) and the virgin calas west of town. Nine kilometers of coves accessible on foot or by bike. Puerto de Mazarrón, 4 km away, offers the best restaurants for tasting moraga de sardines and local lobster rice.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca

visits.text: Madinat al-Zahra was Córdoba's ghost city: a palace-city built in 936 by Abd al-Rahman III for his favorite Zahra ('the flower'), capital of Al-Andalus for 70 years. Three terraced levels, 10,000 workers, pink marble from Tarragona, ivory, jasper imported from Byzantium. Sacked and pillaged in 1010 by the Berbers, abandoned, forgotten for 900 years. Rediscovered in the 19th century, only 10% has been excavated—the rest sleeps beneath olive groves. UNESCO since 2018. Abd al-Rahman III's Rich Hall is the obligatory stop: the only fully restored room, carved stucco in arabesques.

The Interpretation Center, 2 km from the site, recounts the caliphal history in 45 minutes of multimedia projection and models. Well done, essential for understanding what you'll then see on the site itself—which has almost no walls left standing. Do this before the shuttle to the ruins.

activities.text: Lunch on the way to Granada, at Bodegas Campos leaving Córdoba. Century-old cellar-restaurant (1908), produces its own Montilla-Moriles wines. Barrels signed by Charlton Heston, Federico García Lorca, King Juan Carlos. Count on 35-50 euros for the menu.

days_plan.itin_title: Dark Andalusia: 10 Days from Seville to Málaga
Al-Andalus: From the Mezquita to the Alpujarras in 10 Days

places.name: Medina Del Campo

visits.text: Drive east to Medina del Campo in the heart of the Castilian plain. The Castillo de la Mota greets you from afar—it's one of Castile's largest castles. Built in the 15th century by John II and completed by the Catholic Monarchs in 1483, it presents a trapezoidal plan with a 40-meter-high keep flanked by four crenellated towers. The castle served successively as royal archives, state prison—César Borgia was imprisoned here in 1506—and residence of Joanna the Mad. You can visit the parade ground, chapel, dungeons, and Queen's mirador—allow 1h30 with the guided tour.

Descend to the Plaza Mayor de la Hispanidad, one of Spain's largest. This is where the international fairs that made Medina's fortune in the 15th and 16th centuries were held—merchants from Portugal, Italy, and Flanders negotiated wool, textiles, and bills of exchange. On one corner of the square stands the Palacio Real Testamentario: it was in this palace that Isabella the Catholic dictated her will in October 1504 and died on November 26 following. The interpretation center recounts her life and her connection to the city.

Visit the Museo de las Ferias, housed in the former church of San Martín (16th century). This museum traces the history of the medieval fairs that made Medina the commercial capital of Castile. The pieces on display—bills of exchange, weights and measures, coins—tell how this small town became one of 15th-century Europe's most important financial centers.

The Collegiate Church of San Antolín, a Gothic-Renaissance building from the 16th-17th centuries, merits a stop for its richly decorated chapels. Then stroll the streets to spot Renaissance palaces built by bankers and merchants from the fair era: the Palacio de Dueñas, Casa Blanca, and Hospital de Simón Ruiz.

activities.text: Medina del Campo hides beneath its streets a network of medieval underground cellars, once used to store wine. Operators organize guided tours of three cellars with different characteristics—allow 1h30 with a tasting. It's an original way to discover the town's underground heritage.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

map_keywords: Castillo de la Mota Medina del Campo
Palacio Real Testamentario Medina del Campo
Plaza Mayor de la Hispanidad Medina del Campo
Colegiata de San Antolín Medina del Campo
Museo de las Ferias Medina del Campo

visits.text: Head northeast. After 30 minutes, you reach Atienza, a fortified village clinging to a rocky outcrop at 1,169 m altitude. Seven medieval gates still pierce its ramparts (including the Arco de Arrebatacapas and the Puerta de la Virgen). At the summit, the castle ruins—described by Lonely Planet as seeming like 'a natural extension of the rock'—command a complete panorama over the Guadalajara sierras. Atienza preserves several Romanesque churches, including San Gil (12th century) now converted into a sacred art museum. Plaza del Trigo (Wheat Square), lined with half-timbered houses, is the village heart.

Continue 50 minutes east to enter Soria province and reach Medinaceli. This hilltop village at 1,200 m altitude bears the legacy of every civilization that passed through: Celtiberian, Roman, Visigothic, Arab, Castilian. Its star monument is the Roman Arch, dating from the 1st century AD—the only triple-arched monumental gateway preserved in Spain. Set atop the hill facing the Jalón valley, it measures roughly 13 m long and 8 m high. Its exact dating is debated—long thought to be Trajanic, recent research attributes it more likely to the Domitian era.

Beyond the arch, wander through Medinaceli (population 700) which harbours dense heritage: the Collegiate Church of Santa María de la Asunción (16th century), the Palace of the Dukes of Medinaceli on Plaza Mayor (hosting contemporary art exhibitions), the convent of Santa Isabel, and remnants of the Arab walls. Plaza Mayor, with its arcades and discreet evening lighting, has an atmosphere out of time. This is also where Al-Mansur (Almanzor), the fearsome military leader of the Caliphate of Córdoba, supposedly died in 1002, after his defeat at Calatañazor.

activities.text: Savour Medinaceli's tranquillity in the late afternoon. The village is almost deserted—perfect for walking along the ramparts with views over the Jalón valley and the Castilian plateaux. If you're a foodie, the region produces Soria lamb, seasonal mushrooms, and black truffles in winter.

days_plan.itin_title: Alcarria and Guadalajara: Between Madrid and Aragon

visits.text: The Teatro Romano (16-15 BC) seated 6,000 spectators. The two-tiered stage with veined blue marble columns has been reconstructed through anastylosis.
The neighboring Anfiteatro (8 BC) hosted gladiatorial combat. The elliptical arena measuring 64 by 44 meters could hold 15,000 people.
Rafael Moneo's Museo Nacional de Arte Romano (1986) showcases mosaics, sculptures, and inscriptions. The red-brick building forms a contemporary Roman arch.
Cross the Roman Bridge over the Guadiana. At 792 meters with 60 arches, it's one of the longest Roman bridges still in use.
The Temple of Diana (1st century AD) raises its Corinthian columns in the city center. A Renaissance palace was built against it in the 16th century.
The Alcazaba (835 AD) is an Umayyad fortress. Descend into the aljibe, a vaulted cistern fed by the Guadiana through an underground gallery.
The Arco de Trajano (early 2nd century) stands 15 meters tall. This monumental granite gateway marked the entrance to the provincial forum.

activities.text: The Festival Internacional de Teatro Clásico has brought the Roman theater to life every summer since 1933. Greek tragedies and Latin comedies echo from the ancient stage.
Walk along the Guadiana riverbank to the Los Milagros aqueduct. Its 38 arches of brick and granite soar 25 meters high.
Taste DO Ribera del Guadiana wines at downtown bodegas. Full-bodied reds and fruity whites from Extremadura's vineyards.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas
Extremadura Road Trip: Cáceres, Mérida & Trujillo

visits.text: The color is so improbable you think it's a photo filter. The Río Tinto is blood-red permanently, naturally—its pH oscillates between 1.3 and 3, the acidity of vinegar. Bacteria oxidize the pyrite minerals and generate this acid. NASA conducted the MARTE project here, studying a terrestrial analogue of the Martian subsurface.

Five thousand years of mining traces: Tartessos, Phoenicians, Romans, Arabs, then the British of the Rio Tinto Company Limited from 1873. The mining museum traces this sequence with objects found on-site. Casa Número 21, an English manager's house reconstructed identically, induces colonial vertigo.

The Peña de Hierro mine, 10 km from town, is a former open-pit operation whose ore strata stripe the walls in rainbows of reds, oranges, and ochres. A 200-meter gallery crosses the rock to the acid lake that has flooded the bottom of the corta. This is where NASA installed its MARTE research equipment. Helmets provided.

activities.text: Get the combo ticket: train running along the red river (22 km round trip), museum, and Peña de Hierro gallery. The vintage train whistles, rattles, and crosses landscapes that resemble a post-apocalyptic film—the red badlands of Tabernas have competition. Count on the full day. Train seats are limited; book online.

If the afternoon is free, Corta Atalaya—a former open-pit mine among the largest in Europe—can be viewed from a lookout accessible by car from downtown Minas de Riotinto. No interior visit, just the view of this red crater several kilometers in diameter. Free, always open.

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva, the Forgotten Land: Doñana to Faro in 7 Days

places.name: Miranda Del Castanar

visits.text: Miranda del Castañar is, along with Ávila, one of only two villages in Spain to have preserved its entire medieval wall circuit intact. The 631 meters of 13th-century fortification encircle the historic core with four gates oriented to the cardinal points. The village sits on a rocky promontory between the Francia and San Benito rivers—its origins trace back to the 12th century with the Hospitaller Order of Jerusalem. In 1213, Alfonso IX of León made it a villa y concejo, administrative capital of Sierra de Francia.

The Zúñiga Castle (14th-15th century) dominates the walled enclosure from its eastern promontory. Its Torre del Homenaje bears an inscription dated 1451 and a Zúñiga coat of arms. The parade ground in front of the castle still serves as a plaza de toros during festivals—locals claim it as Spain's oldest square-shaped bullring. King Enrique IV created the County of Miranda del Castañar in 1457 for his son Diego López de Zúñiga.

The Hermitage of Cristo del Humilladero (13th century) stands at the village entrance, just before you pass through the walls. Here you'll grasp the scale of the defensive system: after the hermitage, a rectangular esplanade that once served as a moat, then the walls and towers. Climb the camino de ronda—the walkway atop the ramparts—for a plunging view over the sierra.

The Bell Tower (Torre de las Campanas) stands separate from the church—deliberately so. It was financed and built in 1612 by the residents so they could ring the bells at will, without depending on the clergy. The tension between municipal power and nobility is readable in every stone of Miranda: more than 80 houses bearing noble coats of arms belonged to great families drawn to the Villa Condal.

activities.text: The Camino de los Prodigios, a 10 km loop linking Miranda del Castañar to Villanueva del Conde through oaks, strawberry trees, and olive trees, is dotted with contemporary art installations integrated into nature. It's the most popular route in the province's 'Arte en la Naturaleza' series. Count on 3½ hours at an easy pace.

In the evening, stroll out to the Hermitage of Virgen de la Cuesta, outside the walls to the south, for the view of Peña de Francia lit by sunset. Legend says the Virgin appeared to children in the hollow of an olive tree at this spot—a 17th-century apparition that remains at the heart of September's patron saint festivals.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

map_keywords: Ermita Cristo del Humilladero Miranda del Castañar
Torre de las Campanas Miranda del Castañar
Ermita Virgen de la Cuesta Miranda del Castañar

visits.text: Miravet's Templar castle, perched 220 meters above an Ebro meander, is one of Europe's best-preserved Templar fortresses - built from 1153 on the foundations of an Almohad fort, capital of the Temple in Catalonia for 150 years. Here the last knights retreated in 1308 after the Order's dissolution - a year-long siege before surrender. Three enclosures, 13th-century Gothic chapel, enormous cisterns. Admission €4, allow 1.5 hours. Breathtaking views over the river.

The village of Miravet (700 inhabitants) stretches between castle and Ebro, alleys stacked atop each other with roofs nearly touching the street above. The Església Vella, former 17th-century parish church half-sunken in the river since the 1968 construction of Riba-roja dam (which raised the water level 4 meters), is the village's emblematic image. Visible from the opposite bank, accessible when levels drop. Strange, melancholic, much photographed.

Miravet's traditional pottery workshops ("cántirs") have been active since the 12th century - one of the last Spanish villages where wood-fired kiln techniques are still practiced commercially. Three families still throw: Cal Castellnou, Cal Cabrera, Cal Pessetes. Free workshop visits in the morning (8am-noon), demonstration possible if you purchase (cántir €25-40). The cántir is a two-spouted pitcher allowing you to drink à la régalade - a typically Catalan gesture. Authentic souvenir, not the plastic stuff from port markets.

activities.text: The Pas de Barca de Miravet is one of Spain's last two "transport barges" - a motorless ferry crossing the Ebro powered by current and a taut cable, in 5 minutes. Continuous service since the 16th century, free for pedestrians, €4 for cars. Hours: 7am-7pm weekdays, until 8pm in summer. A small tourist crossing but utilitarian - local farmers still use it daily to reach their groves on the opposite bank. Probably the most poetic journey of the entire itinerary.

To finish the stay, kayak down the Ebro between Miravet and Móra d'Ebre - 8 km in 2.5 hours, gentle current, landscapes of limestone cliffs and pine forests. Lo Bot Lluna center in Móra offers rental at €25 with return shuttle included. Easy level, accessible to children from 10 years. Ideal May-June and September; in summer the river runs low and warm. Possible to combine with lunch at Restaurant Sicoris in Móra (black rice with squid, €22) before returning.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Catalonia in 10 Days: Tarragona, Priorat & the Ebro Delta
Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour

visits.text: Arriving in Mogarraz, you'll sense you're being watched—and it's no illusion. Over 800 portraits of former residents gaze down from every house façade in the village. The story behind this gallery is striking: in 1967, a local photographer took ID photos of the 388 inhabitants so they could obtain identity cards without traveling to Salamanca. Those negatives slept in a cigar box for decades until artist Florencio Maíllo—born in the village—rediscovered them and decided in 2012 to paint each face on metal plates and hang them on the façade where the person had lived. The exhibition, called 'Retrata2-388,' was meant to last six months—villagers refused to remove the portraits, and the project has only grown since.

The Hermitage of Humilladero, a small 13th-century temple at the village entrance, marks the start of the visit. Just in front, the crucero de los judíos (Jews' cross from the 17th century) bears carved skulls recalling the village's past as a converted Jewish quarter—Mogarraz was founded and repopulated in the 11th century by French and Gascon settlers according to historical sources, and its historic center is classified as a Site of Cultural Interest.

The Ethnographic Museum Casa de las Artesanías, on the main square, presents Sierra de Francia traditions and crafts: embroidery, leatherwork, filigree in silver and gold. It's a useful complement for understanding the human context behind the portraits on the façades.

activities.text: Don't leave without doing the Camino del Agua—a marked 6 km loop departing from the village through chestnut and oak forests, with waterfalls and ruined mills. It's one of the trails in the 'Arte en la Naturaleza' series developed by Salamanca province: you'll encounter contemporary art installations in the midst of nature. Count on 2½ hours at an easy pace.

At the table, don't leave without trying limón serrano—a cold salad typical of Sierra de Francia blending citrus, cured meat, hard-boiled egg, and canned fish, originating with the converted Jews who inhabited the region and composed their dishes with whatever they had on hand.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

visits.text: Juan Ramón Jiménez was born here on December 23, 1881—the birthplace on Calle Ribera is a museum with free entry. At the Casa Museo Zenobia y Juan Ramón Jiménez, you'll find the poet's personal library, his furniture, his objects. On the living room mantelpiece hangs the framed original telegram announcing the 1956 Nobel Prize.

The Convento de Santa Clara de Moguer houses the sarcophagus of the Porras family, patrons of Columbus who helped finance the expedition. The 14th-century Gothic nave is one of the best preserved in the province. Guided tours offered by the resident Poor Clares. Moguer is also one of the official Lugares Colombinos.

activities.text: The Ruta de Platero connects 42 painted azulejos on Moguer's façades with excerpts from the poet's work. Sculptures of the little donkey Platero dot the plazas. An hour's walk suffices to complete the circuit. The Plaza del Cabildo with the poet's statue is the natural starting point.

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva, the Forgotten Land: Doñana to Faro in 7 Days

visits.text: Mojácar Pueblo perches at 170 meters altitude at the end of the Sierra Cabrera. Its maze of whitewashed alleys preserves the village's Al-Andalus structure. On the facades, the Indalo—a Neolithic cave figure from the Cueva de los Letreros—is painted on doors to ward off the evil eye.
The Mirador del Castillo, built on the ruins of a 16th-century castle, offers 360-degree views: the sea, the Río Aguas valley, and the Sierra Cabrera mountains. Come at sunset for the best light.

The Fuente Mora is where, according to tradition, Mojácar's last Moorish governor negotiated peaceful surrender to the Catholic Monarchs in 1488. Its twelve water jets flow beneath a commemorative plaque.

The Church of Santa María, built around 1560 on the site of a former mosque, retains a fortress-like appearance. At its feet, the arcade-bordered Plaza del Parterre serves as a terrace with valley views.

Pass through the Puerta de la Ciudad, a 16th-century entrance gate adorned with Mojácar's emblem: a double-headed eagle. Just inside, the Casa del Torreón served as a customs house where visitors paid to enter the village.
Descend to Mojácar Playa for its 17 km of coastline. Among the beaches, Marina de la Torre and El Descargador fly the blue flag. Fine sand and calm waters make them family-friendly beaches.

activities.text: Get lost in the flower-filled alleys of Mojácar Pueblo. Purple bougainvillea spills from white balconies, and every corner reveals a new sea view.
Olive oil and roasted almond tasting in the village's grocery shops. Almería province produces renowned extra virgin olive oil from Picual and Arbequina olives.
Grilled fish dinner on the Mojácar Playa waterfront. The espeto—sardines grilled on wood-fire skewers—is a local specialty.

days_plan.itin_title: Almería Road Trip: Volcanic Coves and Tabernas Desert
Wild Coast: Málaga to Cartagena in 9 Days
White Villages & Sierras: 9 Days Málaga-Almería

places.name: Molinaseca

visits.text: Molinaseca is one of the most beautiful villages on the Camino Francés in El Bierzo. Declared a Historic-Artistic Site in 1975, this stone village belonged in the Middle Ages to the lordship of Ramiro Froilaz, nephew of El Cid Campeador. Its seven-arched medieval bridge over the Meruelo River—built of ashlar and cobblestone in the 12th century—is the traditional entrance to the village for pilgrims. In summer, the river below becomes a natural swimming hole. Calle Real, which leads from the bridge, is lined with armorial palaces, stone houses, and the 17th-century sanctuary of Nuestra Señora de las Angustias, whose Virgin is the village's patroness.

Twelve kilometers beyond Molinaseca on the LZ-24 road, the village of El Acebo deserves a stop: slate houses aligned along a single street, wooden balconies overhanging the Camino. This mountain village at 1,150 meters elevation was the dreaded stage for pilgrims before descending to Molinaseca. A wooden cross at the path's edge pays tribute to Camino cyclists—a tradition established by the riders themselves. The return descent to Molinaseca offers panoramic views over the Bierzo valley.

Lombillo de los Barrios, en route to your next stop, has been classified as a Historic-Artistic Site since 2014. Its church of San Martín and the chapel of Nuestra Señora de la Encarnación form the village's monumental core. The Mirador del Bierzo on the heights offers a panoramic view over the entire comarca—stop for 20 minutes to take in the vista.

activities.text: Swimming in the Meruelo River at Molinaseca in late afternoon—the terraces of bars overlooking the bridge and river are the perfect spot to end the day with a glass of Bierzo mencía. In summer you need to arrive before noon to get a good spot on the riverbanks.

Hiking the section of the Camino Francés between El Acebo and Molinaseca: 7 km descending from the mountain hamlet, first through oak forest then along the river. It's one of the Camino's most physical stages—you'll do it in reverse from the pilgrims, downhill, which changes everything.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province

map_keywords: Sanctuaire Nuestra Señora de las Angustias Molinaseca
Mirador del Bierzo Lombillo de los Barrios

places.name: Monforte De Lemos

visits.text: Monforte de Lemos, capital of Ribeira Sacra, keeps inland Galicia's most impressive castle—a donjon-tower called 'Torre da Homenaxe' from the 13th century, 30 m high, among Spain's tallest. Built by the Counts of Lemos, a dynasty that married Pedro Fernández de Castro to a daughter of Charles V in 1574. Today a 4-star parador, 360° views from the top over the Cabe valley. Courtyard open to the public during the day. Allow 1h.

The Colexio do Cardeal, a 17th-18th century Jesuit school south of the castle, houses one of Galicia's most precious religious art collections—works by El Greco ('St Francis in Meditation', two versions), Andrea del Sarto (school), and a polychrome altarpiece by Francisco de Moure (1620). Historic library of 7,000 volumes, some from the 16th century. Building itself: 80 m-long golden stone façade. Entry €4, allow 1h. Often empty, quasi-private experience.

The mosteiro de Santo Estevo de Ribas de Sil, 30 km northwest of Monforte, perched 700 m above the Sil canyon, is one of Ribeira Sacra's three great Benedictine monasteries (with Oseira and Samos). Founded in the 9th century, enlarged 10th-18th centuries, transformed into a parador in 2003. Three superimposed cloisters (Romanesque, Gothic, Renaissance), plunging views over the canyon. Visit entry: €4 if not a guest. Coffee at the parador even without staying—the terrace is worth every penny.

activities.text: The Sil canyon, natural border between Lugo and Ourense, is Galicia's other landscape—500 m cliffs plunging into the river, practically vertical terraced vineyards, cultivated by hand since Roman times. AOC Ribeira Sacra, 1,200 hectares, red mencía and white godello, so-called 'heroic' viticulture (average slope 60%). The catamaran ride from Doade or Belesar port (1h30, €14) crosses the heart of the canyon—French narration available, departures 11am, 1pm and 4pm depending on season.

The mirador dos Balcóns de Madrid, at Parada de Sil (45 min from Monforte), is Ribeira Sacra's most striking view—a metal walkway cantilevered 500 m above the canyon, dizzying void beneath your feet. Free access, parking 200 m away. Combine with the Ruta dos Cavorcos (8 km, 3h, marked), a high-altitude trail along the canyon. Exceptional sunset, but frequent autumn fog—check the weather. Walking poles useful, no refreshments.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Galicia: From Ourense to the Ribeira Sacra
Galicia: Complete 10-Day Circuit

map_keywords: Santo Estevo de Ribas de Sil Monastery Nogueira de Ramuín
Balcones de Madrid Viewpoint Parada de Sil

visits.text: The Salto del Gitano is a 300-meter-high quartzite gorge. The Tagus River threads between rock faces where 80 pairs of griffon vultures nest.
The Salto viewpoint offers one of Europe's premier birdwatching spots. Griffon vultures, black vultures (250 pairs), Spanish imperial eagles, and black storks soar at eye level.
The Centro de Interpretación de Villarreal de San Carlos presents the park's ecosystem. Three exhibition rooms explain Monfragüe's geology, fauna, and flora.
Climb to the Castillo de Monfragüe (134 steps). From this medieval fortress, 360-degree views sweep across holm oak forests and the Tagus meanders.
The Portilla del Tiétar forms another spectacular rocky gorge. Quartzite cliffs shelter nesting golden eagles and eagle owls in the crevices.
The banks of the Tagus and Tiétar harbor otters, kingfishers, and cormorants. The two rivers meet at the heart of the national park.

activities.text: Birdwatching session with spotting scope. Identify griffon vultures, black vultures, Egyptian vultures, Bonelli's eagles, booted eagles, and common buzzards.
Follow the red trail (8 km). Starting from Villarreal, passing the Salto del Gitano, climbing toward the castle along the quartzite ridge.
Picnic beneath the holm oaks of the dehesa. This agro-silvo-pastoral ecosystem typical of Extremadura combines open woodland, pastures, and Iberian pig farming.
The park is certified as a Starlight Destination. On clear nights, watch the Milky Way from viewpoints far from artificial light.

days_plan.itin_title: Extremadura Road Trip: Cáceres, Mérida & Trujillo

visits.text: Monleón is one of Salamanca's best-kept secrets. This village of fewer than 100 inhabitants, perched at 900 meters and encircled by medieval walls, barely registers in guidebooks. At the entrance, just beside the puerta de la villa, a zoomorphic granite sculpture—a verraco (bull), typical of the Vettones and dating to the 4th century BC—announces the site's antiquity. The village appears in archives as early as 1199 and was repopulated around 1215.

The 15th-century castle, attached to the walls at the southwest corner of the enclosure, has a wild history. Its owner in 1477, one Don Bernardo Maldonado called 'the Tyrant,' counterfeited money and committed all manner of abuses on neighboring lands. Troops of King Ferdinand the Catholic had to besiege the castle to put him out of commission. The square keep tower still counts 5 levels and 8 turrets.

Outside the walls, on the path leading to the 'Las ollas de la sapa' site, lie the ruins of a 6th-8th century AD medieval village (Monte Alcaide) and several lagares—wine presses carved directly into granite bedrock. These stone basins scattered across the landscape testify to the region's medieval viticulture.

activities.text: The circular hike on GR-181 'Ruta de los Caminos Históricos de Entresierras' between Monleón and El Tornadizo edges along the Alagón River and crosses landscapes of scrubland and granite ravines that are virtually pristine. Count on 3-4 hours depending on your pace for the complete loop.

Monleón has only one bar—an advantage really, since it concentrates all the village's social life. It's the ideal place to talk with the handful of residents and hear the village's history from their mouths. The tapas are renowned for their generosity.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

visits.text: Montanejos is this itinerary's pleasant surprise. This mountain village owes its reputation to the Fuente de los Baños, a natural thermal spring whose water emerges at 25°C at the foot of a spectacular gorge. The rocks polished by the Mijares River serve as natural loungers—just bring a towel.

activities.text: If the water is 25°C even in November, you might as well make the most of it without waiting for summer. Swimming in the Fuente de los Baños happens directly in the river—no infrastructure, no ticket. The gorge trail starts from the village car park and offers viewpoints over the cliffs in a 45-minute round trip.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages

visits.text: Before Montblanc, take the detour to the Royal Monastery of Santa Maria de Poblet—8 km west, but it's the essential stop. Founded in 1151 by Cistercians from Fontfroide, it's now the largest inhabited monastery in Europe (some thirty monks still live there). UNESCO-listed since 1991. The royal pantheon of the Kings of Aragon—Alfonso II, James the Conqueror, Peter the Ceremonious—rests in the abbey church, suspended on two arches above the transept. The visit covers the church, the two-story cloister, the refectory, King Martin's palace, and the medieval wine cellar.

Arrival in Montblanc, before ramparts unlike anything else in Catalonia: 1.5 km of walls built between 1366 and 1383 by order of Peter the Ceremonious to defend against Peter the Cruel of Castile—a war chroniclers nicknamed "the War of the Two Peters." Thirty rectangular towers, except one, the Torre de les Cinc Cantons, which has a strange pentagonal plan. Climb onto the wall-walk: the Conca de Barberà spreads below, vineyards as far as the eye can see, and in the distance rises the "Giant's Nose," a rock profile you'd swear was sculpted.

The church of Santa Maria la Major dominates the old town with its entire Gothic nave. Begun in the 14th century, never finished—the Baroque facade was added in the 17th after destruction. Inside, the sculpted portal and altarpiece deserve a stop. More discreet but older: Sant Miquel, from the 13th century, Romanesque facade and Gothic body. And around the narrow streets, the 14th-century royal palace, the former Desclergue house, and the Jewish quarter (Call) that reveals itself in the pattern of the houses.

The Pont Vell, spanning the Francolí at the foot of the ramparts, is Montblanc's postcard angle. Medieval stone bridge, arches that have resisted floods for eight centuries. This is where you grasp the complete silhouette of the fortified city: wall, towers, Santa Maria's bell tower. Walk to the far bank and turn around. A shot you never tire of taking.

activities.text: Calçotada at a local home or restaurant if you're here between January and April. The calçot is that long sweet onion like a spring onion, grilled black over vine cuttings, peeled by hand and dipped in romesco sauce. Els Castellets de Montblanc and the Viñas pastry shop sell sweet versions of the specialty. Out of season, fall back on the restaurants along carrer Major, serving escudella, cargols a la llauna (snails on a sheet), and Priorat lamb.

If you arrive the last week of April, you'll catch the Setmana Medieval—Montblanc's medieval festival, starting April 23 (Sant Jordi). The town transforms: jousting tournaments, artisan market in period costume, banquets by torchlight, street theatre re-enacting Saint George's battle with the dragon (a legend that, according to Montblanc, took place right here). An atmosphere unlike anything else in the region.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Catalonia in 10 Days: Tarragona, Priorat & the Ebro Delta
Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour
Southern Catalonia in 5 Days: Roman Tarragona, Poblet, Priorat and the Port of Cambrils

places.name: Montemayor Del Rio

visits.text: Montemayor del Río occupies a spectacular enclave in the Sierra de Béjar, at the historic frontier between the kingdoms of León, Castile, and Extremadura. Its San Vicente castle commands a precious valley planted with chestnut and oak. The fortress was built in the late 13th century on orders of Infante Don Pedro, son of Alfonso X the Wise—but it's the 14th and 15th centuries that gave the castle its present appearance, with four towers (two round, two square) and its keep. In 1538, Charles V created the Marquessate of Montemayor for Juan de Silva, Captain General of Navarre. Declared a Site of Cultural Interest in 1949, the castle now houses a Medieval Interpretation Center with guided tours.

The village itself deserves a long wander. Its medieval streets, church, and old center lanes hosted merchants and muleteers for centuries on the Cañada Real (old transhumance route) and the Calzada—the Roman Via de la Plata linking Mérida to Astorga. The arcades on the plaza and carved lintels bear witness to a town of passage and exchange.

activities.text: In summer, Montemayor del Río's natural pools—carved into granite and fed by a mountain stream—rank among the region's finest. Water flows straight down from the Sierra de Béjar peaks. Count on a half-hour walk from the village to reach them.

Lunch in Montemayor offers a chance to try the stews and mountain sausages typical of the Sierra de Béjar—quality cured meats, some prepared in villagers' own homes. Nearby in Béjar, you'll also find local specialties featuring Iberian ham.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

map_keywords: Château San Vicente Montemayor del Río
Église Nuestra Señora de la Asunción Montemayor del Río

visits.text: En route southeast, cross the Sierra de Cardeña y Montoro Natural Park—the highest in Córdoba province. The Mediterranean forest of holm oaks and cork oaks is dense, and the wildlife rich: Iberian lynx (reintroduced), wolf (last Andalusian populations), otter, Spanish imperial eagle. The Río Yeguas trail crosses spectacular granite rock landscapes. Allow 2 to 3 hours for a hike in the park before descending toward Montoro.

Montoro is a surprise. This village of 10,000 inhabitants, classified as a Historic-Artistic Ensemble since 1969, sits on a promontory encircled by a spectacular meander of the Guadalquivir—almost a peninsula. What strikes you first is the color: the monuments are built in piedra molinaza, a local red sandstone that gives the church, ducal palace, and manor façades terracotta hues. The church of San Bartolomé (15th century), in the center on Plaza de España, has a red sandstone bell tower nicknamed 'the Red Giralda' for its resemblance to the Sevillian campanile. Opposite, the Ducal Palace of the Casa de Alba (16th century), now the town hall, presents Mudéjar elements in piedra molinaza.

Descend toward the Puente de las Donadas, built in 1498 by Enrique Egas—one of the finest bridges in the province. Its name ('bridge of the women who gave') comes from the fact that the women of Montoro sold their jewelry to finance its construction, and the king granted them in exchange the privilege of no longer having to lodge or feed troops. From the bridge, the view of the Guadalquivir's meander encircling Montoro is the village's emblematic image. Before leaving Montoro, seek out the Casa de las Conchas—a house whose façade and interior patios are entirely covered with seashells, 45 million in total, accumulated over 50 years by a passionate local.

activities.text: Montoro is at the heart of Córdoba's olive-growing zone—the surrounding olive groves produce DOP Montoro-Adamuz olive oil. Cooperatives offer visits and tastings. Local gastronomy reflects the terroir: ajo sopeao (tomato, pepper, and garlic stew), tortilla de faisanes (bean omelet), perdices en salsa (partridge in sauce), and tortas de manteca for dessert.

days_plan.itin_title: Córdoba and the Sierra Morena: From the Caliphate to the Dehesas of Los Pedroches

visits.text: The monastery of Santa Maria de Montserrat clings to a ledge at 720 meters altitude, anchored to a massif of weirdly sculpted conglomerate. Founded in 1025, it still houses a hundred Benedictine monks. But it's the Black Madonna—the Moreneta—that draws 2.3 million visitors annually: a 12th-century Romanesque statue, patron saint of Catalonia since 1881. The queue to touch her can exceed two hours in high season. Arrive before 9:30 AM or after 5 PM—it's the only way.

The Museu de Montserrat is the site's big surprise—a collection of 1,300 works including Caravaggio, Picasso, Dalí, El Greco, Monet, and Miró, plus an archaeological section on the biblical Near East with an actual mummy. Built through private donations since the 19th century, it's now one of Catalonia's most important collections outside Barcelona. Allow an hour, entry €8. Almost deserted at midday when everyone's queuing for the Virgin.

At 1 PM on weekdays and noon on Sundays, the Escolania choir sings for 15 minutes in the basilica. One of Europe's oldest boys' choirs, documented since the 14th century, with some fifty resident boys. Remarkable acoustics—Jordi Savall records in the neighboring chapels for this reason. Arrive 30 minutes early for a seat. No singing in July or during Christmas holidays.

Next to the basilica, the Santa Cova funicular descends in 5 minutes to the cave chapel built where, according to legend, shepherds discovered the statue in 880. The walk from the station takes 20 minutes along the Way of the Cross, punctuated by 14 Modernist sculptures by the great Catalan architects of the early 20th century, including Gaudí and Puig i Cadafalch. The path is free, easy trail, plunging views over the valley. Round-trip funicular: €4.50.

activities.text: The Sant Joan funicular climbs in 7 minutes to 1,000 meters altitude—average gradient of 65%, one of Europe's steepest. At the top, five marked trails head toward hermitages and peaks. Most accessible: the Sant Joan loop, 2.7 km in 1 hour, no serious elevation gain, returning with views above the monastery. For seasoned hikers, Sant Jeroni at 1,236 meters, the summit: 4 hours round trip, 360° views to the Pyrenees on clear days.

Buy mató at the monastery exit—fresh Catalan goat cheese, sold for €5 a pot by local farmers, eaten drizzled with Pyrenean honey. A tradition for every pilgrim since the Middle Ages. For dinner, the restaurant at Hotel Abat Cisneros, attached to the monastery, serves honest Catalan cuisine at €35. Booking advised on weekends. Absolute calm after the tour buses depart at 6 PM—one of the benefits of staying overnight.

days_plan.itin_title: Catalonia Wine Route - 7 Days from Penedès to Priorat

visits.text: The scent of pine resin and salt rises through the alleyways: Moraira has kept its fishing village proportions despite the second-home tourism that has settled here. The 18th-century Castillo de Moraira sits on l'Ampolla beach, built to repel pirates. The Cap d'Or tower (11 meters) watches over the port at the opposite end. On Friday mornings, the market spreads along the entire promenade.

activities.text: Climb up to the Cap d'Or tower to take in the sweep of Moraira bay—on clear days the Peñón de Ifach looms to the south. The SL-CV51 trail starts from El Portet beach and takes 1h round trip. Back at sea level, Cala del Moraig merits a stop for diving or swimming in deep water. Closed-toe shoes essential on the rocky path.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

visits.text: Moratalla is a perched village in northwestern Murcia, dominated by a medieval castle from which the view over the mountains and pine forests is superb. The village climbs in narrow lanes up to the fortress, with whitewashed houses and an end-of-the-world atmosphere. But what makes Moratalla famous is its rock art: the region shelters several rock shelters decorated with prehistoric paintings classified as UNESCO World Heritage as part of the Mediterranean Basin Rock Art of the Iberian Peninsula. The most accessible sites are the abrigos del Cañaíca del Calar, with hunting scenes dating back several millennia.

The road between Moratalla and Lorca (about 1h30 drive) crosses landscapes of dry mountains and spectacular gorges. The Cañón de los Almadenes, accessible from Calasparra or Cieza, is one of the most impressive canyons in southeastern Spain. Lorca, your exit point towards Murcia, is dominated by its fortress with seven towers - this place is already covered in another ORT itinerary.

activities.text: Operators offer canoe or kayak descents through the Segura gorges, between Calasparra and Cieza, passing through the Cañón de los Almadenes. Allow 2 to 3 hours for a leisurely descent through rock walls and moderate rapids. It's an activity accessible to beginners and perfect for ending the trip on a nature note.

Before leaving northwestern Murcia, taste the rice of Calasparra - the only rice in Spain to have a protected designation of origin. Grown in the terraces irrigated by the Segura since Arab times, it's smaller and firmer than other Spanish rice varieties, perfect for paellas and caldero dishes (fish and rice stew).

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Granada: Alpujarras, Guadix and the Sierras
Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca

places.name: Morella

visits.text: The castle of Morella crowns the rocky outcrop at 1,070 meters altitude. Built in the 13th century, it witnessed battles involving El Cid and sheltered Pope Luna in 1414.
The ramparts stretch for 2.5 kilometers and have protected the town since the 14th century. Their 14 towers and 6 gates testify to Morella's strategic importance.

The Basilica of Santa María la Mayor displays its Gothic portals—the Portal of the Apostles and the Portal of the Virgins—carved in the 14th and 15th centuries. The spiral staircase of the choir loft is entirely covered with painted reliefs.

The aqueduct of Santa Llúcia dates from 1338. Its two surviving Gothic arches once carried water from the springs to the town. The trail alongside offers panoramic views across the Els Ports countryside.
The Torres de San Miguel form the main gateway. These twin octagonal towers from the 14th century house a collection of medieval games and offer stunning views over Morella's rooftops.
Push open the door of the San Francisco convent to discover the Danse Macabre, an enigmatic 14th-century fresco depicting nobility, clergy, and common people facing death.
Follow the arcades of Blasco de Alagón and climb the stepped alleys. Noble houses—Ciurana, Piquer, Rovira, Cardinal Ram—punctuate the route.

activities.text: Morella is the kingdom of black truffles. Restaurants offer special menus and tastings from November through March, accompanied by local cheeses aged in cellars.
The Paseo de la Alameda circles the mountain. This shaded promenade offers changing perspectives on the ramparts and the surrounding countryside.
Textile workshops keep alive a centuries-old tradition. Some artisans open their doors to demonstrate old-fashioned weaving techniques.
The cave paintings of Morella La Vella lie 6 kilometers away. These UNESCO-listed hunting scenes can be visited in small groups on weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: Castellón and Maestrazgo Route: Castles and Coves
Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages

map_keywords: Basilique Santa María la Mayor Morella

visits.text: The Cathedral of Saint Mary blends a soaring Gothic interior with a baroque façade designed by Jaime Bort in 1751. Its 95-meter tower—Spain's second tallest—offers sweeping views over Murcia's vast orchards and market gardens.

Step through the doors of the Real Casino and prepare to be dazzled. The Moorish patio glitters with 20,000 sheets of gold leaf, the neo-baroque ballroom displays allegorical ceiling frescoes celebrating the arts, and the English library is adorned with wrought-iron flamingos.
Plaza de las Flores fills with terrace cafés by 5 PM. Cross the Puente de los Peligros over the Segura River, where a statue of the Virgin has stood watch since the 18th century.
The Salzillo Museum showcases the baroque processional sculptures of Francisco Salzillo. Polychrome wood, hauntingly lifelike faces, meticulous detail in every gesture.
The Episcopal Palace unfurls an exuberant rococo façade across the Plaza del Cardenal Belluga, a riot of ornamental flourishes.

activities.text: Go on a tapas crawl around Plaza Julián Romea. Marineras (anchovy toasts), michirones (spiced fava beans), pasteles de carne (savory meat pies)—every bar has its signature bite.
Paparajotes are lemon-tree leaves dipped in sweet batter and fried crisp—a uniquely Murcian dessert that tastes like citrus-scented doughnuts.
The Teatro Romea stages concerts, operas, and comedies in a stunning 19th-century hall with remarkable acoustics.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Road Trip: Baroque Splendor & Costa Cálida
Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca

places.name: Muxia

visits.text: The Sanctuary of Nosa Señora da Barca, built in the 18th century on a rocky headland, marks the spiritual terminus of the Fisterra pilgrimage route.
The Pedra de Abalar rocks and sways when storms batter the coast. The Pedra dos Cadrís is said to cure back pain if you crawl beneath it nine times.
The A Ferida monument commemorates the 2002 Prestige oil spill disaster. Two granite blocks separated by a fissure symbolize the wound inflicted on the coast.
Cape Vilán thrusts its white lighthouse 345 feet above the pounding surf. Spain's first electrified beacon, lit in 1896, warns ships of the treacherous Costa da Morte.
The English Cemetery near the cape holds the graves of 172 sailors from HMS Serpent, wrecked on these rocks in 1890.

activities.text: Hike the coastal trail from Muxía to Monte Corpiño for sweeping views across the Ría de Camariñas and out to Cape Vilán.
The Atlantic-battered cliffs draw cormorants and kittiwakes. Bring binoculars to watch the seabird colonies wheel and dive.
Sunset from the sanctuary illuminates the polished granite boulders in fiery gold. Waves explode against the Pedra da Vela in thunderous bursts.

days_plan.itin_title: Galicia Road Trip: Santiago de Compostela and the Coast of Death

map_keywords: Sanctuaire Nosa Señora da Barca Muxía
Cimetière des Anglais Costa da Morte

visits.text: The roar precedes the vision: water bursts from the Cueva de los Chorros at between 80 and 100 metres high, plunging into successive pools before forming the río Mundo. During the phenomenon called 'El Reventón', after heavy rains, the flow multiplies spectacularly and impressively. The trail to the mirador runs 1.5 km from the Chorros parking. Swimming banned in the pools near the source.

activities.text: If you come after heavy rains, watch for the Reventón: a torrential flow pours from the rock with a force that completely changes the landscape—locals speak of it as a spectacle in its own right. Otherwise, a 3-km circular hike from Puerto del Arenal climbs to the upper mirador with an elevation gain of about 170 m. No drones allowed above the waterfall.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: Navacerrada Pass (Puerto de Navacerrada) sits at 1,860 meters, straddling Madrid and Segovia provinces. A natural crossroads between Siete Picos to the west and Bola del Mundo to the east, the latter crowned by rocket-shaped television antennas.
Gaze across Cuerda Larga, a mountain ridge stretching toward infinity. Scots pines (Pinus sylvestris) cling to steep slopes, alpine meadows burst with wildflowers come summer.
Bola del Mundo peaks at 2,258 meters, crowned by historic antennas that once broadcast television across the Iberian Peninsula. From the summit, panoramas unfold in every direction.
The Siete Picos massif arrays seven granite peaks in a dramatic line. The seventh summit reaches 2,138 meters, offering 360° views across the Sierra de Guadarrama.

activities.text: Hike to Siete Picos via the PR-M trail. The rocky ridge demands some easy scrambling. With 764 meters of elevation gain, spectacular panoramas are guaranteed every step of the way.
The Guarramillas chairlift operates in summer. Ride up for panoramic views to Bar La Bola, a high-altitude establishment at 2,000 meters—the highest point in the Community of Madrid.
In winter, the ski resort offers family-friendly slopes and breathtaking views. Light, dry snow characteristic of high altitude. Snow cover typically arrives in January.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Guadarrama Road Trip: Castles and Forests

visits.text: The people of Navajas call it their back garden—the Palancares waterfall and Salto de la Novia meet in a wooded valley 10 minutes' walk from the village. A brief two-hour stop, ideal for stretching your legs between Montanejos and Sagunto. The trail is marked, accessible in trainers.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages

visits.text: On January 12, 1959, five teenagers were looking for bats and punched a hole in the rock wall - that's how the Cueva de Nerja was discovered. Inside: in the Hall of the Cataclysm, a limestone column 32 meters high and 13 meters in diameter, listed in the Guinness Book of Records since 1989 as the world's largest known underground column. Allow 1h30. Book ahead in summer - visitor numbers are capped.

Salt air thick at the end of the promontory, the sea on either side - the Balcón de Europa lives up to its name. Alfonso XII christened it so in January 1885 during a visit after the earthquake that devastated the region. A bronze statue marks the moment, leaning on the railing. Five minutes is enough, but you'll stay longer.

activities.text: If you're staying in Nerja, get down to Burriana before 9am: the beach is still empty and the chiringuitos are just opening up. It's one of the few beaches on the Costa del Sol with proper fresh fish restaurants - no fast food in sight. Espetos de sardinas grilled over wood fire.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Coast: Málaga to Cartagena in 9 Days

places.name: O Cebreiro

visits.text: The road from Monforte to O Cebreiro crosses the mountainous heart of Lugo province—chestnut forests, meadows, stone hamlets where time seems to have stopped. O Cebreiro (1,300 m altitude) is the gateway to the Camino de Santiago in Galicia for pilgrims coming from Castile on the Camino Francés. The village has barely a handful of houses, a pilgrims' hostel, and a church—but the atmosphere is extraordinary, especially when fog rises from the valley and wraps around the pallozas.

The pallozas are the very reason for visiting O Cebreiro. These pre-Roman dwellings—circular or oval stone houses with conical thatched roofs—were inhabited until the mid-20th century. They descend directly from pre-Roman Celtic constructions (the castros). Several have been restored, and one houses a small ethnographic museum showing how peasant families lived: a single room shared with animals, a central hearth with no chimney (smoke escaped through the thatched roof). These structures are unique to this mountainous zone of Galicia and the Bierzo.

The church of Santa María la Real in O Cebreiro, pre-Romanesque (9th century), is one of the oldest in Galicia still standing. It's also the site of the 'Miracle of O Cebreiro': in the 14th century, a priest who doubted transubstantiation saw the bread and wine literally transform into flesh and blood during Mass. The chalice and paten from the miracle are still preserved in the church—and it's precisely these that are said to have inspired the legend of the Holy Grail in Wagner's Parsifal. The simple, moving Romanesque chalice is displayed in a case to the left of the altar.

activities.text: Walk a section of the Camino de Santiago from O Cebreiro—even if you're not a pilgrim, the descent to the village of Liñares then Hospital da Condesa (4 km, 1h) gives you a taste of what walkers experience: an earth path between stone walls, birch forests, views of the Lugo mountains. You'll encounter pilgrims of all nationalities carrying their scallop shells and walking sticks.

If you continue by car to the Alto do Poio (1,337 m), the highest point of the Camino de Santiago in Galicia, you'll cross a landscape of moorland and peat bogs that recalls Scotland or Ireland. It's also excellent territory for spotting wild Galician horses (garañóns). In the evening, dine at O Cebreiro on caldo gallego and empanada—Galician mountain cuisine is simple and comforting.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Galicia: From Ourense to the Ribeira Sacra

map_keywords: Iglesia Santa María la Real O Cebreiro

visits.text: The scent of citrus rises from the huertas before you even enter the valley. The Valle de Ricote, the last Morisco stronghold of the Levant until the 1610 expulsion, has preserved Arab irrigation systems and century-old palms. At Abarán, the Noria Grande—nearly 12 m in diameter, the largest working noria in Europe—still turns in the Segura. The village of Ojós (500 inhabitants) is one of Murcia's most beautiful and quietest.

activities.text: At Abarán, Europe's largest working noria has turned in the Segura for centuries. The Ruta de las Norias (4 km) follows the river between dense orchards and water wheels. The Mirador del Alto de Bayna, a 30 m metal walkway over the river, offers the best view of the valley. Rafting or canoeing available from several local outfitters.

days_plan.itin_title: Murcia Coast and Hinterland: 13 days from La Manga to Cuenca

places.name: Olite

visits.text: The Royal Palace of Olite was commissioned in 1402 by Charles III the Noble. Over 22 years rose one of Europe's most important Gothic civil buildings, complete with seven towers, gardens, and mudéjar decoration.
The royal chambers cluster around the central core. The splayed bay windows in the Queen's chamber overlook the orange tree courtyard.
Climb the Tower of the Three Crowns to discover one of the most breathtaking views of the complex. This tower was allegedly built for the king's children.
The church of Santa María la Real stands adjacent to the castle. Its Gothic sculpted portico displays balanced figures, fantastical animals, and vegetal motifs.
The church of San Pedro retains its octagonal bell tower. Inside, admire the main altarpiece and the baptismal fonts.
Wander the cobblestone lanes of the medieval center. The palace occupies a full third of the old town's surface area.

activities.text: Local bodegas offer tastings of Navarre DO wines. The wine region produces celebrated reds and rosés.
At the foot of the Tower of the Three Crowns, observe the reconstruction of a medieval ice well eight meters deep, once used as a refrigerator.
Head to the ramparts at sunset to watch the towers silhouette themselves against the golden light.
Visit the Chapitel Tower on Plaza Carlos III. It housed one of the first public clocks on the peninsula.

days_plan.itin_title: Navarre Road Trip: Castles, Irati Forest & the Camino

map_keywords: Tower of the Three Crowns Olite
Santa María la Real Church Olite

places.name: Olvera

visits.text: Olvera's silhouette appears from afar: 12th-century Arab castle and neoclassical church side by side, on the ridgeline, photographed in every Andalusian brochure without ever growing stale. Up close, the medieval alleys preserve the typical interlacing of the Nasrid frontera. Declared a Conjunto Histórico-Artístico in 1983.

Olvera castle's Torre del Homenaje can be visited and gives a panorama over the sierra gaditana as far as Grazalema in clear weather. The climb is short. The church of Nuestra Señora de la Encarnación, right beside it, is neoclassical and closes early - check opening times at the village entrance.

activities.text: 36 km of disused railway track converted into a cycle path, through 30 tunnels and 4 viaducts - the Vía Verde de la Sierra crosses sierra landscapes that the road bypasses. Departing from the old Olvera station (converted into a discovery center) toward Puerto Serrano. Bike rental on site. Even an hour round trip through the first tunnels is worthwhile.

days_plan.itin_title: White Villages: Arcos, Grazalema, Ronda - 6 Days

map_keywords: Vía Verde de la Sierra Olvera

visits.text: Locals have come here every summer since the Clariano River carved these pools into the limestone. Six natural swimming holes bear specific names: Pou dels Esclaus, Pou Clar, Pou Gelat, Pou de la Reixa, Pou Fosc, Pou dels Cavalls. The walking or cycling circuit from Ontinyent passes through Mediterranean vegetation of agaves and carob trees. Pedestrian access only in high season—parking closes.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

places.name: Orbaneja Del Castillo

visits.text: Orbaneja del Castillo is perhaps Spain's most improbable village: a 25-meter waterfall emerges directly from the Cueva del Agua, flows through the village dividing houses into two quarters, and plunges into the Ebro below. Along the way, the water has formed terraced pools with turquoise reflections surrounded by green moss—the contrast is striking. The village, declared a historic site in 1993, has about fifty inhabitants and stone lanes where time seems frozen. Park at the lot outside the center.

The Cueva del Agua, from which the waterfall springs, is a karst cave you can visit guided in summer and on weekends. From the upper part of the town, climb to Orbaneja's natural viewpoint for a plunging view over the Ebro canyon—the limestone walls cut out rock silhouettes that locals compare to African profiles. Also look for the natural arch that the river has sculpted in the cliff above the village.

activities.text: The Ebro canyon (Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón) offers spectacular hikes from Orbaneja. The circular route linking Orbaneja del Castillo to Valdelateja follows the canyon for 17 km—allow a good half-day. If you prefer something shorter, the GR-99 between Pesquera de Ebro and Manzanedo offers the best gorge views in 2-3 hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

map_keywords: Cueva del Agua Orbaneja del Castillo

visits.text: A Blue Flag beach in the middle of Extremadura, 200 km from the sea: the Playa de Orellana (called Costa Dulce) was the first inland beach in Spain to win this label. Clean water, eucalyptus, fine sand. Sports harbour and sendero azul complete the facilities.

The Castillo-Palacio de los Altamiranos, a 15th-century fortress transformed into a palace in the 16th, dominates the village. Lesser kestrels nest in its walls in spring. The building can be visited from outside; the interior is closed to the public.

Push on to the Convento de San Benito, from the 16th century, in the village centre. The Iglesia de la Inmaculada Concepción, from the same century, completes the tour. Austere architecture, no frills. Ten minutes' visit for each.

activities.text: The warm water of Orellana Lake invites windsurfing, kayaking, and sailing. A nautical club organises competitions and offers equipment rental. In summer, the chiringuitos on the Costa Dulce serve freshwater fish fry.

If you're into birdwatching, the Orellana reservoir is classified as a ZEPA (Special Protection Area for Birds). Cranes in winter, raptors all year. The marked trail around the lake offers observation points without disturbing the species.

days_plan.itin_title: La Siberia and La Serena: Secret Extremadura in 6 Days

visits.text: Orihuela often goes unnoticed on the road to Murcia, which is a mistake. Its historic center, declared an artistic monument in 1969, brings together El Salvador Cathedral (begun in the 14th century on the ruins of the Aljama mosque, elevated to cathedral in 1564), the convent of Santo Domingo, and the castle on Mount San Miguel. The cathedral preserves a Velázquez painting, The Temptation of Saint Thomas, in its sacred art museum.

activities.text: The market runs along the Segura in a gentle aroma of fruits and vegetables from the Vega Baja—this fertile plain the Romans already exploited. The house-museum of poet Miguel Hernández, born in Orihuela in 1910, merits 45 minutes for anyone interested in 20th-century Spanish poetry. Orihuela sits 10 km from the sea: Cabo Roig beach is the closest.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

visits.text: Ourense—the city changes face: you're entering Galicia. Founded by Romans at the end of the 1st century BCE under the name Aquae Urentes ("burning waters") or Auriense ("city of gold"), for its thermal springs and the gold then found in the Miño. Today it's Galicia's third city, and one of Europe's only ones with urban thermal springs in the very center. The nerve center: As Burgas, three springs gushing at 67°C with a flow of 300 liters per minute. Free-access basin at fixed hours (10am-1pm and 5pm-9pm, free). Romans had already built a thermal sanctuary dedicated to nymphs here—excavations visible.

Five minutes' walk, the Cathedral of San Martiño dates from the 12th-13th centuries. Built on a 6th-century Swabian church. The masterpiece is the Pórtico do Paraíso: a late 13th-century copy of Santiago's Pórtico da Gloria by Master Mateo, but which has preserved its original polychromy—exceedingly rare, Compostela's original was stripped. Here you see what Romanesque portals must have been before centuries wore away their colors. Inside, the Cristo de Ourense, 14th-century polychrome sculpture with human hair, is the object of local devotion.

The Old Bridge spans the Miño with its seven arches, the central arch rising 40 meters above the water. Augustan Roman foundations, medieval reconstruction in 1230 by Bishop Lourenzo, 17th and 19th-century restorations. Today closed to cars, it's Spain's oldest bridge to have kept such a homogeneous silhouette. Beside it, the Millennium Bridge (2001) answers in contemporary architecture—pedestrian walkway suspended 22 meters above the river, accessible by stairs. Panoramic view of both banks.

The San Francisco cloister, behind the former convent, is considered Galicia's finest Gothic cloister.
