Built in the 14th century, 63 pointed arches, 76 double columns, 120 different capitals—each carved with a unique motif: foliage, fantastic animals, anthropomorphic scenes. The Franciscan convent was dismantled in the 19th century, the cloister transferred stone by stone to its current location to save it from destruction. Absolute calm, free access, few visitors.

activities.text: The thermal train—little tourist train—departs Plaza Mayor and tours the thermal stations on the Miño's right bank in 30 minutes. Four stops: A Chavasqueira (water at 41°C, free), Muíño da Veiga, Outariz (Japanese style, €4.80 fee), Burga de Canedo. The thermal promenade follows the Miño for 3 km—also pleasant on foot, about 1 hour. Outariz is the most complete experience: pools at different temperatures, zen gardens. Quieter on weekdays.

Tapas crawl in the Vinos zone—Calle Viriato, Fornos, San Miguel concentrate tapas bars since the 1950s. In Ourense, the tapa is still offered with your drink. Specialties: pulpo a feira (octopus with paprika), bolo de pote (Galician bread), filloas (local crêpes). Regional wines—Ribeiro and Valdeorras—are mineral whites being discovered outside Galicia. Three to four bars suffice for a complete dinner.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Galicia: From Ourense to the Ribeira Sacra
Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Galicia: Complete 10-Day Circuit

visits.text: San Salvador Cathedral blends flamboyant Gothic with pre-Romanesque roots. Its solitary tower dominates the old town and once served as a compass for pilgrims walking the Camino Primitivo. Inside, three ribbed-vault naves converge on a towering main altarpiece.

Step through the door of the Cámara Santa, a 9th-century chapel built by Alfonso II the Chaste. Six pairs of apostles carved as Romanesque statue-columns support the vault. The treasury holds the Cross of the Angels (808), Oviedo's emblem, and the Cross of Victory (908), symbol of Asturias—both masterpieces of chiseled gold and cabochon jewels.

Climb Mount Naranco to Santa María del Naranco, the palace of Ramiro I consecrated in 848. This rectangular 20 × 6-meter two-story structure is the finest example of European pre-Romanesque architecture. The upper floor reveals a barrel vault with transverse arches, triple-arched loggias, twisted columns, and medallions carved with animals and horsemen.

A hundred meters away, San Miguel de Lillo preserves only a third of the original church, but its door jambs carved with Roman circus scenes and stone lattice screens remain unique in 9th-century religious architecture. Both monuments have been UNESCO-listed since 1985.
Descend to Plaza del Fontán, an arcaded square that hosts Oviedo's covered market. Asturian cheeses, fabada in jars, local cider—the stalls overflow with regional treasures.

Detour to La Foncalada, a 9th-century fountain built under Alfonso III the Great. Still functioning today, it's the only hydraulic engineering monument from the early Middle Ages with UNESCO status in Spain.

activities.text: Push open the door of a sidrería in the Casco Antiguo and order a culín. The server pours the cider from high above to aerate it—the escanciado, an unmissable Asturian ritual.
Wander through Parque San Francisco, the green lung of downtown. Its shaded paths shelter free-roaming peacocks and contemporary sculptures scattered among centuries-old trees.
Follow Oviedo's urban sculpture trail: dozens of bronze statues line the pedestrian streets of the center, from La Maternidad to the seated silhouette of Woody Allen on Calle Milicias.

days_plan.itin_title: Asturias Road Trip: Cantabrian Coast and Picos de Europa

visits.text: La Seu Cathedral has commanded the bay since the 13th century. Its nave soars to 45 meters—only Beauvais rises higher. The 14-meter Gothic rose window is the world's largest.
Almudaina Palace was built in the 14th century atop an Arab alcazaba. Still the official royal residence in Majorca, it houses the Chapel of Saint Anne and opulent state rooms.
Parc de la Mar stretches beneath the ramparts. Its artificial lake mirrors La Seu in what's become Palma's most iconic photographic composition.
Old town lanes wind between flower-filled patios and ochre facades. The quarter hides Baroque churches, noble houses, and shaded squares.
Step through the doors of the Arab Baths, dating to the 10th century. Their barrel vaults and columns bear witness to Moorish occupation.
Bellver Castle crowns a hill 3 kilometers away. This circular 14th-century fortress—unique in Europe—commands sweeping views across the entire bay.
Passeig del Born is Palma's elegant artery. Lined with plane trees and modernist facades, it connects Plaça de la Reina to the waterfront.

activities.text: The coastal bike path follows the bay for several kilometers, tracing the waterfront from Parc de la Mar to the city beaches.
Ensaimada is best savored in traditional pastry shops. This spiral brioche dusted with powdered sugar defines Majorcan baking.
Position yourself at Bellver Castle as evening falls. The sun sinks behind Palma while lights begin to twinkle across the city.
Olivar Market overflows with local produce. Fresh fish, sobrassada, Mahón cheese, and Majorcan vegetables crowd the stalls.

days_plan.itin_title: Majorca Road Trip: Serra de Tramuntana & Hidden Coves

places.name: Palos De La Frontera

visits.text: Columbus stayed at this Franciscan monastery, La Rábida, several times—notably in 1485 and 1491, when he was seeking backers for his project. The monks sheltered him, interceded at court, and introduced him to Martín Alonso Pinzón, a local shipowner. On August 3, 1492, the three ships left the port of Palos with 90 men. The monastery preserves Vázquez Díaz's frescoes on the discovery.

On the Río Tinto, the three full-scale replicas of the Pinta, Niña, and Santa María have floated since 1992, built for the 500th anniversary. The interiors are visitable: the cabins are cramped and the bunks unthinkable for a 90-day crossing. Allow 1.5 hours. The river bearing them is already orange—the mine water begins here.

activities.text: Moguer residents will tell you that Platero is as famous as Columbus in the region. Juan Ramón Jiménez, born here in 1881, received the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1956 for his collection about the white donkey. The birthplace is now a museum. Moguer is 7 km from Palos—a 30-minute detour worth the stop.

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva, the Forgotten Land: Doñana to Faro in 7 Days

map_keywords: La Rábida Monastery Palos de la Frontera

visits.text: Pampaneira is touristy. We know it, and we don't care: at 1,058 meters in the Poqueira gorge, this Morisco village classified as a historic ensemble keeps its flat roofs in slate slabs, its tubular chimneys, and its Mudéjar church Santa Cruz, the finest of the three gorge villages. The architecture resembles Rif and Atlas villages—same Berber heritage.

The Santa Cruz church in Pampaneira, built in the 16th century in Mudéjar style, has preserved its coffered ceiling in mulberry wood. Inside, sober and cool. The San Antonio fountain on the square, called the bachelors' fountain, is said to help those who drink from it find a partner—the tradition is still cited seriously by elders.

activities.text: Shops selling jarapas (Alpujarran weavings made from recovered fabric scraps) line Pampaneira's alleys. It's not high craft, but it's authentically local and cheap. The Hilacar workshop, in operation for several decades, is one of the few still weaving on traditional looms—visits are free.

days_plan.itin_title: Al-Andalus: From the Mezquita to the Alpujarras in 10 Days

visits.text: Santa María Cathedral shelters the alabaster tomb of Charles III the Noble and his wife Eleanor. Sculptor Janin Lomme personalized the faces of the recumbent figures and varied the poses of the 28 mourners surrounding them.
The Gothic cloister dates from the 14th and 15th centuries. Its elegant Gothic bays topped with ornate gables lend remarkable lightness to the entire ensemble.
Walk the five kilometers of ramparts classified as a National Monument. The pentagonal citadel from 1571, designed by Giacomo Palearo, is the oldest fortress of its kind in the world.
Pass through the Portal de Francia with its still-operational drawbridge. Pilgrims on the Camino have crossed this gateway for centuries.
The old town preserves its medieval layout, born from the 1423 merger of three rival boroughs: the Navarrería, San Cernin, and San Nicolás.
The Church of San Cernin houses the chapel of the Virgen del Camino, featuring a 12th-century statue set within a spectacular baroque altarpiece dripping with gilt.

activities.text: Join the terraces along Calle Estafeta for pintxos and wine. This narrow street becomes the running route for the encierros during the San Fermín festival.
Taconera Park offers shade and cool respite from the city heat. Its French-style gardens date from the 19th century.
The Museum of Navarre displays Romanesque capitals salvaged from the former cathedral and the Holy Sepulchre reliquary gifted by Saint Louis in 1258.
Climb to the Portal Nuevo viewpoint for sweeping vistas over the fortified city and the distant Pyrenees beyond.

days_plan.itin_title: Navarre Road Trip: Castles, Irati Forest & the Camino

places.name: Parque Natural De Arribes Del Duero

visits.text: The Arribes del Duero Natural Park stretches for nearly 100 kilometers of canyons carved by the Duero River through granite, forming the natural border between Spain and Portugal. The cliffs plunge up to 500 meters—the deepest gorges in the entire Iberian Peninsula. The Mirador del Fraile, at Aldeadávila de la Ribera, offers the most vertiginous viewpoint: a walkway overhangs the canyon directly, not recommended if you have a fear of heights. From there, the Duero's waters seem motionless, they're so far below. Bring water as the trails are exposed.

The Pozo de los Humos is one of the peninsula's most spectacular waterfalls: the Uces River plunges into the canyon from over 50 meters, creating a permanent cloud of spray that gave it its name ('well of smoke'). The trail from Masueco is short (2.3 km round trip) and accessible to all, but stones are slippery in wet season. In summer, flow can be low depending on the year's rainfall—choose spring or autumn to see the falls at full force.

Make a detour through Fermoselle, a town declared a historic and artistic site, perched on the heights with views over the confluence of the Duero and Esla. The town is nicknamed 'the town of a thousand cellars'—caves carved into the rock beneath houses to store wine from the Arribes terroir, a developing denomination of origin with eleven vineyards planted on terraces. The viewpoints at the castle and Las Escaleras are worth the stop.

activities.text: Walk sections of the GR-14, the long-distance trail that follows the Duero from its source to Porto. In the Arribes sector, several sections above the gorges are accessible in 1 to 3 hours. The microclimate warmer than the rest of Castile—due to the deep gorges trapping heat—allows olive trees, almonds and vines to grow at this latitude. Look up: golden eagles, griffon vultures and black storks nest on the rock walls.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

map_keywords: Mirador del Fraile Aldeadávila de la Ribera

places.name: Parque Natural De Las Lagunas De Ruidera

visits.text: The Lagunas de Ruidera natural park strings together 15 lagoons over 20 km along the upper course of the Guadiana, separated by natural travertine barriers. The total elevation drop of 128 meters creates a succession of waterfalls between each basin.
Start at the Cascada del Hundimiento, the park's highest waterfall, just steps from the village of Ruidera. It's the starting point of the left-bank trail, the wildest and best-preserved route.
The Laguna del Rey is the park's deepest lagoon. From the Mirador de la Laguna del Rey, the view sweeps across its turquoise waters bordered by reeds. A developed beach allows swimming in summer.

The Laguna Colgada ('hanging' lagoon) extends into a tongue of land that advances into the water. At its tip, small waterfalls feed the Laguna de Batana below—a bucolic landscape typical of tufa formations.
The Laguna Lengua offers the park's most spectacular colors: deep blue and emerald green. The limestone rock barriers and waterfalls carving their way through create an almost surreal setting.

The Cueva de Montesinos, a cave mentioned by Cervantes in Don Quixote, opens onto the plateau of Campo de Montiel. This is where the errant knight claimed to have spent three days in an enchanted palace.

activities.text: Paddle a kayak on the Laguna del Rey or Laguna Colgada. The calm, crystal-clear waters allow you to glide along the travertine cliffs and access beaches and caves unreachable on foot.
Hike the left-bank trail between the Cascada del Hundimiento and Laguna Redondilla. The path crosses shaded zones and offers plunging views over the successive lagoons.
At dawn, the lagoons attract grey herons, coots, ducks and raptors. The marsh reeds and quiet shores of the park make excellent terrain for birdwatching.
Set up a picnic in the designated areas by the lagoons. The turquoise waters and birdsong compose an ideal setting for a nature lunch break.

days_plan.itin_title: La Mancha Road Trip: Don Quixote's Windmills and Cuenca

visits.text: Drive 45 minutes southeast to Brihuega, nicknamed the 'Garden of Alcarria'. If you visit between late June and mid-July, the lavender fields surrounding the village put on a breathtaking show—endless acres of violet stretching to the horizon, with the Lavender Festival held the second weekend of July. Outside the flowering season, Brihuega still rewards a visit: medieval ramparts with the fortified gates of Cadena and Cozagón, three handsome 13th-century Romanesque churches (Santa María de la Peña, San Miguel, San Felipe), and the former Real Fábrica de Paños, a royal cloth manufactory founded in the 18th century by Charles III.

Continue 30 minutes south to Pastrana, the 'ducal town' of Alcarria, a designated Historic Ensemble since 1966 and member of Spain's Most Beautiful Villages. The Ducal Palace, designed by Alonso de Covarrubias in the 16th century with a façade replicating Toledo's Alcázar, is inseparable from the Princess of Éboli—Ana de Mendoza y de la Cerda. One-eyed (she wore an eyepatch), beautiful, scheming, first Duchess of Pastrana then mistress of Antonio Pérez (Philip II's secretary), she ended up imprisoned by the king in the palace's east tower (the Cámara de la Reja Dorada) from 1581 until her death in 1592. Look up at the barred window from Plaza de la Hora—that's where she watched the world go by.

Don't leave Pastrana without visiting the Tapestry Museum in the collegiate church of the Asunción. The six 15th-century Flemish-Gothic tapestries, woven in Tournai from cartoons attributed to Nuño Gonçalves, depict the conquest of North African cities by King Afonso V of Portugal. They rank among the world's most important tapestries from this period. The collegiate church itself, of Templar origin (12th century), holds paintings by El Greco and Carreño de Miranda. The Princess of Éboli is buried in the crypt.

activities.text: Pastrana is also linked to Saint Teresa of Ávila, who founded two Discalced Carmelite convents here in 1569—one for women (San José) and one for men (San Pablo, now del Carmen), where Saint John of the Cross served as novice master. The Carmen convent houses a small Teresian museum and a natural history museum. Stroll through the lanes of the upper town, between Calle Princesa de Éboli lined with noble mansions and Calle de la Palma with the House of the Inquisition and the House of the Calatrava Knight.

days_plan.itin_title: Alcarria and Guadalajara: Between Madrid and Aragon
Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

visits.text: Pedraza is the best-preserved medieval village in Castile and León—perhaps in all Spain. Declared a historic site in 1951, it has only one access gate (16th-century wood), cobbled lanes lined with houses bearing noble coats of arms, and an arcaded Plaza Mayor ranking among the region's most beautiful. Park at the outside lot and enter on foot. To avoid weekend crowds, prefer a weekday or arrive before 10am.

Pedraza's castle occupies a rocky outcrop at the village's edge. Built in the 13th century, transformed into a Renaissance palace in the 16th-17th centuries, it was saved from ruin by painter Ignacio Zuloaga who made it his residence and studio. Its keep now houses a museum with some of his works. A little-known historical anecdote: the sons of King Francis I of France were imprisoned in this castle in 1526 as hostages to guarantee the Treaty of Madrid—they stayed four years. Book tickets online.

Pedraza's medieval prison (Cárcel de la Villa), a 13th-century building rebuilt in the 16th, is one of Spain's best preserved. It's visited with guided tours that recreate medieval prison life. If you're there on the first or second Saturday evenings of July, don't miss under any circumstances the Noche de las Velas: electric lights are turned off and thousands of candles illuminate the lanes while concerts take place outdoors.

activities.text: Stroll Calle Mayor between blazoned houses and noble 16th-17th century constructions, witnesses to the village's textile prosperity.
Savor cordero asado, wood-oven roasted lamb that made Pedraza's gastronomic reputation. The British Times in 2024 ranked restaurant El Soportal among the 28 best Mediterranean tables.
Attend the Candle Nights on the first two Saturdays of July. Thousands of candles illuminate lanes, balconies and squares while classical music concerts resound.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

visits.text: Pedroche is the village that gave its name to the entire comarca. Perched on a hill, it's the former capital of the Siete Villas de los Pedroches—the seven villages that governed themselves in the Middle Ages. The landmark monument is the Torre de Pedroche, a 56-meter-high bell tower that dominates the entire landscape. Begun in the 15th century, it was completed by Hernán Ruiz II—the same architect as at Hinojosa, the Mezquita, and the Giralda. Four superimposed sections (square, octagonal, square, cylindrical) rise above the church of El Salvador (Gothic-Mudéjar). Climb to the top if access is open—the view embraces the entire dehesa as far as the eye can see. Around the Plaza de las Siete Villas, the narrow lanes with granite houses preserve the atmosphere of a medieval Sierra Morena market town.

Drive 15 minutes east to Dos Torres, whose historic center is classified as a Property of Cultural Interest. This village was born from the merger of two medieval settlements—Torrefranca and Torremilano—in 1839. The Pedroches granite architecture is best preserved here: manor houses with dark stone façades highlighted with white lime, carved coats of arms, wrought-iron balconies. The Centro de Interpretación de la Arquitectura Popular explains local construction techniques. Don't miss the Plaza de Toros integrated into the urban fabric and the Pozo de la Nieve (snow well), where ice was stored in winter.

Push 10 minutes south to Añora, the smallest village in Los Pedroches. Its dark granite houses striped with white bands create a unique architectural style in the comarca. The church of San Sebastián (16th century) is considered one of the purest examples of Mudéjar art in Los Pedroches. The village is tiny—30 minutes suffices to tour it—but the end-of-the-world atmosphere is total.

activities.text: The Valle de los Pedroches is classified as a Starlight Reserve by UNESCO for its night sky quality—very little light pollution in this rural area. If you stay a night in Pedroche or Pozoblanco, take advantage of it to observe the stars. Operators offer astronomical observation sessions with telescopes, especially in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Córdoba and the Sierra Morena: From the Caliphate to the Dehesas of Los Pedroches

places.name: Penafiel

visits.text: Two hours from Barajas, and suddenly the landscape tips: goodbye Sierra de Guadarrama, hello Castilian plateau. Wheat fields stretching forever, sparse trees, endless sky. This is Ribera del Duero—a fertile desert at 800 meters altitude, and that's precisely why the grapes here are exceptional.

Peñafiel Castle is absurd—in the best possible way. 210 meters long, 20 meters wide, perched on a cerro (an oblong hill), locals say it looks like a ship at anchor. The 34-meter Torre del Homenaje spirals upward to views across three valleys (Duero, Duratón, Botijas). Built from the 10th century onward, rebuilt in the 14th-15th centuries by Don Pedro Girón, declared a National Monument in 1917. Since 1999, the southern courtyard houses the Provincial Wine Museum, a modern and pedagogical journey through Valladolid province's five DOs. Ticket €6.60, closed Mondays.

Plaza del Coso de Peñafiel: one of the few wooden bullfighting squares still active in Castile. Rectangular, surrounded by houses with wooden balconies that serve as stands during the ferias. Outside festival season, it's simply a beautiful medieval plaza where old men gather in the shade. The neighboring collegiate church of San Juan Bautista dates from the 15th century.

Pago de los Capellanes in Pedrosa de Duero—the name comes from a chaplaincy that existed on these lands in the 13th-14th centuries, confiscated during the 1855 disentailments, but locals continued calling the place by its historic name. The estate was reconstituted in 1996 by the Rodero Villa family. One hundred hectares of Tempranillo, mostly trained on espalier. The value is excellent at every level—Crianza, Reserva, Parcela El Nogal. Tour + tasting €25, warm staff, real pedagogy on Ribera soils.

Sunset from the bridge at Quintanilla de Onésimo over the Duero. The river is wide, vineyards descend to the bank, late-day light in May or September turns the valley golden. This isn't a monument. It's just beautiful, and worth 20 minutes.

The village of Pedrosa de Duero, 600 inhabitants, has an 18th-century Baroque church and several stone manor houses that 19th-century disentailment didn't entirely dismantle. One of many Ribera villages where vernacular Castilian heritage—granite, slate, interior courtyards—survives between ultramodern bodegas.

activities.text: Welcome dinner at Mesón Las Carrigüelas or any asador in Peñafiel for lechazo: Castilian milk lamb, roasted at 180°C in a wood-fired clay oven, served by the quarter with a few potatoes and nothing else. This is Ribera's totem dish. Pair it with a Crianza from Protos or Pesquera—you can't go wrong.

Comparative tasting at Bodegas Protos in Peñafiel—one of the DO's oldest cooperatives (1927). The modern winery, designed by Richard Rogers (architect of Centre Pompidou), plays with ceramic vaults. Tour + 3-wine tasting, around €20. Good warm-up to calibrate your palate for the coming days.

Lunch in Pedrosa after the Pago de los Capellanes visit, in any village bar. If nothing opens on a Tuesday at 2pm—which happens in Castile—head to Pesquera or Peñafiel. Flexibility is the primary skill required for traveling in Ribera del Duero.

Closing tasting of the Golden Mile at Bodegas Pago de Carraovejas in Peñafiel. The estate, founded in 1987, is one of the most serious in the Peñafiel zone—Pago de Carraovejas Reserva is the reference cuvée. The tour includes underground cellars beneath the vineyard. Around €30.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria
Castile and León - 14-Day Grand Tour
Ribera del Duero in 7 days: The Plateau That Rivals Rioja
Ribera del Duero in 7 days: The Plateau That Rivals Rioja

map_keywords: Pago de los Capellanes Pedrosa de Duero

places.name: Penalba De Santiago

visits.text: To reach Peñalba de Santiago, you must cross the entire Valley of Silence by a single-lane road once called 'the monks' path.' Perched at 1,100 meters in the Aquilanos mountains, this village of about fifty inhabitants is one of Spain's most beautiful: stone and slate houses with wooden balconies, lanes so narrow they seem never to have changed since the 10th century. Park at the entrance lot—the lanes are impassable by car.

The Mozarabic church of Santiago de Peñalba is the only remnant of the monastery founded by San Genadio in the 10th century, completed in 937 by Abbot Salomón. It's a jewel of Spanish Mozarabic architecture—a style born from the fusion of Visigothic traditions and Arab influences. Enter through the double door with horseshoe arches supported by three marble columns: inside, the horseshoe arches divide the nave, and remnants of medieval wall paintings survive on the walls. This is where the body of San Genadio rests.

From the village, a half-hour walk on the old monks' path leads to the Cave of San Genadio, carved into a vertical rock face at the very entrance to the Valley of Silence. The Bishop of Astorga retreated here to meditate in the 9th century. A local legend says he ordered the Oza River to be silent so as not to disturb his prayer—and the river disappeared underground at that spot.

activities.text: The Valley of Silence is also a beautiful invitation to hike in a landscape of oak, chestnut and walnut forests crossed by the Oza River. The trail climbing to the Monastery of San Pedro de Montes (founded in the 7th century by San Fructuoso, rebuilt in the 12th-13th centuries) is one of the region's most beautiful. Allow 3 hours round trip from Peñalba.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

map_keywords: Monasterio San Pedro de Montes Bierzo

visits.text: Playa de Sotavento stretches for kilometers, famous for its lagoon that forms at low tide. Shallow turquoise waters, ever-shifting sandbanks, and spectacular vistas make this beach a photographer's dream come true.

The Faro del Matorral lighthouse, standing 60 meters tall, visually divides Playa del Matorral into two distinct zones. It's a prime observation point for watching sunsets paint the Atlantic in brilliant hues.
The Saladar de Jandía, a protected wetland, harbors the Canaries' largest salt flats. This area of scientific interest preserves a rare salt-resistant plant species found almost nowhere else.

Journey to the Punta Jandía lighthouse at the island's southwestern tip, built in the 19th century to guide ships toward Africa. The dramatic route cuts through Jandía Natural Park, revealing desert landscapes and wave-battered cliffs.
Stroll through Morro Jable village, once a humble fishing port. The marina now welcomes up to 300 yachts, while narrow streets shelter boutiques and restaurants serving the day's fresh catch.

activities.text: Relax on Playa del Matorral's 4 kilometers of golden sand, equipped with beach bars, restrooms, sun loungers, and lifeguards. Perfect for families seeking comfort and safety without sacrificing natural beauty.
Kitesurf and windsurf at Playa de la Barca. Constant Atlantic winds make Sotavento one of the world's premier spots, hosting international competitions that draw elite riders.
Join a boat excursion to observe dolphins and whales. Success rates run 50-70% depending on conditions. Curious and playful dolphins often approach boats, delighting passengers with their acrobatics.
Hike to Pico de la Zarza (807m), Fuerteventura's highest peak. The summit reveals panoramic views over the wild Cofete beach and the entire Jandía Peninsula stretching toward the horizon.

days_plan.itin_title: Fuerteventura Road Trip: Dunes, Lagoons & Timeless Villages

visits.text: The castle of Peñíscola has dominated the rocky promontory since 1307. Built by the Knights Templar atop an Arab alcazaba, it became the palace of Pope Benedict XIII—Papa Luna—after 1411.
Step through the Portal de San Pedro to enter the old town. Cobbled lanes climb between whitewashed facades all the way up to the castle's parade ground.
Renaissance ramparts wrap around the peninsula for 2 kilometers. From the muralla del Olvido, you can take in the entire coastline and the old town spread out below.
The church of Santa María occupies a former mosque converted in the 14th century. Its bell tower has served as a landmark for sailors for centuries.
The Casa de las Conchas boasts a facade entirely covered in seashells—a unique decoration commissioned by a local family in the 19th century.
Walk out to the lighthouse built in 1899, still keeping watch over the Mediterranean horizon. The surrounding maritime garden offers a cool refuge from the sun.
The Artillery Park spreads out at the foot of the castle. Its terraced gardens host bird-of-prey shows and provide access to the underground casemates.

activities.text: Playa Norte stretches for 2 kilometers of fine sand. Its calm waters are perfect for swimming with views of the castle perched above.
Rice with cuttlefish stains the dish a deep black. This emblematic specialty is best savored at restaurants along the harbor or the seafront promenade.
Find a spot on the rocks as the day draws to a close. The sun drops behind the castle while lights begin to flicker on throughout the old town.
Want to see the castle from a different angle? Rent a kayak to paddle along the base of the rock and discover its marine caves.

days_plan.itin_title: Castellón and Maestrazgo Route: Castles and Coves
Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages

visits.text: Plasencia keeps its treasures close to the chest. Founded in 1186 by King Alfonso VIII of Castile, it boasts a unique quirk in Spain: two cathedrals stuck side by side. The Catedral Vieja, begun in the 13th century, is a fine example of Romanesque-Gothic transition—sober, compact, with a baroque altarpiece and a stunning ivory Christ. The Catedral Nueva, started in the 15th century, was never finished—but what exists is breathtaking: soaring ribbed vaults, a magnificent altarpiece attributed to Gregorio Fernández, and a heady mix of flamboyant Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque. They're visited together—don't choose, see both.

The Plaza Mayor of Plasencia has been the beating heart of the city since medieval times. Irregularly shaped and ringed by soportales (arcades) lined with bars and restaurants, it hosts a market every Tuesday that's been running uninterrupted since the 12th century. Grab a terrace seat and watch the unhurried rhythm of this 40,000-soul town that carries itself like a regional capital.

Plasencia is part of the Red de Juderías de España—the network of Spain's historic Jewish quarters. The Jewish community settled here from the city's founding in 1186. Traces survive in two neighborhoods: the old judería de la Mota (around today's Parador, a former San Vicente Ferrer convent built on the site of the first synagogue confiscated in 1477) and the "new judería" around Calle Trujillo and Calle Zapatería. In these alleys, plaques set into the pavement bear the names of Jewish families who lived here—Yuçe Alaçán, Abraham Almale, Samuel... Don't miss the Jewish cemetery of El Berrocal outside the walls: it's the only preserved Jewish burial ground in Extremadura, with about twenty anthropomorphic tombs carved into rock, facing Jerusalem.

Walk the circuit of Plasencia's medieval ramparts, still well-preserved along much of their length. You'll pass through the Trujillo, Coria, and Berrozana gates and cross paths with the acueducto—a restored medieval aqueduct running through the San Antón neighborhood. The Palacio del Marqués de Mirabel, built on the site of the old judería de la Mota in the 15th century, is worth a look for its facade and garden (visible from the street).

activities.text: Northern Extremadura's cuisine is generous and underrated. Try Torta del Casar—a creamy soft-paste cheese eaten with a spoon—migas extremeñas (fried breadcrumbs with chorizo and pimiento), jamón ibérico de bellota, and Valle del Jerte cherries in season. The Plaza Mayor and surrounding streets are packed with tapas bars where you'll eat well and abundantly.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas

places.name: Ponferrada

visits.text: The Templar Castle of Ponferrada is one of the most impressive medieval fortresses in northwest Spain. In 1178, the kings of León ceded the town to the Order of the Temple to protect pilgrims on the Camino. The Templars remained until 1312, when the Order was dissolved. The castle spreads over 8,000 square meters with a complex system of courtyards, barbicans, and towers—the original twelve reproduced constellations. In 1850, the town council sold the walls as a quarry—it took designation as a National Monument in 1924 to halt the destruction. Climb the rampart walk for views over the city and the Bierzo mountains.

The 16th-century Renaissance Basilica de la Encina houses the statue of the Virgin de la Encina—patroness of El Bierzo. According to legend, the Templars found her hidden in an oak trunk (encina) during the Reconquista. Right next door, the Renaissance Clock Tower and the Baroque Town Hall form one of the city's best-preserved civic ensembles.

Peñalba de Santiago perches at 1,110 meters elevation at the bottom of the Valle del Silencio—a valley so deep-cut into the Aquilanos mountains that the Oza River briefly disappears underground, hence the name. Saint Genadio, bishop of Astorga, withdrew here starting in 920 to live as a hermit until his death in 936. He had founded a monastery of which only the Mozarabic church of Santiago remains, completed in 937 by Abbot Salomon—one of the rare and most beautiful examples of preserved Spanish Mozarabic art. Its south portal, with its double horseshoe arch supported by three marble columns with Corinthian capitals, is remarkably graceful. The interior harbors early-medieval wall paintings uncovered during recent restorations. The village itself, with its stone and slate houses with wooden balconies, has been classified as a Cultural Heritage Site since 1998. Plan for the road—narrow and winding, it can be closed in winter.

From the village, a 30-minute trail leads to the Cueva de San Genadio, the cave carved into the vertical cliff at the valley's entrance where the saint withdrew each night to pray. The view from the cave over Peñalba's slate roofs below is the iconic image of the Valle del Silencio.

activities.text: Visit the Museum of Bierzo, housed in the former 15th-century Convent of San Francisco. It traces the comarca's natural and human history, with a remarkable room devoted to the Roman mines—Ponferrada sits at the heart of Las Médulas territory, the most productive gold mine in the Roman Empire.

Evening stroll along the Río Sil. The riverbank has been turned into a tree-lined promenade; it's locals' favorite spot for jogging and aperitifs. The illuminated castle viewed from the riverbanks is the city's signature photo.

Hiking in the Valle del Silencio from Peñalba: the trail continues beyond the Cueva de San Genadio toward the monastery of San Pedro de Montes, founded in the 7th century by Saint Fructuoso and restored by San Genadio in 895. The complete round trip from Peñalba is about 10 km for a half-day in oak forest.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province
The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province

places.name: Pontevedra

visits.text: Pontevedra is a revelation. City of 80,000 inhabitants, provincial capital, and above all: historic center entirely pedestrianized since 1999. Not a single motor vehicle in the entire old town—one of Spain's most radical urban mobility projects, rewarded with several international prizes. Result: quality of life, terraces everywhere, children playing in streets, near-zero accident rate. The center organizes around five interconnected squares: Ferrería, Curros Enríquez, Leña, Verdura, Teucro.

25 km north of Pontevedra, the excursion to Cambados is essential for anyone interested in Galician wine. The town is Albariño's capital, this mineral and fresh white that's become one of Spain's great whites in thirty years. The center focuses around Plaza de Fefiñáns, classified national monument: the Renaissance Pazo de Fefiñáns (1535-1647) with its baroque arch spanning the street, the late Gothic Church of San Bieito, and the 14th-century defense tower. Irregular cobbles, wine merchants under arcades. Sublime at sunset.

In Cambados, on Monte da Pastora hill 10 minutes from the center, the ruins of Santa Mariña Dozo. 16th-century Gothic church deserted in the 19th, fallen to ruin. But the ground between collapsed arches still serves as parish cemetery: tombstones set in grass between broken columns, vaults open to sky, lichens, moss. All of Galicia's most photogenic place, and one of the most moving. Freely accessible, never many people. View over Arousa ria.

The Church of La Peregrina, at the historic center's entrance, has a unique form: octagonal cross plan, meaning 45° angles rather than 90°. Built in the 18th century (1778) between baroque and neoclassical. Symbolic form: the scallop shell. Pontevedra was a Camino Portugués stop, and La Peregrina is the Portuguese way's patron saint. The interior is equally unexpected—small, central, luminous. Statue of the Virgin in 18th-century polychrome wood, venerated by sailors.

The Basilica of Santa María la Mayor, overlooking the Lérez River, has a Plateresque façade carved by Cornelis de Hollanda (1541-1559)—Renaissance ornamentation of a fineness recalling goldsmithing. Inside, late Gothic hall plan, baroque altarpieces, and the curious "Chapel of the Christ of the Good Voyage"—devotion of sailors departing on the high seas. Built with guild funds from the mareantes (sailors), at Pontevedra's 16th-century commercial peak. The city was then larger than Vigo or A Coruña.

The Museum of Pontevedra is one of Galicia's best museums—six interconnected buildings, free. Archaeology collection: Caldas treasure (Bronze Age gold, 12 kg), Roman funerary stelae. Fine arts: Castelao (caricaturist-painter, Galician cultural hero), Sotomayor, Flemish painting. Room with the intact cabin of the frigate Numancia, first armored ship to circumnavigate the world (1865-1867). Count 2 hours for everything. Free access. Often nearly empty.

activities.text: The Pazo de Fefiñáns in Cambados houses the oldest Albariño cellar, founded in 1904 by the Marquis of Figueroa himself. Guided tour of the Renaissance palace, tasting of the estate's three signature Albariños and marc brandies. One hour, around €18. Here you understand the wine's identity: granite soils, Atlantic climate, manual harvest, parcels under one hectare divided among hundreds of winegrowers. Over a hundred producers in a few square kilometers.

Tour of the five plazas. Ferrería (former blacksmiths, terraces), Curros Enríquez with its fountain, Leña (most picturesque, vegetable market in the morning), Verdura (shopping street), Teucro (most aristocratic, 18th-century palaces). Each square has its atmosphere: Leña for morning coffee, Ferrería for aperitif, Verdura for shops. Pontevedra is a city where you happily waste time—it's rare.

Tapas dinner around Plaza de la Leña—Ribadavia, Casa Fidel, Eirado da Leña (fancier). Pontevedra has an excellent network of traditional bistros that haven't yielded to tourist fashion. Specialties to try: empanada de zamburiñas (queen scallop pie), berberechos al natural (cockles), tarta de Mondoñedo (almond and cider cake). Wines: Albariño, Rosal, Condado do Tea—all Rías Baixas appellations represented.

days_plan.itin_title: Galicia Road Trip: Santiago de Compostela and the Coast of Death
Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Galicia: Complete 10-Day Circuit

map_keywords: Basilica Santa María a Maior Pontevedra
Ruins of Santa Mariña Dozo Cambados

visits.text: From the Torre del Homenaje of Puebla de Alcocer Castle, the embalse de La Serena stretches out like an inland sea. This dam, the largest in Spain (3,220 hm³), could contain the city of Madrid within its 530 km of shoreline. The 13th-century castle, remodelled in the 15th by the Master of Alcántara, is open for guided tours.

The old folk of Puebla tell of a secret passage linking the castle to the nearby village of Galizuela, repopulated by Galicians after the Reconquest. No one has found it, but the story fuels the evenings. The other local celebrity: Agustín Luengo, born here in 1826, stood 2.35 m tall.

The Iglesia de Santiago, a former mosque converted in the 15th century, blends Gothic and Mudéjar with a Plateresque portal. Inside, 13th-century frescoes sit alongside later additions. Look for the funerary slab of Fray Fernando de Moraga, tireless traveller.

Look for the Ermita de la Cueva, nestled on the castle hill. Its white bell tower gives it away. Spaniards know the nursery rhyme "Que llueva, que llueva, la Virgen de la Cueva": tradition places it here.

activities.text: If you arrive at sunset, climb to the castle mirador: reflections on the embalse de La Serena and the Zújar turn orange. Sunrises are equally spectacular. Parking at the foot of the village, walk up.

The Palacio de los Duques de Osuna, in the centre, now houses the library and cultural centre. Fine seigneurial building, free. Also spot the Casa de la Inquisición and the Casona de San Juan, witnesses to the town's judicial and religious past.

days_plan.itin_title: La Siberia and La Serena: Secret Extremadura in 6 Days

visits.text: Puebla de Sanabria is one of Spain's most beautiful villages—official label—perched at 960 meters at the confluence of the Tera and Castro, 20 km from the Portuguese border. In the 13th century, it was one of the Kingdom of León's most important strongholds facing Portugal. The Counts of Benavente owned it for four centuries. The rua, the main cobbled lane, climbs in switchbacks between houses with stone coats of arms, flowering gardens, iron balconies. The center crosses in 30 minutes but you'll spend the morning.

The Counts of Benavente's castle crowns the town—15th century, square plan, multi-story keep nicknamed "El Macho." One of Castile's best-preserved castles. Restored, it today houses a fortifications interpretation center on four levels: models, videos, interactive games on the Luso-Castilian border's military history. Ideal for children. From the keep, panoramic view over the Sierra de la Culebra and—on clear days—to Portugal's peaks.

On Plaza Mayor, the Church of Nuestra Señora del Azogue—late 12th century, Romanesque transitioning to Gothic. Three semi-circular apses, 13th-century baptismal font adorned with carved figures, Renaissance altarpiece. "Azogue" comes from the Arabic name for the market held here. The Virgin of Victories, inside, is the town's patron saint—feast September 8, procession through medieval streets with giants and big heads. More discreet: the Giants and Big Heads Museum, next door, displays processional figures since the 19th century.

activities.text: Excursion to Lake Sanabria, 12 km north—the Iberian Peninsula's largest glacial lake, 369 hectares, 50 meters deep, formed 100,000 years ago by glacier retreat. It even has small "waves" in strong wind, like an inland sea. Possible summer swimming beaches (Viquiella and Custa Llago). Outside summer, hike to Laguna de los Peces—altitude lagoon at 1,707 meters—or to the Sotillo waterfalls. The water is icy all year, even in August.

15 minutes south, the Iberian Wolf Center in Robledo (Centro del Lobo Ibérico) hosts injured wild wolves or those born in captivity. Several nature observation trails with hide-cabins allow seeing packs from a distance—without disturbance. The neighboring Sierra de la Culebra shelters Western Europe's largest wolf population. Open only at certain hours for guided tours, mandatory reservation in season. Moving, especially for children.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Castile and León - 14-Day Grand Tour

places.name: Puente Del Congosto

visits.text: Puente del Congosto owes its name and existence to a single feature: the bridge over the Tormes. This strategic crossing on the Cañada Real Soriana Occidental—the great transhumance route descending toward Extremadura—generated enough toll revenue to finance castle, church, and village. The old bridge, built on orders of the Catholic Monarchs around 1500, is solidly founded on granite bedrock and counts 13 arches; it still retains one of its two original defensive towers. Declared a Site of Cultural Interest with historic site status in 2019, it's the last village in the province to receive this title.

The Dávila Castle, on the left bank of the Tormes, was built in the 14th century to defend and control the strategic bridge crossing. In 1393, King Enrique III ceded the lordship of Puente del Congosto to Gil González Dávila, who rebuilt the fortress. In 1539, the Duke of Alba bought it from Charles V and added a second twin tower. Beautifully restored, it's now private property and can only be viewed from outside—but the silhouette from the bridge is remarkable.

Don't leave without walking the riverside path along the Tormes—the crystalline waters and natural swimming holes make Puente del Congosto a popular summer stop. The entire setting, declared a UNESCO biosphere reserve in 2006, is ideal for spotting European bee-eaters and grey herons along the banks.

activities.text: For whitewater enthusiasts, the Tormes upstream from Puente del Congosto offers rafting sections navigable in spring and early summer. Local outfitters offer 2- to 3-hour trips on the river's rapids. Inquire at the tourist office.

Come evening, the menu del día in village bars invariably features hornazo—a savory pie with ham and hard-boiled eggs, a Salamanca specialty you'll find throughout the province. Also try the cured meats from the Sierra de Béjar.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

map_keywords: Église Nuestra Señora de la Asunción Puente del Congosto

places.name: Puerto De La Cruz

visits.text: Lago Martiánez covers 100,000 m² of seawater pools, gardens, and terraces. Designed by César Manrique in 1977, the complex integrates volcanic rock, sculptures like Las Raíces al Cielo (upside-down eucalyptus trees), and a giant fountain of volcanic stone feeding 27,000 m³ of seawater.
The La Orotava Botanical Garden was founded in 1788 by order of King Charles III. Across 20,000 m², more than four thousand tropical species from around the world flourish among fountains and ponds.

Head to Plaza del Charco, the beating heart of the old town, then walk down to the old harbor where fishing boats land their catch in the early morning. The Hermitage of San Telmo, built in 1780, watches over sailors from the waterfront.

The Castillo de San Felipe, a former defensive fortress, marks the entrance to Playa Jardín—a trio of black volcanic sand beaches landscaped by César Manrique with gardens of endemic species and waterfalls.
The Church of Nuestra Señora de la Peña de Francia, built in 1684 on the site of an older hermitage, dominates the main square. Its tower dates from 1898. Inside, paintings by Canarian masters.

activities.text: Climb to Mirador de La Paz for sweeping views of the Atlantic, Martiánez beach, the Lago, and the Orotava Valley with Teide rising in the background. On clear days, the island of La Palma emerges on the horizon.
Martiánez beach—350 meters of black sand beneath cliffs—draws surfers year-round. Waves roll in with a laid-back atmosphere.

Sample volcanic wines from the Tacoronte-Acentejo appellation at a guachinche, those ephemeral traditional restaurants where you'll also find papas arrugadas, rabbit in salmorejo sauce, and grilled vieja fish.

days_plan.itin_title: Canary Islands Road Trip: Tenerife & Gran Canaria
