places.name: Puerto Del Rosario

visits.text: The city has transformed into an open-air museum with over 150 sculptures scattered throughout its streets. Juan Bordes' whimsical snail-shaped works line the coastal promenade, bringing contemporary art to the waterfront.

The Iglesia Nuestra Señora del Rosario, a 19th-century neoclassical edifice, houses a sculpture of the Virgin of the Rosary and remarkable red stone stained glass windows. The square bell tower commands the historic quarter.

Visit the Casa Museo Miguel de Unamuno, the former hotel where Spain's great philosopher lived in exile in 1924. Period furniture, photographs, and his writings chronicle his island wanderings and philosophical reflections.

The lime kilns (Hornos de Cal) stand as monuments to the city's economic past. In the 18th century, Puerto del Rosario became the "Port of Lime," exporting burned limestone to England for construction.
Playa Chica, recently expanded, offers an urban beach right in the heart of the capital. Showers and facilities make it perfect for a quick swim between city explorations.

activities.text: Stroll along Calle Primero de Mayo, the main pedestrian street lined with boutiques and terraces. This is where locals and visitors converge for evening paseos.
Browse the weekly artisan market for PDO Majorero cheese, aloe vera products, and traditional crafts. It's your chance to taste the island's renowned goat cheese at its source.
Sample vieja (local parrotfish) grilled or fried, roasted goat, and papas arrugadas with mojo sauce. The island's goat cheese carries a protected designation of origin and appears on every menu.

days_plan.itin_title: Fuerteventura Road Trip: Dunes, Lagoons & Timeless Villages

map_keywords: Iglesia Nuestra Señora del Rosario Puerto del Rosario
Promenade Maritime Sculptures Puerto del Rosario
Casa Museo Miguel de Unamuno Puerto del Rosario
Hornos de Cal Puerto del Rosario

visits.text: Puertollano is an industrial town with little charm, but it marks the start of the route into Valle de Alcudia. The Municipal Museum displays Carboniferous fossils from the area and traces the zone's mining history. Quick stop.
If you're passing outside museum hours, fill up and take the road west. The N-420 cuts through dehesa and holm-oak landscapes that already hint at Extremadura.

days_plan.itin_title: Campo de Calatrava and Valle de Alcudia in 5 Days

places.name: Rascafria

visits.text: The Monastery of Santa María del Paular was founded in 1390 by Enrique II of Castile. Castile's first Carthusian monastery blends Gothic and Baroque styles. An exceptional cloister, intricately worked high altar retable, and transparente await inside. Architects Jean Goas and Juan Gil de Hontañón left their mark.

The 18th-century Puente del Perdón crosses the río Lozoya in three granite arches. Stone benches on the pillars once allowed witnesses to watch trials. The condemned could receive pardon on this bridge—their last hope before the Casa de la Horca (House of the Gallows).

The Purgatory Waterfalls cascade down the Arroyo del Aguilón. Two spectacular drops: the first plunges 10 meters and is accessible by platform, the second falls 15 meters and requires scrambling. Carthusian monks once meditated here to purge their sins.

The Lozoya Valley, dubbed the "Jewel of Guadarrama," unfolds in forests of oak, pine and birch. Crystal-clear waters of the Lozoya wind through verdant meadows. Panoramic views stretch to Cuerda Larga and the Montes Carpetanos.
Explore the village of Rascafría, whose name derives from "carrasca fría" (cold oak). Traditional mountain architecture, cobbled lanes leading toward forest trails and high country.

activities.text: Hike to the Purgatory Waterfalls. Start from the monastery, cross the Puente del Perdón, then follow the Arroyo del Aguilón through oak groves. About 6 km one way with 250 meters of elevation gain.
Discover Las Presillas, natural swimming pools in the río Lozoya. Cool, clear waters, shaded picnic areas. Very popular in summer, ideal for cooling off after a mountain hike.
Wildlife watching in the forests: red deer, wild boar, squirrels. Raptors soaring above the valleys. Early morning or late afternoon offer the best chances for sightings.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Guadarrama Road Trip: Castles and Forests

map_keywords: Monasterio Santa María del Paular Rascafría

visits.text: Valencians simply call it 'la Villa'—Requena's Medieval Quarter, built by the Arabs at the top of the hill between the 8th and 11th centuries, has been a BIC since 1966. Access via the Cuesta del Castillo under a stone arch. Inside: cobbled alleys, Palacio del Cid, Torre del Homenaje, and Gothic churches (Santa María, El Salvador). Free stroll.

The Cuevas de la Villa, under the Plaza de la Villa, form a Moorish-era underground labyrinth (12th-18th centuries): 22 galleries that served as wine cellars, silos, and wartime shelters. Entry €4 (reduced €3). Tuesday-Thursday and Sun 9:30 a.m.-2 p.m. The guide explains how the hill's topography allowed these galleries—fascinating for 45 minutes.

activities.text: Take time for a tasting in the bodegas of the DO Utiel-Requena before leaving town: the Bobal variety (documented from the 15th century in the region according to Jaume Roig) produces frank, fruity rosés and reds here, unlike anything found in France. The tourist office directs you to bodegas with visits and tastings, including those in historic underground galleries.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: Reus witnessed Antoni Gaudí's birth in 1852 - the house where he was born has disappeared, but the Gaudí Centre, opened in 2007 on Plaça del Mercadal, reconstructs his childhood and formal obsessions. Three floors, interactive models, French audioguide included. The most moving piece: his final glasses, found on him after the 1926 tram accident. Small but well-executed, allow 1 hour, about €9. Combine with coffee on Plaça del Mercadal right in front - shaded terraces and aligned modernist facades.

Casa Navàs, on the same Plaça del Mercadal, is Lluís Domènech i Montaner's masterpiece in Reus - built in 1901-1907 for a textile wholesaler, it's the only modernist house in Spain entirely preserved with its original furniture. Stained glass by Rigalt, mosaics by Mario Maragliano, marquetry by Gaspar Homar. The 1938 bombing destroyed the tower but spared the interior. Guided tour required, 50 minutes, €16 - not cheap, but without equivalent. Advance booking essential, limited time slots.

The Institut Pere Mata, on Reus's northern edge, is Domènech i Montaner's most overlooked project - a private psychiatric hospital built in 1898 within a 25-hectare park, still operating today. The Distinguidos pavilion (for wealthy clientele) is open for visits: ceramics, mosaics, stained glass, floral sculptures. Freer and more experimental than Casa Navàs - a laboratory before constructing Barcelona's Hospital de Sant Pau. Guided tour €6, Saturday mornings only. Niche but sublime.

The Modernisme Route in Reus connects 26 buildings across 2 km in the center - Casa Rull, Casa Gasull, Casa Pinyol, Casa Anguera. Free map at the tourist office, downloadable audioguide. Reus was Catalonia's second-largest city by population after Barcelona in the 18th-19th centuries, its fortune built first on aiguardent trade (the expression "Reus, Paris, London" designated the three markets where its global price was set), then on vermouth, viticulture, and textiles. Industrialists competed through extravagant facades. Ideal stroll in late afternoon when sunlight gilds the ceramics. Allow 2 relaxed hours.

activities.text: Reus is also the birthplace of Catalan vermouth - the city where Yzaguirre, Miró, and Rofes began distilling it in the 19th century. The Museu del Vermut, a bar-museum on Carrer Sant Joan, displays 6,000 different bottles from five continents and serves the ritual glass at €4-5. Guided tasting at €12 with reservation. Reusencs still say "Anem a fer un quinzet" - let's have a quinzet (15 cl), an expression born here. At noon or aperitif hour, difficult choice.

The central market at Plaça de la Llibertat, built in 1949 in neo-modernist style, buzzes every morning except Sunday. Stalls of dried fruits (Reus is Spain's hazelnut capital, PDO Avellana de Reus), fish from Cambrils, hinterland charcuterie. Tapas bars in the upper gallery: toasts with anchovies and roasted peppers, €6. Noisy, local atmosphere, ideal between 10am and noon.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Catalonia in 10 Days: Tarragona, Priorat & the Ebro Delta
Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour

visits.text: First stop: Celanova, 30 minutes south of Ourense. The Monastery of San Salvador, founded in 936 by San Rosendo (a Galician bishop and saint), is one of the most important in Galicia. The church, rebuilt in Baroque style in the 17th–18th centuries, impresses with its monumental façade and regular cloister. But Celanova's true treasure hides behind the monastery: the Chapel of San Miguel, a tiny 10th-century Mozarabic oratory, one of the few pre-Romanesque buildings in all of Galicia. Its horseshoe arches and miniature proportions transport you to an era when Christians and Muslims shared a common architectural vocabulary.

Continue to Allariz, 20 minutes north. This medieval village on the Arnoia River won the European Prize for Urban Planning in 1994 for its exemplary restoration. Stroll along the river, cross the stone bridges, and climb into the historic centre where granite lanes preserve the atmosphere of a medieval Galician town. The 12th-century Romanesque church of Santiago and the Convent of Santa Clara (which houses a rare medieval rock-crystal crucifix) are worth a stop. Allariz is also a gastronomic village, renowned for its artisan bakeries and the summer bica (traditional cake) festival.

End the day at Ribadavia, the former capital of the medieval province of Ribeiro. The historic centre preserves one of Galicia's best-preserved Jewish quarters—the judería is organized around narrow lanes, with noble houses bearing coats of arms and the former synagogue (identifiable by its floor plan). After the expulsion of the Jews in 1492, the quarter was gradually absorbed, but the architectural traces remain visible. The castle of the Counts of Ribadavia dominates the village, and the Praza Maior, lined with granite arcades, is the perfect place to enjoy a glass of Ribeiro—Galicia's oldest white wine, produced here since Roman times.

activities.text: If you're visiting Ribadavia during the last weekend of August or the first of September, you'll stumble upon the Festa da Istoria—a medieval re-enactment where the entire village dresses up, the streets transform into a medieval market, and life in the judería is recreated. It's one of the most authentic historical festivals in Galicia.
Sample Ribeiro wine at the bars around Praza Maior—it's a light, fresh white, perfect with fish tapas and Galician empanadas. Several estates in the region offer tours and tastings.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Galicia: From Ourense to the Ribeira Sacra

places.name: Ribeira Sacra

visits.text: Welcome to the Ribeira Sacra, one of Spain's most spectacular yet least-known regions. Canyons 500 metres deep carved by the Sil and Miño rivers, Romanesque monasteries clinging to cliffsides, and terraced vineyards so steep that harvesters sometimes work roped to the vines. The name Ribeira Sacra (Sacred Shore) comes from the concentration of monasteries founded between the 6th and 12th centuries along these gorges. Begin at the Mirador de Balcones de Madrid, one of the most vertiginous viewpoints: you're suspended more than 300 metres above the Sil canyon, with terraced vineyards plunging towards the river like a natural amphitheatre.

Descend to the Monastery of Santo Estevo de Ribas de Sil, founded in the 6th century and rebuilt in the 12th–13th centuries. Its three superimposed cloisters—Romanesque, Gothic, and Renaissance—are a lesson in architectural history in a single place. The monastery has been converted into a national Parador (state-run hotel)—even if you're not staying, you can enter to visit the cloisters and have coffee in the gallery overlooking the canyon. The Romanesque church preserves the tombs of nine bishops who retired here in the Middle Ages.

Push on to the Monastery of San Pedro de Rocas, the oldest monastic site in Galicia (founded in the 6th century). Three chapels are carved directly into the granite rock—a unique case in Galicia. Inside, a rare medieval mappamundi (world map) painted in fresco is partially preserved. The place is isolated, deep in a chestnut forest, and the silence is total. The three stone bell towers emerging from the rock are the iconic image of the Ribeira Sacra.

activities.text: The catamaran cruise through the Sil canyons is the signature experience of the Ribeira Sacra. For 1.5 to 2 hours, you glide up the gorge between 500-metre walls covered with oaks and terraced vineyards. Several embarkation points exist (Santo Estevo, Abeleda). Book ahead in summer—places fill up quickly.

If you're a wine lover, visit a Ribeira Sacra bodega. The terraced vineyards, some with gradients of 50–60%, produce reds based on Mencía—an indigenous grape variety that yields elegant, fruity wines. Harvesting is done by hand, sometimes with ropes to prevent slipping. It's one of the most heroic vineyards in Europe.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Galicia: From Ourense to the Ribeira Sacra

map_keywords: Mirador de Balcones de Madrid Ribeira Sacra

visits.text: Riópar Viejo was abandoned in the 18th century when Carlos III authorised Viennese engineer Johann Georg Graubner to exploit the zinc mines in 1772—the workers left to settle in the new industrial town below. What remains today is a small restored stone hamlet, with the ruins of the 13th-century Muslim castle, a cemetery in the parade ground, and the Iglesia del Espíritu Santo whose façade hides recently rediscovered 15th-century frescoes. Free access, free visit; road from Riópar (6 km).

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

places.name: Roa De Duero

visits.text: Valbuena de Duero shelters the most mythology-laden estate in Spanish viticulture. Vega Sicilia, founded in 1864 by Eloy Lecanda, who returned from Bordeaux with 18,000 vine cuttings in his luggage—Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Malbec—to plant alongside the local Tinto Fino. The Álvarez family purchased the estate in 1982, the very year the DO Ribera del Duero was created. Coincidence or destiny? The first cuvées (Único and Valbuena) date from 1915. Único, the grand vin, can spend ten years in barrel before bottling—a patience virtually no other great house in the world demands. Annual production never exceeds 200,000 bottles.

Bodegas Alión, 10 minutes from Vega Sicilia, is the Álvarez family's second property. More modern style, more accessible, excellent value. Tour + tasting around €50. If Vega Sicilia granted you a slot, do Alión the following day. If not, this is your Plan A, and there's no shame in that.

Hotel Castilla Termal in Valbuena occupies a former 12th-century Cistercian monastery converted into a Relais & Châteaux. Even if you're not staying overnight, the architecture merits a look: the cloister, vaults, gardens facing the Duero. Stop for lunch at the restaurant if budget allows.

The Dane arrives in 1995, finds 4 hectares of old Tinto Fino vines in Quintanilla de Onésimo, rents a garage, and makes his first wine. Peter Sisseck names his cuvée 'Pingus,' his childhood nickname. Robert Parker tastes it at the March 1996 en primeur and writes on the cover of his Wine Advocate that it's one of the greatest young wines he's ever tasted. First vintage production: 325 cases of 12 bottles. The shipment crossing the Atlantic to the United States sank in November 1997—virtually all of the American 1995 was lost. Today, fewer than 500 cases are produced annually, sold en primeur before bottling. Visits by appointment only, highly restricted.

Hacienda Monasterio in Pesquera de Duero—the estate Peter Sisseck has managed since 1990, even before Pingus. This is where he learned Ribera, and the atmosphere is far more relaxed than at his personal project. Certified organic, 30 rooms in a former abandoned monastery. Tour + tasting €30. The Reserva Especial is the reference cuvée, complex and approachable.

Bodegas Aalto, in the municipality of Quintanilla de Arriba. Mariano García did his first harvest at Vega Sicilia in 1968—he was 22. He left 30 years later, in 1998, and founded Aalto the following year with Javier Zaccagnini. The stated goal: make a wine that reaches world-class level within 15-20 years. Many think it's already done. The winery controls 130 hectares of old vines across 9 municipalities in Burgos province, Tinto Fino only. The PS (Pagos Seleccionados) is the great parcel cuvée, fewer than 10,000 bottles per year.

Bodegas Emilio Moro in Pesquera de Duero—three generations of the same family on the same terroir. The particularity: Malleolus de Valderramiro is vinified from vines planted in 1924, exactly one century old. These vines produce less than one kilo of grapes each, but with a concentration that crushes all young plantings. The tour (€30) is well-constructed, didactic, pedagogical without condescension—a good entry point if this is your first encounter with Ribera.

Roa de Duero, seat of the DO's Consejo Regulador since 1982, is also a medieval town with a secret beneath its cobblestones: over 150 cave cellars dug from the 13th century onward to store wine at stable temperatures. The galleries form a network running beneath almost every house. Some are open to the public via the tourism office (Bodega de la Coopérative, private caves by appointment), others remain privately owned—their owners still keeping a few barrels down there. The contrast is striking: steel and concrete bodegas on the surface, an 800-year-old underground network just below.

The church of Asunción de Roa, 15th-century Gothic, and the remains of medieval ramparts merit a tour. The mirador at the edge of the old town offers views across terraced vineyards descending toward the river. This is the landscape that explains why Roa is here and not elsewhere.

activities.text: Lunch at Restaurante Liturgia in Quintanilla de Onésimo—revisited Castilian cuisine, pleasant dining room, terrace. Without excessive gastronomic pretension, but sincere and well-executed. 10 minutes from the bodegas.

Walk along the Duero between Valbuena and Quintanilla in late afternoon. The riverside path runs alongside vineyards, the river is wide and calm at this latitude. This is the kind of walk where you understand why so many people wanted to make wine here.

Lunch between afternoon visits in Pesquera de Duero—Asador Mesón La Vid for another lechazo, or simply croquetas and Castilian charcuterie in a village bar. Riojan gastronomy loses ground here to a more sober, direct cuisine.

Dinner in Roa at Restaurante Asador Nazareno: cordero lechal in traditional wood-fired oven. This is the most authentic version you'll find in the zone—no white tablecloths, no menu in French, just an oven and a few tables. Reservations advised on weekends.

Lunch in Pesquera after the Emilio Moro visit—Asador Mesón La Vid if you haven't done it yet, otherwise any village bar for pinchos and a glass from the tap. There's something deeply healthy about eating roast lamb standing up in a village bar of 300 inhabitants after visiting a winery that produces 800,000 bottles a year.

Guided tour of Roa's underground caves with the local tourism office—about 1.5 hours. The guide explains the medieval technique of digging into limestone rock, the natural ventilation system, and shows some caves still used for aging. Book the day before or two days ahead.

days_plan.itin_title: Ribera del Duero in 7 days: The Plateau That Rivals Rioja
Ribera del Duero in 7 days: The Plateau That Rivals Rioja
Ribera del Duero in 7 days: The Plateau That Rivals Rioja

map_keywords: Vega Sicilia Winery Valbuena de Duero
Dominio de Pingus Quintanilla de Onésimo
Bodegas Emilio Moro Pesquera de Duero

places.name: Roncesvalles

visits.text: The Collegiate Church of Santa María at Roncesvalles is a major example of French Gothic in Navarre. Built in the 13th century on the orders of Sancho VII the Strong, it follows the model of Notre-Dame de Paris.
The square Gothic cloister replaced in 1600 its predecessor that had collapsed under snow. Its galleries provide access to the chapel of San Agustín.

In the chapel of San Agustín lies King Sancho VII the Strong in a 2.3-meter tomb corresponding to his height in life. The chains taken at the Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa adorn the coat of arms of Navarre.
The Chapel of the Holy Spirit, or Silo de Charlemagne, from the 12th century is the oldest building. According to legend, Roland planted his sword here after the defeat of 778.
The museum displays Charlemagne's Chessboard, a gilt and enameled silver reliquary, a 12th-century evangeliary, and Miramamolín's emerald taken from the Moorish king.
Since the 10th century, Roncesvalles has welcomed pilgrims after the arduous Pyrenean ascent. For many, it marks the starting point of the Camino de Santiago in Spain.

activities.text: Walk a short stage of the Camino toward Zubiri through beech and oak forests. Yellow waymarkers line the path.
Restaurants serve river trout and local sheep's cheeses. Pyrenean gastronomy marries products of livestock farming and fishing.
Ascend to the Ibañeta Pass at 1,057 meters elevation where the original fortress once stood. A modern chapel commemorates the 778 Battle of Roncesvalles.
At twilight, watch the raking light sweep across the Lindus and Ortzanzurieta mountains, which rise above 1,000 meters. Silence reigns over the high-altitude pastures.

days_plan.itin_title: Navarre Road Trip: Castles, Irati Forest & the Camino

map_keywords: Collegiate Church of Santa María Roncesvalles
Chapel of the Holy Spirit Roncesvalles

places.name: Ronda

visits.text: Ronda sits astride a 100 m deep canyon—el Tajo—that cuts the city in two. The Puente Nuevo, completed in 1793 after 40 years of construction, is the mythical bridge linking the two banks. During August 1936, the Republicans of Ronda who controlled the city threw hundreds of Nationalists alive from the top of the Tajo—the episode is recounted by Ernest Hemingway in chapter 10 of 'For Whom the Bell Tolls' (1940), who had lived in Ronda. A few months later, the Nationalists recaptured the city and executed in turn. The bridge today serves as a postcard; the toll of both purges remains a sensitive local subject.

The Plaza de Toros de Ronda, completed in 1785, is Spain's oldest bullfighting arena still in operation and the birthplace of modern bullfighting on foot. Before 1726, bulls were killed from horseback. Francisco Romero (from Ronda) invented bullfighting on foot with muleta here, codified by his grandson Pedro Romero (1754-1839), who reportedly dispatched more than 5,000 bulls without a single injury during his career. Capacity 5,000 seats, two levels of golden stone arcades. Bullfighting museum inside, open except on bullfight days. 9 euros.

The Arab Baths of Ronda, built in the 13th century in the Moorish quarter of San Miguel downhill, are the best-preserved Arab baths in Spain. Classic three-room plan (cold, warm, hot), vaults pierced with star-shaped skylights for light, still-visible functional hypocaust. Public baths from Al-Andalus times, abandoned after 1485 (reconquest of Ronda). 4 euros, closed Mondays.

The Palacio de Mondragón, former Nasrid palace remodeled in the 16th century after the reconquest, preserves three Mudéjar patios with original azulejos. Now houses the municipal archaeology museum, with Bronze Age objects found in the Sierra de Grazalema. Spectacular view from the terrace over the sierra and plain. 4 euros.

The Casa del Rey Moro, on the edge of the Tajo, never housed a Moorish king despite its name—it's an 18th-century palace built on Nasrid foundations. Its centerpiece: the Mina, a staircase carved into rock in the 14th century that descends 231 steps to the bottom of the gorge to reach the Guadalevín River. During sieges, Christian prisoners were forced to haul water up in jugs. Very narrow, dim light, not for the claustrophobic. 8 euros.

activities.text: Dinner at Tragatá (calle Nueva 4), inventive tapas by Benito Gómez—double Michelin-starred chef with Bardal 100 m away. Tragatá is the informal version, gently priced: liquid potato tortilla, baby squid croqueta, fried sea anemones. 25-35 euros per person. No reservations, line at 8 PM.

Sunset at the Mirador de Ronda (paseo Blas Infante, just in front of the bullring). Plunging view over the sierra, golden light on the old town's white walls. Classic Tajo photo accessible from the edge by walking 200 m.

days_plan.itin_title: Andalusia Road Trip: Seville, Córdoba, Granada & Ronda
Dark Andalusia: 10 Days from Seville to Málaga
White Villages: Arcos, Grazalema, Ronda - 6 Days
White Villages & Sierras: 9 Days Málaga-Almería

map_keywords: House of the Moorish King Ronda

visits.text: Climb into the Maestrazgo mountains. Cantavieja, the comarca's capital, perches on a limestone rock at 1,300 m altitude. This triangular-plan village was a Templar commandery from 1169, then passed to the Hospitallers after the dissolution of the Order of the Temple. The medieval streets converge on the Plaza Porticada, one of the province's most beautiful arcaded squares, dominated by the imposing Baroque church of the Asunción and the town hall. The Carlist War Museum traces the battles of the 'Tiger of Maestrazgo,' General Ramón Cabrera, who made Cantavieja his headquarters in 1836.

Descend south (1h mountain drive) to Rubielos de Mora, in the Gúdar-Javalambre comarca. Nicknamed the 'Court of the Sierra' for the number of nobles who settled here in the 16th-17th centuries, this town at 1,000 m altitude possesses a remarkable collection of Renaissance mansions and palaces. The Excolegiata Santa María la Mayor (16th century) houses a large 15th-century International Gothic altarpiece dedicated to the life of the Virgin. The Casa Consistorial (town hall) is a fine Renaissance building. Rubielos is part of the Cittaslow (slow cities) network - a label that suits it perfectly.

activities.text: Enjoy Rubielos de Mora's peaceful atmosphere at day's end. Stroll beneath the arches of the Portal del Carmen and Portal de San Antonio, the two medieval gates still standing. The convents of the Discalced Carmelites and Augustinians are open for visits. In the evening, the Plaza de la Villa gently comes alive with its terraces - this is deep Spain, without tourists, where time seems to slow down.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel

visits.text: Fifteen lagoons linked by travertine waterfalls over 30 kilometres: the Lagunas de Ruidera shift colour with the light—turquoise to the west, emerald green to the east. The Natural Park straddles Albacete and Ciudad Real. The CR-650 road runs alongside the lower lakes; stop at each viewpoint. Avoid August: area is packed. Spring and autumn offer the clearest waters.

In the Quijote, Cervantes sets here the episode of the Cueva de Montesinos: Don Quixote descends 80 metres into a cave and sleeps three days (outside, only two hours pass). The cave still exists, accessible to the public with fitted stairs—a strange, dark place inhabited by bats. Ideal if you're reading Cervantes en route.

Castillo de Peñarroya marks the lower entrance to the park, on the edge of the reservoir lake of the same name. The partly ruined medieval fortress dominates the lake: from its ramparts, the entire chain of lower lakes becomes visible. Free access. Budget 20 minutes to walk around it.

activities.text: You can rent a kayak at Ruidera to paddle Laguna del Rey—one of the few areas where swimming is allowed in the park. Rental outlets on site (check in season). Alternative: the hiking trail from Laguna Cenagosa to Laguna Blanca (6 km one way, linear, little elevation) runs alongside several waterfalls and crosses fragrant tamarisks.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: Hannibal besieged Sagunto for eight months in 219 BC—the episode that triggered the Second Punic War with Rome. The hill still bears the traces of that clash: Iberian enclosure, then Roman, then Arab, all superimposed in readable strata. Allow a good hour for the full circuit.

From the castle summit, the plain spreads to the sea and the chimneys of Valencia port point southward. The 2nd-century Roman theatre, controversially restored in the 1990s, still hosts summer performances. Tickets available on the municipal website.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages

places.name: Sajazarra

visits.text: The castle-palace of Sajazarra raises its rectangular keep flanked by four octagonal turrets, built in the 15th century when the Velasco lords made this town their stronghold. Still privately owned, it can't be toured inside but you can walk the entire perimeter.

The church of Santa María de la Asunción, dating from the 12th–13th centuries, stands a few steps from the castle. Founded with the Cistercian monastery in 1171, it houses a statue of the Virgen de la Antigua.
Wander the cobbled lanes of this 120-resident village named one of Spain's Most Beautiful Villages. Stone houses bear family crests and ogival arches.
Pass under the restored medieval archway that pierces the remnants of the ramparts. This gate once marked the fortified entrance to the medieval town.
Walk down to the banks of the Río Ea. This stream borders the village and irrigates the surrounding vineyards.

activities.text: Visit a small family estate for a tasting. Sajazarra's winemakers cultivate their parcels on the slopes of the Montes Obarenes.
Settle on a village bench at sunset. The hillsides turn golden and the castle stands in dark silhouette against the sky.
Walk among the vines that surround Sajazarra. Rows of Tempranillo roll toward the Río Tirón, which flows into the Ebro.

days_plan.itin_title: La Rioja Circuit: Wine Route and Monasteries

map_keywords: Iglesia Santa María de la Asunción Sajazarra

visits.text: Salamanca's Plaza Mayor is considered Spain's most beautiful baroque square—and it's hard to argue. Commissioned in 1729 by Philip V in gratitude to the city for its support in the War of Succession, designed by Alberto de Churriguera, completed in 1755. 88 arcades on three sides, the fourth occupied by the town hall. Look for the stone medallions in the spandrels: Christopher Columbus, Cervantes, Cortés, Franco (yes, removed and restored depending on the era). The local sandstone turns orange at sunset—hence the nickname "golden city."

The University of Salamanca, founded in 1218 by Alfonso IX of León, is Spain's oldest university and Europe's fourth oldest (after Bologna, Paris, Oxford). Christopher Columbus defended his project here in 1486; Antonio de Nebrija published the first Spanish grammar here in 1492. The Plateresque façade of the Escuelas Mayores (1529) is so finely carved it resembles stone goldsmithing. The challenge: find the lucky frog on one of the three skulls in the pediment. Students say you'll pass your exams if you spot it alone. Hint: on the right, on the rightmost skull. Tell no one.

Salamanca has two adjoining cathedrals, the only one in Spain. The Old Cathedral, from the 12th century, Romanesque, preserves in its apse an altarpiece of 53 panels painted by Nicolás Florentino around 1445—one of Spain's largest Gothic ensembles, topped by a fresco of the Last Judgment. The New Cathedral, late Gothic then baroque, was begun in 1513 without destroying the old one—which is rare. Curiosity: on the north façade (Ramos door), an astronaut and a faun eating ice cream were carved in 1992 during restoration. Look closely.

Facing the university, the Casa de las Conchas—"House of Shells." Built between 1493 and 1517 by Don Rodrigo Maldonado de Talavera, university professor and knight of the Order of Santiago. The façade is adorned with 365 carved scallop shells (one for each day of the year), symbol of the order to which he belonged. Today a public library, free access to the interior courtyard and first floor. According to a legend at least four centuries old, one of the shells hides an ounce of gold. No one has ever found it. And no one has the right to look underneath.

The Convent of San Esteban, ten minutes from Plaza Mayor, is one of Salamanca's finest Plateresque buildings—a 30-meter-high façade-altarpiece in stone carved by Juan de Ribero between 1524 and 1610, with a relief scene of Saint Stephen's Stoning. A Dominican convent where Christopher Columbus stayed in 1486 during negotiations with university theologians. The main altarpiece by José de Churriguera (1693) is a 27-meter-high baroque explosion. The Renaissance cloister, by Juan de Álava, is a haven of calm.

Right next door, the Convent of Las Dueñas has an absolutely unique hexagonal cloister—there's no other in Spain with this shape. Built in the 16th century for Dominican nuns, it's still inhabited by 9 cloistered sisters who sell pastries via a rotating turnstile (you speak through the wall, put money in the turntable, it rotates, cookies arrive). Especially: the cloister capitals. Grotesque sculptures, demons, monsters, rare for a convent—scholars still disagree on their meaning.

Casa Lis, above the Tormes against the old walls, houses the Museum of Art Nouveau and Art Deco, one of Spain's best. The house itself, built in 1905 by modernist Joaquín de Vargas, is a marvel—multicolored stained glass flooding the hall, floral ironwork, marble and bronze staircase. Collection: 19,000 pieces including porcelain dolls by Bru and Jumeau, bronzes by Demêtre Chiparus, Émile Gallé furniture. Very intimate visit, never crowded.

The Roman Bridge—Puente Mayor del Tormes—dates from the 1st century CE and was part of the Vía de la Plata, the Roman road linking Mérida to Astorga. The southern half was rebuilt in the 17th century after a flood. At the bridge's end, a pre-Roman sculpture—perhaps a Vettonian verraco, perhaps an Iberian bull—over 2,000 years old. The bridge is pedestrian, perfect for photographing both cathedrals from the other bank. Lazarillo de Tormes, Spain's first picaresque novel (1554), begins precisely when Lazarillo's master forces him to ram his head against this stone.

activities.text: Tapas crawl in Calle Van Dyck or around Plaza Mayor. Salamanca is one of Spain's tapa capitals—here it's still offered with your drink, unlike Madrid or Barcelona. Try the hornazo (pâté of meat, serrano ham, chorizo and hard-boiled egg), chanfaina (ratatouille with rice and beef tongue), and especially Guijuelo ibérico ham, 30 km from Salamanca—one of Spain's best PDOs, raised on acorns in the oak groves of the Sierra de Béjar.

Evening nightlife. Salamanca is 30,000 students out of 145,000 inhabitants. The Calle Bordadores barracks and Plaza de San Juan Bautista concentrate student bars; for mojitos and young party scene, Calle Compañía. For something more intimate, Calle Quintana where the tradition of the tuna (student songs in medieval costume, lamp and guitar) still survives—one of the last places in Spain where it's still living and not tourist folklore.

If the sky is clear, hot air balloon flight at sunrise over Salamanca—the operation starts around 6am, lasts one hour, unmatched view of the two cathedrals and the Tormes plain. Several operators (Aerotours Castilla, Globos Boreal) from €175. The whisper of the burner, slanting light on sandstone, and upon return traditionally a champagne country brunch. Essential reservation in season.

Walk in Barrio del Oeste, behind the train station. Post-Franco working-class neighborhood that has metamorphosed into an open-air street art gallery since 2013—Galería Urbana festival. Over 100 murals by international muralists: Kraser Tres, Phlegm, Borondo. It's the perfect antithesis to the golden sandstone of the center. Pick up the free map at the Plaza Mayor tourist office.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks
Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route
Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Castile and León - 14-Day Grand Tour
Castile and León - 14-Day Grand Tour
Mystical Castile: Toledo, Ávila, Salamanca & Burgos in 11 Days

visits.text: From a distance, the castle seems to crush the village - up close, it's what saved it. Originally a Phoenician fortress, rebuilt by the Romans, then transformed into a Nasrid palace. The Hall of the Sultans preserves its stucco work. Entry €4. Ticket includes the archaeological museum below. Allow 1h.

The mirador at the village summit opens onto a double panorama: the Mediterranean on one side, the sugar cane plain on the other. These fields between Salobreña and Motril are the last in Andalusia - cultivation dates back to the Arabs and has survived thanks to the Costa Tropical's subtropical microclimate. Five-minute walk from the castle.

activities.text: The whitewashed limestone lanes hold the cool even in high summer - the Arabs designed it well. La Bóveda, an underground passage that once linked the Albaicín to the medina, is still traversable. Short but striking: the acoustics are those of a cellar. Allow 30 minutes for a tour of the center.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Coast: Málaga to Cartagena in 9 Days

places.name: San Carlos Del Valle

visits.text: Locals call it the 'Vatican of La Mancha': the Plaza Mayor of San Carlos del Valle, built between 1713 and 1729, forms a 53-by-21-meter rectangle ringed by Tuscan columns and wooden galleries. The church of Santísimo Cristo del Valle dominates the ensemble with its dome and four towers topped by Madrid-style capitals. Listed as a Site of Cultural Interest since 1993.

The church interior stuns with its proportions: the octagonal dome rises over 28 meters, and the spire peaks at 47 meters. Late Baroque with neoclassical touches. The village barely tops 1,100 residents, which makes the building's scale all the more striking.

activities.text: Sit under the plaza's arcades and observe: the rectangular shape, designed like a Baroque theatre, once hosted performances and public ceremonies. Three brick arches frame views of the Manchego landscape beyond.

days_plan.itin_title: Campo de Calatrava and Valle de Alcudia in 5 Days

map_keywords: San Carlos del Valle Plaza Mayor Ciudad Real

places.name: San Felices De Los Gallegos

visits.text: San Felices de los Gallegos encapsulates the turbulent history of the Spanish-Portuguese frontier. The castle was built in 1296 by King Denis I of Portugal, who had just occupied the town. The annexation by Portugal was confirmed in 1297 at the Treaty of Alcañices. In 1326, San Felices returned to Castilian control as a royal marriage dowry. But that was far from the end of sovereignty exchanges: the town was retaken by the Portuguese during the Castilian War of Succession in the 15th century, then pillaged again during the Portuguese Restoration War in 1646. Its castle is the best-preserved of the four in Arribes del Duero Natural Park.

The Puente de los Franceses (Bridge of the French) spans the Águeda River below the village—the only crossing point of the Arribes de l'Águeda. According to tradition, it was built by Gauls settled in the region, whose name may be the origin of 'Gallegos' in the village name. The descent to the bridge crosses steep slopes of the riverbank with dense Mediterranean vegetation (olive trees, cork oaks) characteristic of the Arribes microclimate.

The Arribes del Duero form one of Spain's most spectacular landscapes: 200-meter-deep canyons carved by the Duero and its tributaries on the Portuguese border. The viewpoint at Mesa del Conde (Table of the Count), accessible from San Felices, offers a vertiginous panorama over the gorges. In 1476, the inhabitants of San Felices revolted against their tyrant lord Gracián de Sessé and hanged him from the tower arch—legend says the shadow of the hanged man stretched all the way to the square.

activities.text: The marked trails of the Arribes del Agueda departing from San Felices offer impressive views of the gorges and colonies of griffon vultures, black storks, and Bonelli's eagles. The avifauna here is among Spain's richest. Choose a morning outing for the best observation conditions.

The AOC Arribes del Duero wines, produced from centuries-old vines on terraces exposed to the gorges' Mediterranean microclimate, are a discovery for many visitors. The heat reflected from the rock walls produces powerful reds based on Juan García and Rufete. A visit to one of the local estates is a must.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

map_keywords: Château San Felices de los Gallegos
Muraille médiévale San Felices de los Gallegos
Pont des Français San Felices de los Gallegos
Belvédère Table du Comte San Felices de los Gallegos

places.name: San Jose

visits.text: The strangest beach in the country: black volcanic rock, golden sand and turquoise water that have nothing to do with each other—except that geology has assembled them across 300 metres. Playa de Mónsul, accessible from San José, served as the backdrop for Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade in 1989, for the scene of the Luftwaffe plane crash. Parking 400 m away, €5 in season.

Black volcanic rock, turquoise sea, short grass and constant wind: the Cabo de Gata lighthouse marks the south-eastern tip of the Iberian Peninsula. From the viewpoint, the Arrecife de las Sirenas is visible in transparency. The natural park covers 37,500 hectares on land and 12,000 hectares at sea, a UNESCO biosphere reserve since 1997.

The Salinas de Cabo de Gata are active salt marshes that attract flamingos on migration stopover, visible from September to February. The tower of the Las Salinas church, built in 1907 right in the middle of the basins, is one of the most improbable visual landmarks in Andalusia. Free ornithological observatory on the dyke, car access.

activities.text: If car access to Playa de los Genoveses is closed (common in summer), the path from San José is 4 km. This large golden sandy bay surrounded by dunes is one of the finest protected Mediterranean coasts in Western Europe. Set off at 7:30am to arrive before the morning rush.

Local divers jealously guard their spots in the park: the seabed of Cabo de Gata shelters posidonia, grouper and sea bream in waters among the clearest on the Spanish coast. Several dive centres in San José offer 2-hour trips for all levels.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Coast: Málaga to Cartagena in 9 Days
White Villages & Sierras: 9 Days Málaga-Almería

map_keywords: Playa de Mónsul Cabo de Gata

places.name: San Millan

visits.text: The monastery of Suso clings to the flank of the Sierra de la Demanda, founded in the 6th century. Hermit disciples of San Millán carved these caves that became the cradle of the Spanish language. The Glosas Emilianenses—10th-century annotations in Castilian and Basque—were written here.

At the monastery of Yuso, built downhill in the 11th century and rebuilt between the 16th and 18th centuries, marvel at the Baroque frescoes in the sacristy and the 11th-century ivory caskets holding San Millán's relics.
The library at Yuso preserves medieval manuscripts and illuminated codices. Its scriptorium was one of medieval Spain's most brilliant centers of learning.
Climb to the burial chamber at Suso. The founder's 12th-century black alabaster tombstone rests in the shadowy Romanesque vaults.
Look up at Yuso's bell tower and slate roofs dominating the Cárdenas Valley. The Sierra de la Demanda unfolds behind, crowned by San Lorenzo peak (2,262 m).

activities.text: Hike the Iregua Valley winding between oak groves and vineyards. Marked trails lead to hermitages tucked into the hills.
Photograph Yuso's Renaissance facades at dusk. The low light carves out the reliefs of the 1661 Baroque portal.
Settle in for a picnic at the edge of the vineyards. The silence of the monastic valley is broken only by birdsong.

days_plan.itin_title: La Rioja Circuit: Wine Route and Monasteries

map_keywords: Sierra de la Demanda San Millán

visits.text: La Concha Bay and old quarter Parte Vieja.
Mount Igueldo — funicular and views.
Aquarium and fishing port.

activities.text: Dinner at gastronomic society (by reservation).
Morning swim at Ondarreta.
Paddle boarding along the bay.

days_plan.itin_title: Bay of Biscay Coastal Drive: Fishing Villages & Basque Culinary Soul in France

places.name: Sanlucar De Barrameda

visits.text: Santiago Castle has dominated Sanlúcar since the 15th century. Built by the Dukes of Medina Sidonia to control the Guadalquivir estuary, it preserves the Puerta de la Sirena, a sculpted Gothic arch. From the Torre del Homenaje, panoramic views embrace whitewashed houses, the river, and Doñana's marshlands across the water.

Magellan set sail from Sanlúcar in 1519 on the first circumnavigation of the globe. The "Legua Cero" monument at Bajo de Guía marks this historic departure point. In the former ice factory, now Doñana's visitor center, a replica of the Nao Victoria retraces the expedition that changed the world.

Climb from the Barrio Bajo to Barrio Alto through zigzagging lanes inherited from Moorish times. The 14th-century Gothic church of Nuestra Señora de la O features a richly carved Mudéjar portal. The Palace of the Dukes of Medina Sidonia, built in the 16th century atop the old Andalusian alcázar, houses exceptional historical archives.

Plaza del Cabildo, Sanlúcar's heart, buzzes around its central fountain and tapas bar terraces. Whitewashed facades and wrought-iron balconies compose an authentic Andalusian scene, refreshingly off the tourist trail.

Visit a manzanilla bodega, home to this unique dry white wine whose saline taste comes from humidity captured by vines exposed to Atlantic breezes. These "cathedrals of manzanilla"—vast cellars with soaring ceilings—shelter centuries of winemaking tradition passed down through generations.

activities.text: Board a traditional barge from Bajo de Guía to cross the Guadalquivir estuary into Doñana National Park. The 3-hour cruise follows the river along marshlands populated by pink flamingos and gray herons, offering intimate access to one of Europe's wildest landscapes.

Settle into a restaurant at Bajo de Guía facing the Guadalquivir and Doñana beyond. This former fishing quarter has become one of Andalusia's seafood capitals: Sanlúcar langoustines, white shrimp, coquina clams, and shrimp fritters, all washed down with ice-cold manzanilla.

In August, Sanlúcar hosts Spain's oldest beach horse races, documented for over 200 years and formalized in 1845. At sunset, riders launch their mounts across the sand of Las Piletas beach, with Doñana as a dramatic backdrop.

days_plan.itin_title: Cádiz Road Trip: Jerez, Sherry Bodegas & White Villages

places.name: Santa Cruz De La Palma

visits.text: Santa Cruz de La Palma was founded on May 3, 1493 by Alonso Fernández de Lugo. In the 16th century it became Europe's third port after Antwerp and Seville—riches from the American colonies passed through here. This prosperity left a historic center classified as a Historic-Artistic Ensemble, with 17th and 18th-century palaces and mansions, colorful facades, and carved wooden balconies facing the Atlantic. Start with Avenida Marítima: the 17th-century wooden balconies, flowering and each unique, form one of the Canaries' most photogenic promenades. Note that these enclosed balconies (ajimeces) drew inspiration from Arab architecture and served to cool houses through sea breezes.

Walk up Calle Real—also called Calle O'Daly, named after an Irish banana merchant who lived there. This long cobbled pedestrian street concentrates palaces, craft shops, and small squares. Plaza de España, in the city's heart, is one of the Canaries' finest Renaissance ensembles: the arcaded town hall, public fountain, and El Salvador Church (whose origins date to 1500) form a setting seemingly preserved for five centuries. Casa Salazar, a bit further, is the city's most remarkable Baroque palace—built in the second half of the 17th century, its patio surrounded by wood-balconied galleries is open to the public through temporary exhibitions.

On the port esplanade, don't miss the Barco de La Virgen—a full-scale replica of Christopher Columbus's Santa María, housing the city's naval museum. Just beside it, Castillo de Santa Catalina, a 16th-century fortification, defended the port against corsair attacks. From the esplanade, the view of the Avenida balconies and the castle facade is Santa Cruz's iconic photo.

Caldera de Taburiente is La Palma's most spectacular national park—protected since 1954, one of Spain's first. This gigantic erosion crater, 8 km in diameter and 1,500 meters deep, is covered with Canarian pine forests and cut by ravines. If you don't wish to hike, Mirador de La Cumbrecita (1,287 m), accessible by car with a permit between 8:30 AM and 4 PM, offers a vertiginous view into the caldera's interior. From up there, the walls rising to over 2,000 m give you the feeling of standing at the bottom of a natural amphitheater.

For those wanting to enter the caldera on foot, trail PR-LP 13 from Los Brecitos is the most popular (16 km, about 6 hours, -700 m elevation). It descends through Canarian pine forests to the riverbed and passes the Cascada de Colores—a 6-meter waterfall whose rocks are naturally tinted yellow, orange, and red by iron dissolved in the ferrous water of Barranco de las Angustias. The green comes from algae and moss. The spectacle changes with season and flow. Bring plenty of water—there are no services inside.

Roque Idafe, a basaltic monolith rising in the caldera's heart, was venerated by the Benahoaritas—La Palma's prehistoric inhabitants—who attributed supernatural powers to it. It stands at the confluence of the Almendro Amargo and Limonero ravines, just before the Cascada de Colores. If you hike here, take a break at this spot: the view of the caldera walls is breathtaking.

activities.text: Lunch in the historic quarter around Calle Real. La Palma's gastronomy revolves around palmero cheese (PDO), palm honey, and bienmesabe—an almond cream you'll find in every dessert. Pair it with a glass of malvasía wine, produced in the island's vineyards.

The city is small and entirely walkable in 2 to 3 hours. Late afternoon, climb to Mirador de la Concepción for a panoramic view over the city, colorful balconies, and port. It's the best time of day: the low light ignites the yellow and ochre facades.

Swim in a natural pool on the coast near Santa Cruz, depending on sea conditions. Volcanic pools are numerous on La Palma's east coast—check with your hotel about conditions, as Atlantic swells can be strong.

Guided hike into the caldera from Los Brecitos: several operators offer accompanied descents in small groups, with a 4x4 bus to reach the starting point from Los Llanos de Aridane. It's the recommended option if you don't know the trail, as ravines can be slippery and weather changes fast inside.

Stargazing from La Palma's heights. The island obtained UNESCO's first Starlight Reserve status worldwide in 2011—no light pollution, perfectly stable skies. Roque de los Muchachos (2,426 m), the island's summit, houses one of the world's most important astronomical observatories. Outside observatory visiting hours, the mountain roads are freely accessible for night observation.

Picnic at the Playa de Taburiente camping area, inside the park. This sandy riverbed with small natural pools is one of the Canaries' only permanent streams. The water is cold and clear—a welcome break after the descent.

places.name: Santa Cruz De Tenerife

visits.text: The Tenerife Auditorium rises like a frozen wave of white concrete against the Atlantic. Designed by architect Santiago Calatrava and inaugurated in 2003, its roof soars to 58 meters. The main hall seats 1,800 beneath crystalline wood paneling engineered for perfect acoustics.

Walk through Parque García Sanabria, the largest urban park in the Canary Islands. Since its inauguration in 1926, this 67,000 m² green space has sheltered outdoor sculptures from international exhibitions, century-old ficus trees, and Art Deco fountains.

The Church of the Immaculate Conception was first built in 1500, then reconstructed after a 17th-century fire. Its bell tower dominates the historic center—a triangle bounded by Plaza de España, Plaza de Candelaria, and Plaza de Weyler.
Step into the Palmetum, a botanical garden built on a transformed landfill and now home to palm species from every corner of the world. Views sweep across the harbor and out to the open ocean.
Plaza de España opens onto the waterfront with its reflecting pool designed by Swiss architects Herzog and de Meuron. Terraces overlook the cruise port beyond.

activities.text: Head to Las Teresitas beach, north of the city. Its golden sand—imported from the Sahara—stretches 1,300 meters beneath the fishing village of San Andrés.
Mercado Nuestra Señora de África has operated since 1944 under its North African–style arcades. Tropical fruits, goat cheeses, flowers, and fresh fish crowd the stalls.
Try gofio, the toasted grain flour that accompanies Canarian soups and desserts. Pair it with papas arrugadas and either mojo rojo or mojo verde.

days_plan.itin_title: Canary Islands Road Trip: Tenerife & Gran Canaria

visits.text: Ten minutes by boat and the bustle disappears. Tabarca is the only inhabited island in the Valencian Community, fortified in the 18th century by Charles III with Genoese freed from captivity in Tunisia. What remains are the walls, the three gates (San Gabriel, San Miguel, San Rafael), and the church declared a national monument in 1964. No cars. Nine streets, two squares, a handful of fish restaurants.

activities.text: The marine reserve of 1986: Spain's first designated as such. Beneath the surface, Tabarca's seabeds are remarkably rich. Mask and snorkel suffice from the coves—snorkeling is free and accessible. Equipment rental available on the quay upon arrival. Departure from Santa Pola: boats roughly every hour, round trip from €9.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

visits.text: The Magdalena Palace commands the peninsula of the same name. Built between 1908 and 1912 for the royal family, it blends English and French influences across 2,500 square meters of Belle Époque grandeur.
Wander through the 30 hectares of Magdalena Park. Coastal trails wind past a small marine zoo housing penguins and seals, while viewpoints at the peninsula's tip frame sweeping bay vistas.
The Centro Botín, designed by Renzo Piano, appears to hover above the bay on its stilts. Its 270,000 pearlescent ceramic discs shimmer with the ever-changing Cantabrian light.
Step inside Santander Cathedral, rebuilt after the 1941 fire. Its Gothic cloister and the Cristo crypt shelter Roman-era remnants beneath vaulted stone.
El Sardinero beach sweeps in a gentle arc of fine sand facing the Piquío gardens. A fashionable resort since the Belle Époque, it retains its elegant seafront facades.
The Museum of Prehistory and Archaeology of Cantabria gathers artifacts from the Paleolithic to the early centuries. French-language commentary is available.

activities.text: Board a cruise around Santander Bay. From the water, the silhouette of the Magdalena Palace and the cliffs of Cabo Mayor reveal their full drama.
The Mercado de la Esperanza spreads its stalls across two floors: fish and shellfish below, cheeses and cured meats above. Sample Cantabrian anchovies and rabas (crispy fried squid).
Follow the coastal path to Cabo Mayor lighthouse. The cliffs plunge into the Atlantic, and the sunset here is breathtaking.
The Puertochico quarter brims with pintxo bars and terraces overlooking the harbor. Grilled sardines, rabas and Cantabrian wines make for the perfect local aperitif.

days_plan.itin_title: Cantabria Road Trip: Atlantic Bays and Picos de Europa

visits.text: Santiago de Compostela—Santiago in Galician. End of the Camino, where hundreds of thousands of pilgrims arrive each year. Plaza del Obradoiro is the scenic arrival: on the right, the cathedral and its baroque façade by Fernando de Casas Novoa (1738-1750), that stone filigree whose very name—obradoiro—means "goldsmith" in Galician; on the left, the Hostal dos Reis Católicos, royal pilgrim hospital founded in 1499 by the Catholic Monarchs, today a 5-star Parador; opposite, the Pazo de Raxoi (18th-century neoclassical, city hall + Galician government); to the south, the Pazo de San Xerome, university rectorate.

The cathedral is consecrated to James the Greater, whose body tradition claims was found in a field around 813 by the hermit Pelagius, guided by a star ("campus stellae" → Compostela). Three successive cathedrals: the first by Alfonso II in 829, burned by al-Mansur in 997, rebuilt pre-Romanesque by Alfonso III, replaced by the current Romanesque edifice begun in 1075 under Alfonso VI. The Pórtico da Gloria—Romanesque entrance gate carved by Master Mateo, completed in 1188—was restored in 2018 and recovered its medieval polychromy. Separate paid visit (€15), but arguably Europe's finest Romanesque portal.

The Botafumeiro is the circulating image: the giant 1.6m-high, 54kg thurible that swings until reaching 68 km/h under the transept vault, 21 meters high. Tradition documented since 1322. Eight tiraboleiros pull ropes to make it oscillate until grazing the vault. It operates only at Friday 7:30pm pilgrim mass, during Holy Years (daily at noon), and by paid reservation (€450)—possible for individual pilgrims with credencial on request at the office.

Tour the three other squares around the cathedral: Plaza das Praterías to the south (with horse fountain and original 12th-century Romanesque façade), Plaza da Quintana to the east (monumental staircase, eastern façade, Holy Door that opens only Holy Years), Plaza da Inmaculada to the north (entrance to San Martiño Pinario monastery). Each square opens a different perspective on the cathedral—that's the urban intelligence of Santiago. The cathedral is designed to be seen from all sides, never just frontally.

The Monastery of San Martiño Pinario, right beside the cathedral, is Spain's second-largest Benedictine monastery after El Escorial. Founded in the 9th century, rebuilt in the 16th-17th centuries in baroque-Renaissance style. The façade-altarpiece by Bartolomé Fernández Lechuga (1606) is spectacular—50 meters high, carved like a cliff. Inside, two baroque altarpieces by Casas Novoa and Fernando de Casas, more impressive than the cathedral's according to many. Cloistered sisters still sell cookies via turntable. Quiet visit, €5 no queue.

The College of Fonseca, on Rúa do Franco, houses the university library. Renaissance cloister by Alonso de Covarrubias (1532-1544), perfect illustration of Spanish humanism. The University of Santiago, founded in 1495 by Archbishop Alonso III de Fonseca, is one of Spain's oldest. Buildings are scattered throughout the city. Detour via Pazo de San Xerome, on Obradoiro—15th-century façade, one of the only vestiges of the first university.

Alameda Park offers the finest panoramic view of the cathedral—from Paseo dos Leóns, at the western end. Camellias blooming January to March (Galicia produces 80% of Europe's camellias), centenary eucalyptus. Along the way, the baroque Church of Santa Susana and the carballeira (sacred oak grove). This park was the green lung of Compostelan bourgeoisie in the 19th century. Poet Rosalía de Castro came here to dream—her statue is at the entrance.

For modern art lovers: the City of Culture of Galicia, on Monte Gaiás at the city's edge (5 km, taxi or bus). Contemporary complex designed by American architect Peter Eisenman, inaugurated in 2011—six buildings with sinuous roofs imitating ground curves and Camino traces. Three buildings are complete (Museum of Galicia, library, archives), three remained unfinished for lack of funding (and it became a public debate in Galicia). The ensemble is worth the detour for architecture, even if isolation makes it somewhat ghostly.

activities.text: The Mercado de Abastos, 5 minutes from the cathedral, is Santiago's other heart. 1941 neoclassical building, seven granite pavilions. Producers from all Galicia: fishmongers from Marín, mariscadoras from Rías Baixas, Cebreiro cheese makers, local huerta vegetables, Caurel meats. Pavilion 5 does catering: you buy your products, they cook them on site. Galician-style experience, no tourist fuss. Closed Sundays.

Evening in Rúa do Franco and Rúa da Raíña—the two tapas streets of the historic center. Mandatory specialties: pulpo a feira (octopus with paprika and olive oil), empanada gallega (red or bonito), zorza (spiced pork), pimientos de Padrón ("some are hot and some are not"—table game), Santiago tart (almond, powdered sugar cross). Wines: Albariño (white) or Mencía (red). Off-season, very lively student atmosphere—Santiago has 30,000 students.

The Centro Galego de Arte Contemporánea (CGAC), designed by Álvaro Siza Vieira in 1993, is one of northern Spain's best modern art museums. Often excellent temporary exhibitions (Tàpies, Chillida, contemporary Galician art). Remarkable building itself: local granite, natural light, cascading garden designed by Siza. Combinable with the Museo do Pobo Galego right next door, in the former Convent of Santo Domingo de Bonaval—Galician ethnography, unique triple helicoidal staircase.

For pilgrims who've reached the end of the Camino: passage to Oficina del Peregrino (Rúa Carretas) to collect the Compostela, pilgrimage certificate. Almost systematic queue in season. Believers or not, the atmosphere is touching—emotions, tears, embraces. If you haven't walked, just go witness the queue: it's a human spectacle. Office open daily 9am-7pm, free.

places.name: Santillana Del Mar

visits.text: The Collegiate Church of Santa Juliana dates from the 12th century and stands as Cantabria's finest Romanesque monument. Its cloister preserves carved capitals depicting biblical scenes and snapshots of medieval daily life.
The Altamira Museum houses the Neocave, a faithful replica of the original cavern. The polychrome bison paintings date back 14,000 years—dubbed the 'Sistine Chapel of cave art.'
Walk along Calle del Rey to Plaza Mayor. The stone mansions of the Peredo, Tagle and Estrada families line cobbled lanes that have barely changed since the 16th century.
The Towers of Merino and Don Borja rank among the village's oldest civil structures. The Don Borja Tower now serves as headquarters for the Santillana Foundation.
On Plaza de las Arenas, the Velarde Palace displays its 16th-century Renaissance facade. Underfoot, scallop shells remind visitors that Santillana is a waypoint on the Camino del Norte.
The Regina Coeli Diocesan Museum exhibits a collection of Cantabrian religious art: altarpieces, gold and silverwork, and medieval sculpture.

activities.text: Taste Cantabrian specialties: cocido montañés (a hearty stew of beans, cabbage and pork), sobaos pasiegos (buttery sponge cakes) and local cheeses.
Head down to Santa Justa beach in Ubiarco, 4 km away. A hermitage carved into the cliff overlooks a wild cove where waves break against golden rock.
The artisan shops on Calle Juan Infante sell ceramics, leather goods and regional produce. Stroll beneath the arcades in the late afternoon light.

days_plan.itin_title: Cantabria Road Trip: Atlantic Bays and Picos de Europa
