places.name: Santuario De Belen

visits.text: The Santuario de Nuestra Señora de Belén rises among the holm oaks, 11 km from Cabeza del Buey. Its origins go back to the 13th century: the Templar cloister, austere and massive, sets the tone. The mural paintings in the cabecera are worth the detour. It's the most visited monument in the whole area.

The gardens of the Santuario de Belén, walled-in, form an unexpected haven in this arid sierra. Orange trees, stone benches, silence. The celdas (cells) of the old convent adjoin the cloister. Free visit, no ticket.

activities.text: From the Santuario de Belén, a trail heads towards the sierra del Pedregoso. Dehesa landscape, holm oaks, chances of spotting vultures. Allow an hour round trip for a first taste.

days_plan.itin_title: La Siberia and La Serena: Secret Extremadura in 6 Days

map_keywords: Sanctuary of Belén Cabeza del Buey

visits.text: Welcome to Segovia. The city reveals itself from afar by its silhouette—some say it resembles a ship. The Alcázar as bow, the cathedral as mainmast, the aqueduct as anchor cast into the sierra. The Roman aqueduct is your arrival point: 167 arches, 28 meters high at its peak, granite assembled without mortar—not a drop of cement across the 800 visible meters. Built around 50 CE, more likely under Domitian than Trajan. For the best photo, climb the Postigo stairs on the right: you'll find yourself level with the water channel, and it's from there you grasp the brilliant absurdity of this engineering feat.

The Alcázar, perched at the tip of the promontory, genuinely inspired Walt Disney for Snow White's castle—he visited in 1935. Built from the 11th century on former Roman fortifications, expanded in the 13th by Castilian kings, ravaged by fire in 1862 and rebuilt immediately afterward in fairy-tale fashion. Inside you'll find the throne room, the chamber of the Catholic Monarchs where Isabella was proclaimed queen in 1474, and—warning to the truly brave—152 spiral steps up the Torre de Juan II. The view plunges toward the Sierra de Guadarrama and the convents of the Eresma valley.

The cathedral, nicknamed "the Lady of Cathedrals" for its elegance, is the last Gothic cathedral built in Spain—construction began in 1525 under Charles V after the previous one burned during the Comuneros revolt in 1520. Latin cross plan, 18 chapels, spectacular 16th-century Flemish stained glass. In the Concepción chapel, Ignacio de Ries's Tree of Life is a macabre curiosity: a giant crucifix as tree root, the damned falling to hell from the lower branches. Far from joyful.

The Church of Vera Cruz, 10 minutes downhill from the Alcázar (then plan for the climb back), is one of the city's best-kept secrets. Dodecagonal plan—twelve sides—built by the Templars in 1208 and consecrated April 13 of that year by papal decree of Innocent III. At its center, a two-story shrine where knights kept vigil over a relic of the True Cross. It's rare in Europe, and that's what makes it remarkable: Vera Cruz has barely changed since the 13th century. Further on, the Parral monastery is under permanent restoration but accessible.

On Plaza Mayor, facing the cathedral, don't miss Casa de los Picos—"House of Spikes"—a Renaissance façade bristling with 617 diamond-cut granite points, designed by the Marquises of Quintanar in the late 15th century. Calle Juan Bravo concentrates Segovia's finest façades: Renaissance sgraffito with geometric motifs, noble palaces, Casa de la Tierra, Torreón de Lozoya. Five minutes on foot between all these points. Everything in Segovia fits within two kilometers.

activities.text: Roast suckling pig—cochinillo asado—is Segovia's gastronomic institution. The reference: Mesón de Cándido, steps from the aqueduct, serving the dish since 1786. Tradition dictates that the maître d' carves the pig with the edge of a plate to prove it's cooked to perfection—tender enough not to need a knife—then breaks the plate on the floor. Expensive (€60-70), but it's an experience. More modest but excellent alternatives: José María (Cronista Lecea) or Mesón de José María.

Sunset walk along Paseo del Salón or higher up, around the Fuencisla sanctuary—Segovia's patron saint. The view of the Alcázar ablaze with slanting sun is the image you'll remember. In winter, the old town's façades are illuminated and the aqueduct becomes even more dramatic. Locals take their evening paseo here: Segovians are so accustomed to their city they forget it while loving it.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks
Madrid & Castile Road Trip: Royal Palaces, Ancient Walls & World-Class Museums
Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Castile and León - 14-Day Grand Tour

places.name: Segura De La Sierra

visits.text: Final stop: Segura de la Sierra, ranked among Spain's Most Beautiful Villages, perched at 1,230 metres altitude in the park's northeast corner. The village of Muslim origin (conquered in 781 under the name Saqura) has preserved its steep alleyways, vaulted passages, and end-of-the-world atmosphere. The castle, built by the Almohads then transformed by the Order of Santiago in the 13th century, dominates the village from its rocky outcrop. Its 18-metre keep offers a 360° panorama over the sierras, pine forests, and olive groves. The Arab baths, discovered beneath the village, are among the best preserved in the province.

Segura is also the birthplace of the poet Jorge Manrique (15th century), author of Coplas por la muerte de su padre—one of Spanish literature's most famous poems. His father, Don Rodrigo Manrique, was commander of Segura for the Order of Santiago. The poet's birthplace, in the upper part of the village, is open to visitors. Wander the alleyways looking for details: carved coats of arms, Charles III's Imperial Fountain, the Catena Gate, and remnants of the old ramparts.

If you have time before leaving, detour to Hornos de Segura, another spectacular hilltop village 30 minutes' drive away. Its ruined castle dominates the Tranco lake—the view from the village terraces over the lake and mountains is one of the park's most photographed. The Cosmolarium, installed in the castle tower, is a small astronomical observatory and planetarium that takes advantage of the Sierra de Segura's excellent night sky quality.

activities.text: Segura de la Sierra produces a Sierra de Segura PDO olive oil, considered one of Spain's finest. The mountain terroir (700-1,200 m altitude) and the Picual variety produce an intense oil with notes of fresh grass and green tomato. Village cooperatives offer tastings. Leave with a bottle—it's the best souvenir you can bring back from the province of Jaén.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance

map_keywords: Baños Árabes Segura de la Sierra

visits.text: The Irati Forest spans 17,000 hectares of beech and fir between the Aezkoa and Salazar valleys. It's the second-largest beech forest in Europe after the Black Forest.
The Casas de Irati Visitor Center provides maps and information. Marked trails depart from this point: Cubo Waterfall, Urbeltza River, Zabaleta Forest.
Follow the Urbeltza River trail as it winds beneath the beeches. Clear water flows over a pebble bed between the roots.
The Irabia Reservoir mirrors the wooded peaks. A trail circles the artificial lake through pristine surroundings.
Ochagavía at 764 meters elevation is one of Navarre's loveliest villages. Its Pyrenean houses with steep-pitched roofs line cobbled lanes.
The Plateresque bridge over the Zatoya River marks the village entrance. Admire its stone arches and Renaissance sculptures.

activities.text: Hike the Errekaidorra trail: a 10-kilometer loop through fir and beech forest with clearings offering superb views.
Watch for black woodpeckers, red deer, and roe deer inhabiting the forest. Early morning offers the best chances of spotting wildlife.
Picnic at one of the designated areas along the Irati or Urbeltza rivers. The murmur of water accompanies your meal.
In autumn, the beeches turn from green to red, yellow, and orange. The chromatic spectacle draws photographers and hikers from across Europe.

days_plan.itin_title: Navarre Road Trip: Castles, Irati Forest & the Camino

places.name: Sequeros

visits.text: Sequeros, once the administrative capital of Sierra de Francia, sits at 870 meters altitude with three-story houses and wrought-iron balconies that tell the story of a prosperous 19th-century local bourgeoisie. Declared a site of historic interest in 2004, the village counts fewer than 250 inhabitants, yet its center remains remarkably preserved. Don't miss Plaza de Eloy Bullón—this is what's left of a former aljama, the medieval Jewish quarter, with lanes so narrow the rooftops nearly touch. The labyrinthine layout reflects centuries of coexistence among Christian, Jewish, and Muslim communities.

The Church of Virgen del Robledo stands as Sequeros's most symbolic monument. Its baroque altarpiece and mudéjar coffered ceiling at the main altar deserve your attention. In its crypt lie the remains of Simón Vela—the French pilgrim Simon Roland who, according to legend, received an apparition of the Virgin in 1434 directing him to a statue hidden for centuries atop Peña de Francia. When he died in 1438, he was buried here in Sequeros, not on the mountain.

Push up to the summit of Peña de Francia at 1,727 meters—count on 20 minutes' drive from Sequeros. The sanctuary of Notre-Dame-du-Rocher-de-France, run by Dominicans since the 15th century, offers 360-degree views spanning the plains of Salamanca, Portugal, and Extremadura. Cervantes mentioned it in Don Quixote, and the Black Virgin is venerated as far away as Latin America and the Philippines. The road is passable from April to November—avoid other months when the summit can remain snowbound.

activities.text: Walk to the Cruz viewpoint to contemplate the Béjar-Candelario range—the village's finest lookout. The Barrero trail leading there edges past the old circular bullring, now converted to public space. Have a glass of mountain wine in one of the bars on Church Square, beneath the granite arcades.

Come evening, explore the Robledo trail departing from the village—a marked 2-3 km path crossing oak forests and reaching the hermitage of Robledo where rest the bones of Juana, 'la Moza Santa,' Sequeros's local prophetess whose legend is intimately bound to the discovery of the Virgin of Peña de Francia.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route

map_keywords: Sanctuaire Peña de Francia El Cabaco

places.name: Setenil De Las Bodegas

visits.text: Setenil resembles nothing else: houses and bars built under enormous tuff ledges that literally form their roofs. The calle Cuevas del Sol (south side, sunny) and calle Cuevas de la Sombra (north side) are the two streets to see - two rows of façades either side of the same ravine, with rock as a common ceiling.

Setenil's Nasrid castle, at the summit of the cerro, offers the overview of the carved architecture. What you see from above finally makes sense of what you've just walked through below: the ravine, the overhanging ledges, the rock roofs. The climb takes ten minutes from the main street.

activities.text: The popular etymology of Setenil - septem nihil, seven times nothing in Latin - refers to the unsuccessful Christian assaults before the final capitulation to the Catholic Monarchs in 1484. The name's origin is however disputed by some historians. A terrace café on calle Cuevas del Sol to grasp the setup: behind your cup, several tons of rock overhang the tables.

days_plan.itin_title: White Villages: Arcos, Grazalema, Ronda - 6 Days
White Villages & Sierras: 9 Days Málaga-Almería

map_keywords: Calle Cuevas de la Sombra Setenil

places.name: Sevilla

visits.text: Beneath the stalls of Triana Market lie the ruins of a castle that made Europe tremble: the Castillo de San Jorge, seat of the Spanish Holy Office from 1481 to 1785—with an interruption from 1626 to 1639 when the tribunal had to relocate to the Casa de los Tavera due to flooding from the Guadalquivir. Spain's first Inquisition tribunal, the most active, the most brutal. Three hundred years of interrogations, torture, and trials unfolded in these cellars until its definitive transfer in 1785, when the walls were buried beneath the present-day market. Today it's a free basement museum: visible cells, torture instruments, nominal lists of the condemned. Chilling and necessary. Closed Mondays.

Plaza de San Francisco, right in the heart of downtown, is the visible flip side of the Inquisition: here stood Seville's main quemadero, the stake where the Holy Office's condemned were publicly burned. Six pyres in the first year, 1481, which consumed primarily Jewish conversos and later Moriscos. The square today serves as the starting point for Holy Week processions—the pasos emerging with hooded Nazarenos have been passing through since 1521. No plaque mentions the pyres: it's this silence that hits hardest.

Triana, the former gypsy and sailor suburb across the Guadalquivir, deserves more than a detour: this is where those expelled from Seville after the 1391 pogroms lived, and where the historic rivalry with 'los de Sevilla' was born. The Iglesia de Santa Ana, the city's first Gothic-Mudéjar church (begun in 1276), houses the Virgin of Hope—Triana's pride during the Madrugá on Good Friday. Calle Betis, along the river, is the place for a fino facing the sunset over La Giralda across the way.

The Puente de Triana (Puente de Isabel II), built in 1852 on the model of Paris's Carrousel Bridge, connects Triana to downtown. The city's first fixed bridge—before that, you crossed via a pontoon bridge. At the foot on the Triana side, the Carmen chapel and the Altozano, where statues of bullfighter Juan Belmonte and the flamenco singer stand. Obligatory landmark for Sevillians at aperitif hour.

Seville Cathedral is the world's largest Gothic cathedral—11,520 m², more than 50 chapels, central nave 42 m high. Built from 1402 on the site of the great Almohad mosque, whose architects reportedly said: 'Let us build a church so large that those who see it finished will take us for madmen.' Mission accomplished. Inside, Christopher Columbus's tomb is borne by four crowned statues representing the kingdoms of Castile, Aragon, León, and Navarre. DNA analysis in 2006 showed that the Seville remains are at least related to Columbus, without total authentication; Santo Domingo, which also claims to hold the remains, refuses testing. La Giralda, a 1198 Almohad minaret, now serves as the bell tower: climb via 35 ramps (no stairs, designed for horses).

The Hospital de la Caridad, 300 m from the Cathedral, preserves one of Spain's most brutal pictorial cycles. Founded in 1674 by Miguel de Mañara—a historical figure often associated with the Don Juan myth (wrongly: Tirso de Molina had published his play in 1630, three years after Mañara's birth). According to legend, he repented after a macabre vision in which he saw himself dead; in reality, his wife Jerónima's death in 1661 likely turned him toward charity. He commissioned Valdés Leal to create two funereal paintings for his hospital for the poor: 'In Ictu Oculi' (a bishop struck down dying) and 'Finis Gloriae Mundi' (a decomposing knight, worms included, with Christ weighing his sins on a scale). The Golden Age's most brutal memento mori. Murillo painted eight softer canvases alongside to balance it out. 6 euros.

The Reales Alcázares, right next to the Cathedral, are Europe's oldest royal palace still in use. Built by Peter the Cruel in the 14th century on Almohad foundations, with Morisco artisans from Toledo—it's the archetype of Mudéjar style. The terraced gardens, dating in part to the 12th-century Almohads, became famous as the filming location for Game of Thrones (the gardens of Dorne). Book online; the shared line with the Cathedral is unbearable in season.

Barrio Santa Cruz, the former Jewish quarter transformed into a picturesque labyrinth, holds the memory of an ancient presence erased by the 1492 expulsion. A few commemorative plaques, a street still called 'calle de la Judería,' but mainly the compressed alleys climbing toward the Cathedral. Touristy, certainly, but after 8 PM when the groups have left, it becomes manageable again.

The Torre del Oro on the Guadalquivir, a 1220 Almohad watchtower, controlled the port entrance and marked the end of a chain stretched across the river. This is where the galleons returned from the Americas loaded with gold docked, and where they were unloaded directly at the Casa de Contratación. Small naval museum inside, interesting if you have time. 3 euros.

activities.text: Dinner at Casa Morales (calle García de Vinuesa), a taberna open since 1850. Classic atmosphere with barrels of manzanilla lined against the wall, slates for chalking up your tab, hand-sliced Iberian ham. Classic tapas: croquetas, espinacas con garbanzos, montadito de pringá. Gentle prices (15-20 euros per person). No reservations, elbow your way to the bar.

Flamenco tablao at La Casa de la Memoria (Cuna 6), a small venue in a 15th-century patio. Daily shows at 7:30 PM and 9 PM, one hour, 22 euros. Unlike the big tourist tablaos, here the palo (style) varies each evening and the artists are references—Patricia Guerrero, Antonio Rey have performed here. Book 2-3 days ahead.

Dinner at Eslava (calle Eslava, San Lorenzo), 15 minutes' walk from downtown. Creative tapas: egg on boletus-truffle biscuit (2010 Spanish champion, still served), pork ribs with 'purr-purr' sauce. Packed every evening, lines from 7:30 PM. Count on 25-30 euros per person with wine. Reservations only for the restaurant's table side—bar is direct queue.

Nighttime stroll along the Guadalquivir from Torre del Oro to Puente de Triana, then crossing and drinks on Calle Betis. View of the illuminated Giralda and the Hospital de la Caridad across the way. Allow 1.5 hours, golden light at dusk.

places.name: Sierra De Cazorla

visits.text: Contemplate the Tranco Reservoir, the heart of the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park. One of Spain's largest reservoirs storing Guadalquivir waters, its calm waters are suitable for swimming, kayaking, canoeing, and electric boat rides.

Visit the source of the Guadalquivir River, Andalusia's main river that rises in the Sierra de Cazorla. After traveling a 12-kilometer track, you reach a quite peculiar spot—not the typical rock but a powerful springtime watercourse surrounded by nature and native wildlife.

Enjoy Charco de la Pringue, a natural pool in the Natural Park whose name comes from the legend of a donkey that fell in loaded with oil. The area is equipped with a swimming zone with clear Guadalquivir waters, recreational area, restaurant, and hiking trails.

activities.text: Take in the views from the Félix Rodríguez de la Fuente Viewpoint, named in honor of the documentary the famous naturalist filmed about bearded vultures on the island of Bujaraiza Castle. From Puerto de las Palomas Viewpoint at 1,200 meters, you can see the beginning of the Guadalquivir valley.

Hike the Borosa River and Cerrada de Elías route, one of the best hiking trails in the sierra. The 22-kilometer trail runs along wooden walkways beside the canyon to the Salto de los Órganos waterfall, Black Waters lagoon, and the source of the Borosa River.

Visit the Bearded Vulture Breeding Center in Nava de San Pedro, where work is being done to reintroduce this endangered bird that became extinct in Andalusia in 1986. Guides explain how the center operates and the breeding with minimal human intervention.

days_plan.itin_title: Jaén's Renaissance Jewels: Olive Grove Landscapes & Wild Sierras of Andalusia

map_keywords: Félix Rodríguez de la Fuente Viewpoint
Río Borosa Cerrada Elías Hiking Trail
Bearded Vulture Breeding Center Nava de San Pedro

visits.text: The Sierra de Gredos is Castile and León's wildest massif—and one of Spain's most beautiful. Pico Almanzor, at 2,592 meters, is the highest summit in the Spanish Central System. The starting point for all major hikes is the Plataforma de Gredos, at 1,780 meters altitude, accessible from Hoyos del Espino via the AV-931 (12 km, toll per vehicle at park entrance). In summer and on weekends, the barrier opens at 7:30am—arrive early as parking fills fast. For the return, Ávila is 80 km away (1h15) and Madrid 170 km (2h) via the A-5.

activities.text: The hike to Laguna Grande de Gredos from the Plataforma is the massif's most beautiful and most frequented (4.6/5 rating on AllTrails, 162 reviews). The trail follows the old Calzada Romana, gradually climbing toward the Mirador de los Barrerones (2,149 m) where the glacial cirque suddenly appears—a breathtaking spectacle. The lake is nestled at 1,950 meters in the rocky amphitheater, guarded by Pico Almanzor. Along the way, Iberian ibex—protected in the park since 1905—are frequently visible on rocky slopes. Allow 14 km round trip, 677 m elevation gain, 5-5.5 hours walking. The Refugio Elola at the lake's edge (80 places) allows overnight stays for summit attempts the next morning—reservation mandatory in season.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

visits.text: Head north across the Alcarria plateau. After 45 minutes, stop at Cifuentes (population 2,000). This quiet town boasts a 14th-century castle built by Don Juan Manuel—a lettered prince, nephew of Alfonso X the Wise, and author of El Conde Lucanor. The castle, square-planned with round corner towers, dominates the Cifuentes valley. Below, the arcaded Plaza Mayor and the church of El Salvador (13th century), transitional Romanesque-Gothic, round out the visit. Allow 45 minutes.

Drive 30 minutes onwards to Sigüenza, one of Castile's hidden gems. Perched at 1,000 m altitude, this town of 4,500 souls, designated a Historic and Artistic Monument, is crowned by its 12th-century castle—now one of Spain's finest Paradores. Built on an Arab citadel which itself stood on a Roman site, it hosted the Catholic Monarchs and survived the Napoleonic Wars, Carlist Wars, and Civil War. Even if you're not staying overnight, climb up to see the cobbled courtyard, the 13th-century Romanesque chapel, and the vaulted dining hall.

The Cathedral of Santa María de Sigüenza is a fortress-cathedral—its two massive façade towers give it the air of a castle keep. Begun in 1124 after the reconquest from the Muslims, it blends Romanesque, Gothic, and Renaissance styles. But it's the tomb of the Doncel that justifies your pilgrimage. Martín Vázquez de Arce, a page to Isabella the Catholic who died in battle in 1486 aged 25, is sculpted in alabaster in a unique pose: not lying asleep like other recumbent effigies, but half-reclining, one leg crossed over the other, reading a book. It's one of the most celebrated funerary sculptures of Spanish Gothic, a symbol of wisdom wedded to strength.

activities.text: Take your time descending from the castle to Plaza Mayor through the cobbled lanes of Travesaña Alta, the medieval quarter. Plaza Mayor, laid out in the 15th century, is surrounded by arcades and noble blazoned mansions. Push on to the Casa del Doncel (15th century), the former residence of the Vázquez de Arce family, with its crenellated façade—it now hosts exhibitions. In the evening, the plaza's terraces come gently to life in the golden sunset light—Sigüenza is a town to savour at a slow pace.

If you have time the following morning before departing, the Barranco del Río Dulce Natural Park (10 km from Sigüenza) offers a limestone canyon with griffon vultures, golden eagles, and hiking trails between the cliffs. The village of Pelegrina, clinging to the canyon walls, is worth the detour.

days_plan.itin_title: Alcarria and Guadalajara: Between Madrid and Aragon
Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria
Mystical Castile: Toledo, Ávila, Salamanca & Burgos in 11 Days

places.name: Sitges

visits.text: The church of Sant Bartomeu i Santa Tecla, perched on a rocky promontory between two beaches, creates Sitges' postcard-perfect image. Built between 1672 and 1721 in restrained Catalan Baroque style, it replaced a Romanesque chapel destroyed by pirates in the 16th century. The square bell tower topped with glazed tiles still serves as a landmark for sailors. The interior is unremarkable, but the sea-facing terrace is worth the detour—accessible to all. Visit at sunset when the white facades turn peachy orange.

The Cau Ferrat was the house-studio of painter Santiago Rusiñol, a leading figure of Catalan Modernisme. Between 1893 and 1931 he amassed an extraordinary collection here: Gothic ironwork, Hispano-Moorish ceramics, and two El Greco canvases purchased for 1,000 pesetas in 1894—now worth millions. This is where the Modernist movement was born during the legendary parties Rusiñol hosted here. Combined ticket with the neighboring Maricel museum: €10. Allow 90 minutes for both.

The Maricel museum occupies a palace reconstructed stone by stone in 1910 by American industrialist Charles Deering. Stunning sea views from the Gothic arched gallery—arguably the finest vista in Sitges. The eclectic collection spans medieval altarpieces, Modernist painting, and Noucentista sculpture. The bridge spanning the street leads to the 1324 Hospital of Sant Joan, a stop on the Camino de Santiago.

activities.text: Stroll along the Passeig Marítim, two kilometers of seafront lined with houses built by the americanos—Catalans who returned wealthy from Cuba in the 19th century. Look for Casa Bacardí-Massó at number 19: yes, the rum family originated in Sitges. The town claims 17 beaches, including Platja de Sant Sebastià to the north, quieter and favored by locals. If you're here between late February and early March, Sitges Carnival draws 250,000 people over five days—one of Spain's most extravagant celebrations.

Aperitif on Plaça de l'Ajuntament with a well-chilled xarel·lo—the local white grape, foundation of cava. Penedès vineyards begin 15 km inland, and several Sitges wine bars offer comparative tastings. For dinner, order xató, a Catalan salad of endives, salt cod, and tuna with romesco sauce. Reliable address: El Pou on Carrer Sant Pau, around €35 per person.

days_plan.itin_title: Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour
Catalonia Wine Route - 7 Days from Penedès to Priorat

map_keywords: Sant Bartomeu i Santa Tecla Church Sitges

visits.text: Recommended midway stop: Falset, capital of Priorat. The Castle of Falset, now the Regional Museum within the 12th-century walls of the former Counts of Prades, tells the comarca's history across three floors. Further on, the cooperative wine cellar designed in 1919 by César Martinell—a disciple of Gaudí—is one of what are called here the "cathedrals of wine." Bricks, parabolic vaults, enormous vats: to step inside is to understand why Priorat shifted from mass wine to high-end wine starting in the 1990s.

Siurana, clinging at 750 meters to the top of a limestone cliff that plunges straight down into the reservoir. Some 50 inhabitants in winter. This is where the last Moorish bastion of Catalonia held out—taken in 1153-1154 by the troops of Ramon Berenguer IV after three centuries of resistance. Legend has it that the Moorish queen, rather than be captured, leapt from the precipice on her horse. The animal supposedly left the imprint of its horseshoe in the rock, which you can still see at the base of the plateau. True or false, the place has the raw beauty of world's end.

The church of Santa Maria, at the edge of the village, is Siurana's only religious building—and the most moving. Built between 1154 and the late 12th century immediately after the Reconquista, it has remained intact. Pure Romanesque style, single nave, semi-circular apse. The tympanum over the portal is a curious relief resembling a Last Supper but with fewer apostles. The interior isn't open to visitors, but you can see it through a window. It's small, austere, perfect.

The ruins of Siurana Castle, at the far end of the plateau, dominate everything. Little remains—a few walls, part of a tower—but what strikes you is the position: impossible to take with medieval weapons, it was by starvation that the Christians finally forced the place to yield. The Mirador de la Trona, just steps away, gives you the postcard view you're after: the dam below, Montsant in the background, and the limestone cliffs that make this corner famous among rock climbers worldwide.

activities.text: Priorat wine tasting—one of only two DOQ regions in Spain, alongside Rioja. The vines here grow on llicorella, that black schist that gives the reds their minerality and power. Mas Doix, Álvaro Palacios (Finca Dofí, L'Ermita), Clos Mogador, or the Gratallops cooperative welcome visitors. The wines age easily for twenty years and rival the finest Bordeaux in price. For a more modest budget, head down to Masroig: vineyard tour by 4x4 plus picnic among the vines for €20.

If you feel like walking, the Camí Antic de Siurana descends from the village down to Cornudella de Montsant—about 1h30 one way, through scrubland and holm oaks. For climbers, Siurana is world-renowned: 1,700 routes, exceptional limestone, sectors for all levels. And even without ropes, you can kayak around the reservoir in summer, though the water is surprisingly cold even in July.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Catalonia in 10 Days: Tarragona, Priorat & the Ebro Delta
Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour
Southern Catalonia in 5 Days: Roman Tarragona, Poblet, Priorat and the Port of Cambrils

visits.text: The Sóller train has rattled along in varnished wooden carriages since 1912. It traverses 27 kilometers of Serra de Tramuntana between Palma and Sóller, threading through tunnels, over bridges, and around impossible curves.
Modernist Sant Bartomeu Church was restored by Joan Rubió i Bellver, a disciple of Gaudí. Its neo-Gothic facade and colored windows dominate Plaça Constitució.
The wooden tram links Sóller to the Port in 30 minutes. It meanders between orange groves, lemon trees, and traditional houses for 5 kilometers.
Port de Sóller curves around a protected bay. Its waterfront restaurants serve Sóller's red prawns and seafood paella.
Can Prunera—a modernist museum—occupies an elegant 1911 mansion. Its Nolla ceramics, ironwork, and stained glass illustrate the wealth of citrus merchants.
Can Prunera's gardens spread beneath palms and magnolias. An 18th-century pergola shades paved walkways and fountains.

activities.text: Boats depart for Sa Calobra and its vertiginous cliffs. The cove nestled at the base of a canyon offers turquoise waters and a pebble beach.
The Port's crystalline waters invite snorkeling. Fish, starfish, and posidonia meadows populate the rocky seabed.
The Sóller Valley has cultivated oranges and lemons since the 10th century. Orange sorbet is an unmissable specialty.
The village of Fornalutx perches just 10 minutes away. Ranked among Spain's most beautiful, its flower-draped lanes climb between stone houses.

days_plan.itin_title: Majorca Road Trip: Serra de Tramuntana & Hidden Coves

visits.text: San Juan de Duero, on the river's opposite bank, 15-minute walk from the center—one of Spain's strangest architectural objects. Knights Hospitaller commandery founded around 1152, abandoned in the 17th century, of which only the Romanesque church and early 13th-century cloister remain. And this cloister is unique: four sides, four different arch styles—round, pointed, Mudéjar horseshoe, and especially Sicilian-Arab interlaces inspired by Amalfi that cross like rigging. Roofless, open to the sky. It's the image that inspired Gustavo Adolfo Bécquer's two tales The Mountain of Souls (1861) and The Moonbeam (1862)—you understand why arriving at blue hour. Entry €1, free Sundays.

800 meters along the river, the Hermitage of San Saturio literally burrows into the cliff above the Duero. Saturio lived here as a hermit in the 6th century; the caves accessible today are his. The baroque church added atop (17th-18th century) dates from another age, but the climb up the stairs, the troglodyte chapels and the plunging view over the river are truly worth the effort. Twenty-minute walk from San Juan de Duero, along the Machado trail.

The Machado trail is Soria's other thread. Poet Antonio Machado taught French at the local high school from 1907 to 1912. He married Leonor Izquierdo here in 1909, she was 15; she died in 1912 at 18, tuberculosis. Machado never stopped mourning Soria in his Campos de Castilla (1912). A plaque on his house on Calle Estudios, another at the municipal cemetery on Leonor's grave. Sober, no fuss.

If you still have two hours, the Celtiberian site of Numancia is 7 km north—the city that resisted the Roman legions for 20 years before burning itself in 133 BC rather than capitulate to Scipio Aemilianus. Only foundations remain, but the plateau is immense and windswept: hard not to think of the siege when walking the reconstructed ramparts. Entry €5.

activities.text: Walk the Machado trail end to end, about 4 km along the Duero between San Juan de Duero and San Saturio, with the poet's verses on steles every 200 meters. True pleasure late in the day, few people. Bench facing the water, riverside poplars—those poplars that Machado sang like no one else.

Dinner at Baluarte, starred by Óscar García Marina—Sierra game, black truffle from Soria (the province is Spain's leading producer, harvest December-February), IGP lamb from Castile and León. Tasting menu from €85. More modest but excellent: Casa Augusto, on Plaza Mayor, for torreznos (fried bacon) and Soria blood sausage.

days_plan.itin_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks
Castile and León - 14-Day Grand Tour
Mystical Castile: Toledo, Ávila, Salamanca & Burgos in 11 Days

visits.text: On the road from La Seu d'Urgell, stop at the Congost de Collegats—spectacular gorges carved by the Noguera Pallaresa between 500-meter limestone walls. Now bypassed by a tunnel, the old road still winds along the bottom of the defile: drive slowly, stop at the viewpoints. This is where Gaudí supposedly found inspiration for the undulating columns of the Sagrada Família, seeing the rock called "L'Argenteria." True or false, the landscape fully justifies the stop.

A little treasure 8 km south of Sort: Gerri de la Sal, medieval village enclosed within its 14th-century ramparts. Rock salt was exploited here since Roman times—hence the name. The monastery of Santa Maria, founded in 807, is one of Catalonia's oldest. And just beside it, the old salt warehouses (Alfolí) overlook the Noguera. It's typical of these Pyrenean villages, both rough and refined. Count 45 minutes to walk around.

Sort, capital of Pallars Sobirà, doesn't look like much at first glance—classic mountain village, stone houses and slate roofs. But it's the epicenter of whitewater sports in Spain, since the 1980s. This is where rafting was introduced to the country. The name Sort means "luck" in Catalan, and the municipality is famous for its lottery office: La Bruixa d'Or—the golden witch—sells winning tickets all across Spain, people come expressly to buy their ticket. Touristy, but it's become an institution.

If you have time in late afternoon, push on to Esterri d'Àneu, 30 km up the valley toward Bonaigua. The Ecomuseu des Valls d'Àneu reconstructs early 20th-century Pyrenean herders' life in period buildings: restored Casa Gassia, stable, barn, bread oven. The Valls d'Àneu still maintain active transhumance today. For Romanesque art enthusiasts, take advantage to see the church of Sant Just i Pastor de Son, 5 km away.

activities.text: The grand classic: rafting descent from Llavorsí to Sort, 18 km, the most intense stretch of the Noguera Pallaresa. On the program: legendary rapids La Rentadora (the "washing machine"), La Sirga, Quatre Llarg, and—if flow permits—jump off the Sort dam. Level class III-IV, accessible to beginners in good shape. The full descent takes 1.5 to 2 hours. Several operators depart from the base at Llavorsí or directly from Sort.

For those who prefer to keep their feet (almost) dry: canyoning in the Barranc de Sant Pere or hydrospeed descent (whitewater swimming on a board). Children from age 4 can do mini-rafting on the Baró-Figueretes section, calmer. Sort's entire ecosystem revolves around water, and it has obvious educational virtues for family travel.

days_plan.itin_title: Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour
Catalan Pyrenees and Romanesque Art in 8 Days: Lleida, Pallars, Vall de Boí and Val d'Aran

places.name: Sos Del Rey Catolico

visits.text: Head west (2h30) to reach Sos del Rey Católico, in the Cinco Villas comarca. Perched on a rocky promontory on the border between the ancient kingdoms of Aragón and Navarre, this fortified city witnessed the birth on March 10, 1452 of one of the most important figures in Spanish history: Ferdinand II of Aragón, the Catholic King, who, together with Isabella of Castile, unified Spain and financed Christopher Columbus's voyage. The village, classified as a Historic Site since 1968, is one of Aragón's best preserved - seven fortified gates still pierce the Gothic ramparts.

Begin at the Palacio de Sada, Ferdinand's birthplace, recognizable by its austere facade with coats of arms and crenellations. It now houses the tourist office and an interpretation center about the monarch. The church of San Esteban, the village's Romanesque jewel, preserves the baptismal font where Ferdinand was christened. But the real treasure is its 11th-century crypt, the oldest structure in the village, adorned with Gothic murals depicting scenes from Christ's life.

Lose yourself in the cobbled streets of the casco antiguo. The Jewish quarter (Judería), around the Plaza de la Sartén, retains its medieval atmosphere - about thirty families lived here in the 12th century before their expulsion in 1492 by the Catholic Monarchs. Climb up to Peña Feliciana castle (12th century), whose keep offers a panorama over the comarca's five villages and, on clear days, the Pyrenees. The Plaza de la Villa, with its Renaissance town hall and medieval market hall (Lonja), completes the visit.

activities.text: Enjoy Sos's tranquil atmosphere at day's end. The Parador, housed in a new building integrated into the ramparts, offers a terrace overlooking the village rooftops. For dinner, try Cinco Villas specialties: ternasco de Aragón (roast suckling lamb), migas, and artisanal sheep's cheese. Before leaving the village, stop by the Delfín Puente pastry shop for its handmade chocolates - a local institution.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel

map_keywords: Palacio de Sada Sos del Rey Católico
Iglesia San Esteban Sos del Rey Católico
Castillo Peña Feliciana Sos del Rey Católico

visits.text: The Tabernas Desert stretches across 280 km², Europe's only true desert with less than 250mm of annual rainfall. The ravines, canyons, and badlands in ochre hues recall American West landscapes—which attracted filmmakers from the 1950s onward.

Fort Bravo preserves Europe's finest western film sets, built in the 1960s for Sergio Leone's films. The wooden Far West town, Mexican village, and military fort still serve as active filming locations. Stunt and shootout shows are performed on weekends.

Oasys MiniHollywood, on the filming locations of A Fistful of Dollars and The Good, the Bad and the Ugly, has been transformed into a theme park. The reconstructed western town, 800-animal wildlife reserve, and cactus garden round out the visit.

The Castillo de Tabernas, an 11th-century Arab fortress, dominates the village from its hill. Though in ruins but undergoing restoration, it offers panoramic views over the desert and the Río Andarax valley.
The Plataforma Solar de Almería, a solar thermal energy research center, takes advantage of the desert's 3,000 hours of annual sunshine. Its giant parabolic mirrors are visible from the road.

activities.text: Hike through the desert badlands on the GR-140 trail. From Oasys, a 9km route crosses plateaus, canyons, and ravines among eroded sandstone and clay formations.
Photography walk through the desert landscapes. The golden hours (sunrise and sunset) offer raking light that sculpts the relief and brings out the colors in geological strata.
Stargazing in the Tabernas Desert. Low light pollution and almost permanently clear skies make it one of Andalusia's best astronomical sites.

days_plan.itin_title: Almería Road Trip: Volcanic Coves and Tabernas Desert
Wild Coast: Málaga to Cartagena in 9 Days
White Villages & Sierras: 9 Days Málaga-Almería

visits.text: In 1294, Guzmán chose to throw down his own knife rather than surrender the city to the besiegers: his captive son was executed, Tarifa held. The episode built a Spanish national myth. His castle, built in the 10th century by Caliph Abd al-Rahman III, is open Wednesday through Sunday. From the ramparts, Africa is 14 km away (Punta Cires)—on clear days, you can make out Tangier's buildings at 32 km.

The white alleyways smell alternately of sea and jasmine. The Arab-origin walled old town can be explored on foot in 1 hour: the Puerta de Jerez, San Mateo church, the vaulted passages. Tarifa is small, intense, young—the town has lived off windsurfing since the 1980s and it shows in the old town bars.

Six kilometers from the center via the coastal road, the Mirador del Estrecho is the best viewpoint over the strait. Two continents, two seas, tankers in single file: maritime traffic through the Strait of Gibraltar is among the world's densest. Free parking, 10-minute walk from the road. Best in late afternoon when raking light catches the waves.

activities.text: Tarifa's best show is free: Playa de Los Lances unfurls 7 km of sand with dozens of colorful kites constantly in the air. Two winds, Levante (east) and Poniente (west), make this strait the European kitesurfing capital. Don't come here expecting flat calm—if the Levante blows hard for several days, ferries to Tangier are canceled and the beach becomes unmanageable.

If the Levante is calm, take the ferry to Tangier: 35-minute crossing, from €37 round-trip with FRS or Inter Shipping. Passport required (EU ID card accepted). A day is enough for the medina, Souk market, and a tagine lunch. Return in the afternoon to enjoy the evening in Tarifa.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa de la Luz: 7 Days from Seville to Tarifa

visits.text: Tarragona's Roman amphitheatre is arguably Spain's most photogenic - not the largest (14,000 seats versus the Colosseum's 50,000), but the only one built facing the sea, with waves crashing just 30 meters behind the arena. Constructed around 120 AD under Hadrian, it witnessed the martyrdom of Bishop Fructuosus, burned alive in 259 during the Christian persecutions in Hispania. At the center stand the foundations of a 6th-century Visigothic basilica built directly over the arena floor. Allow 45 minutes; admission is part of the combined Tarraco ticket (€15, covering 4 sites).

The Passeig Arqueològic follows Tarraco's cyclopean ramparts for 1,100 meters, shaded by cypress and olive trees. Built in the 2nd century BC by Romans who arrived in Hispania in 218 BC during the Second Punic War, these walls rank among the oldest surviving Roman structures outside Italy. The Archbishop's Tower and Minerva Tower (the latter bearing a Latin inscription from the 2nd century BC, among the oldest on the peninsula) punctuate the route. This shaded promenade is ideal for early morning. Allow 1 hour; closed Mondays.

The Praetorium and Roman Circus form the most striking urban ensemble: 325 meters stretching beneath the old town, preserved in the basements of medieval and Renaissance buildings constructed above. The circus held 30,000 spectators for chariot races, while the Praetorium tower was converted into a Visigothic palace and later a residence for the Kings of Aragon. The underground galleries are impressive; flashlights come in handy. Allow 1 hour.

Tarragona Cathedral, perched at the city's highest point, sits atop the foundations of a Roman temple dedicated to Augustus. Construction began around 1171 under Archbishop Bernat Tort and continued until 1331, straddling late Romanesque (west facade with 12th-century portals) and Gothic (nave rising 26 meters high, stretching 104 meters long). The main altarpiece in polychrome alabaster by Pere Johan (15th century) is a jewel of Catalan Gothic sculpture. The Gothic rose window, measuring 10.50 meters in diameter, is Catalonia's largest. Admission €11, cloister access included. Remarkable acoustics during Sunday masses.

The Ferreres Aqueduct, known as Pont del Diable (Devil's Bridge), emerges from the pine forest 4 km north of Tarragona: 217 meters long, 27 meters high, two tiers of arches, built under Augustus around the late 1st century BC. It supplied Tarraco from the Francolí River over a total distance of 25 km. Legend has it the devil himself built it in one night in exchange for the soul of the first to cross - so peasants sent a dog across instead. Free access, free parking; you can walk across the top with a slight shiver (no railings).

The Museu Nacional Arqueològic de Tarragona houses the finest pieces excavated from Tarraco: the fish mosaic (47 marine species depicted in the 3rd century, discovered in 1955 at the Roman villa of Cal·lípolis in Vila-seca, measuring 6.25 × 4.50 meters), the colossal head of Augustus, and early Christian sarcophagi. The building itself, constructed in 1844, is Spain's oldest archaeological museum. Closed for renovation until 2027; temporary exhibitions at Tinglado 4 in the port - verify before visiting. When it reopens, allow 1.5 hours.

The Centcelles Mausoleum, 7 km from Tarragona in the village of Constantí, is southern Catalonia's most mysterious artifact: a 4th-century Roman tomb whose 11-meter dome bears one of the world's oldest Christian mosaics - discovered in 1877. Scenes from the Old Testament, hunts, seasons, with quality comparable to those in Ravenna. Whose tomb was it? The leading hypothesis, defended by the German Archaeological Institute, identifies Emperor Constans I, son of Constantine the Great, assassinated in Hispania in 350. Others see an ecclesiastical dignitary or local aristocrat. Guided tour required, €4, limited time slots, reservation recommended.

The Early Christian Necropolis, within the annex archaeological museum grounds, is one of the largest Christian cemeteries in the Roman world: over 2,000 tombs from the 3rd to 5th centuries, aligned around the venerated tomb of martyr Fructuosus. Carved marble sarcophagi, funerary mosaics, early Christian lamps. The site was discovered in 1923 during expansion works for the tobacco factory (Tabacalera), still standing beside the museum today. A more moving visit than you'd expect. Allow 45 minutes.

activities.text: The Balcó del Mediterrani at the end of Rambla Nova embodies the Tarragona evening ritual: locals come to "tocar ferro" - touch the metal railing for good luck before watching the sunset over the port and amphitheatre below. Sidewalks packed on weekends, nearly empty on weekdays. Bar Cosmos right beside serves decent vermouth at €3.50. Allow 30 minutes, more if you descend afterward toward Serrallo, the old fishing quarter.

For dinner, head straight to Serrallo, a still-active fishing port 1.5 km from the Rambla. Trawlers return around 5pm, with the fish auction at 5:30pm - free access from the gallery to watch buyers' frenetic pace. Restaurants Mar Salà and Cal Joanet serve excellent fideuà with fish (a paella variant using vermicelli), a Tarragona specialty. Expect €25-30 for the menu. Reserve on weekends; dining rooms always packed.

If you catch the right July-August weekend (even years), Tarragona hosts the international Castells competition - human towers reaching up to 10 stories and 12 meters high, listed as UNESCO Intangible Heritage in 2010. Plaça de la Font and the Roman arena serve as main stages. Off-season, free demonstrations on the Rambla some Saturdays. If you miss the event, the Casa Castells center in Valls (35 km) offers initiation sessions at €12. Touristy but genuine, and far more instructive than simple demonstrations.

For lunch, head to the Roman villa dels Munts in Altafulla (12 km east): a 2nd-century imperial wine estate perched on cliffs above the sea, with mosaics in place and private baths preserved. After the visit (40 min, €4), descend to Altafulla beach - 1.5 km of fine sand, shallow waters, seafront restaurants. Grilled calamari at Brisamar, €18 for the dish. One of the few Costa Daurada stretches still relatively unbuilt.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Catalonia in 10 Days: Tarragona, Priorat & the Ebro Delta
Southern Catalonia in 10 Days: Tarragona, Priorat & the Ebro Delta
Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour
Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour
Catalonia Wine Route - 7 Days from Penedès to Priorat
Southern Catalonia in 5 Days: Roman Tarragona, Poblet, Priorat and the Port of Cambrils
Southern Catalonia in 5 Days: Roman Tarragona, Poblet, Priorat and the Port of Cambrils

visits.text: Teide National Park sprawls across 19,000 hectares around the Las Cañadas caldera—a 17-kilometer-wide crater. A UNESCO site since 2007, it's the most visited park in Spain and Europe, drawing three million visitors annually.

The Roques de García thrust their rocky spires up from the volcano's base. Roque Cinchado, a precarious lava column balancing impossibly, is the park's defining image. The Mirador de la Ruleta offers sweeping views across the caldera.
The cable car departs from 2,356 meters and reaches La Rambleta at 3,555 meters in eight minutes. From the upper station, the Pico Viejo and La Fortaleza viewpoints are accessible without a permit.
At the Minas de San José, lava fields stretch endlessly beneath Teide. Variations in texture and color tell the volcano's geological story. Interpretive panels help decode the landscape.
The El Portillo Visitor Center presents exhibitions on the park's geology, fauna, and endemic flora: the Teide bugloss, which can reach 3 meters tall; the Teide violet; the blue chaffinch.

activities.text: Hike the Roques de García loop (Trail #3). The well-maintained path runs along the rock formations with constant views of Teide, then descends to the Llano de Ucanca plateau.
At over 2,000 meters elevation and far from any light pollution, Teide ranks among the world's finest stargazing sites. Guided telescope sessions begin after sunset.

Drive the road that crosses the park from north to south to appreciate the contrast between slopes: humid pine forests to the north, arid landscapes to the south. Stop at the many viewpoints along the way.

days_plan.itin_title: Canary Islands Road Trip: Tenerife & Gran Canaria

visits.text: Arrival in Teruel to conclude the itinerary. This small provincial capital (36,000 inhabitants) possesses Aragón's finest Mudéjar ensemble - four towers and the cathedral's timber ceiling are UNESCO World Heritage listed since 1986. Begin with the Torre de El Salvador (14th century), which you can climb for a panoramic view. Its structure is typical of Aragonese Mudéjar: an outer tower in brick decorated with green and white ceramics enveloping an older inner tower, with a vaulted passage between the two.

Step inside the Cathedral of Santa María de Mediavilla - you'll be amazed by its painted Mudéjar timber ceiling (13th century), nicknamed the 'Sistine Chapel of Mudéjar.' Over a surface 32 meters long, painted wood panels depict scenes from medieval daily life: craftsmen, animals, knights, as well as Islamic geometric motifs and human figures in a rare style. Look up at the cimborrio (octagonal lantern) - it's a masterpiece of fusion between Christian and Muslim traditions.

Stop at the Mausoleum of the Lovers of Teruel - it tells one of Spain's most famous love stories, the Spanish Romeo and Juliet, but three centuries earlier. Diego de Marcilla and Isabel de Segura, childhood sweethearts, were separated by Isabel's father who refused a suitor without fortune. Diego left to make his fortune and returned five years later, on the very day of Isabel's wedding to another. He died of grief asking for a kiss she refused; she in turn died the next day on his coffin. Look at the two alabaster tomb effigies - their hands reaching toward each other without touching convey rare emotion.

Finish at the Plaza del Torico, Teruel's living room, with its column topped by a tiny bull (the torico) - the city's symbol. The surrounding Modernist facades (including Casa El Torico from 1912) testify to an era of prosperity. The Escalinata, a monumental neo-Mudéjar staircase built in 1921 by architect José Torán, links the train station to the old town - its ceramics and ornamental bricks encapsulate Teruel's entire aesthetic.

activities.text: For a final Aragonese meal, Teruel is the city of jamón. Jamón de Teruel PDO was the first Spanish ham to receive protected designation of origin status (1984). The pigs are raised at altitude in the surrounding sierras, producing firm, aromatic meat. Order a ración of jamón with pan con tomate and a glass of Garnacha from Bajo Aragón - it's the perfect conclusion to this journey through Aragón.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel
Aragon Circuit: Zaragoza & Mudéjar Villages

places.name: Toledo

visits.text: The Primate Cathedral is Toledo's ostentatious heart—13th-century French Gothic plated with every imaginable richness. Its treasury weighs over sixteen tons of gold and silver, including the Custodia d'Arfe (1517), paraded annually at Corpus Christi: 2.5 meters tall, 18 kg of solid gold, over 5,000 assembled pieces. In the sacristy, don't miss El Greco—apostles, Christ being stripped. And look up toward the Transparente by Narciso Tomé (1729), baroque aberration that brings a shaft of light into the middle of the Gothic ambulatory. It's ugly, it's brilliant, it's totally Toledo.

More contemplative: the Jewish quarter, one of Spain's best preserved. The Synagogue of El Tránsito, built in 1357 by Samuel Halevi, treasurer to Peter the Cruel, preserves mudéjar stucco of rare fineness, with inscriptions in Hebrew and Arabic on the same walls. It houses the Sephardic Museum, tracing Jewish life in Spain until the 1492 expulsion. Two streets away, Santa María la Blanca, older still: 12th century, one of Europe's oldest standing synagogues, converted to a church after the 1391 pogroms. Five naves separated by horseshoe arcades that give the impression of a mosque. Combined ticket with other monuments via tourist bracelet, around €12.

Cross the Tagus to the Mirador del Valle on the south bank. This is where El Greco painted his View of Toledo, from virtually the same angle. The city presents itself as a block, huddled on its granite outcrop in the river's loop, with the square Alcázar and cathedral spire. At sunset, the limestone turns pink then orange. Go by car (free parking) or tourist bus. On return, cross the Alcántara bridge on foot, Roman in origin then reworked in the Middle Ages.

A modest church, a single painting. At Santo Tomé, El Greco's Burial of the Count of Orgaz occupies the entire chapel wall for which it was painted in 1586-1588. The canvas measures 4.80 m by 3.60 m and has never left this wall. Count Gonzalo Ruiz de Toledo had died in 1323: legend held that Saints Augustine and Stephen descended from heaven to bury him themselves. El Greco painted the scene in two tiers—earth below, heaven above—and slipped his own son into the foreground (the date 1578 on the handkerchief marks Jorge Manuel's birth). We recognize ourselves in the circle of faces: realistic portraits of contemporary Toledan notables. Entry €4.50, separate ticket. Go at opening to avoid groups, the chapel is narrow.

The Cristo de la Luz mosque is one of Spain's best-preserved Islamic buildings—a Kufic inscription on the façade dates it precisely to 999, signed by architect Musa Ibn Ali. Tiny square plan, nine different vaults supported by four recycled Visigothic columns. After the 1085 reconquest, the Hospitallers of Saint John added a Romanesque-Mudéjar apse in 1187 with a Christ Pantocrator fresco. Legend says El Cid's horse knelt before the west wall, behind which hid a statue of Christ illuminated by a lamp that had stayed lit for over three centuries of Arab rule—hence the name. Entry €4, excavations of a Roman road visible on the north side.

Plaza de Zocodover is today bar terraces and a McDonald's. But for three centuries it was the main pyre of Toledo's Inquisition, site of public autos-da-fé where sentences were read before executions. The name comes from Arabic sūq ad-dawābb, 'beast market.' A few meters higher, the Alcázar—burned four times, including the terrible 70-day siege of 1936 during the Civil War, transformed into Francoist myth, today an Army Museum. Rather cold visit if you're not a military enthusiast.

The San Juan de los Reyes monastery, at the old town's other end, closes the day. Built by Isabella and Ferdinand in 1477 to celebrate the Battle of Toro, intended as their future tomb (before they chose Granada). The exterior façade is covered with chains—those of Christian prisoners freed from Málaga, hung as ex-votos. The two-story cloister, Isabelline flamboyant Gothic, is one of the city's most beautiful things and considerably calmer than the cathedral.

activities.text: Tasting workshop of Toledo's three cuisines (Christian, Sephardic Jewish, Arab) at one of the Jewish quarter's culinary schools. Sample medieval mazapán, slow-cooked carcamusas, bizcochos de las clarisas made by cloistered nuns at San Antonio convent—sold through a revolving wooden window to never see the sister's face. Allow two hours, around €35.

Dinner at Adolfo, Calle Hombre de Palo, soberly starred and one of the city's oldest restaurants in a 14th-century house. The chef's Toledo-style partridge and venison in escabèche are truly worthwhile. Otherwise, El Trébol for carcamusas (very spicy pork in tomato) tavern-style—better atmosphere, price divided by three.

Evening guided tour 'Toledo de Leyenda': two hours through the alleys with a costumed guide recounting local ghosts—the legend of the bloody fountain, El Greco's apparition, King Rodrigo's pact in Hercules' cellar. It's touristy but well done, and the city empty of tourists after 9pm takes on another dimension. Usual departure Plaza Zocodover, around €12.

Taste the mazapanes—Toledo has produced them since the 13th century, recipe inherited from the San Clemente nuns after the Muslims' defeat at Navas de Tolosa in 1212. Santo Tomé (since 1856) and Confitería Telesforo remain the references. Enjoy with a glass of sweet moscatel. Open late, ideal late afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: The Don Quixote Route: A Road Trip Through Castilla-La Mancha's Windmills & Plains
Madrid & Castile Road Trip: Royal Palaces, Ancient Walls & World-Class Museums
Mystical Castile: Toledo, Ávila, Salamanca & Burgos in 11 Days
Mystical Castile: Toledo, Ávila, Salamanca & Burgos in 11 Days

map_keywords: Cristo de la Luz Mosque Toledo
San Juan de los Reyes Monastery Toledo

places.name: Tordesillas

visits.text: A thirty-minute drive from Valladolid brings you to Tordesillas, perched on the banks of the Duero. This small town witnessed one of world history's most decisive moments: on June 7, 1494, Spain and Portugal signed the treaty that divided the world between the two crowns, drawing a line 370 leagues west of the Cape Verde Islands. Everything west went to Spain, everything east to Portugal—this treaty explains why Brazil speaks Portuguese today. The Museo del Tratado, housed in the Casas del Tratado, recounts this episode through period documents and maps. The original treaty is listed in UNESCO's Memory of the World Register.

The Real Monasterio de Santa Clara is Tordesillas's star monument. This Mudéjar palace, built in the 14th century by Alfonso XI and transformed into a convent by his son Peter the Cruel, showcases Mudéjar art—Christian art created by Muslim artisans—in all its splendor: carved stucco, azulejos, 15th-century Arab baths, and an arcaded patio reminiscent of the Alhambra. But another episode haunts these walls: Joanna I of Castile, called Joanna the Mad, was imprisoned here from 1509 to 1555—46 years of captivity ordered by her own father, Ferdinand of Aragon, then maintained by her son Charles V. She left the palace only to reach San Antolín church via an underground passage. The guided tour lasts about an hour.

Climb to the church of San Antolín, now converted into a museum of sacred art. From its tower—the 57 steps that Joanna the Mad climbed to show herself to her people, according to legend—the view over the Duero and the Castilian plain is superb. On your way down, wander the lanes around the Plaza Mayor: you'll notice modern artistic graffiti depicting historical scenes, including a face-off between Joanna and her son Charles V.

Cross the medieval bridge over the Duero—ten stone arches spanning the river since the 13th century. It's one of Castile's finest bridges. From the opposite bank, Tordesillas's silhouette with its bell towers and ramparts offers a highly photogenic view, especially late in the day.

activities.text: Tordesillas is renowned for its Castilian gastronomy: lechazo (milk-fed lamb), judiones (large white beans), and rosquillas (traditional doughnuts). Settle into one of the arcaded restaurants on Plaza Mayor for an unhurried lunch, as befits the rhythm of the Meseta.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

map_keywords: Real Monasterio de Santa Clara Tordesillas

visits.text: Full day dedicated to Ordesa and Monte Perdido National Park, UNESCO World Heritage since 1977 and Spain's second oldest national park (established 1918). The Monte Perdido massif (3,355 m) is Europe's highest limestone massif. Stay the night before in Torla, a Pyrenean village of stone and slate guarding the entrance to the Ordesa Valley. In the morning, reach Pradera de Ordesa (by car off-season, by shuttle from Torla in summer) and set off on the Cola de Caballo trail - 17.5 km round trip, 500 m elevation gain, 5 to 6 hours of hiking.

The trail ascends the Ordesa Valley along the Arazas River. You'll first pass through dense forests of beech and fir, then by the Arripas waterfall, the Estrecho waterfall (water plunges between two narrow walls into turquoise pools), and the Gradas de Soaso - stepped waterfalls of surreal beauty. At the end, the glacial cirque of Soaso opens onto Cola de Caballo (Horsetail), a waterfall over 50 meters high cascading in a fan from the cirque walls, with the snow-capped Monte Perdido as backdrop. Marmots accompany you for the final kilometers. This is one of the most beautiful hikes in the Pyrenees.

activities.text: Back in Torla at day's end, enjoy the village. Stone streets converge on the church of San Salvador (16th century) and the viewpoint over the valley. In the evening, restaurants serve Upper Aragón specialties: migas de pastor (fried breadcrumbs with garlic and chorizo), roast lamb, setas (wild mushrooms) in season. It's a well-earned meal after the hike.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel

places.name: Toro

visits.text: Toro rises on a cliff above the Duero, 30 minutes west of Tordesillas. The Collegiate Church of Santa María la Mayor, begun in the late 12th century, is the major monument. Its model is Zamora Cathedral: a tower-lantern crowned by a ribbed dome, typical of the Duero valley Romanesque. But it's the Portada de la Majestad, the west portico completed around 1240, that alone justifies the journey: over 125 carved figurines and—exceptionally rare—they still preserve their original polychrome from eight centuries ago. It's the only intact polychrome Romanesque portico in Spain.

Tour Toro's Romanesque-Mudéjar churches: San Lorenzo el Real (12th century), the oldest, with its brick walls; El Salvador, San Pedro del Olmo, and the hermitage of Santa María de la Vega. Toro had 40 churches in the Middle Ages—about twenty remain, some in poetic ruin. The Alcázar, partially restored, offers a plunging view over the Duero and the vineyards below.

Descend to the medieval bridge spanning the Duero below the town. From the bridge, look up: Toro's silhouette perched on its cliff, with the collegiate's dome rising from the rooftops, is one of the most spectacular sights in Duero Castile.

activities.text: Toro is the heart of DO Toro, a wine appellation whose wines already traveled in Christopher Columbus's holds. The local grape, Tinta de Toro—a cousin of Tempranillo—grows on sandy soils that protected it from phylloxera in the 19th century. Some vines are pre-phylloxera, among Europe's oldest. Operators offer cellar visits with tastings. The town's subsoil hides over 300 medieval underground cellars—some can be visited.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria

map_keywords: Colegiata Santa María la Mayor Toro
Iglesia San Lorenzo el Real Toro

visits.text: Vivid pink in summer, lilac in winter: the lagoon shifts with salt concentration and light. Its color is produced by halobacteria and the algae Dunaliella salina. At 350 grams of salt per liter, the concentration matches that of the Dead Sea. Pink flamingos feed here regularly. Swimming prohibited—but the walk along the greenway at sunset ranks among the finest on the Costa Blanca.

activities.text: The salt flats have produced salt since Roman times at this site—remains of a probable Roman factory were found in underwater excavations. Today Europe's largest salt producer, the complex extracts significant quantities annually, much of it sent to salt Northern European roads in winter. A tourist train departs from Paseo de la Libertad for a guided tour of the salt flats. Available in high season.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

visits.text: Tortosa preserves the most visible trace of seven centuries of Muslim rule in Catalonia: the Castell de la Suda, an Arab fortress founded in the 10th century under Caliph Abd al-Rahman III (interior well dated 944), perched 60 meters above the Ebro, converted to a parador in 1972. The interior courtyard mixes Almohad cistern and repurposed Visigothic chapel. Plunging views over the Ebro meanders, the cathedral 100 meters below, and on clear days, the delta 30 km away. Free visit of exterior; to enter the courtyard, have coffee at the parador bar (€5). Clever and legal.

Tortosa's Cathedral of Santa Maria, begun in 1347 on the foundations of the great mosque, is Gothic in the lineage of Catalan cathedrals (Barcelona, Girona) - single wide nave, polygonal apse with ambulatory. Peculiarity: six centuries of construction, and still no proper west facade. The 15th-century alabaster altarpiece and treasury (manuscripts, goldwork) justify the €5 admission. Shaded cloister, ideal for catching your breath between 1pm and 4pm when the city melts under 38°C in July.

The Reials Col·legis, Catalonia's most important Renaissance ensemble, were founded as an institution by Charles V in 1544 to educate the sons of Moors converted to Christianity. Work on the Col·legi de Sant Jaume i Sant Maties - the main building - began in 1564, attributed today to Francisco de Montehermoso. Renaissance patio unique in Catalonia, sculpted frieze of royal couples of the Crown of Aragon from Ramon Berenguer IV to Philip III. The evangelization experiment ended with the expulsion of Moriscos in 1610; the buildings became a seminary and continued functioning as a college. Today a museum and Renaissance Interpretation Center. Admission €3, allow 1 hour.

The Jardins del Príncep, created in 1992 along the Ebro, display 26 sculptures by Tortosa native Santiago de Santiago (1925-2024) - monumental bronzes on themes of couples and dance. Original, sometimes surprising, never boring. Shaded 800-meter promenade, free access 24/7. At the end, the Pont de l'Estat (1944, old metal bridge) and views of the illuminated castell at twilight. Numerous benches, ideal in the evening.

activities.text: Tortosa conceals one of Spain's last historical memory sites: the Battle of the Ebro (July-November 1938), the Civil War's longest and deadliest battle - 30,000 dead in 115 days. The Centre d'Interprétació de la Batalla de l'Ebre (COMEBE) offers 4 memorial sites in the region, including 115 Dies in Corbera d'Ebre (25 km away) - a ruined village kept in its state since the 1938 bombing. Moving visit, French audioguide €5. Sensitive subject, treated with gravity. Allow 2 hours round trip with drive.

For lunch, suquet d'anguila - delta eel stew - is the Tortosa specialty. Restaurants Mas de Pau and La Galera serve a traditional version at €18, accompanied by aioli and potatoes. Baix Ebre olive oil (PDO Terra Alta) is used everywhere - fruity, peppery, among Spain's finest. Also try coques de recapte, thin savory tarts with peppers and anchovies. Weekend reservations recommended.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Catalonia in 10 Days: Tarragona, Priorat & the Ebro Delta
Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour

places.name: Tossa De Mar

visits.text: The Vila Vella of Tossa de Mar is the only intact medieval fortified town on the entire Catalan coast, listed as a historic monument in 1931. Its walls were built from 1186 to defend against Barbary pirates - Ramon de Berga, the abbot who governed the town, ordered their construction after centuries of raids. The perimeter includes three cylindrical towers and four keeps, including the Clock Tower (which housed the town's only public clock) and the Tower of Homage overlooking Codolar beach. Climb up to the lighthouse - built in 1917 on the site of the old castle - for the best coastal view.

Platja Gran stretches 400 meters at the foot of the medieval ramparts - it's one of the rare beaches in the world where you sunbathe beneath the walls of a 12th-century fortress. The waters are clear and sheltered. For more tranquility, walk 15 minutes to Cala Codolar, a small rocky nook nestled beneath the Vila Vella ramparts, ideal for snorkeling.

The Tossa Municipal Museum, founded in 1935 in the former governor's residence, is Spain's first contemporary art museum. It holds a painting by Marc Chagall - who stayed in Tossa and dubbed it 'blue paradise' - and Roman mosaics from the 1st-century BC villa dels Ametllers found on the hillside.

activities.text: Sea kayaking along the cliffs and coves between Tossa and Sant Feliu de Guíxols: the coast is carved with caves and small beaches accessible only by water. Local operators offer 2 to 4-hour guided outings. Allow 2 hours to paddle to Cala Giverola.

Snorkeling at Es Codolar cove or Cala Pola: the transparent waters of the Costa Brava allow you to see the bottom several meters deep. Posidonia (seagrass meadows) and sea urchins are the most common species. Bring your mask if you have one.

Dinner at the port with suquet de peix, a local fish stew simmered with potatoes and saffron - the house specialty in almost every waterfront restaurant. The region's white wine (Alella or Empordà) pairs perfectly with seafood.

days_plan.itin_title: Barcelona and Costa Brava Road Trip: Coves and Villages

map_keywords: Platja Gran Beach Tossa de Mar

visits.text: Take the road east through the Alpujarras, passing through Pitres and Pórtugos. At Pórtugos, stop at the Fuente Agria - a spring rich in iron that stains the waterfall and surrounding rocks rust-orange. Locals attribute healing properties to it. It's a lovely 20-minute stop in a surprisingly verdant setting for Andalusia.

Trevélez is the highest village in mainland Spain, at 1,476 meters altitude, on the slopes of Mulhacén. The village is divided into three neighborhoods (barrios) - bajo, medio and alto - connected by steep cobbled lanes. The barrio bajo is the most touristy, the alto the most authentic. Here, the smell of ham is everywhere: Trevélez is famous throughout Spain for its jamón serrano, naturally cured for 14 to 24 months in drying rooms above 1,200 meters altitude, without any preservatives. The IGP designation "Jamón de Trevélez" dates back to 1862, when Queen Isabella II granted the village the privilege of stamping its hams with the royal seal after they won the national competition.

Continue to Válor, 30 minutes east of Trevélez. This discreet village carries a dramatic history: this is where Fernando de Córdoba y Válor was born, who took the Arabic name Abén Humeya claiming descent from the Umayyad caliphs of Córdoba. On Christmas Eve 1568, he led the last great Morisco revolt against Philip II. Within weeks, 25,000 armed men joined him and the insurrection engulfed the entire region. It took the intervention of Don Juan of Austria with 20,000 soldiers to crush the revolt in 1571. More than 80,000 Moriscos were subsequently deported across the peninsula. Every September, Válor reenacts this episode during its Moors and Christians festival.

activities.text: Visit a ham-drying facility in Trevélez - several producers open their doors to visitors. You'll see hundreds of legs suspended from the ceiling, smell the dry Sierra Nevada air doing the curing work, and taste slices cut right in front of you. The secret of this ham is the altitude: the cold, dry air of the Sierra Nevada, combined with the Mediterranean breeze, creates natural curing conditions that no cold storage facility can replicate.

If you're a hiker, Trevélez is the starting point for climbing Mulhacén (3,479 m), the highest peak of the Iberian Peninsula. The round trip via the Siete Lagunas trail takes 8 to 10 hours and requires good physical condition. For a gentler option, the trail along the río Trevélez through the chestnut groves is family-friendly.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Granada: Alpujarras, Guadix and the Sierras
Al-Andalus: From the Mezquita to the Alpujarras in 10 Days

visits.text: The equestrian statue of Francisco Pizarro (1929) dominates Plaza Mayor. Charles Rumsey's bronze depicts the conqueror of Peru, born in Trujillo in 1478.
The Castillo de Trujillo (9th century) crowns the hilltop. This Moorish fortress offers panoramic views across the plains stretching 30 kilometers in every direction.
The Iglesia de Santa María la Mayor blends Romanesque, Gothic, and Renaissance styles. Fernando Gallego's plateresque altarpiece is magnificent.
The Palacio del Marqués de la Conquista belonged to Hernando Pizarro. Its carved corner balcony displays busts of the Pizarro family and their Inca wives.
Step inside the Casa-Museo de Pizarro. This 15th-century mansion tells the conquistador's story through period documents and artifacts.
The Iglesia de San Martín (14th-16th century) combines Gothic and Renaissance. Two towers flank a single nave with an octagonal presbytery.
The Palacio de Orellana-Pizarro features a Renaissance portal and plateresque courtyard. It belonged to Francisco de Orellana, first European to navigate the Amazon.

activities.text: Climb to the castle for sunset. Golden light sets the tile roofs ablaze and the Renaissance palaces of Plaza Mayor glow below.
The local market sells Ibores cheese (goat cheese aged with pimentón), Iberian cured meats, and honey from the nearby sierras.
Settle into a terrace on Plaza Mayor at dusk. Migas extremeñas, moraga, and ibérico tapas pair with local wine.
The Festival Nacional del Queso transforms Plaza Mayor into a massive cheese market in late April-early May. Over 100,000 visitors sample cheeses from across Spain.

days_plan.itin_title: Extremadura Road Trip: Cáceres, Mérida & Trujillo

visits.text: If Baeza is the ecclesiastical and university town, Úbeda is the noble and palatial one—the other half of the UNESCO inscription. It all begins at Plaza Vázquez de Molina, considered one of Spain's most beautiful Renaissance squares. Francisco de los Cobos y Molina, Secretary of State to Charles V, and his nephew Juan Vázquez de Molina, who succeeded him under Philip II, had an ensemble of palaces and chapels built here that rival anything in Tuscany. The architect Andrés de Vandelvira, a native of the region, designed most of the buildings—his influence would later be found in Latin American cathedrals.

The Sacra Capilla del Salvador, commissioned by Francisco de los Cobos as a private funerary chapel, is Vandelvira's masterpiece in Úbeda. Completed in 1559, its Plateresque façade displays extravagant sculptural richness—mythological scenes, grotesques, atlantes, the entire vocabulary of the Italian Renaissance translated into Andalusian stone. Inside, Vandelvira's sacristy is a jewel of proportion, with its caryatids and atlantes framing the windows. Berruguete's monumental altarpiece was partially destroyed during the Civil War, but what remains is impressive.

Opposite the chapel, the Palacio del Deán Ortega (16th century), now a Parador hotel, deserves a visit even if you're not staying there—go in to see the two-level arcaded Renaissance courtyard and the gallery with views over the olive groves. At the other end of the square, the Palacio de las Cadenas (Palace of the Chains), former palace of Juan Vázquez de Molina, now serves as the town hall. Its façade is a model of Spanish purist Renaissance.

Head down to the old quarter to discover the Sinagoga del Agua, discovered by chance during renovation work in 2007. This underground site comprises a prayer hall, a ritual bath (mikveh), a women's gallery, and a well—the most complete medieval synagogue ensemble found in Spain. The guided tour is fascinating and illuminates the life of Úbeda's Jewish community before the 1492 expulsion.

activities.text: The alfarero quarter (potters' quarter) of San Millán, in the lower part of town, perpetuates a pottery tradition dating back to Moorish times. The workshops produce pieces in emerald green on a white background—Úbeda's typical style. Push open a workshop door to see the artisans at work, and leave with a dish or jar as a souvenir. It's one of the few places in Spain where traditional pottery is still a living craft, not a tourist attraction.

days_plan.itin_title: Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
Jaén's Renaissance Jewels: Olive Grove Landscapes & Wild Sierras of Andalusia
Al-Andalus: From the Mezquita to the Alpujarras in 10 Days

visits.text: The sanctuary-fortress of Santa María de Ujué dominates the village from the hilltop at 815 meters elevation. Built between the 11th and 14th centuries, it blends Romanesque and Gothic styles.
Two crenellated towers and the rampart walk encircle the church. This medieval fortification was one of the principal strongholds of the kings of Navarre from the 10th century onward.

Inside, the sculpture of the Virgin of Ujué from 1190 stands as one of the finest examples of Romanesque carving in Navarre. King Charles II the Bad had his heart deposited in a niche near this statue.
Walk the Gothic rampart path with its covered galleries. The 14th-century wooden balustrade offers views across the Ribera plain and the Pyrenees.
The medieval village ranks among Spain's most beautiful. Its 300 inhabitants live in stone houses with vaulted passageways along steep climbing lanes.

activities.text: Follow a circular trail from the village to observe the landscapes of middle Navarre, where vineyards meet grain fields.
