Sample the migas de pastor, a traditional dish of bread, tomatoes, garlic, ham, and mushrooms. On the third Sunday of September, the entire village celebrates Migas Day.
Ascend to the Ujué viewpoint at twilight. The sky ignites above the Bardenas Reales desert visible to the south.
After dark, the absence of light pollution transforms Ujué into a natural observatory. Gaze upward at the Milky Way stretching overhead.

days_plan.itin_title: Navarre Road Trip: Castles, Irati Forest & the Camino

visits.text: To finish in style: the Bodega Redonda of Utiel, dating from 1891, today houses the Museo del Vino de la DOP Utiel-Requena, C/ Sevilla 12. It's a former cooperative cellar with dome, converted into a museum on the history of the vine in the region since Iberian Antiquity. Free entry. Buy a few bottles of Bobal on the way out—you'll hardly find it outside Spain.

activities.text: If time permits, descend into Utiel's underground bodegas—the town has hundreds of medieval cellars under its streets, the most important underground wine heritage in the Valencian Community according to the local tourist office. Several are accessible via the Ruta del Vino Utiel-Requena (rutavino.com). Ideal to close this circuit between windmills, sierra, and vines.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: Locals named it Ciudad Encantada—the Enchanted City—because the limestone formations resemble animals, ships, faces. Declared a Site of National Interest in 1929, it has been taking shape for 90 million years: back then, this plateau at over 1,400 metres altitude lay at the bottom of the Tethys Sea. The circuit runs about 3 km, well marked and easy; allow 1h30. Buy your ticket at the taquilla on site—no online sales.

Declared a site of national interest in 1929, the Ciudad Encantada holds named formations: the Tormo (mushroom rock), Los Barcos (three giant ships), La Tortuga. Look for 'La Lucha del Elefante y el Cocodrilo'—the only spot where the rock seems alive. Opt for the guided tour if you come on a weekend; the explanatory workshops are worth the extra hour.

activities.text: Take the 3-km circular trail on your own—turquoise markers outbound, pink for the return. Altitude: 1,450 m, so even in June it can be cool. Bring a layer, water, and a snack. Entry is paid; check seasonal hours before you leave, as the Ciudad Encantada sometimes closes early off-season.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

visits.text: The Wine Museum of Valdepeñas occupies the former Leocadio Morales winery from 1901: Manchego-style courtyard with well, barrel hall with giant tinajas, and underground cellar. The tour traces the history of the Valdepeñas DO in engaging detail. The professional tasting room is open to visitors.

Valdepeñas means 'valley of stones' and takes its name from the Sierra Morena foothills that begin here. Plaza de España, the town center, buzzes with tapas bars. The Gregorio Prieto Museum, dedicated to the local painter and friend of Lorca and the Generation of '27, rounds out the cultural visit.

Visit a traditional bodega to see the clay tinajas: some hold over 1,000 liters of wine. The Valdepeñas DO is one of Spain's oldest. Vineyards around town are dominated by tempranillo, locally known as cencibel.

activities.text: Three kilometers from Valdepeñas, the Cerro de las Cabezas is an Iberian hilltop settlement excavated since the 1990s. Dry-stone streets and walls are clearly visible. Free guided tours on weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: Campo de Calatrava and Valle de Alcudia in 5 Days

visits.text: Drive east to enter Lower Aragón. First stop at Alcañiz (1h40 from Daroca), the second city of Teruel province. Climb to the Calatrava castle, a fortress of the military Order of Calatrava that has dominated the town since the 12th century - it now houses a Parador. The Gothic Lonja (market hall) from the 15th century, one of Aragón's finest, stands out with its three pointed arches opening onto the Plaza de España. The collegiate church of Santa María la Mayor and the medieval underground passages beneath the square complete the visit.

Continue 30 minutes east to reach Valderrobres, capital of the Matarraña comarca, nicknamed 'Aragonese Tuscany.' The arrival is spectacular: the medieval stone bridge spans the Río Matarraña and leads straight to the San Roque gate, one of seven gates in the old wall. At the village summit, the archbishop's castle-palace (14th-15th centuries) connects to the church of Santa María la Mayor, one of Aragón's finest examples of Levantine Gothic. Its sculpted facade and rose window are remarkably delicate.

If time permits, drive 15 minutes south to Calaceite, the other jewel of Matarraña. Classified as a Historic Site, this stone village with sloping streets and seigneurial houses in wrought iron has kept its Mediterranean character intact. The Plaza de España, the Renaissance town hall (1609), and the church of the Asunción form a harmonious ensemble. The Catalan influence - the border is just kilometers away - is felt in the architecture and gastronomy.

activities.text: In the evening, dine in Valderrobres. Matarraña's gastronomy is Mediterranean countryside cuisine: PDO Bajo Aragón olive oil, roast lamb, black truffle in season (Lower Aragón is one of Europe's top producers), goat cheeses, and Calanda peaches. Accompany your meal with a wine from the DO Bajo Aragón - powerful reds based on Garnacha.

days_plan.itin_title: Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragón: From Ordesa to Teruel

places.name: Valencia

visits.text: The City of Arts and Sciences impresses by its sheer scale—the complex designed by Calatrava and Bofill stretches two kilometers along the old Turia riverbed. The Hemisfèric and Museu de les Ciències merit the entrance fee; the Oceanogràfic, Europe's largest aquarium according to its official site, deserves an entire half-day.

The Lonja de la Seda dates from 1482 and was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1996. This Gothic trading hall—built when Valencia was one of the Mediterranean's busiest commercial centres—gives a tangible idea of the city's mercantile power at that time. Quick visit, under 45 minutes.

Valencia's central market is perhaps Spain's most beautiful covered market—an Art Deco nave of iron and ceramic inaugurated in 1928 that still houses over 900 stalls today. Come between 9am and 11am to see it in action. After 1pm, half the stalls close.

The railway station's architecture astonishes: closer to a Belle Époque palace than a utilitarian station, the Estació del Nord was inaugurated in 1917 with Valencian ceramic frescoes on every wall. It still functions—travellers await their trains surrounded by orange-tree mosaics.

activities.text: Explore the Barrio del Carmen in the evening, when galleries and tapas bars open their doors. This medieval quarter between the city's two old walls concentrates street art, vermouth taverns, and lively terraces. No reservation needed before 9pm; after that, it's packed.

The blue smoke and scent of toasted rice escaping from the large paëlleras signal the good addresses. The true paella valenciana contains no seafood—beans, chicken, rabbit, rosemary. Avoid restaurants that offer it all hours: a proper paella is prepared to order and takes 30 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages
Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages

map_keywords: City of Arts and Sciences Valencia

visits.text: On the surface, Vall d'Uixó has nothing remarkable. Beneath its feet, that's another story: the San José caves shelter the longest navigable underground river in Europe, according to official site data. The main gallery extends over 700 meters explored by boat.

activities.text: The boat glides under calcite vaults where stalactites brush within centimeters of your face. The underground river remains at a constant 18°C year-round—bring a light jacket even in August. Book online in high season, morning slots fill quickly.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages

places.name: Vall De Boi

visits.text: First of all, stop at the Centre del Romànic in Erill la Vall to understand what you're about to see. Small museum—30 minutes suffices—that places the valley's nine churches in context: Lombard masters from northern Italy in the 12th century, fresco al fresco technique, Erill sculptural school, rediscovery by Puig i Cadafalch in 1907 during an expedition by the Institute of Catalan Studies. The combined ticket gives access to several valley churches—economical if you plan to see three or four.

The journey's highlight: Sant Climent de Taüll. Consecrated December 10, 1123 by the Bishop of Roda, it's the valley's best-preserved Romanesque building. Basilica plan with three naves, three apses, and especially that six-story square bell tower—characteristic Lombard decoration with its arcatures and saw-tooth friezes. Inside, the original apse was decorated with a masterful fresco of Christ Pantocrator, removed by strappo in 1919 and preserved at the MNAC in Barcelona. On site, a video-mapping projection reconstructs the original frescoes on the walls: technically spectacular, and more moving than you'd think.

200 meters uphill in the village, Santa Maria de Taüll—consecrated the day after Sant Climent, December 11, 1123, by the same bishop. More modest, rougher, but the atmosphere is unique: surrounded by its cemetery, encircled by medieval houses. The nave holds a reproduction of the original apsidal paintings, including the characteristic Lombard orant. Difference in personality from Sant Climent: Santa Maria has remained the village's living center, still used for worship.

Sant Joan de Boí, in the village of Boí 3 km away, preserves more than the others the architecture of the valley's first construction phase—11th century. Three naves, three apses, medieval paving. The original paintings—known as the "Bestiary paintings"—are at the MNAC, but the church has a raw, almost prehistoric presence you don't find elsewhere. For Romanesque architecture enthusiasts, it's the most archaic of the nine.

Santa Eulàlia d'Erill la Vall, right next to the Centre del Romànic, possesses one of the valley's most elegant bell towers—six stories, square plan, like Sant Climent and Sant Joan. This is where the Erill workshop, the sculptural school that carved the famous Descent from the Cross whose originals are at the MNAC and the Episcopal Museum of Vic, worked. On site, a polychrome wooden copy reconstructs the ensemble: Christ, Nicodemus, Joseph of Arimathea, Virgin, Saint John, two thieves. Moving.

To enter the western zone of Aigüestortes National Park, leave your car at the Palanca de la Molina parking (free) and take the "Otter Path"—1-hour walk to the Planell d'Aigüestortes through the Sant Nicolau valley. The river meanders peacefully between meadows and centuries-old black pine forests. On the route, stop at the hermitage of Sant Nicolau, modest Lombard Romanesque with a single nave. If you prefer to save your legs, official taxis depart from Boí to the plain—it's the only authorized transport.

Sant Feliu de Barruera, in the valley's administrative center, is one of the nine UNESCO-listed churches. Dating from the 11th-12th centuries, it has kept its rural aspect almost intact. It's the most modest of the valley's great Romanesque churches—small, low, with a single nave—but it's also the most representative of ordinary rural architecture of the period. It housed medieval frescoes now removed and preserved at the diocesan museum of Lleida.

The Ermita de Sant Quirc de Durro, perched aside on a ridge above the village, is the ninth and last of the valley's UNESCO inscriptions. Smaller than the others, almost a chapel, it has a singular charm—isolated, battered by winds, accessible by a 20-minute path from Durro. Panoramic view over the entire basin and the Maladeta massif in the distance. The patron feast on June 16 still draws villagers for open-air mass.

activities.text: If you arrive early and have time left in late afternoon, climb to Durro (1,386 m)—the highest of the valley's villages. The church of the Nativitat has a fortified bell tower unique in Catalonia, and the village center has kept a rough medieval atmosphere. Away from the main road, few tourists climb up here. Count 30 minutes for the visit, but especially the view over the entire valley basin.

Evening dinner at the Caldes de Boí thermal baths or in an inn in Barruera—the valley's administrative center. Catalan Pyrenean cuisine classics: olla aranesa (stew), trinxat (mashed potato and cabbage with bacon), duck confit with hazelnut, homemade tarts. The Caldes baths are at 1,500 m altitude—37 springs, temperatures from 4 to 56°C, exploited since Roman times. Possibility of therapeutic bathing during the day.

Hiking in Aigüestortes: from the Planell, several options depending on your fitness. The accessible trail (wooden boardwalks) loops around the plain in 1 hour, no difficulty, ideal for families. More committed: climb to Estany Llong (round trip 3h, 200m elevation gain) or to Portarró pass to cross to the other side of the park. The park counts more than 200 glacial lakes—the highest concentration in the entire Pyrenees chain. Highest peak: Pic de Comaloforno, 3,030 m.

Thermal alternative: spend the afternoon at the Termes de Caldes de Boí. Spa Siente Boí, 1,200 m² of facilities, pools at different temperatures, sensory circuit, sauna. It's one of the Pyrenees' finest thermal baths, and a good antidote to hiking aches. At 1,500 m altitude, the waters emerge at 56°C at the hottest springs. The Romans went there already.

days_plan.itin_title: Catalan Pyrenees - 6-Day Discovery
Catalan Pyrenees - 6-Day Discovery
Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour
Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour
Catalan Pyrenees and Romanesque Art in 8 Days: Lleida, Pallars, Vall de Boí and Val d'Aran
Catalan Pyrenees and Romanesque Art in 8 Days: Lleida, Pallars, Vall de Boí and Val d'Aran

map_keywords: Centre del Romànic Vall de Boí

visits.text: Valladolid, final stop. 300,000 inhabitants, capital of Castile-León, but a city often underestimated. Yet: Spain's capital under Philip II and Philip III (1601-1606), place where Cervantes published the first edition of Don Quixote in 1605, city where Christopher Columbus died in 1506, and site of the Catholic Monarchs' marriage in 1469. Plaza Mayor (1561), rebuilt after the same year's fire, is one of Spain's oldest enclosed plazas mayores and served as model for Madrid and Salamanca. Red façades, white arcades, lively square.

The National Museum of Sculpture, in the Colegio de San Gregorio, is probably the finest museum in Castile-León—and one of northern Spain's most unrecognized. The college's Gothic-Isabelline façade (1488-1496), carved like stone lace, is itself an artwork. The collection: the world's most complete of 15th-18th-century Spanish polychrome sculpture. Berruguete, Juan de Juni, Gregorio Fernández—the three giants of Castilian Hispanic baroque. Raw realism of crucified Christs, hyperrealistic expressions, preserved polychromies. Count 2 hours.

The Cervantes House Museum, on Calle Rastro. House where the writer lived from 1604 to 1606 with his family—his wife, sisters, natural daughter. Here he corrected proofs of Don Quixote's first edition, released January 1605. Small two-story house typical of 17th-century Valladolid, refurnished as it was by the Hispanic Society of America (Archer Huntington, American patron). Free Sundays, modest but evocative. Steps from Plaza Mayor.

The Church of San Pablo, on Plaza San Pablo, has a 1486-1492 Plateresque façade-altarpiece considered one of Spain's most extraordinary—four levels of sculptures, biblical scenes, coats of arms. Work of Simon of Cologne. Philip II was baptized here in 1527 (his father Charles V was in Valladolid). Right beside it, the Royal Palace (16th century) where court temporarily settled between 1601 and 1606. Inside the church, altarpiece by Pedro de Mena.

activities.text: Valladolid Cathedral is an incompletion story. Designed by Juan de Herrera in the 1580s—the Escorial's architect—to be Spain's largest cathedral. But court moved to Madrid in 1606, and construction never finished. Today, we see a quarter of the original project: austere Renaissance façade, neoclassical interior, single tower (the twin collapsed in 1841). The adjoining diocesan museum shows 13th-18th-century pieces. Historical curiosity more than aesthetic, but worth passing through to find it less finished than expected.

Walk in Campo Grande park—115,000 m², Valladolid's Central Park. Free-roaming peacocks strolling the paths (since 1877), Swan fountain, French gardens, music kiosk. Ideal for a final stroll before leaving the city. Beside it, Plaza Zorrilla and the monument to José Zorrilla—author of Don Juan Tenorio (1844), play that became an annual All Saints' tradition throughout Spain. Born in Valladolid in 1817.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria
Castile-León and Galicia: 21 days from Roman aqueducts to the Atlantic
Castile and León - 14-Day Grand Tour
Ribera del Duero in 7 days: The Plateau That Rivals Rioja

visits.text: Valldemossa Charterhouse was built in 1309 as a royal residence for the asthmatic son of Jaume II. Converted to a monastery in 1399, it hosted Chopin and George Sand during the winter of 1838-1839.
Chopin and Sand's cells can be visited with the authentic Pleyel piano. The walls preserve memories of that winter which inspired the Preludes and 'A Winter in Majorca.'
The monastic pharmacy dates to 1722. Considered one of Europe's best-preserved, it displays jars, alembics, and medicinal plants once cultivated by the monks.
The church's turquoise bell tower is visible from afar. Its azulejo tiles depict Saint Catalina Tomàs, Majorca's patron saint, born in Valldemossa in 1531.
Cobbled lanes climb between stone houses with potted geraniums. Every facade displays a different azulejo illustrating the beata's life.
The Charterhouse gardens cascade in terraces. Orange, lemon, and almond trees offer panoramic views over the verdant valley.

activities.text: Serra de Tramuntana trails wind between centuries-old olive groves. The hike toward Deià crosses karst landscapes and dry-stone terraces.
Several olive oil estates offer tastings and tours. Majorcan olive oil has held protected designation status since 2002.
Coca de patata is dunked in thick hot chocolate. This local potato-based pastry is served in cafés around Plaça Cartuja.
The Chopin Festival runs each summer. Piano concerts resonate through the Charterhouse's Chopin Room from August through September.

days_plan.itin_title: Majorca Road Trip: Serra de Tramuntana & Hidden Coves

places.name: Vejer De La Frontera

visits.text: Vejer de la Frontera, declared a Historic-Artistic Ensemble in 1976, reveals itself as you climb cobbled lanes to the 10th-11th century castle. Pass through its single horseshoe arch: two patios and ramparts offer plunging views over the Barbate valley and the distant sea.

Medieval ramparts snake 2 kilometers around the old town. Four gates survive: Arco de la Segur, Arco de Sancho IV, Arco de la Villa, and Puerta Cerrada. The latter, facing the Jewish quarter, was walled up for centuries to protect the town from North African pirates.

Plaza de España, the village heart, centers on a fountain adorned with multicolored Sevillian tiles. The 16th-century church of Divino Salvador, built atop the former mosque, retained the original minaret converted into its bell tower.

Head to Mirador de la Cobijada to discover the statue of this enigmatic figure: a woman wrapped in a black cloak revealing only one eye. This vestimentary tradition of Castilian origin, dating to the 16th century, has become Vejer's symbol. The viewpoint extends to Barbate's beaches and, on clear days, to Africa.

Vejer's seven windmills, built in the 1960s, stand on the windy ridge above the village. They've become the town's iconic image, offering panoramic views of the coast, El Palmar beach, and the rolling hills of La Janda.

Lose yourself in the old Jewish quarter, the judería, where vaulted passages and whitewashed houses press so close they nearly touch. The 18th-century Casa del Mayorazgo displays a patio overflowing with 450 flowerpots in a riot of color.

activities.text: The Barbate and Zahara de los Atunes region perpetuates bluefin tuna fishing by almadraba, a millennia-old technique inherited from the Phoenicians. From May to June, Vejer restaurants showcase tuna in every form: encebollado, tartare, tataki, grilled belly—celebrating this ancient harvest of the sea.

Descend to El Palmar beach, part of Vejer municipality, 8 kilometers from the village. This wild ribbon of sand pounded by Atlantic waves draws surfers and sunset lovers to one of the coast's most spectacular and unspoiled stretches.

Sample local gastronomy in a downtown restaurant: Vejer-style chicken pie, couscous with tuna and pumpkin, or retinta—meat from indigenous cattle raised free-range in the surrounding countryside, prized for its deep flavor and tenderness.

days_plan.itin_title: Cádiz Road Trip: Jerez, Sherry Bodegas & White Villages
Costa de la Luz: 7 Days from Seville to Tarifa

visits.text: Market documented since the 9th century. Tuesday and Saturday morning, Vic's Plaça Major—78 × 47 m, vast medieval esplanade bordered by arcades on three sides—transforms into a living producers' market, not a tourist market. Residents and market gardeners truly meet. Neo-Gothic, baroque, modernist façades: three centuries of architecture blended seamlessly. The center, in packed earth, is unique in Catalonia. Time your visit for a Tuesday or Saturday, it changes everything.

Sert painted Vic cathedral twice. First version done between 1926 and 1930, deliberately burned in July 1936 at the start of the Civil War. Second version redone between 1940 and 1945—2,000 m² of murals in black, gold and red, overwhelming, theatrical, expressionist. The artist's last major work, completed just before his death. Sant Pere cathedral blends four eras: Romanesque crypt and bell tower (11th century, under Bishop Oliba), Gothic cloister, 18th-century neoclassical nave. Never renovated to look pretty. Touristy, but genuine.

Twenty thousand pieces. That's what the Episcopal Museum of Vic houses, one of the most important collections of Romanesque and Gothic art in Europe. The 'Davallament d'Erill la Vall' (12th century), polychrome wood carved group depicting the Descent from the Cross, is perhaps the best-preserved Romanesque wood ensemble in the world. Magnificent modern building attached to the cathedral, impeccable chronological route. If you only do one museum in Catalonia to understand Romanesque art, this is it.

Unexpected historical curiosity: the Roman Temple (2nd century) was rediscovered in 1882, hidden inside a medieval castle. The lords of Montcada had simply transformed the temple into a keep in the Romanesque era, without demolishing. It's one of the rare Roman temples preserved in Catalonia. Small, sober, moving.

activities.text: Perfect souvenir: 'llonganissa de Vic', dry sausage with protected designation of origin, first written mentions dating to 1456. Several historic butchers sell it: Casa Riera Ordeix (since 1852), Salgot, Jaume Tió. The sausage travels well. At El Petricó, a local institution, they serve it with pa amb tomàquet (bread rubbed with tomato), as it should be.

If you're here in September, Mercat de Música Viva is an international festival that transforms the city into a giant stage: concerts in monuments, theaters, bars, squares. Four days, festival atmosphere, book accommodation well in advance. If you're here in December, the Mercat Medieval recreates a life-size medieval market with craftsmen, jugglers, troubadours. A fine way to end the journey.

days_plan.itin_title: Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour

places.name: Vielha

visits.text: Welcome to the Val d'Aran, Catalonia's only valley facing the Atlantic—the Garonne rises at Pla de Beret. Vielha (974 m), the capital, has 6,000 inhabitants and 40% of the valley's population. Aranese is spoken here, a variety of Gascon Occitan, co-official language on equal terms with Catalan and Spanish. The historic center, with its stone houses and steep slate roofs, feels decidedly more French Pyrenees than Catalan. Obligatory stroll along the Nere, the tributary that cuts through town.

On the main plaza, the church of Sant Miquèu—jewel of Aranese Romanesque, built in the 13th-14th centuries in transition toward Gothic. Sculpted façade with angels and biblical scenes, tympanum with Saint Michael slaying the demon. The octagonal bell tower of 1506 was added by order of Ferdinand the Catholic to serve as defense against French incursions. But the piece that changes everything, inside, is the Christ of Mijaran: 12th-century polychrome sculpture, of which only the torso remains, attributed to the Erill la Vall workshop—the same one that sculpted the Descents from the Cross of the Vall de Boí.

The Musèu dera Val d'Aran occupies the Tor deth Generau Martinhon, late Gothic mansion from the 17th century. Three floors dedicated to the valley's history, ethnography and language—a singular case in Europe for a territory of 10,000 inhabitants. Rooms on transhumance, secular isolation before the 1948 tunnel, the civil war (the Aran was the last territory reconquered by Franco), winter smuggling via the Toro pass. The original Christ of Salardú is not here but in Salardú itself, which we'll visit tomorrow.

Southwest of the valley, 17 km from Vielha, Bossòst harbors Era Mair de Diu dera Purificacion—one of the finest Romanesque churches in the Val d'Aran, 12th century. Basilica plan with three naves, barrel vaults, and a fragment of wall fresco of the Epiphany on the central apse, dated to the late 13th century. Bossòst is also the last village before the French border, which explains its slightly different atmosphere from the rest of the valley—busier, more commercial too (gasoline and alcohol cheaper than in France).

Follow the C-28 toward Baqueira to Arties, 7 km from Vielha. The church of Santa Maria d'Arties, declared a national monument, is one of five major stops on the Aranese Romanesque Route. Built in the 12th-13th centuries, halfway between Romanesque and Gothic. Inside, the Gothic altarpiece attributed to the Master of Arties depicts scenes from the Virgin's life, and especially: a wall fresco of the Last Judgment—heaven and hell side by side, with a demon devouring a miser still spitting out his gold coins. 13th-century parishioners knew what they were dealing with.

Salardú, 5 km higher (1,267 m), shelters Sant Andreu—fine example of late Romanesque architecture (late 13th century), with Gothic vaults and 17th-century wall frescoes. The masterpiece: the Christ of Salardú, 12th-century polychrome wooden sculpture also from the Erill la Vall workshop, stylistic brother of Vielha's Christ of Mijaran and the works of the Vall de Boí. It's one of the rare cases where the original Christ has remained in situ, where it was carved for. Moving for that reason.

Another 4 km higher and steeper: Bagergue (1,419 m), the highest inhabited village in the Val d'Aran, ranked among Spain's most beautiful villages. A hundred inhabitants, stairways, lanes flowered from April to October, cats everywhere. The church of Sant Feliu (12th-13th centuries), at the village entrance, has the particularity of an altar placed on a Roman funerary stele—itself sculpted in low relief, turned over to serve as a base. An almost unique case of visible reuse.

activities.text: The Fabrica dera Lan, former wool mill operating from the 19th century to 1960, has been converted into a living museum. You see the complete process: from shorn sheep to dyed skeins, through carding, spinning and weaving. Water from the Nere once powered the hydraulic machines. The old wooden cards are still in place, and seasonal demonstrations are well done. Curiously very little visited when it's unique in the valley.

Aranese dinner at Taberna Urtau, right downtown. Tapas and traditional dishes: olha aranesa (the stew that defines the valley's cuisine—beef, hen, bacon, vegetables, simmered three hours), game pâtés, stream trout à la planxa, local cheeses including Tarter de Bagergue, made at 1,419 m. For dessert, ask for crepa aranesa (sort of thick crêpe with honey) or prunes in armagnac. Family atmosphere, reasonable prices for the quality.

Saut deth Pish, in the Varradòs valley, is the Val d'Aran's best-known waterfall—20 m drop, accessible from parking then 5 minutes' walk. Spectacular in spring with snowmelt, a bit thin in August. Can be combined with Uelhs deth Joeu ("devil's eyes" in Aranese) at Es Bòrdes: underground resurgences of the Joeu river in full forest, easy trail amid successive waterfalls. Photo enthusiasts will spend hours there.

To finish the journey beautifully: the Banhs de Tredòs, thermal baths at 1,720 m altitude—the highest in Europe. Sulfurous water at 39°C, outdoor pools in full nature, view of snow-capped peaks. 8 km from Salardú, access by forest track the last kilometers. Rate around €25 per session. Imagine the scene: neck in hot water, white peaks around, not a sound. It's exactly what you hope to find at the end of a Pyrenean week.

map_keywords: Mair de Diu dera Purificacion Bossòst

visits.text: Vigo, Galicia's largest city (300,000 inhabitants), isn't the most beautiful at first glance—industrial port, tower blocks, traffic. But the Casco Vello (old quarter) sloping down to the port, with its cobbled lanes and oyster bars, retains a rough charm. Rúa Pescadería, called 'calle de las Ostras', is where women ('ostreiras') have sold oysters directly from the fish market since 1860—€12 a dozen, opened in front of you, no fuss. Allow 1h for the quarter and tasting.

The Castelo do Castro, on the hill of the same name at 147 m, dominates the entire Vigo bay. 17th-century fortress built on remains of a Celtic castro (Iron Age fortified village). Ramparts, moats, ruins of Civil War bunkers. The surrounding park offers the most complete panoramic view of the ría: bridges, islands, mussel farms in parallel lines. Free access, free parking. Perfect at sunset. Allow 1h.

The Museo do Mar de Galicia, 3 km from the centre in a former Alcabre sardine cannery, tells 2,000 years of Galician maritime history—13th-19th century whaling (Vigo was a major whaling port), evolution of sardine canneries, Galician emigration to the Americas (over a million departures between 1850 and 1960). Basement aquarium with ría species. Allow 1h30, €3. Perfect for rainy days.

activities.text: Vigo's key attraction is the Illas Cíes—an archipelago of three islands 14 km offshore, designated national park since 2002. Rodas beach (1.5 km of white sand, turquoise water) voted the world's most beautiful beach by the Guardian in 2007. Access only by boat (Mar de Ons, departure from Estación Marítima de Vigo, €19 return, 45 minutes). Quota of 1,800 visitors per day in summer—authorisation mandatory, request 5-7 days ahead at autorizacionsillasatlanticas.xunta.gal. Marked trails on the island, western lighthouse with Atlantic views. Full day.

If Cíes is full or off-season, the gastronomic tour of O Berbés (old port, under the metal bridge) remains the best evening in Vigo. Tabernas Otilio (rúa Carral) for zorza (paprika-marinated pork) at €8, Bar Eligio (rúa Cesteiros) for oysters at €1.50 each and red ribeiro in bowls. Expect €20-25 per person with 4-5 bars. Avoid the tourist chains on rúa Velazquez Moreno. Rúa Real, parallel, is more authentic.

days_plan.itin_title: Galicia Road Trip: Santiago de Compostela and the Coast of Death
Galicia: Complete 10-Day Circuit

visits.text: MACVAC occupies the Palau del Batlle, a 15th-century Valencian Gothic palace. Since 1972, it has exhibited over 700 works of contemporary art—including pieces by Miró—donated by the artists themselves.
The Roca Grossa dominates the village. This monumental pink limestone boulder supports several traditional houses and defines Vilafamés' visual identity.
Climb up to the castle perched at the summit. Conquered by James I in 1233, it blends Roman, Moorish, and Christian remains. The circular tower and cistern are its most striking features.
The lanes of Arab origin wind between ochre facades. The El Cuartijo quarter, enclosed by walls, preserves its medieval layout intact.
The Church of the Assumption dates from the late 16th century. Its baroque style is enhanced by frescoes by the Guilló family and a remarkable main altarpiece.
The Abric del Castell shelters UNESCO-listed schematic rock paintings. These panels with religious motifs bear witness to the prehistoric art of the Levant.

activities.text: Artistic cafés in the village attract painters and sculptors. Settle onto a terrace to watch visitors drift between galleries and workshops.
Every lane offers a photogenic scene. Pink stone houses, pots of geraniums, and vistas across the countryside compose natural tableaux.
Vilafamés produces wines of Castellón. Several village cellars offer tastings of Mediterranean grape varieties grown on the surrounding hillsides.
The Fuente de las Picas is a cascading fountain surrounded by ancient olive trees. The spot combines cool shade with panoramic views of the old town.

days_plan.itin_title: Castellón and Maestrazgo Route: Castles and Coves
Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages

places.name: Vilafranca Del Penedes

visits.text: VINSEUM, housed in the medieval palace of the Kings of the Crown of Aragon, is one of the world's oldest active wine museums—inaugurated as the Museum of Wine in 1943, Spain's first. The visit descends from the third floor through six themed spaces down to the tavern, concluding with a tasting included in the ticket. Don't miss: Àngel Escudé's collection of 100,000 wine labels from around the world, part of a total holding of 150,000 references—unique in Europe. Allow 90 minutes.

The Basilica of Santa Maria de Vilafranca, pure 14th-century Catalan Gothic, lacks VINSEUM's appeal but rewards a 20-minute detour. Vaults soar to 30 meters, partially preserved original stained glass. The bell tower climb (€3) reaches a viewpoint overlooking the Penedès sea of vineyards—on clear days you can spot Montserrat 40 km north. Typically empty on weekdays.

Head to Sant Sadurní d'Anoia, world capital of cava 12 km away. Over 60 producers coexist in this town of 12,000, their kilometers of underground galleries sheltering millions of bottles aging on racks. Codorníu, founded in 1551, is Spain's oldest family business, and here Josep Raventós crafted the first Spanish cava in 1872, from macabeu, xarel·lo, and parellada grapes. The buildings designed by Puig i Cadafalch, Gaudí's contemporary, have been a national historic monument since 1976.

Bodegas Torres in Pacs del Penedès is Spain's largest winery and has remained family-owned since 1870—five generations. Guided tour with tasting: €25, electric train to traverse the cellars (really), three wines with commentary at the end. Renowned for reds, but this is also where Sangre de Toro was created in 1954, one of the world's first wine brands. Booking essential.

activities.text: Tour and tasting at Codorníu, 90 minutes, €24. A little electric train takes you into underground cellars 30 meters deep—85% humidity, constant 14°C temperature, perfect for secondary fermentation. The network extends 30 km, equivalent to the distance from Vilafranca to Barcelona. The visit ends with two flutes of cava and the opportunity to buy bottles at producer prices, roughly 30% cheaper than retail.

If you're here the last Sunday of August or anytime in October, don't miss the grape harvest festivals (Festes de la Verema)—Vilafranca has celebrated its vendanges since 1948 with castellers, wine markets, and free tastings in the square. Otherwise, dinner at Cal Ton, an award-winning local institution, market-driven single menu €32, Penedès wine pairing extra. Booking advised.

days_plan.itin_title: Catalonia - Complete 24-Day Grand Tour

map_keywords: Basílica de Santa Maria Vilafranca del Penedès

places.name: Villafranca Del Bierzo

visits.text: Villafranca del Bierzo owes its name to the French pilgrims who settled here in the 11th century at King Alfonso VI's invitation—'villa francorum' became Villafranca. The Cluniac monks the king brought from Occitania founded Santa María de Cluniaco and introduced viticulture to the valley. The Romanesque church of Santiago, built between the 12th and 13th centuries, is one of only two in the world—along with the Cathedral of Santiago de Compostela—to possess a Puerta del Perdón (Door of Pardon): pilgrims who could not physically continue received here the same indulgences as at Compostela. Pope Callixtus III granted this extraordinary privilege, acknowledging that the climb to the Puerto de Cebreiro was the Camino's most formidable stretch.

The castle of the Marquises of Villafranca, a 16th-century construction with massive lateral towers, dominates the town from above. Part is still inhabited—you can't visit inside, but the view from its surroundings over the confluence of the Burbia and Valcarce rivers is magnificent. In 1822, Villafranca briefly became capital of Bierzo province—a status that lasted only a decade.

Calle del Agua is the town's historic spine: an arcaded street lined with Renaissance and Baroque palaces, a 15th-century Mudéjar manor, and several convents. It's now an open-air heraldry museum—the coats of arms carved on façades tell five centuries of Bierzo noble families. The old town has been classified as a Cultural Heritage Site.

activities.text: Tasting Bierzo wines—the protected designation produces reds from mencía, a grape unique to this region, prized for its notes of red fruit and volcanic minerality. The vineyards cling to the valley's slate-covered slopes. Several producers offer tastings at their estates around Villafranca.

Dinner around Plaza Mayor to try botillo del Bierzo—a local butcher describes it as 'smoked pork ribs and backbone, stuffed into the stomach.' Served boiled with vegetables, it's the region's emblematic dish, recognized with protected geographical indication status.

days_plan.itin_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of León Province

visits.text: Trapped between Alicante and Benidorm, La Vila Joiosa chose to be itself. Those red, orange, and yellow façades overlooking the Amadorio River? Fishermen painted them with leftover boat paint so they could spot their homes from offshore. The 15th-century Gothic church-fortress of the Assumption and the 16th-century ramparts complete a tour of the old quarter in 45 minutes.

activities.text: Valor chocolate: a family factory founded in Villajoyosa in the 19th century, now in its fifth generation. The Valor chocolate museum offers free tours but with very limited spots—online booking essential. Fans of fish auctions can also attend the port's sale, Monday through Friday at 5 PM.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

places.name: Villanueva De Los Infantes

visits.text: Quevedo died here in 1645, in a cell at the convent of Santo Domingo. His remains were discovered in 2007 in the crypt of the Iglesia de San Andrés on the Plaza Mayor. To see them, stand on the glass floor panel in the chapel: the light switches on automatically. Historic-Artistic Site since 1974.

The neoclassical Plaza Mayor of Villanueva de los Infantes ranks among the most harmonious in Castilla-La Mancha. The arcaded town hall, the classicist façade of San Andrés (1612), and the coat-of-arms townhouses form an intact Golden Age stage set. The Complutense University has identified this village as the 'Lugar de La Mancha' from Don Quixote.
Head to the Casa del Arco, whose neoclassical portico is the town's most spectacular. The building now houses a hotel, and from inside you can access the cell where Quevedo spent his final days.

activities.text: Villanueva is the capital of the Campo de Montiel, Don Quixote's homeland. The town boasts more noble coat-of-arms per capita than almost anywhere else in Spain. Wander the lanes to spot them.

days_plan.itin_title: Campo de Calatrava and Valle de Alcudia in 5 Days

map_keywords: Villanueva de los Infantes Ciudad Real

visits.text: The Treasure of Villena: 66 Bronze Age objects discovered in 1963 in a gravel pit. This cache, displayed in the José María Soler Archaeological Museum in the town hall, is the most important find of prehistoric goldwork on the Iberian Peninsula. The collection includes bracelets, cups, and bottles in gold, silver, amber, and iron—including two pieces of meteoric iron, the oldest known in the peninsula.

The castle proudly dominates this overlooked town, which works out well for those who stumble upon it. The Castillo de la Atalaya, of 12th-century Almohad origin, preserves a four-story keep with cross-vaulted ceilings—a unique type of construction among Spanish castles. Declared a Historic-Artistic Monument in 1931. Guided tour recommended to understand its full history.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa Blanca & Alicante Hinterland: 12 days

places.name: Viso Del Marques

visits.text: An Italian Renaissance palace in the heart of La Mancha: the Palacio del Marqués de Santa Cruz conceals behind its austere façade 8,000 m² of Mannerist frescoes painted by Italian artists between 1564 and 1588. When Philip II saw it, he hired the same Italians for the Escorial balustrades. National Monument since 1931.

Álvaro de Bazán, first Marquis of Santa Cruz and admiral of the Spanish Navy, built this palace between the Madrid court and his operational ports. He fought at Lepanto in 1571. The chapel houses his funerary statues and those of his wife María de Figueroa. The palace also hosts the Naval Historical Archive.
The courtyard frescoes span naval battles, mythological scenes, and city views. Pompeian red dominates. The guided tour, led by a local guide, offers the best way to decode the iconographic program.

activities.text: The palace served as a set for the film Captain Alatriste starring Viggo Mortensen. After your visit, detour to the Ermita-Santuario de las Virtudes, a few kilometers away, to see the Baroque frescoes in its camarín.

visits.text: Xàtiva is the town most travellers miss between Valencia and the Costa Blanca. That's a mistake: its double castle—one Iberian, the other Arab-medieval—commands the plain from a 120-meter ridge and can be visited in two hours. The lower town preserves several intact Gothic palaces.

The portrait of Philip V hangs upside down in the municipal museum—symbolic revenge for the city's burning in 1707 ordered by that same king. This small detail of iconographic defiance sums up the city's pride better than any explanatory panel.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Valencia Road Trip: 11 days Through Medieval Villages

places.name: Yaiza

visits.text: Timanfaya National Park is Lanzarote's volcanic heart—and one of Europe's most surreal landscapes. Between September 1, 1730 and April 16, 1736, dozens of volcanoes erupted successively for six years, burying 167 km² of the island, destroying 420 houses, and forcing half the population to emigrate. The 5,107-hectare park was created in 1974 to protect this intact lunar landscape. The visit begins with the Route of the Volcanoes: a 14 km bus circuit through craters, lava tunnels, and red ridges, with multilingual commentary. Arrive early—parking fills fast and the last Route of the Volcanoes entry is at 5 PM.

At Islote de Hilario, don't miss the geothermal demonstrations: staff thrust brushwood into a simple hole in the ground—it ignites within seconds. Then a bucket of water poured into a pipe erupts in a geyser of boiling steam, because the earth's crust is only two meters thick here and temperatures exceed 400°C below. At 27 meters depth, near the main crater, the thermometer reads 700°C. Since 2013, NASA astronauts have trained in this park—the basaltic soil geologically resembles Mars.

Restaurant El Diablo, designed by César Manrique above a natural volcanic chimney, is the world's only restaurant that cooks meat and fish using geothermal heat—at about 400°C, via a grill positioned over a five-meter pit. The volcanic stone and glass architecture blends into the landscape so seamlessly it's almost invisible from the road. Note that this restaurant doesn't take reservations.

activities.text: Dromedary ride at Echadero de Camellos, just outside the park gates. Camels were long essential to Lanzarote's agriculture, and a small museum on-site traces their history. The 20-minute ride takes you up the volcano flanks—an unexpected perspective on the sea of lava.

Detour to Los Hervideros in late afternoon: a stretch of volcanic coastline where Atlantic waves surge into caves and chimneys formed by the 1730-1736 lava flows. The effect is especially spectacular when the sea is rough. Just nearby, El Golfo's green lagoon—an emerald lake nestled in a former crater open to the sea—offers one of the island's strangest landscapes.

End the day in Yaiza, considered Lanzarote's prettiest village. Its flower-filled white houses and manicured streets contrast with the lava fields surrounding it. The Iglesia Nuestra Señora de los Remedios dates to the 17th century. It's also the best sunset viewpoint over the Montañas del Fuego—the red light on black lava is stunning.

days_plan.itin_title: Lanzarote & La Palma: Where Fire Shaped Art and Nature

map_keywords: Parc national Timanfaya Montañas del Fuego
Iglesia Nuestra Señora de los Remedios Yaiza

visits.text: Castillo de Yeste, planted on Cerro del Águila, dates back to the 11th century under Islamic rule and was reconquered by Castile in 1242, then passed to the Order of Santiago. Its Gothic parade ground today houses a Medieval Interpretation Centre 'Tierra de Frontera' with seven visitable halls: Order of Santiago, watchtowers, Muslim life, armoury. Entry is modest; the audio guide is worth the detour.

Head down to the Iglesia de la Asunción, the first Gothic fortress-church style of the entire comarca, from the 16th century, with a Renaissance façade with Corinthian columns and statues of Saint Peter and Saint Paul. Worth seeing if it's open—hours vary. The village also retains some noble houses and an intact frontier-town atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilla-La Mancha: Windmills and Sierra in 10 Days

places.name: Zafra

visits.text: Zafra is nicknamed "little Seville" for its white streets, flower-filled patios, and Andalusian atmosphere—but it's stayed far quieter than its big neighbor. Start with the Alcázar de los Duques de Feria, a 15th-century fortress flanked by nine crenellated towers, now converted to a Parador. Hernán Cortés stayed here before sailing off to conquer Mexico. Even if you're not spending the night, step inside to see the arcaded interior courtyard and coffered ceilings—Parador staff are used to curious visitors.

Zafra has two adjoining squares forming the town's heart. Plaza Grande, vast and airy, is lined with porticoes sheltering cafés and terraces. Plaza Chica, more intimate, was the medieval market site—the stone columns bordering it still bear the marks of standard measures carved by merchants to verify weights and lengths. The covered passage connecting the two squares is a charming detail.

The Colegiata de la Candelaria, from the 15th century, is Zafra's main church. Its main altarpiece is impressive and its position on Plaza Grande makes it a natural landmark. Just next door, the Convento de Santa Clara (15th century) shelters remarkable altarpieces and wrought-iron grilles.

Enter Zafra through the Puerta de Jerez, one of Extremadura's finest town gates. From there, wander the historic center's streets, declared a Bien de Interés Cultural—the whitewashed palaces, wrought-iron balconies, and patios glimpsed through half-open doors justify the town's nickname. In the evening, the atmosphere on Plaza Grande's terraces is delightful.

Dedicate the day to Olivenza, 85 km northwest of Zafra. It's one of Spain's most unusual places: a Portuguese town on Spanish soil. Founded by the Templars in the 13th century, it remained Portuguese for five centuries (1297–1801) before being annexed by Spain during the brief War of the Oranges. Portugal has never recognized this annexation—officially, Olivenza is still claimed by Lisbon. Result: black-and-white Portuguese-style cobblestones, Manueline architecture, and azulejos coexist with Spanish signage. Since 2010, street names are posted in both languages.

The Igreja de Santa María Magdalena is Olivenza's masterpiece and the finest example of Manueline architecture outside Portugal—after Lisbon's Jerónimos Monastery. Commissioned by King Manuel I in 1510 and completed in 1560, it has three naves resting on eight twisted granite columns typical of Manueline style. The walls are covered with 18th-century blue-and-white azulejos, and six baroque altarpieces complete the ensemble. In 2012, Spanish internet users voted it "Spain's most beautiful corner"—a delicious paradox for an utterly Portuguese monument.

Olivenza's castle, built by the Templars then reinforced under Portuguese kings, has a keep tower 37 meters high—the tallest on the entire Spanish-Portuguese border. Erected in 1488 on flat terrain, it had to compensate through height what the land didn't provide in natural defense. It's accessed by a ramp (no stairs)—so it's accessible to all. From the top, the panorama sweeps across the plain to the Guadiana and Portugal. The castle also houses the Museo Etnográfico González Santana, one of Extremadura's richest ethnographic museums.

Stop by the Santa Casa de la Misericordia, founded in 1501 by King Manuel I himself. The chapel's interior walls are covered with 18th-century Portuguese azulejos depicting Old and New Testament scenes—a remarkable collection. The town hall on Plaza de la Constitución preserves a Manueline doorway that's the town symbol.

activities.text: Zafra sits at the heart of Tierra de Barros, Extremadura's main wine region. Tour operators offer bodega visits in the area, especially toward Almendralejo (30 km), the region's "wine capital." Extremadura wines, long underrated, have improved tremendously—try reds from tempranillo and garnacha, and whites from pardina and cayetana.

If you prefer staying in town, Zafra hosts a lively flea market on Thursdays in Plaza Grande, a direct legacy of the medieval fairs that made the town prosperous. The Feria Internacional Ganadera (livestock fair) in September–October is one of Lower Extremadura's major events—if you're there then, don't miss it.

Olivenza is a gastronomic border post: you'll eat migas extremeñas alongside bacalhau Portuguese-style, and convent pastries blend traditions from both countries. Lunch in the historic center and taste both cuisines—it's the only place in Spain where that's possible in the same village.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas
Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas

map_keywords: Alcázar de los Duques de Feria Zafra

visits.text: In late December 1481, Nasrid warriors scaled Zahara's walls by night and retook control - an act that triggered the final war for Granada. In 1483, Rodrigo Ponce de León recaptured it for the Catholic Monarchs. The Nasrid castle's Torre del Homenaje still overlooks the turquoise reservoir. Steep but short climb: 20 minutes from the village.

Climb up to the church of Santa María de la Mesa to see the village from within - its white roofs crowded above the reservoir water, with the sierra as backdrop. Only takes a few minutes, it's a waypoint between the main street and the castle path.

activities.text: The turquoise of the reservoir appears between white houses at every street corner - it's this contrast that gives Zahara its reputation. A swim in the reservoir lake is possible from the banks accessible on foot from the village. In April-May, the water stays cold but the setting is incomparable.

days_plan.itin_title: White Villages: Arcos, Grazalema, Ronda - 6 Days

visits.text: Walk the 5 km of sand facing Africa and stop at the Castillo de las Almadrabas, a ruined watchtower that reminds you Zahara lived exclusively from tuna fishing before tourism. The village is small, with no monuments to speak of—its beauty lies in the wide, preserved beach, the white houses, and the off-season silence. In high season, the population jumps from 2,000 to 20,000.

The Iglesia de Nuestra Señora del Carmen, built between the 16th and 17th centuries, is Zahara's patron. Small, unadorned, it sums up this village's relationship with the sea: sober and direct. Calle Marqués de Montemar opposite concentrates the few local craft shops—mojama (salt-cured dried tuna) vacuum-packed, worth taking home.

activities.text: A simple fishing village, but tuna is treated like religion here. The almadraba—a maze of nets fixed to the seabed to trap tuna migrating toward the Mediterranean—has been practiced on these coasts for over 3,000 years, a Phoenician technique perpetuated by the Arabs. The season runs from April to June. El Campero in Barbate (20 minutes) is the regional reference for gastronomic tuna cuisine. In Zahara itself, beachfront chiringuitos offer the simpler, equally honest version.

days_plan.itin_title: Costa de la Luz: 7 Days from Seville to Tarifa

places.name: Zalamea De La Serena

visits.text: The Dístylo of Zalamea, erected in AD 102 in honour of Trajan, stands on the Plaza de la Constitución. 23.23 m high, two columns, the only preserved example in Europe. The other survivors are in Syria. Free and visible from the street, impossible to miss.

The ochre walls of Cancho Roano emerge from a moat that holds water year-round. This Tartessian palace-sanctuary, occupied from the 6th to the 4th century BC, is the best-preserved on the Iberian Peninsula. The bull-hide-shaped altar, reproduced at the nearby interpretation centre, evokes a cult of Astarte. 6 km north of the village.

The Casa de Pedro Crespo, character from Calderón de la Barca's El alcalde de Zalamea, is a bourgeois house in the centre. The link between the play and this village is disputed by historians, but the Zalameños hold fast to it. A quick glance in passing.

Nebrija stayed here, at the Palacio de Don Juan de Zúñiga, Extremadura's first literary court in the 15th century. It was in these surroundings that he worked on his Castilian grammar. The Renaissance façade survives; the rest is private. Zalamea was then a cultural hub, surprising given its current size.

activities.text: Reach Cancho Roano by the road to Quintana de la Serena. The on-site interpretation centre presents a model, Phoenician objects (caballito pasarriendas, necklaces), and detailed panels. The large 2,000 m² shelter has protected the site since 1988. Check opening hours before you leave.
If you climb up to the Castillo de Arribalavilla, above the village, the view stretches across all La Serena. Modest ruins but a formidable position. Free access, steep 15-minute climb.

days_plan.itin_title: La Siberia and La Serena: Secret Extremadura in 6 Days

map_keywords: Roman Dístylo Zalamea de la Serena

places.name: Zamora

visits.text: On the road from Salamanca, recommended stop at Toro—30 km detour via N-122. PDO Toro vineyards and the Romanesque-Byzantine Collegiate Church of Santa María la Mayor from the 12th century, with golden octagonal façade. If time is tight, go straight to Zamora. Otherwise, quick tasting at Bodegas Liberalia or Estancia Piedra is enough to understand why Toro wines have been rising for 20 years: tannic tinta de toro, lots of structure, perfect with Castilian meats.

Zamora is nicknamed "the pearl of the Duero" and "the city of Romanesque art." With 24 Romanesque churches from the 12th-13th centuries, it's the world's greatest urban concentration of Romanesque. The cathedral, founded by Alfonso VII, consecrated in 1175 after 25 years of construction, is a unique blend of Romanesque, Byzantine, and Herreran. Its lantern tower—the cimborio—is its signature piece: a 16-sided dome covered with stone scales like a Japanese mushroom, inspired by Constantinople's Hagia Sophia via the Crusaders. Don't miss the Bishop's Door, one of Spain's finest Romanesque portals.

The cathedral museum preserves one of the world's most important collections of Flemish tapestries—12 pieces from the 15th and 16th centuries, workshops of Arras, Tournai, and Brussels. The Trojan War series (1480), 4 by 7 meters each, recounts the fate of Hector and Achilles with a thousand figures embroidered in gold thread. Acquired by Zamoran bishops at mind-boggling prices, they survived the Napoleonic Wars and the Civil War. It's the textile equivalent of the Sistine Chapel—and nobody queues here.

Five minutes on foot, the 11th-century castle, restored, today houses a public park—free entry, panoramic view over the Duero and stone bridge. Further on, the Church of San Pedro y San Ildefonso (12th century) guards the tomb of Saint Ildefonsus, Archbishop of Toledo in the 7th century, whose relics were transferred here in the 12th to protect them from the Moors. La Magdalena, on Calle Damas, has an exceptionally ornate Romanesque portal—one of Spain's finest, finely worked with floral motifs and figures.

The stone bridge—Puente de Piedra—is Zamora's symbol. Built in the 12th century on the remains of a Roman bridge, 16 arches, 320 meters long. It was the obligatory Duero crossing for 800 years. From here you get the best view of the old town's silhouette at dusk. Locals do their evening jog here and stop to watch lights come on one by one on the façades. Walk along the banks (Senda del Duero) to the Aceñas de Olivares, ancient 12th-century water mills.

activities.text: If you're here during Holy Week (Easter), Zamora's is one of Spain's most impressive—declared of international tourist interest. 17 brotherhoods, 50,000 nazarenos parading in pointed hoods through silent streets, lit only by their candles. The Cristo de las Injurias, 16th-century sculpture, processes in absolute silence at 11:30pm on Holy Wednesday—even coughing forbidden. Off-season, the Holy Week Museum (being transferred to a new building, check opening hours) shows the frozen pasos.

The Ethnographic Museum of Castilla y León, 5 minutes from the center, is one of northern Spain's most surprising. Four thematic floors—space, time, forms, body—tracing Castilian rural lifestyles before industrialization. Tools, costumes, transhumance objects, toys; modern museography far from dusty. Count 1.5 hours. A museum you leave feeling you've learned something, which isn't so common in the provinces.

days_plan.itin_title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria
Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks

map_keywords: Church of San Pedro y San Ildefonso Zamora
Ethnographic Museum Castilla y León Zamora

places.name: Zaragoza

visits.text: The Basilica del Pilar, Spain's largest baroque sanctuary, stretches 130 meters along the Ebro riverbank. Its eleven domes and four towers dominate the Plaza del Pilar. Inside, two cupolas bear frescoes by the young Goya, a native of the region: the Adoration of the Name of God and Regina Martyrum.

Take the elevator to the top of the Pilar tower, 62 meters high: the panorama sweeps across Zaragoza's rooftops, the ribbon of the Ebro, and distant mountain ranges. Below, the Holy Chapel houses the jasper column where the Virgin is said to have appeared to the apostle James in the year 40 AD.

La Seo del Salvador, Zaragoza's oldest cathedral, layers seven centuries of architecture on the site of the ancient Roman forum and later mosque. Its exterior Mudéjar wall, a UNESCO World Heritage site, is a tapestry of brick and glazed ceramics in geometric patterns. Inside, the Gothic polychrome alabaster altarpiece ranks among Europe's finest.

The La Seo Tapestry Museum preserves one of the world's finest collections of Flemish tapestries, woven between the 15th and 17th centuries. Biblical and mythological scenes unfold in remarkable condition.

The Aljafería Palace, built in the 11th century as the "Palace of Joy" by King Al-Muqtadir, is the northernmost Islamic palace in Europe. Its polylobed arches, the Patio de Santa Isabel with its orange trees, and the Golden Hall form—together with the Alhambra and Córdoba's Mosque—the triad of Hispano-Muslim architecture.

Cross the Puente de Piedra, a 15th-century stone bridge spanning the Ebro. From the opposite bank, the view of the Basilica del Pilar reflected in the river composes Zaragoza's most iconic image, especially striking at dusk.

activities.text: Dive into El Tubo, the labyrinth of narrow downtown alleys dedicated to tapas. Each bar has its specialty: borage in béchamel, Aragonese migas, ternasco lamb. The ritual: one glass of wine and one tapa per counter, then move on.

The Fountain of Hispanidad, on Plaza del Pilar, represents Latin America in the shape of an inverted map. In the evening, the square comes alive and the illuminated basilica reflects in the fountain's basin.

Every October 12th, the Fiesta del Pilar transforms Zaragoza into a week of processions, giant floral offerings to the Virgin, concerts, and fireworks. Off-season, the landscaped banks of the Ebro invite strolls among bridges and gardens.
